Fire watchers spot and report fires. Do you consider their job heroic? Why or why not? How does their job compare to other job controlling and combatting fires? What other jobs do you consider heroic and why?

articles.title: Stop the Atlantic Express!
articles.author: Kate Sharp and Sarvinder Naberhaus
articles.description: In 1881, Kate Shelley warned people about a washed-out railroad track to prevent a train crash.
articles.intro: On July 6, 1881, a storm in Iowa caused a railroad track to be flooded. Seeing this, Kate Shelley took action to ensure that no train would cross the flooded track.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Kate endured to help people who were in danger.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “You can only imagine what a fearful thing it is to see the heavens grow black and blacker until the light of day is all shut out.”
-Kate Shelley, about the events of July 6, 1881
The wind was whipping from all directions, trying to snatch the flapping linens from Kate’s hands. Another Iowa storm was brewing.

Dodging raindrops, Kate ran with an armload of clothes into the farmhouse nestled on the hill above Honey Creek. Thunder rattled the panes as she looked out the window to the railroad tracks below. They reminded her of Father, who had been a railroad man. Kate loved hearing the trains rumble back and forth over Honey Creek Bridge. Every day she heard the trains’ whistle and knew their schedules, when they would arrive and when they would depart.

Fifteen-year-old Kate Shelley knew her family’s survival depended on her. Her father and brother James had died and now her mother was sick. Kate gave up going to school. She sent her younger brother and sisters instead. She tended the crops, fed the animals, and hunted with her dogs in the nearby woods. She was a good shot and protected the family’s livestock from chicken hawks and wolves.

The panes of the little house continued to rattle as the storm invaded the night. Kate thought about the hundreds of Chicago-bound passengers on the Atlantic Express. At midnight they would cross the valley. Would dispatchers send a train out in this weather? With each flash of lightning, Kate watched the creek rise higher — almost touching the tracks of Honey Creek Bridge.
Suddenly, Kate heard the clanging bells of a pusher engine, a loud crash, and the hissing of steam. She knew that the engine must have gone off Honey Creek Bridge.
Kate to the Rescue

Grabbing her lantern, Kate slipped down the muddy hillside, amidst the lightning and crashing thunder, to the washed-out bridge. Peering through the rain, she saw a partially submerged engine and two men clinging to tree branches in the angry water.
Kate shouted above the wind — and listened. The men yelled back, but the roaring water carried their voices away.

Kate would have to make her way through the dark woods to the Moingona train depot. There she could get help and stop the train — the Midnight Express — but first she would have to cross the Des Moines River Bridge.

As Kate finally reached the bridge, the fierce wind snuffed out her lantern. Nearly 700 feet of bridge lay ahead. Would she meet the Midnight Express while crossing? Tired, soaked, and shivering, Kate got down on her hands and knees and began to crawl.

She felt the slippery, splintered boards beneath her hands. She stretched her hand toward the next railroad tie. It was so far away. She tried not to think how easy it would be to slip through to the rushing torrent below.

When she was halfway across the bridge, lightning revealed a giant tree in the river barreling toward her. She raised to her knees, clasped her hands, and prayed. Would the river swallow her? Suddenly, the tree swerved so closely she felt its spray of water.

Finally, Kate inched her way across to ground on the other side. Exhausted, she ran to the train depot. Bursting through the door, she shouted something about stopping the Midnight Express, then collapsed on the floor.

When she recovered, she told the men her story and guided them back to Honey Creek to rescue the survivors. Luckily, the Midnight Express hadn’t arrived. It had stopped farther west because of the storm.
Kate’s Story Spreads

It was early morning before Kate was put to bed, where she stayed for months. The ordeal had made her ill, probably from exhaustion. She kept repeating to her sister Mayme that she could still feel the cold rain on her face.

Kate’s story did not go unnoticed. Mayme later recalled that Kate woke up famous. Newspapers spread her story. And Kate received a medal for her courage along with a lifetime train pass, which she used often.

It has been more than 100 years since Kate’s heroic act, but she is still remembered. By her grave is a memorial tablet placed by the Order of Railway Conductors and Brakemen that reads: “Hers is a deed bound for legend... a story to be told until the last order fades and the last rail rusts.”

options.label: Kate felt that she had a duty to help her family rather than help those who might be injured on the Atlantic Express.
The dangerous route that Kate had to take to get to the train station made her actions more foolish than brave.
Kate risked her own safety to help people in danger and to ensure that a greater disaster did not take place.
Railroad conductors continue to remember Kate's brave actions because without them an entire train of people would have died.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement identifies the central idea of the text?

"There she could get help and stop the train — the Midnight Express — but first she would have to cross the Des Moines River Bridge." (Paragraph 8)
"It was early morning before Kate was put to bed, where she stayed for months. The ordeal had made her ill, probably from exhaustion." (Paragraph 14)
"'Hers is a deed bound for legend... a story to be told until the last order fades and the last rail rusts.'" (Paragraph 16)

options.label: The author discusses how well known Kate is, and then describes her heroic actions.

The author discusses what it means to be a hero, and then provides Kate's actions as an example.

Even though Kate is only fifteen, her family's survival depends on her. How does Kate handle growing up so fast? What situations might children face that would cause them to grow up sooner rather than later?

articles.title: Clash of Cultures: Two Worlds Collide
articles.author: UShistory.org
articles.description: Herán Cortés was a Spanish explorer who conquered the Aztec empire.
articles.intro: The Aztec empire was an advanced civilization that ruled in Mexico before Spanish explorers arrived. This informational text discusses the events that lead to the fall of this great civilization.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Spanish were able to defeat the Aztecs.
articles.permissions: "Clash of Cultures: Two Worlds Collide" is a derivative of USHistory.org, "Clash of Cultures: Two Worlds Collide" by UShistory.org. Copyright © 2017. This text is licensed under CC BY 4.0.

blocks.text: The Aztec empire was large and powerful. It stretched across central Mexico and had a population of 5 million people. Montezuma was the king of the Aztec empire from 1502 to 1520 CE.

During this same time, explorers from Spain came to the Americas. Christopher Columbus was the first Spanish explorer to come to the Americas. He and the other explorers were looking for gold. They also wanted to convert Native Americans to Christianity. Another Spanish explorer to come was Hernán Cortés. He arrived in the Aztec empire in 1519. By 1520 he had killed Montezuma, and in 1521 he conquered the Aztec empire.
How was he able to take control of the Aztec empire so fast?
Cannons vs. Clubs

The Spanish army was the best on earth during this time. It had not lost a single battle for 150 years. The Spaniards had cannons and arquebus (simple guns). These weapons scared the Aztecs because they had never seen them before. The Spanish also had horses. This allowed them to travel easily. The Aztecs didn’t have horses. The Spanish also had strong and deadly swords. The Aztecs’ main weapons were only wooden clubs.

Aztec warriors had several opportunities to kill Cortés. However, it was their custom to capture their enemies alive. Each time they tried to capture Cortés alive he escaped. The Spaniards, on the other hand, killed their enemies as quickly as they could. When an Aztec leader was killed, the other Aztec soldiers lost hope and ran away.
Conflict

Many Aztecs disliked Montezuma because of the taxes he forced them to pay. These Aztecs supported Cortés. When Cortés attacked and conquered the Aztec capital, he had only 900 Spanish soldiers, but he was joined by 150,000 native Aztecs.
Disease

The Europeans also brought with them diseases such as measles and smallpox. The American tribes immediately became sick. The diseases spread quickly and killed many Aztec leaders and millions of other people.
Superstition

Montezuma was also superstitious. When Cortés landed in the Aztec empire, Montezuma thought he might be the feathered snake god, Quetzalcotl. This god was thought to have vanished into the eastern ocean long before. Montezuma half-believed that Cortés was this god returning from the sea. This made Montezuma afraid and respectful of Cortés.
Battle

When Cortés and his army of 500 Spanish soldiers faced off with Montezuma, they had actually been invited into the city. This was a big mistake because Cortés kidnapped Montezuma. Montezuma was killed a couple months later, although it’s not clear who killed him. Cortés then left the city, but came back several months later with a larger army and conquered the city on August 13, 1521.
The combination of good weapons, internal conflict, disease, and superstition helped Cortés conquer the Aztec empire.

options.label: The Spanish had several advantages over the Aztec that led to their defeat.
Cortés was considered a savior by Aztecs, as he freed them from Montezuma's rule.
The Aztecs would have likely kept their empire, if they hadn't trusted Cortés.
Cortés believed he was has helping the Aztecs when he took over their empire.

options.label: "He and the other explorers were looking for gold. They also wanted to convert Native Americans to Christianity." (Paragraph 2)
"Many Aztecs disliked Montezuma because of the taxes he forced them to pay. These Aztecs supported Cortés." (Paragraph 6)
"When Cortés and his army of 500 Spanish soldiers faced off with Montezuma, they had actually been invited into the city." (Paragraph 9)
"The combination of good weapons, internal conflict, disease, and superstition helped Cortés conquer the Aztec empire." (Paragraph 10)

articles.title: Basant Birthday
articles.author: Maya Kanwal
articles.description: A young girl wants to kite fight on her birthday.
articles.intro: Maya Kanwal is a Pakistani-American writer and editor. This story takes place in Pakistan, which is a country in Southern Asia. In this story, a young girl wants to go kite fighting on her birthday. Kite fighting is a centuries old tradition, or cultural custom, that started in India. Girls and women are usually not allowed to take part in kite fighting.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the events in the story help the narrator build courage.
articles.permissions: "Basant Birthday" by Maya Kanwal, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: I sucked in my breath as I stepped into the kite seller’s dingy shop, hidden in the cavernous bazaar of Lahore. From floor to ceiling, multicolored kites were stacked in precarious piles. Others lit up the walls like neon signs. Some hung with their noses up, golden reflectors flashing, like battleships ready for takeoff.

It was February, and basant, spring was all around us in Pakistan. Basant brings rain showers to dance in, acres of mustard fields in yellow bloom, and festivals. In Lahore, we wear clothes the color of mustard flowers, while our grandmothers labor over cauldrons of curried mustard leaves, preparing pungent, soul-warming saag. And we fly kites.
But we don’t just fly kites — we fight them!
I ached to send up my own fighter kite, cut away my opponent’s string, and yell, “Bo kata! There, I cut it!” And this year, I would finally get the chance to try!

Crowds of excited visitors jammed the streets outside as I gazed at the kite shop’s selection. Honking horns and whooping yells sounded like war cries for the kite fighting that would begin later that day.

How many kites would I cut down? I could fly a kite as well as any other nine-year-old. But every basant, once my brother Amir and his friends began the rooftop kite fighting, I had to tear myself away and trail downstairs behind the other girls. Changing into my new silk clothes, I would join my mother at the party we always hosted.

This year my ninth birthday fell on the festival weekend, and for my present, I’d asked if I could stay on the roof with the boys. My surprised parents had laughed at first, but then handed me a wad of rupees to go with Amir to buy my very own stack of fighter kites.

“A little sharla for the missy?” the kite seller snickered at me, his craggy fingers busy bending bamboo splints. “Or do you deserve a pretty patang?” He dangled a round butterfly kite in front of me.
Amir frowned at the kite seller. “Give her some real fighter kites, the guddas. We’ll also need some good string,” he said, “not the cheap stuff.”
“All right, little fighters. Come with me, then.” The kite seller ducked through a door in the back of the shop. We followed him into a narrow alleyway.
Two men were looping string around a pair of poles. A third man, fingers thickly bandaged, was coating the string with glittering yellow paste.
“This is the manjha that will make your string sharp enough to cut down other kites,” Amir explained to me.
“I know what manjha is for!” I replied. “But what’s it made of?”
“It’s rice paste and ground glass,” replied the kite seller, rubbing his hands together with glee, “mixed with some of my secret ingredients.”

Back inside the shop, the kite seller wrapped our kites and string in waterproof bags. We wandered back out to the bazaar to join our family at the food stalls. As I scooped up saag with hot bread, I realized I had forgotten all about the bangles I’d wanted to buy to match my new dress. Not that I cared now — I had battles to fight.

As soon as we got home, we rushed to the rooftop. The city below was dressed up like a bride. Cheery fabric adorned every building and strings of bright flags flapped across streets and alleys. Drums were booming and bugles started to wail all around us.

People would send up their reflective white kites at twilight, when giant searchlights began to flood the dark sky. But while the sun still shone, thousands of multicolored kites swayed in the sky like a joyous swarm of birds.

I warmed up with my sharlas first, letting out the strings in short jerks. They all got cut away in no time, plummeting to the street to be chased down by rowdy children. I teetered back and forth, trying to protect my kites.
“Oy!” Amir grabbed my arm. “Stop running around like a chicken or you’ll fall off the roof.”
I sighed. How would I ever cut down another kite if I couldn’t take my eyes off my own?
Then our neighbors on the opposite roof challenged Amir to a duel.

“All right! With the rules!” Amir jumped up to trace out a rectangle with chalk. I watched him calmly maneuver his kite upward, leaning back, keeping his body still and strong, never stepping out of the rectangle. His fingers and right elbow did all the work. Then his kite swooped down like a hawk, slashing through the neighbors’ string. Amir danced a victory jig to the beat of the drums pounding below on the street.

“Still and strong,” I told myself. “Still and strong.” My gudda caught the wind and became one with it. I fell into a rhythm and began swaying with my kite. In my trance, I forgot to worry about being cut down.

Then I found my kite flapping close to Amir’s. Slowly, I let the string out, the kite soaring higher with each tug. Squinting to see the two strings, I tugged as they touched. Up and down, swiftly! Amir’s kite spiraled down, and he wheeled about, surprise twinkling in his eyes.
“Bo kata!” I yelled.
“No more teaching,” he replied. “Now we fight.”
I was ready!

options.label: Boys are better at kite fighting.

It takes money to achieve your goals. E. Holidays should be celebrated yearly. F. It takes courage to go after what you want.

articles.title: The Bear and the Two Travelers
articles.author: Aesop
articles.description: A traveler abandons his friend in the face of danger.
articles.intro: Aesop was a storyteller who lived in ancient Greece between 620 and 560 BCE. This story is part of his collection of tales known as "Aesop's Fables," which did not survive in writing but were passed down by people retelling them. They have deeply influenced children's literature and modern storytelling culture.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the travelers react to the bear and what that reveals about them.
articles.permissions: "The Bear and the Two Travelers" by Aesop is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Two men were traveling together, when a Bear suddenly met them on their path. One of them climbed up quickly into a tree and concealed himself in the branches. The other, seeing that he must be attacked, fell flat on the ground, and when the Bear came up and felt him with his snout, and smelt him all over, he held his breath, and feigned the appearance of death as much as he could. The Bear soon left him, for it is said he will not touch a dead body. When he was quite gone, "Cinnamon Black Bear" by Valerie is licensed the other Traveler descended from the tree, 3 under CC BY-NC-ND 2.0. and jocularly inquired of his friend what it was the Bear had whispered in his ear. “He gave me this advice,” his companion replied. “Never travel with a friend who deserts you at the approach of danger.”

options.label: People can only rely on themselves in times of trouble.
Smart and resourceful people accomplish more than cowards.
True friends do not abandon each other in hard times.
Being betrayed by a friend is worse than being betrayed by an enemy.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence best describes a major theme of the story?

options.label: "Two men were traveling together, when a Bear suddenly met them on their path."

"The Bear soon left him, for it is said he will not touch a dead body."

articles.title: Life of a Slave on a Southern Plantation
articles.author: BirdBrain History
articles.description: The United States' history of owning slaves in the South to work on plantations is discussed.
articles.intro: In this informational text, the United States' history of enslaving black people in the South is discussed. No matter the role a slave possessed on a plantation, they were mistreated and considered to be the property of white plantation owners.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, consider why it is important to remember the moments in history we are not proud of.
articles.permissions: "Life of a Slave on a Southern Plantation" by BirdBrain History. Copyright © 2016 by BirdBrain. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: It’s important to know how your ancestors behaved... how they acted or how they were treated. For much of America’s history, many black people were thought of as the property of white people. It’s an uncomfortable truth that we need to talk about in order to understand how America became what it is today and also how our past changes how we act today. So come, let’s get uncomfortable for a little while. "Southern Slaves" by BirdBrain History is used Why did people keep slaves? Before the with permission. 1900’s, people in America made black people work for them for free so that they could make new goods without spending much money, making businesses in the South grow very fast. A plantation is a large piece of land that grows plants like coffee, tobacco, or cotton with a large number of people working. In the 1700s and 1800s, many of these had mostly slaves working on them. One plantation could be thousands of acres and had dozens if not hundreds of slaves who were made to work against their wills. It was an ugly business, and many Americans, especially those living in the South, did not see anything wrong with it. It was easier for them to overlook their bad behavior because so much money was rolling in. They also thought of people from Africa as being less than whites. In order to make sure the money kept rolling in, the government made laws that would keep slaves working hard and stop them from getting away. Slave codes were laws that controlled what slaves were allowed to do and what their masters could do to them. This was anything from the number of lashes a master could give the slaves to whether slaves could trade with 1. An acre is a measure of land area and is slightly smaller than the size of a football field. other slaves without their master saying they could. The government said that owning other people was okay and that treating them in some ways was okay. This led slave-owners to make some very scary choices. When people make other people work against their wills, there is going to be some push back. On large plantations, they needed someone to make sure these slaves worked as hard as they could. An overseer is a white man who is paid to keep an eye on the slaves on the plantation to make sure they work as hard as they can. It was often this person’s job to give punishments if the masters were unhappy with the slaves’ work. They used whips, which if used with enough force, would tear open the slaves’ skin. Overseers became so used to whipping a slave that they stopped thinking about the pain they were creating. It was as easy to them as clipping a nail. Their goal was to get slaves to work as hard as possible without overworking them or killing them.

Things were not easy on the plantation. Inside the master’s household, things could be a lot better... or a lot worse. Domestic slaves were people owned as property who worked in the home, instead of the field. This means they were given more comfortable places to sleep and better food. Some of them thought of themselves as better than the slaves in the field. But as well as the masters treated them, the domestic slaves were often reminded that they were still property. They had no more rights than a dog or a broom. The plantation might have meant harder work, but slaves could be alone or with their friends and families. In the house, slaves were always under the eye of their master and mistress. Knowing your ancestors’ history is important. Thinking about how their choices changed life today is important. Plantation slavery made America’s economy boom. It was driven by overseers, whites who kept an eye on the workers, and drivers, black slaves who were made to keep an eye on their own. Domestic slaves might have had an easier time working in the households, but if their masters were strict, life was could be even harder. Plantation slavery affected everyone who lived in America, and it still does today.

options.label: Black people were treated as less than human in the southern states of America.
Plantation slavery was the least harsh form of slavery in the South.
Plantation owners were pressured to produce a certain quantity of work or face punishment themselves.
Domestic slaves were ensured more rights than slaves who worked in the fields.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best identifies the central idea of the text?

options.label: "America made black people work for them for free so that they could make new goods without spending much money" (Paragraph 2)
"They used whips, which if used with enough force, would tear open the slaves' skin." (Paragraph 4)
"This means they were given more comfortable places to sleep and better food." (Paragraph 5)
"Plantation slavery affected everyone who lived in America, and it still does today." (Paragraph 6)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which quote from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: The master owns the overseer, a slave who manages other slaves.
The master and the overseer are equals on a plantation.
The master employs an overseer to violently discipline slaves.
The master owns the plantation, while the overseer owns the slaves.

articles.title: The Big Melt?
articles.subtitle: The Arctic and Antarctic might seem far away. But the ice at the top and bottom of the world has a big impact on all of us.
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Tracy Vonder Brink explains how melting ice impacts the planet.
articles.intro: The polar ice located in the Arctic and Antarctic is melting. In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains why this is happening and what could happen to the planet as a result.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens when polar ice melts.
articles.permissions: "The Big Melt" by Tracy Vonder Brink, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: At the edge of Antarctica, a cliff of ice stretches a mile high. Suddenly, with a boom, a huge chunk the size of a city block breaks off and splashes into the sea. A new iceberg is born! In summer, polar ice is always breaking and melting. But now it’s happening more, and faster. That worries scientists.
A Warmer World

As far back as 870 CE, Vikings recorded how many days a year the sea near them was covered in ice. In modern times, satellites take daily pictures of the ice caps. Scientists can compare pictures to see how the ice changes year to year. And what they see is less and less ice.

In the distant past, Earth has been both warmer and colder than it is now. A hundred million years ago, Antarctica was ice-free. It was warm enough to have forests full of dinosaurs. But in colder times, ice has covered much of the globe.

Usually, Earth’s climate changes very slowly, over thousands or millions of years. Plants and animals have time to adapt. But now, human activities like burning fossil fuels are causing Earth to heat up much faster. And the poles are warming more quickly than anywhere else. That’s something new.
What does that mean for the Arctic and Antarctic?
Less Ice, More Water

A warmer world means less ice, for a start. It used to be impossible to sail a ship from Europe to Alaska over the top of Canada. The way was blocked by ice, even in summer. But over the past 42 years, the Arctic has lost half of its winter ice. Every year, more ice melts in summer and less re-freezes in winter. For the first time in modern memory, there are open stretches of water across the top of the world.

When more ice melts, it also melts faster. Why? Shiny white ice and snow reflect sunlight. That helps keep the poles cool. But dark blue water absorbs the sun’s heat and warms up. This melts more ice, which traps more heat, which melts more ice. This is what scientists call a feedback loop.

Warming also dumps more land ice into the sea. In Arctic Greenland, immense ice sheets cover rocky land. Warm weather can melt the top of the ice. The water seeps down to form a slippery layer over the rock. This sends huge slabs of ice sliding downhill, into the ocean. The ice sheets have always shed some ice in summer. But now they are losing more ice than ever before.
Rising Seas
When the ice melts, where does all that water go? Back into the oceans.

Earth’s oceans are immense. But the ice sheets hold a LOT of water. Antarctica’s ice cover is larger than the United States and several miles thick. If all that ice melted, sea levels would rise by around 200 feet (60 m). Antarctica is huge and very cold, so there’s no danger of it all melting tomorrow. But even slow melting can dump a lot of extra water into the oceans. If we don’t do anything to stop warming, by the end of the century, sea levels around the world could be several feet higher. That would flood many towns built near coasts.
Rising seas could also make wells more salty and cause problems for farmers who need fresh water for crops.
Changing Weather

Warmer poles will also change weather all over. Big weather systems are driven by cold air circling around the poles and deep ocean currents that move heat around. These deep currents start at the poles, when cold, salty water sinks and flows toward the equator.
If these ocean currents shift or stop, our weather could change in ways we can’t predict. Some areas could become rainier. Some could become drier. Storms could be more frequent and powerful.
What Can We Do?

The best way to save the ice is to reduce heat-trapping gases in the air. Using clean energy, driving less, and recycling can all help. It will take hard work. But if we all pitch in, we can keep the poles icy.

options.label: The ice on the poles is melting quickly and we must work together to stop this from happening.
Scientists do not know why the Arctic and Antarctic poles have less ice than ever before.
The Arctic and Antarctic poles have been important places to travel throughout history.
There is not much we can do to stop the ice on the poles from melting.

articles.title: How Do I Look?
articles.author: BirdBrain Science
articles.description: The text discusses the influences of nature and nurture on who a person becomes.
articles.intro: There are many factors that contribute to who a person becomes. In the text, the author discusses the different influences responsible for shaping a human being. The two categories are nature and nurture.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify the differences between nature and nurture.
articles.permissions: "How Do I Look?" by BirdBrain Science. Copyright © 2016 by BirdBrain. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: Have you ever wondered why you look the way you do? Do you ever think about what you are going to look like in five years, or in 25 years? When you were born, you didn’t look the way you do today. In fact, when you were born, you probably looked like a lot of other babies: small, a bit round, no hair, no teeth and little chubby arms and legs. Although we start out looking like other babies, we all change and grow to look different, as we get older. Some people grow tall, and some do not. Some people’s feet are bigger than others. Some people need glasses, and others see clearly. Some people have skin that is smooth, and some have skin that is wrinkled or freckled. Some people seem to have strong "Marian and Vivian Brown" by Christopher muscles and others look like they have no Michel is licensed under CC BY 2.0. muscles at all. We all look very different. The reason for our differences involves both what we were born with and what happens to us after we are born. Some things about us are set before we are born. When a mother and father have a child, they give things like eye color and hair color to their child. Traits are the parts of us, and all living things, that we get from our parents that make up what we look like. Traits are things like eye color, hair color, height, and whether our second toe is longer than our big toe. Let’s say a mother and father both have red hair. Their child will most likely have red hair too. Even if their child is born with no hair at all, you can make a good guess that the hair that will grow will be red because his or her hair color is already decided. It is a trait that the child’s parents passed along to their baby. Some things about us, though, are shaped by what happens after we are born. Imagine twin girls who are born with the same exact traits: same hair, same eyes, and same size. As they grow up together they will look alike, and most likely will sound alike. If they dress alike, too, it could be very hard to tell them apart. Now think about what would happen if those twins spend their childhood living in two different homes. One home might be in a very cold place and the other in a warm area. Then these girls would most likely wear different clothes, play different sports and eat different foods. They might have been born with the trait of having crooked teeth, but if one twin gets braces and the other does not, their smiles will end up looking very different. When they become adults, even though they were born with the same traits, the way they grew up may have changed them in some ways. Environmental influences are things in the world around us that change what we need and the way we grow. Even if two people start out almost the same, like twins, where and how they live can change the way they look as they grow up. If twins who are born with many of the same traits can grow up to look different, then what is it that really makes us who we are? Believe it or not, scientists have been talking about this question for hundreds of years. Some believe that it is mostly the traits that we get from our parents that decide who we are. They call this our nature, or all the parts we get from our parents that make up who we are when we are born. Think about making chocolate chip cookies. You need sugar, flour, butter, eggs and chocolate chips. The nature of the chocolate chip cookie comes from those ingredients that have been mixed together. If you use raisins instead of chocolate chips, they would no longer be chocolate chip cookies. They would be raisin cookies. Your nature is all of your body’s ingredients put together. They are the parts and pieces of you that you have when you enter the world. If you change one of them, like the chocolate chips and the raisins, then you would be different too.

However, we know from our look at those twins that a person’s nature can be changed by where they live and the way they grow up. Nurture is the way the world around us acts upon us and changes us as we live and grow. Over the years, many people have believed that this is what really decides who we are, no matter what we have in us when we are born. They thought that we could raise a child to become just what we wanted him or her to be. Let’s go back to that cookie. We know the nature of the cookie, or all the stuff that goes into it, but that’s only the first part of what your cookie will be like. Once you have everything mixed together, you shape the cookie dough into balls, put them on the baking sheet and place it in the oven. The heat of the oven bakes the dough, and the cookie becomes flat and round. Now, if you put that cookie dough into a square-shaped pan, it would not come out round. The shape of the pan, or the cookie’s environment, changes the way the cookie “grows up.” You get two different looking chocolate chip cookies, even though all the ingredients are the same. Now make believe that you are the cookie. Your nature might say that your feet will grow to be a certain size, but if you grow up wearing shoes that are too small for you, your feet might not reach their full size. You can change the way your feet grow just by wearing the wrong size shoe! As these two ideas of nature and nurture have been studied over the years, many people have come to agree that both are very important to how we grow and who we become. What we get from our parents forms the beginning of what we are going to look like and act like. It has a hand in how we look at the world and how the world looks at us. However, the place where we live and the way we grow up matters very much as well. What we go through in our lives and the way the world changes us plays a big part in how we become who we are.

options.label: The traits your parents pass down to you are more responsible for the person you become than outside influences.
Outside influences, such as where you grow up and the way you grow up, have a stronger impact on the person you become than traits passed down by your parents.
While the traits your parents pass down help make you who you are, outside influences also play an important role.
Scientists are completely unsure of how nurture and nature combine to make people who they are.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best identifies the central idea of the text?

options.label: "The reason for our differences involves both what we were born with and what happens to us after we are born." (Paragraph 1)
"When a mother and father have a child, they give things like eye color and hair color to their child." (Paragraph 2)
"If twins who are born with many of the same traits can grow up to look different, then what is it that really makes us who we are?" (Paragraph 4)
"As these two ideas of nature and nurture have been studied over the years..." (Paragraph 7)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which quote from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It contrasts the traits of nature and nurture.

It gives examples of how outside influences can impact the person you become.

questions.prompt: PART A: How does paragraph 3 contribute to the development of ideas in the text?

options.label: "Let's say a mother and father both have red hair. Their child will most likely have red hair too." (Paragraph 2)
"As they grow up together they will look alike, and most likely will sound alike." (Paragraph 3)
"One home might be in a very cold place and the other in a warm area. Then these girls would most likely wear different clothes..." (Paragraph 3)
"Some believe that it is mostly the traits that we get from our parents that decide who we are." (Paragraph 4)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

discussionQuestions.prompt: What environmental influences have contributed to shaping the person you are today?
Why is it important to consider both traits and environmental influences when studying the development of people?

In the context of the article, what makes you who you are? In your opinion, are traits or environmental influences more important in shaping who a person becomes? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: The Worst Sin
articles.author: Joshua Salik
articles.description: In this short story, set on Judgment Day, God must evaluate the sins of multiple men.
articles.intro: How do we judge what is right and wrong? Are there some actions that are better or worse that others? These are just a few of the questions raised in this story by Joshua Salik.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes about the central idea of the story, and what makes the author's argument successful.
articles.permissions: "The Worst Sin", © 2015, StoryShares. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: It was on Yom Kippur, the Jewish judgment day. After taking care of all the easy cases it was time to judge the heavy sinners. They were all going to hell, for sure, but even in hell there are bad places and worse places, and each of the sinners had to be assigned his proper place.
There are seven chambers in hell. Chamber 1 is bad but not as bad as Chamber 2. Chamber 2 is worse than Chamber 1, but still not nearly as bad as Chamber 3. The worst is Chamber 7.
After sitting in his chair, God told the bailiff, “Ok, send in the first sinner.”
The first sinner came in.
“What is his sin?” God asked the clerk.
“He is a thief. He stole things that did not belong to him.”
“What exactly did he steal?” asked God.

“You name it,” said the clerk “When he was a small boy he stole toys from other children. He stole money from his mother. When he grew up he stole cars. People worked all their lives to get those things, and he just took them.”
“Hm,” said God. “This is bad, but I’ll give him a break. Put him in Chamber 1.”
The bailiff took the sinner out.
“Ok,” said God. “Send in the second sinner.”
The second sinner stepped in.
“What sins did he commit?” asked God.

“He is a bigot,” said the clerk. “He hates everybody who is different from him. He hates people with different colors of skin, different religions, and different accents. He offended people, discriminated against them, and made their lives miserable.”
“This really makes me angry,” said God. “I created all those people, and I created them equal. No one is better or worse than the others. Send him to Chamber 4.”
The bailiff took the sinner out.
“Do we have more?” God asked the clerk.
“I’m afraid so,” said the clerk.
“Then what exactly are we waiting for?” God told the bailiff. “Bring in the next one.”
The third sinner entered the room.
“What did he do?” asked God.
“He is a liar,” said the clerk.
“And to whom did he lie?” asked God.

“To everybody,” said the clerk. “He lied to his parents, he lied to his teachers, he lied to his friends, to his wife, even to his children. Everybody. He cheated people out of their money. He even lied to the police and had innocent people put in jail.”
“This is bad,” said God. “Take him to Chamber 2. How many more do we have?”
“Four,” said the clerk.
“Four?” God sighed. “This job is getting harder and harder or maybe I am getting older. Looks like every year we have more sinners. All right, send in the next one.”
The bailiff brought in the fourth sinner.
“What is his sin?” God asked.
“He was cruel to animals,” replied the clerk. “He beat dogs, cut lizards tails off, burnt cats, plucked the legs off cockroaches …”
“Ok, Ok,” God got sick. “I’ve heard enough.”
He turned to the sinner and said, “Do you know who created those animals? I did. When you hurt them you’re hurting me. I am sending you to Chamber 5.”
“Chamber 5?” the sinner could not believe his ears.
“You heard me,” God answered.
“I am ready for the next one,” God told the bailiff. “Bring him in.”
The next sinner showed up.
“What did he do?” asked God.
“Just a minute,” said the clerk. “I need to check my notes.”
“OK,” said God. “But hurry up. We don’t have all day, you know.”
“Here,” said the clerk after a few minutes. “I found it. This guy was irresponsible.”
“What do you mean?” asked God.

“He left his family – a wife and five children. As if it wasn’t bad enough for his children to grow up without a father, he also took all the money and left them with nothing. His wife had to take three jobs to put food on the table,” the clerk said.
“What did he do with the money?” God asked.
“He spent it, or more correctly wasted it, on his own pleasures,” the clerk answered. “He bought himself a new car, a boat. He gambled, he drank. This kind of stuff.”
“This guy makes me sick,” said God. “I have no sympathy for him. Put him in Chamber 3.”
“Shall we take a break?” asked the clerk.
“No,” said God. “Let’s get it over with. Bring in the next one.”
The next sinner was brought in.
“And what is he here for?” God asked.
“This is a heavy one,” said the clerk. “He is a killer. He took from people the most important thing – their lives.”
“I am not sure that this is the most important thing,” God interrupted the clerk, “but it is very important. Continue.”
“Not only did he take the lives of his victims, but he also ruined the lives of their families, caused pain to their friends…”

“Ok, Ok,” said God. “I get the picture, and I agree with you. This is a very heavy sin. I am the one who gives life and I am the only one who can take it away. Put him in Chamber 6. Is he the last one?”
“No,” said the clerk. “There is one more.”
“And I thought we’ve heard all the possible sins,” said God. “What did this one do?”
“Nothing,” said the clerk.
“What do you mean nothing?” asked God. “Then why is he here? Doing nothing may be lazy, but it isn’t a sin, last time I checked.”
“Well, then, check again,” said the clerk. “I believe that doing nothing is a horrible sin.”
“How so?” asked God.

“This guy saw horrible crimes and did nothing to prevent them. He saw people cheated and did not tell them the truth. He saw animals tortured and did not treat them. He saw bigotry and did not fight it. He saw injustice and did not correct it. He saw children starving and did not feed them. He saw people attacked and did not defend them. I accuse him of the worst sin – indifference. It is people like him who make all the other sins possible.”
“You know what?” said God. “You convinced me. This is the worst sin. He belongs in Chamber 7.”

options.label: Sometimes, God's judgment is unfair.
Seeing injustice and doing nothing is worse than the injustice itself.
God relies on the judgment of man.
Horrible crimes will always be punished in the end.

articles.title: Outpouring of Support for France
articles.author: Joyce Grant
articles.description: This text highlights the outpouring of support France received following a terrorist attack in 2015.
articles.intro: In November of 2015, a mass shooting and suicide bombing happened in several locations around Paris, France. 139 people were killed and over 350 people were injured. The terrorist group ISIS claimed responsibility for the attacks the following day. Following the tragedy, people all over the world expressed grief and support for the people of France.
articles.annotationTask: As you read the following article, take notes on the different ways that people dealt with this tragedy.
articles.permissions: "Outpouring of Support for France" from Teaching Kids News , © 2015, Joyce Grant. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: People around the world are expressing encouragement and support for the people of France. In particular, for the people in its capital city, Paris.
In most big cities, there are large buildings or monuments that are famous. For instance, the CN Tower in Toronto, Canada or the “London Eye” Ferris wheel in London, England.
Last week, many of them were lit up in the three colors of France’s flag: blue, white and red. In that way, the countries were showing support to express their solidarity with France.

People are feeling this way about France because something awful happened there last Friday. Explosions killed people in Paris. A terrorist group known as ISIS says that they set off the explosions in order to gain awareness for the group and the group’s beliefs. The group also led five other attacks in France that day.
The President of France, Francois Hollande, is putting many measures in place to help protect the country’s citizens.

Anne Hidalgo, the mayor of Paris, thanked people for their support. She posted on Twitter “un grand merci du fond du coeur,” — “a big thank-you from the bottom of my heart.” She also posted pictures of various monuments around the world lit up in blue, white and red.

People around the world have also been gathering in support, holding “vigils” (peaceful, supportive gatherings) often outside their country’s French embassies. In Canada, people gathered in many cities across the country including Quebec City, Montreal, Ottawa, Calgary, Vancouver and Toronto.

In Paris on the weekend, many people stood in line for hours to donate blood to help people wounded in the attacks. On Twitter, many people used the hashtag #porteouverte (open door) to let people who had been hurt or frightened by the incident know that if they needed a place to stay, they were welcome in their home. More than 300 Red Cross workers have been sent to France to help.

options.label: Terrorism is a problem that the world's leaders must work harder to stop.
France experienced a terrible attack in which many people died.
Peace is more powerful than violence.
After experiencing a tragedy, people around the world showed their support for France.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states a central idea of the article?

options.label: Unity and support
Fame and popularity
Peace and non-violence
Tragedy or disaster

questions.prompt: PART A: What does the word "solidarity" most closely mean as it is used in paragraph 3?

options.label: "large buildings or monuments that are famous"
"lit up in the three colors of France"
"showing their support"
"something awful happened"

questions.prompt: PART B: Which phrase from the text provides the best clue to the answer to Part A?
Using evidence from the article, explain some of the ways that people around the world reacted to France's tragedy.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Had you heard of the attacks in France before reading this article? How does it make you feel to learn about these types of incidents? Why?

This article focuses on the positive reactions to a tragedy. Do you think that when tragedies like this happen, the news tends to be more positive or negative? Should news organizations try to focus on both angles to a story? Why or why not?
In your opinion, how can tragedies like this be prevented in the future?
In the context of this text, what can we learn from tragedy? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: On Second Anniversary, Haiti Still Recovering from Earthquake
articles.author: Nancy Miller
articles.description: This text shares the slow progress that has been made in Haiti after 2010's devastating earthquake.
articles.intro: In this article from 2012, author Nancy Miller of Teaching Kids News speaks to Catherine Porter of the Toronto Star newspaper about Haiti's progress recovering from a major earthquake in 2010. The earthquake affected about three million people. Many buildings collapsed during the disaster, leading to deaths and homelessness.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the problems that Haiti faces two years after the massive 2010 earthquake.
articles.permissions: "On Second Anniversary, Haiti Still Recovering from Earthquake ", © 2012, . Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: Jan. 12 was a significant date for the people of Haiti. It was two years ago on that date that the country suffered a terrible earthquake.
More than a million people had their homes destroyed.
After two years, many people think that new homes, jobs and clean streets are not coming fast enough.
Haiti’s new President, Michel Martelly, is known as Sweet Mickey. He believes education is one of the most important things for the government to fix first.
TKN talked to Catherine Porter, who writes about Haiti for The Toronto Star newspaper.
“In Haiti,” Catherine said, “forty per cent of children never go to school because most schools are private and cost a lot. The president wants all primary students to go to school for free.”
Another hard worker for education in Haiti is Canada’s last Governor-General, Michaelle Jean. She was born in Haiti and works with the president and others to improve the school system.
Porter said she can see change happening, but only because she knows Haiti so well.
“If you only go to Haiti for a short visit it’s hard to see change because there is still so much to be done,” she said.

She told TKN the story of Carlos, who lost his family in the earthquake. He somehow made his way to a shelter supported by Air Canada boss Duncan Dee. When Porter visited Haiti last September, she saw that Carlos and others were going to school, playing soccer and living in a children’s home that the Dee family built.
People in Canada can help Haitians by donating money through an organization like the Red Cross. Children can become pen pals with children in a school in Haiti to help them keep up their spirits.

options.label: Haiti has physically recovered from the 2010 earthquake, but no one there is working to improve education.
Haiti's education system improved after the 2010 earthquake because all of the private schools were turned into public schools.
There have been few attempts since the 2010 earthquake to improve Haiti's damaged housing or education.
Haiti is slowly recovering from the 2010 earthquake, but it may not be improving fast enough.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best identifies the central idea of the text?

options.label: "many people think that new homes, jobs and clean streets are not coming fast enough." (Paragraph 3)
"He believes education is one of the most important things for the government to fix first." (Paragraph 4)
"Another hard worker for education in Haiti is Canada's last Governor- General, Michaelle Jean." (Paragraph 7)
"People in Canada can help Haitians by donating money through an organization like the Red Cross." (Paragraph 11)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It shows why children have lost hope in Haiti.
It shows how difficult it has been for Haiti to rebuild.
It shows an example of the progress being made in Haiti.
It shows how much damage the earthquake caused.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes how paragraph 10 contributes to the development of ideas in the text?

options.label: Educating students to build strong houses will help Haiti recover faster.
Recovery has been slow because people are distracted by improving education.
Some people believe it is important to make education free so families can afford to rebuild homes.
Haiti is using this period of recovery to ensure that more children can receive access to education.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the connection between education and Haiti's recovery in the article?
How does the author's reference to Carlos' experiences contribute to the development of ideas in the text?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you agree with President Michel Martelly, should fixing education be the priority in Haiti? Why or why not?
In your opinion, how could the devastating earthquake actually improve access to education for children?
In the context of the text, what can we learn from tragedy? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: The Chicken That Crossed the Road
articles.author: Gary Soto
articles.description: A young boy learns a valuable lesson after finding a chicken.
articles.intro: Born to Mexican-American parents, Gary Soto's work focuses on daily experiences, growing up and his life and relationships within his community. "The Chicken That Crossed the Road" is about a young boy who learns a valuable lesson after finding a chicken.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, pay attention to how Miguel's ideas about bravery and courage develop throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "The Chicken That Crossed the Road" by Gary Soto. Copyright © 2021 by Gary Soto. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Miguel looked across the school campus. He spotted two boys, second graders he guessed, playing soccer with a foursquare ball. He could think of nothing more fun than challenging them. But no, he was in sixth grade and had a bigger challenge: that boring English assignment. Why did his English teacher torture him—and the entire class—with such a stupid topic: “The Most Courageous Person I’ve Known”?

He groaned at the painful thought of writing two full pages about a person who didn’t exist. He couldn’t think of anyone, and this included his parents, who had done anything more heroic than help some people jumpstart their car. Did that make them courageous? He snickered at this trivial act of kindness. His father was an accountant who worked with figures all day, and his mother sold real estate.

He pushed a hand into the nearly empty potato-chip bag and brought out crumbs. He threw them into his mouth and rummaged through his brain. Who could he write about? His grandparents? They just sat in recliners all day. His aunts and uncles? They were lazy as old dogs. He knew of a girl at school whose father had jumped in the river to save a drowning boy. That’s courage, he acknowledged. But he didn’t know the man.

At twelve, Miguel just wanted to be a boy. He wanted to play, get his homework done as quickly as possible, and eat. He dropped his potato-chip bag into a trash can, licked his fingers, and concluded that he didn’t know any courageous people.

He left the school grounds, then reached into his pocket for a baby Snickers, a candy bar left over from a not particularly profitable Halloween. As he was ripping open the wrapper with his teeth, he heard someone behind him call, “Hey.”
Miguel turned. It was his classmate, Robert. At first glance, Robert appeared to be chewing mud. Then Miguel realized that he too must’ve been devouring leftover Halloween candy.
Robert hurried to catch up with Miguel, who hadn’t bothered to wait. He didn’t really like Robert, but Robert acted as if they were best buds. After all, weren’t they both getting Cs in English?
“I got to show you something,” Robert said, after swallowing his muddy candy. His cheeks were pink, his hair tousled as if he had been wrestling on someone’s lawn.
“Wipe your mouth,” Miguel ordered.
Robert wiped his mouth with a thumb.
“What?” Miguel asked. “What do you want to show me?”
“I found a chicken.”
“A chicken,” Miguel remarked slowly. He was curious. “Where?”
Robert pointed toward the alley.
Miguel assumed that the chicken had escaped from a yard. “So?”
“So? So let’s go see it.”
“What are we going to do with a chicken?” Miguel asked.
“Just look at it—that’s all.”

Miguel sighed. He had been enjoying his candy in solitude and now Robert had ruined the moment. What did he want them to do? Pull the chicken’s feathers off and roast it over a fire? He shrugged his shoulders. “OK,” he said. “But you better not be messing with me.”
In the alley, sure enough, there was a white chicken, plump as a football, plucking at the guts of a dirty pillow that lay on a pile of garbage.
The two boys stared at the chicken. The chicken straightened up and stared at them. It jumped from the pile, fluttering its wings. Then it returned to poking at the pillow.
“Why’s it eating that?” Robert asked.
Miguel had visited the Little Farm at the city park and was aware that chickens would gobble anything they could get down their throats. Then, like soup, an idea bubbled in his mind.
“Help me,” Miguel said. He grabbed the dirty pillowcase, handed it to Robert, then told him to hold it open.
“Why?”
“There’s no time for ‘why.’ Just do it.”
As Miguel slowly approached the chicken, it lifted its head, took a few gangly steps, and clucked.
“Come on, little chickie,” cooed Miguel.
The chicken clucked again.
“Come on,” he repeated.

The chicken moved slowly, a grave mistake, because Miguel was quick. He grabbed it by the neck and held on, despite the chicken’s fluttering efforts to get loose. The chicken spread and retracted its wings like two folding fans. When it finally calmed down, accepting its fate, Miguel stuffed it into the pillowcase and sloppily tied the end closed.
“What are you going to do with it?” Robert asked.

“Sell it,” Miguel answered. For a moment, he felt like a fox, the most devious of all animals. He would sell this strutting piece of poultry to the neighbor down the block, Mr. Shafer, a retired railroad man. Mr. Shafer had once kept chickens in his yard. But those chickens, Miguel recalled, were long gone.
“I think it’s wrong,” said Robert, with a look of anger. “I’m outta here.”
“Go, then!” Miguel bristled.
Robert, head down, walked out of the alley. Before leaving, he turned and shouted at Miguel. “You’re gonna mess up!” he cried. “I know it!”

Yeah right, Miguel thought. But he felt queasy at Robert’s pronouncement. Maybe this wasn’t such a good idea, maybe it was just wrong. Nevertheless, he picked up the pillowcase. He hefted it with one hand — the chicken was surprisingly heavy. Maybe it had been eating rocks. He had read on the Internet that a man had been carving a turkey — to Miguel, a turkey was just a big chicken with an ugly thing hanging from its neck — when a diamond ring rolled out of the stuffing. With only a twinge of guilt, Miguel hoisted his catch over his shoulder, then started toward home.
Mr. Shafer was in his front yard, tinkering with a truck. The hood was up and some of the engine parts were set on newspaper in the driveway. A red chest of Craftsman tools was perched on the fender.
“Mr. Shafer,” Miguel called from the bottom of the driveway. He didn’t dare set foot on the old man’s property without permission.
Mr. Shafer turned, with a socket wrench in his hand, and slowly offered up a gray smile. “What?” he asked. His eyes were flat as coins and his face streaked with grease.
“I got a chicken,” Miguel announced. He held up the pillowcase, which began to sway from the chicken’s clawing struggle.

Mr. Shafer narrowed his right eye suspiciously. A large man, he wasn’t anyone to fool with. He had a temper when solicitors knocked on his door, and would sometimes yell at the neighborhood kids to stop making noise.
“It’s a nice chicken,” Miguel added. “It’s really young.”

Mr. Shafer growled something that Miguel couldn’t decipher, then wiped his oily hands on the funny pages of the newspaper. He hiked up his pants and walked toward Miguel, saying, “You selling chickens now, boy? Last year it was Girl Scout Cookies.”

Miguel ignored this remark. “My grandma wants me to get rid of it,” he lied. “She can’t eat chicken anymore.” The words sounded lame once they exited his mouth, but there it was, a story for Mr. Shafer to weigh on his own.
“Why do you think I need a chicken?” Mr. Shafer asked.
Miguel searched his mind for an answer. “Because they’re nice?”
Mr. Shafer’s chuckling wobbled his belly. He peered into the pillowcase then cradled the chicken in one hand. “She don’t seem very big.”

Miguel looked into the pillowcase as well and was immediately saddened by the hen gazing up at him. His heart sank. Had he become so unfeeling that he would deliver an innocent hen to a man who could do only one thing with it? For a moment, he imagined the hen as a fluffy little chick, temporarily out of harm’s way. Then he pushed aside his misgiving. “Nah,” he said, “it weighs pretty good.” He rubbed his arm to make the point, as if it had been a great effort to carry the hefty hen.
Mr. Shafer offered Miguel two dollars.
“Just two dollars?” Miguel squeaked. His Adam’s apple went up and down as he swallowed. “It’ll make a nice pet.”
“Three then,” Mr. Shafer bargained.
“OK,” said Miguel, reluctantly.

Mr. Shafer grabbed the pillowcase from Miguel. He pushed one large, veined hand into his pocket and pulled out three crumpled bills. He deposited them into Miguel’s outstretched palm then sauntered back up his driveway. He tossed the pillowcase into the bed of the truck as if it were a bag of aluminum cans.

What have I done? Miguel moaned to himself. The ole guy’s really gonna cook it! He turned and ran along the block toward his own house, trying not to think of the defenseless hen looking up from inside the pillowcase.

Miguel sat on the front porch, catching his breath. Then he recalled Robert’s words: “You’re gonna mess up!” He hadn’t cried for at least a year, but now he felt his eyes growing moist. When tears began to roll down his cheek, he let them fall. Yes, they were salty and, yes, he had done a bad, bad thing.

But Miguel’s sadness turned to fear when he saw his mother’s car pulling into the driveway. He had completely forgotten his promise to vacuum the living room and tidy up the bathroom — as soon as he got home from school. He scurried across the porch on his hands and knees, thus avoiding detection as the car squeaked to a halt. Then he stood up and quickly unlocked the door, sliding inside like a burglar. He immediately went to the hallway closet and pulled out the vacuum cleaner. He plugged it in and turned it on. Minutes later, his mother came in, lugging two bags of groceries.
“You could’ve helped me,” she said, putting the groceries down on the coffee table. She kicked off her shoes in the living room; without the high heels, she was no taller than Miguel.

“Sorry,” Miguel lied again. “I didn’t hear you.” He turned off the vacuum cleaner, pushed the vacuum cleaner hose aside, then carried the bags of groceries into the kitchen, where he began unpacking their contents. He stopped when his hands discovered a pre-roasted barbecue-flavored chicken, its plastic container fogged from heat.
“Oh,” he said, picturing the little white hen on Mr. Shafer’s kitchen table. He swallowed, tasting a spit-like lozenge of guilt.

Miguel gathered the cans of tomatoes and plastic bottles of fruit juice in his arms and carried them to the pantry. When he opened the pantry door, he was greeted by a staunch row of soup cans — chicken noodle. He closed his eyes and imagined Mr. Shafer dunking a plucked chicken into a large pot. Some of the neighbors said he had been mean to his wife. No wonder he lived alone.

His mother came into the kitchen. “I see you didn’t clean the bathroom like I told you,” she remarked. She made a face at him, then opened the refrigerator. The light bulb brightened her cheeks, but her mood remained frighteningly dark.

“I’ll do it right now,” Miguel chimed. He hurried to the bathroom, where he sprinkled the basin with cleanser then scrubbed it with a sponge. He sprayed the mirror and wiped it clean but didn’t like what he saw there. He didn’t like himself. He was hateful. He would never like himself again.

“I’ll save you,” he whispered, the mist of his words clinging briefly on the glass. Then he looked at the toilet. It seemed pretty clean to him. Still, he flushed it twice before returning to the kitchen.
In the time he had been cleaning the bathroom, Miguel’s mother had received a phone call. The call, he could see, had put his mother in a really bad mood.
“I thought I told you to set the table,” she snapped.

She hadn’t, but Miguel wasn’t about to argue. He set the table, filled three water glasses, then returned to the living room. He put the vacuum cleaner away, then perched nervously on the edge of the couch, praying that the little white hen was OK.
At six-fifteen, his father arrived, his tie undone beneath his shadowy beard.
“Hi, Dad,” Miguel said.
“Hey, son,” said his father in return. He set his briefcase on the coffee table and, with Miguel on his heels, went to the kitchen.
“Hey, Cutie,” he said, hazarding a smile.

Miguel’s mother, however, remained sullen. Both father and son knew enough not to ask, “What’s the matter?” The family sat down for dinner. Miguel did his best to eat the drumstick his mother shoved onto his plate. But he couldn’t stop imagining the little hen in the pillowcase without a leg. I’m terrible, he moaned in his heart. I’m bad.
“The deal fell through,” his mother remarked as she stabbed at a coin-shaped carrot. “The buyers backed out. I knew they would get cold feet!”
Real estate was a frequent topic at the family dinner table: home buyers, mortgage companies, termite inspectors, landscapers and decorators — all of whom regularly failed her.
Miguel’s father lifted his water glass. “Don’t worry,” he said.
Miguel watched the fork in his mother’s hand. It looked deadly.
“I mean,” his father corrected. “You’ll sell the house. You’ll get multiple offers — you’ll see.”
Miguel’s mother continued cutting savagely through the chicken breast, a beastly slaughter of bones on her plate.
Miguel’s father looked sympathetically at his son.
To keep himself from speaking, Miguel stuffed his mouth with mashed potatoes.
They ate in silence until Miguel, testing the air, announced, “I think I’m going to get an A in English.”
“That’s great, son,” his father said, smiling. “You’re good at writing.”

“And I’m good for nothing,” Miguel’s mother remarked. She stared at her husband, then her son, and then jabbed a cherry tomato with her fork. “For what we have to pay for these,” she mumbled, “they should taste better!”
“But, Mom, it’s true,” volunteered Miguel. “It’s like Dad said. The house will sell. People need a place to live.” But he had to agree about the tomatoes, which were flavorless and hard.

After dinner Miguel cleaned up the kitchen, grinding the chicken bones and gristle in the disposal. He felt a little dizzy watching the soapy water spiral down the drain, bearing scraps of food. His mom was right — it hadn’t been a dinner worth eating. After he wiped down the table with a clean sponge, he went into the living room. The clock on the wall read 7:30. He told his father that he was going to see Robert for a bit.

“This late?” his father asked. He was sitting in front of the television, which was muted but showing the Golden State Warriors playing the Los Angeles Lakers. His mother was on the phone in the den, with the door closed. In this age of foreclosures, selling real estate was a 24/7 occupation.

“Yeah, I gotta borrow a book,” he explained. He said that he would be back soon, then slipped into the hoodie that hung on a peg near the front door and left. He paused on the porch for a few moments, his hands fingering the greasy bills in his pocket. They smelled, he thought, of motor oil.

“I’ll save you,” he found himself saying, his breath a white mist in the glare of the porch light. He sprinted down the porch and hurried in the direction of Mr. Shafer’s house. For the first time, he thought about all the “for sale” signs on lawns — one at every third or fourth house. Where were people going, and why? He imagined his mother in the den, making phone calls to clients who sometimes picked up but mostly just let their voicemail take messages that they never returned. There were plenty of houses for sale, but where were the buyers? He felt sorry for his mom, who was often disappointed but still kept trying. Now he sort of understood. Could she be the courageous one after all?

Miguel continued along the sidewalk, security lights coming on at his approach. Dogs barked, and two cats crossed his path in a great hurry. Cars passed, their cool silvery headlights prowling the curbs. Then two boys, thin as vampires, emerged from behind a tree and stood in his way.
Miguel stiffened with fear. He could tell that neither boy was a friend. One was frowning. The other smiled evilly.
“What are you doin’ here?” snarled the taller one.
“I’m going home,” Miguel said weakly.
“You ain’t goin’ home!” the other boy sneered, “I know where you live.”

Miguel looked more closely at this boy. Something long and bloody seemed to be wriggling from his lower lip. Miguel realized it was a red whip of licorice, slowly disappearing into the boy’s mouth as he chewed.
Miguel recognized them both now. They were a year ahead of him, seventh graders, at the middle school this fall. He swallowed and spoke. “I mean, I’m going somewhere else, then home.”
The tree above them filled with wind. Three black birds lifted, each one crying a deathly cry as it flew away.
“Why don’t you try to hit me?” the taller boy dared.
Miguel took a step backwards. “Nah,” he said. “I don’t want to.”
“You’re scared, huh?” taunted the boy. “Tell me!” he crowed. “You scared?” Even in the dark, this boy’s front teeth shone white.
“Nah, I just don’t want to fight. You’re bigger.”

In the next moment, the shorter of the two boys pushed a hand into Miguel’s front pocket and brought out the three dollars from Mr. Shafer. The boy stuffed them into his own pocket, skipped backwards and, along with his friend, disappeared down the street.

The tree above rained down a few leaves. A dog barked in the distance. A car alarm went on and no one seemed to care. Miguel was breathing fast. He had to admit to himself that he really had been scared. He swatted the tears from his eyes. I hate them, he told himself. They’re bullies.

He swallowed again, tasting a terrible truth. The last time he’d gotten into a fight had been in third grade. He had not won that battle. Thinking of the distance remaining to Mr. Shafer’s house, he lost the courage to continue. He turned and started home, head down, his thoughts as dark as the unlit street.

Within minutes he was back home. But he didn’t go inside. He stood on the front lawn, bathed in the glow of the porchlight. Even behind the closed curtains, his living room seemed brightly lit. The far window — his mother’s office — looked dim by comparison. Mom and Dad, they work hard, he told himself, they’re good parents. But he didn’t feel like a good son. He had sold a chicken to a mean man.

“I’m sorry I did it,” he said, taking a deep breath. When he exhaled, he noticed that the puff of air was shaped like a fist. As he grew angry at himself, his breath came faster. Fist after fist blew from his mouth. “Why am I being a coward?” he asked. He rolled his hands into fists and promised to fight if necessary.

He hurried back in the direction of Mr. Shafer’s house, keeping a careful eye on the shadows. He knew that the boys — those vampires, he thought — were out there in the night. Hiding behind a tree, or throwing rocks at passing cars. Miguel pictured them eating candy stolen from a convenience store, the empty wrappers at their feet.

He arrived at Mr. Shafer’s house then paused on the sidewalk, shivering in the cold night air. He briefly appraised the house, as if he were a potential client ready to snatch up a distressed property. The place was in poor shape, with a lawn chewed up from parked cars and trucks. Part of the wooden fence lay on its side. A bag of aluminum cans, large as an inflatable Santa, stood upright on the cement porch, next to an old sofa.

Miguel walked slowly across the lawn. The living room flashed with the colors of a large-screen television. Miguel heard canned laughter, but Mr. Shafer didn’t join in. Maybe there was nothing funny inside him, Miguel thought.
Then Miguel heard a cluck, and then a second cluck. Next came a scratching sound, like claws against paper.

It’s her, he told himself, and felt a sorrowful lump in his throat. He tiptoed over the lawn and leaned his body against the front of the house. He had been shivering with the cold but now he was shuddering from fear. Again, he remembered the essay topic: “The Most Courageous Person I’ve Known.” Did rescuing a chicken from a mean man require courage?
As his heart pounded, Miguel heard the noise of pounding inside the house. What was Mr. Shafer doing? he wondered. Nailing a bear rug to the wall? Stomping on cockroaches?

Then Miguel saw the hen. One of its legs was tied with plastic twine to the porch rail. How was he going to get her loose? He hadn’t thought to bring a knife. He dropped to all fours and slowly crept across the porch. The concrete felt as cold as ice cubes. Still, he had to do something, even if that something was reckless.

Don’t make a noise, Miguel pleaded silently. Please don’t make a noise. When he reached the hen, he paused. The little chicken stood quietly, as if unaware of the plans for its future. The hen waited patiently for him to untie the knot with his numb fingers, showing more courage than anyone Miguel had ever met.

But the knot wouldn’t come loose. “Come on,” Miguel whispered to himself, then lowered his face to the knot and began to nibble. He nibbled until his teeth, like blunt scissors, finally cut through the twine. He spat out flakes of plastic. Miguel wiped his mouth. Was this an act of courage or was it more like getting rid of shame? The shame of selling the chicken and the shame of facing those two boys whose names he couldn’t remember. He knew their faces from the years when they had gone to his school, but what were their names?

When the pounding inside the house resumed, Miguel lifted the hen into his arms and duck-walked across the porch. After it was safe to stand up again, he realized that he was no longer cold; in fact, he was sweating. He had never really been brave before and now, on a regular night in the middle of the week, he had surprised himself.

“I’m sorry,” he told the hen, squeezing it lightly until it let out a small cluck. Then he walked away from Mr. Shafer’s house, back straight, and crossed the dark, dark road. He had intended to leave the three dollars on the porch, but that was no longer possible. But he could still take the little hen back where he’d found it — the alley — and then go home. It would be all right. Then he would feel like his old self again—a boy, not a thief.

The next morning, Miguel imagined an elderly woman descending from her back porch, with nothing to look forward to but her garden. But she would stop, suddenly, only halfway down the steps. What is that white thing in the yard? A towel from the clothesline? Her heart would flutter and her hands come together in a single clap when the towel moved — the hen! “So there you are, you naughty, naughty girl! I was so worried,” she would say, clucking to herself. “You didn’t cross the road, did you?”
