articles.title: Professor Lowe's Adventure
articles.author: Robert Feeman
articles.description: A scientist and inventor who took a hot air balloon from Cincinnati to South Carolina in 1861.
articles.intro: The Civil War was fought in America from 1861-1865 between the North, called the Union, and the South, called the Confederate States of America. In this text, a professor from Cincinnati prepares to fly his hot air balloon over the Atlantic Ocean.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Professor Lowe’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Professor Lowe's Adventure" by Robert Freeman, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Thaddeus S.C. Lowe wanted to be the first person to fly in a balloon across the Atlantic Ocean. He had built his own balloon, called the Enterprise , but he knew he had to make an overland run to test wind currents before he attempted to sail across the ocean. He decided to start the test run in Cincinnati, Ohio, and then fly his balloon back to the East Coast.

On April 19, 1861, the people of Cincinnati held a banquet in Lowe’s honor. It was an exciting occasion. There was much discussion not only about Lowe and his flight but also about the growing problems between the North and the South. Earlier that month, rebels had fired on the Union garrison at Fort Sumter in Charleston, South Carolina! People wondered where the trouble would end.

Later that evening, Lowe learned that the winds were right and the weather was perfect for a balloon ascension. He decided to leave immediately. He ordered the balloon to be filled and began last-minute preparations. At three o’clock on the morning of April 20, he stepped into the basket underneath the huge balloon and gave the signal to cast off. The Enterprise rose quickly into the sky. Lowe took instrument readings and made notes. The balloon rose to 15,000 feet — almost three miles above the earth. His calculations showed that he was traveling close to 100 miles per hour.

On the horizon, Lowe saw the sky growing light. Far in the distance, he could see the Blue Ridge Mountains. As his balloon drifted gracefully over them, he realized that he was traveling more toward the southeast than true east.

After floating for about nine hours, Lowe saw his goal in the distance — the Atlantic Ocean! He looked for a place to set down the balloon, but most of the land below was too marshy. Finally, he spotted a ridge onto which he dropped his balloon. Even before he landed, a small crowd had gathered. Many of the men carried guns.

No one in the crowd made any effort to help him. He asked for some assistance to tie down the balloon. At first, no one moved. Then a tall woman grabbed one of the ropes and steadied the balloon as Lowe secured the other lines. The people in the crowd did not know what to think of a man who had dropped out of the sky. None of them had ever seen a balloon before.

Lowe introduced himself as Professor Thaddeus S.C. Lowe, balloonist, scientist, and inventor. He explained that he had flown from Cincinnati. Someone in the crowd called him a Yankee spy. Another person suggested that he be shot.

The tall woman who had helped Lowe tie down the balloon took charge. She said that the crowd should take him to the county seat at Unionville. As a wagon was brought over to carry Lowe’s gear, he asked where he was. The tall woman smiled. “This place is called Pea Ridge in South Carolina. And since you are a Northerner, you’d better consider yourself a prisoner of the Confederate States of America!” All of Lowe’s equipment was loaded into the wagon, and the long, rough trip to Unionville began. They arrived late in the evening and took Lowe to an inn for the night.

Someone in the town recognized Lowe and knew that he was a young scientist. The next morning, after a long talk with the town leaders, Lowe was released and put onto a train. A few days later, he was back in Cincinnati.

Lowe never made his journey across the Atlantic Ocean, but he convinced President Abraham Lincoln to allow him to act as a spy for the Union army. Building a fleet of five airships, he used his balloons to float above enemy territory. From his position a thousand feet in the air, he could determine Confederate troop sizes and movements, which he communicated by telegraph with Union forces on the ground.

Although the Confederates tried, they were never able to shoot down Lowe. He was the first person in history to use aeronautics to determine the strength and position of an enemy’s forces. He and his staff in the Union army’s Balloon Corps made an estimated 3,000 reconnaissance flights.

options.label: Professor Lowe set out on his journey to become famous.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Professor Lowe lands his balloon among a crowd. Do you think that was a smart decision, or should he have landed somewhere else? If you were in his position, what would you have done? Why?

articles.title: Break A Leg, Macbeth (Shh!)
articles.subtitle: Theater Superstitions for the Unsuspecting
articles.author: Jennifer Sneed
articles.description: Jennifer Sneed explains why stage actors are superstitious.
articles.intro: A superstition is something that someone believes in that is not a fact. For example, some people believe that seeing a black cat brings bad luck. Superstitions are very common in theater performances, or entertainment for an audience. In this text, Jennifer Sneed explains some superstitions in theater.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify reasons why actors are superstitious.
articles.permissions: "Break A Leg, Macbeth (Shh!)" by Jennifer Sneed, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: It’s 1606. The stage is set, and the King’s Men players are costumed and ready to perform a new play before King James I at Hampton Court in London. The play, written by their director, William Shakespeare, is Macbeth . (Shh! Don’t say that!) However, there’s a problem. In Shakespeare’s time, women’s roles were played by young men with high voices, and the teenage boy who is cast to take the stage as Lady Macbeth has fallen deathly ill. Even in 1606 actors believed the show must go on, especially when you’re performing before a king. So taking to heart Lady Macbeth’s advice to her trembling husband to “screw your courage to the sticking place,” Shakespeare stepped into the role at the last minute. Later the boy actor died, and the idea that Macbeth (Hush now!) is a cursed play was born.

Shakespeare’s tragedy is filled with foul murders, ghostly apparitions, and weird witches chanting “Double, double toil and trouble; Fire burn, and cauldron bubble.” Four hundred years later, superstitions surrounding its performance remain strong among actors. In fact, merely saying the title of the play inside the theater is strictly taboo, unless a cast is rehearsing or performing the tragedy. If you must mention the play, please call it “the Scottish play” or “the Bard’s play” instead.

However, if you slip up, don’t worry. There’s an antidote to prevent bad luck and terrible performances, though it will make you look very silly. Simply exit the theater, spin around three times, spit, and recite a Shakespearean insult, such as you “poisonous bunch-backed toad,” or “thou cream-faced loon!” or “thou art a boil, a plague sore!”

Referring to the above-mentioned play as “the Scottish play” is not the only superstition that pervades the theater. Performers are, as a group, a surprisingly superstitious bunch, perhaps because they have a talent for the dramatic. If you try out for a school play or a community theater performance you should be aware of the following superstitions to avoid possible disaster and vexing your castmates.

If your fellow actors tell you to “break a leg” on opening night, they aren’t wishing you harm, they’re wishing you a good performance. Saying “good luck” to an actor is considered bad luck! There are several theories about how this superstition began. One of the most imaginative theories is that some actors believe there are mischievous theater ghosts and fairies who cause trouble by doing the opposite of what you say. Saying “break a leg” is meant to confuse them into allowing a good performance.

When the stage manager leaves a light bulb lit on the stage at the end of rehearsal, they are not wasting electricity. They are setting the “ghost light.” The single burning light is meant to scare away the ghost of Thespis, the first actor in ancient Greece. Before Thespis came along, Greek drama was performed by a group of people speaking lines together, a chorus. Thespis was the first person to appear onstage, not as himself, but as an actor playing a character, who would speak with the chorus. He wrote individual parts for actors and cast himself to act these parts in his own plays. Superstition has it that Thespis likes to sneak into empty theaters and wreak havoc. Maybe he’s missing his days as a star thespian , a word used today for an actor. Whether you believe in ghosts or not, leaving on the ghost light is very practical because it allows people who enter the empty theater to find the light switch without tripping over the set.

You’ve just had a disastrous dress rehearsal, the final practice before opening night when the actors wear their costumes and makeup. The leading lady forgot her lines, the microphones didn’t work, and Eddie knocked over the set. You’re sure all hope is lost. Don’t be surprised if the director says, “Bad dress, good opening.” The superstition is that if the dress rehearsal is terrible, opening night will be great. Maybe it’s wishful thinking, but it often turns out to be true because opening night excitement can give actors energy and cause them to perform at their best.

When you step onstage, be sure to leave your peacock feathers, mirrors, and real money and jewelry at home. These items are said to cause forgotten lines and other performance problems. Mirrors can interfere with the onstage lighting and valuables lying unprotected on the prop table can encourage theft — both practical reasons for the taboo. However, the ban on peacock feathers is pure superstition. Some people believe the design of the feathers contains an evil eye that is an omen of calamity.

Times change, but traditions and superstitions stick around, so please don’t whistle when you’re in a theater. Originally, this was a safety rule. In the early days of large theaters, the ropes used to raise and lower sets and curtains were much like the rigging on a ship. Off-duty sailors would take jobs backstage. They communicated with each other by whistling, just as they did when they were at sea. If a member of the audience happened to whistle, the sailors might think they were getting a direction to lower a set at the wrong time — on top of an actor’s head.

You think the character you’re playing should wear a blue dress. The wardrobe supervisor says, “No way, no how.” Why? Blue dye used to be the most expensive, and wearing blue, onstage or off, was a sign of wealth. Theater companies that were failing to attract a large audience and earn enough money began to dress their actors in blue, trying to trick audiences into believing that the company was popular and successful. Today, blue cloth isn’t more expensive than other colors, but in the theater world blue is still considered to be an unlucky costume color — unless it’s paired with silver, which was a sure sign of a successful company.

If you’re having trouble learning your lines, you might want to sleep with the script under your pillow. The idea behind this superstition is that the lines will transfer from the page to your brain overnight by a magical process of osmosis. If you’ve ever tried this with your history book before a big test, you know it doesn’t work. Science completely debunks this one. You’ll need to memorize your lines the old-fashioned way.

And finally, when your great-aunt Sally comes to watch you perform on opening night, make sure she knows to give you that big bunch of flowers after the performance, not before. Superstition has it that receiving flowers before the show can cause a boring performance. It’s also a point of pride for actors. They want to earn their applause and their bouquets by putting on a great show.

Now you know what not to do in the theater but sticking to these superstitions won’t guarantee a perfect performance. That requires practice, working together with your thespian friends, and perhaps a few more rehearsals. Break a leg!

options.label: "If you must mention the play, please call it "the Scottish play" or "the Bard's play" instead." (Paragraph 2)

"If a member of the audience happened to whistle, the sailors might think they were getting a direction to lower a set at the wrong time — on top of an actor's head." (Paragraph 9)
"That requires practice, working together with your thespian friends, and perhaps a few more rehearsals." (Paragraph 13)

articles.title: Loch Ness Monster
articles.author: India James
articles.description: In this informational text, India James shares about the legend of the Loch Ness monster, a mysterious creature whose existence has never been confirmed.
articles.intro: In this informational text, author India James shares the legend of a mysterious creature.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on evidence that supports that the Loch Ness monster may be real.
articles.permissions: "The Loch Ness Monster" by India James. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Green hills rise in waves above the dark, glassy surface of a lake. Like a mirror, the water reflects the gray clouds that fill the sky overhead. All of a sudden, a dark shape seems to poke up from the surface. It looks like a head rising on top of a long neck. Then, as quickly as they appeared, the head and neck disappear again below the water.
What just happened? What was that giant creature? Was it just a trick of the sunlight on the water, or did a monster just appear and disappear in the blink of an eye?

Scotland has many lakes. The word “loch” means “lake” in Scottish. Loch Ness is a freshwater lake in northern Scotland. People have claimed to have seen a monster in these waters for almost 1,500 years. Does it really exist? No one knows.
An Early Sighting
St. Columba was a Christian missionary who brought his religion to Scotland. The first stories of the Loch Ness monster are from this saint protecting people near the lake.

The story goes that St. Columba heard of a man who had been bitten by a beast living in the lake. He went down to the lake, and the monster came out of the water. With a loud voice, St. Columba told the monster to go no farther. He commanded the monster not to touch anyone, and to go back from where it came at once! When the monster heard St. Columba’s words, it disappeared under the water.
That story dates back to the year 565! Stories about the Loch Ness monster have been around for a long time.
Sightings Today
The Loch Ness monster isn’t just a story from long ago — people today still think they see the monster. In fact, sightings of the monster really took off about 100 years ago.

In 1933, a husband and wife were driving along a road near Loch Ness when they stopped to look at the water. They said they saw a creature in the water “rolling and plunging.” The creature has a body like a whale. For a full minute, the monster made the lake’s water look as if it was “cascading and churning like a simmering cauldron.”

A newspaper reported their sighting, and then many other people began writing to the newspaper. They claimed they had seen a monster in Loch Ness, too. Since then, an official register has started recording sightings of the monster. As of 2023, there have been 1,141 different times that people have caught sight of the creature!
Do Monsters Exist?
There are many rivers, lakes, and streams all over Scotland. Glaciers from the last ice age carved deep grooves in the earth. These grooves filled with water, making especially deep rivers and lakes.

Loch Ness has the most water of all of the lakes in the United Kingdom. This includes Scotland, Wales, England, and Northern Ireland. Loch Ness is more than 750 feet deep — that’s bigger than two football fields placed end-to-end! This makes it a particularly good place for a monster hideout.

Loch Ness isn’t the only place people claim to see monsters or fairytale creatures. Kelpies are magical, shape-shifters that often take the form of horses with reeds in their manes. Kelpies are said to live in several of the different lochs around Scotland. Some people even think that the Loch Ness monster might be a kelpie.

Even if Loch Ness might be a good home for a monster, there isn’t any proof that a monster actually lives there. Every time someone claims to have a picture of the monster, it turns out to be a joke or a hoax. One of the most famous hoaxes was a picture taken in 1934. For a long time, people believed the photograph really showed a monster. It wasn’t until 1994 that the picture was shown to be a hoax.

There’s a lot of mystery around creatures that might live in the gray, deep lake in Scotland. Do you think there might be a monster living down there? You never quite know what’s lurking below the surface!

options.label: People in ancient Scotland did not believe the Loch Ness monster was real, but people believe it is real today.
Loch Ness was a popular place to visit, but people became less interested after the first Loch Ness monster sighting.
People have reported sightings of the Loch Ness monster for over a thousand years, but no one can prove that it is real.
The Loch Ness monster was invented to keep people from swimming in Scotland's lakes, but it has been proven to be a hoax.

articles.title: The Return of Sal Caputo
articles.author: Timothy Tocher
articles.description: A boy tries to convince his father to go to a baseball game.
articles.intro: During World War II, hundreds of baseball players joined the army. Many major league teams were made up of older players and those who were unable to fight in the war. In this story, a boy misses his father who is fighting in World War II.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Dad’s feelings about baseball change throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "The Return of Sal Caputo" by Timothy Tocher, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Two years I waited for my father to come home from the war. Winter nights, I pored over the scorecards from games we had attended at Yankee Stadium. On summer Sundays, I studied the batting averages printed in the newspaper and longed for his presence. Our shared love for baseball had made us as much friends as father and son. I was proud to be named Lou Gehrig Caputo after his all-time favorite player.

He sounded like the same old Dad in his letters. After basic training, he wasn’t allowed to say where he was stationed or what he was doing, so we filled the pages with baseball. I told him which major leaguers had joined up, and we speculated on who would win the pennant. Neither of us guessed the St. Louis Browns. Their team was filled with veteran players who had seen better days. When Uncle Sam came looking for soldiers, he passed the Browns and their limping lineup by. Playing against the other teams’ substitutes, the Browns won the 1944 flag.

For a nervous few weeks, Dad’s letters stopped. One September afternoon, I came home from school and found my mother crying. Tears filled my own eyes, until she said, “He’s safe, Lou, and coming home to us.”

The bad news came later. My father had landed in Normandy as part of the D-Day invasion. All summer he was in the thick of the fighting. When a mortar landed near his foxhole, the explosion took his right hand. My first thought was that we couldn’t play catch anymore. That was selfish. How would he work his mail route with one hand? How would he earn a living?

The Army never said it, but my father lost more than his hand in that explosion. The man who came home looked like Sal Caputo, but he sure didn’t act like him. He seldom spoke, instead choosing to sit on the edge of his bed and stare out the window. If I made a reference to baseball, he would wave his hand as if swatting away an annoying insect. My mother kept up a stream of cheerful chatter, but he ignored her, too.

Mr. McDougal, Dad’s boss at the post office, came to visit. When my father wouldn’t come into the kitchen to talk, Mr. McDougal went into his bedroom. Mother and I couldn’t make out the words, but the only voice we heard was McDougal’s. On the way out, he took my mother’s hand. “Tell him to come see me, Rose. I’m sure we can work something out.”
“As soon as he feels better,” she promised. But months went by, and Dad never left our apartment.

One May evening, I lay on my bed reading the sports page of the Journal-American. Sunday was my birthday, and the schedule showed that the Yankees would play a double-header against the Browns. In seasons past, my father would have taken me. It was almost too painful to read about baseball, until I came across an article on a St. Louis rookie outfielder who was tearing up the league. Then I knew I had to get my father to Yankee Stadium.
At dinner that night, Mom asked, “Well, Lou, have you decided what you want for your birthday?”
I nodded. “The Yankees and Browns play two Sunday. I want Dad to take me to the ballpark .”
“That’s a great idea. And my present will be all the hot dogs you both can eat while you’re there. What do you say, Sal?”
“I don’t care about baseball anymore,” Dad said.
“Come on, Dad. Remember how you taught me to watch how the fielders moved to defend different hitters?”
“Go with a friend, go with your mother, or go alone,” he said, sliding back his chair. “I’m not parading around Yankee Stadium, and that’s that.” Moments later the bedroom door slammed.
Next morning, I left for school, but as soon as I turned the corner, I sat on the curb and waited for my mother. She showed up ten minutes later on the way to her part-time job at the diner.
“If it’s about the game, don’t ask,” she said. “I spoke to your father again this morning. He won’t admit it, but he’s embarrassed. He thinks everyone will stare and feel sorry for him.”
“That’s why he has to go to this double-header,” I insisted, and handed her the clipping I had cut from the newspaper.

She read for a minute, then her face lit with the brightest smile I had seen since before the war. “I’ll buy the tickets after work today and pester him until he gives in. If there’s one person in New York who should be at the ballpark Sunday, it’s Sal Caputo.”
Mom was as good as her word. At noon on Sunday, my clean shaven father and I walked to the bus stop. Though it was a warm day, he wore a sports jacket, his empty right cuff tucked into its pocket.
Mr. Belmonte, the grocer, called, “Welcome home, Sal.”
Dad kept walking. I had our bus fares ready, so he wouldn’t have to grope in his pockets for coins. We rode in silence, Dad staring out the bus window, his face blank.

In the stadium, I watched for the sparkle in his eye that used to accompany his first glimpse of the lush, green grass and manicured dirt of the diamond. He might as well have been looking at a brick wall. Ordinarily, we would have sat near the infield, but I had asked Mom to get us seats near the left field foul pole. I wanted my father to get a close look at the Browns’ newest outfielder.

As the visiting team, St. Louis batted first. They looked like the pre-war Browns. Two weak pop-ups and a strike out sent them scurrying for their gloves. Dad wore his vacant stare until rookie Pete Gray trotted to his position, right under our seats.

Gray looked like a ballplayer. He was six feet one inch tall, wiry and strong at 170 pounds. He wore his uniform with the confidence of a man who is comfortable in his work. But something set Gray apart from his teammates, opponents, and any other man who had ever played in the major leagues. Gray had only one arm.
Pete wore his glove on his left hand. An empty sleeve drooped from the elbow where his right arm should have been.
“Can he play?” Dad asked, inching forward on his seat.
I shrugged. “He was Minor League Player of the Year last season at Memphis.”

In the third inning, Gray came to bat for the first time. To the delight of the crowd, he ripped a fastball up the middle for a clean single. Dad leapt up to cheer, not even noticing when his right cuff popped out of his jacket pocket.

That afternoon, Pete Gray enjoyed the finest day of his career. Dad and I gulped down hot dogs and swigged sodas while he singled four times, scored twice, and knocked in two of his teammates. His defense was even more amazing. When Pete ran down a fly ball, he would flip the ball into the air, wedge his glove under the stump of his right arm, catch the ball in his bare hand, and gun it back to the infield. He did it so quickly that base runners didn’t dare try to advance. The Browns split the double-header and couldn’t have done it without their star rookie.

When the long day ended, Dad and I walked down the ramp to street level, his good arm resting on my shoulder. “Lou, I always told you there were lessons to be learned from the baseball diamond. If this guy Gray didn’t quit, I won’t either. Tomorrow morning I’m going down to the post office to see about a job.”
I wrapped my right arm around him and squeezed, too happy to talk.
“And,” he went on, “if you don’t mind chasing a few wild ones, I’d like to try throwing with my left hand.”
“Welcome home, Dad,” was all I could say.

articles.title: The Fox and the Lion
articles.author: Aesop
articles.description: A fox has never seen a lion before, but he is still terrified.
articles.intro: Aesop was a storyteller who lived in ancient Greece between 620 and 560 BCE. This story is part of his collection of tales known as "Aesop's Fables," which did not survive in writing but were passed down by people retelling them. They have deeply influenced children's literature and modern storytelling culture.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how and why the fox’s attitude changes, and how his behavior could be considered similar to human behavior.
articles.permissions: The Fox and the Lion by Aesop is in the public domain.

blocks.text: When a Fox who had never yet seen a Lion fell in with him by chance for the first time in the forest, he was so frightened that he nearly died with fear. On meeting him for the second time, he was still much alarmed, but not to the same extent as at first. On seeing him the third time, he so increased in boldness that he went up to him and commenced a familiar conversation with him. "Red Fox at Shepreth" by Feggy Art is licensed Acquaintance softens prejudices. under CC BY-NC-ND 2.0.

options.label: Be kind to everyone you meet, because you never know what someone else is going through
First impressions are very important
The familiar something becomes, the less frightening it is
You can't judge a book by its cover

articles.title: The Elves and the Shoemaker
articles.author: Barbara Radner
articles.description: In this classic story, a poor shoemaker received a fortuitous visit from a group of elves.
articles.intro: What follows is a retelling of a story that dates back to the 19th century. The original story was published in an 1812 book of fairy tales written by the Grimm brothers, Jacob and Wilhelm. The Brothers Grimm, as they came to be known, were German researchers, authors, and academics who brought a wide variety of folktales to a very large audience and helped them become popular.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, think about the lesson that the author is trying to reveal to the readers.
articles.permissions: "The Elves and the Shoemaker" by Barbara Radner. Copyright © 2005 by Barbara Radner. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: Once upon a time there was an honest shoemaker who was very poor. He worked as hard as he could, and still he could not earn enough to keep himself and his wife. At last there came a day when he had nothing left but one piece of leather, big enough to make one pair of shoes. He cut out the shoes, ready to stitch, and left them on the bench; then he said his prayers and went to bed, trusting that he could finish the shoes the next day and sell them.

Bright and early the next morning, he rose and went to his workbench. There lay a pair of shoes, beautifully made, and the leather was gone! There was no sign of anyone having been there. The shoemaker and his wife did not know what to make of it. But the first customer who came was so pleased with the beautiful shoes that he bought them, and paid so much that the shoemaker was able to buy leather enough for two pairs.

Happily, he cut them out, and then, as it was late, he left the pieces on the bench, ready to sew in the morning. But when morning came, two pairs of shoes lay on the bench, most beautifully made, and no sign of anyone who had been there. It was a puzzle. That day a customer came and bought both pairs, and paid so much for them that the shoemaker bought leather for four pairs, with the money.
Once more he cut out the shoes and left them on the bench. And in the morning all four pairs were made.

It went on like this until the shoemaker and his wife were prosperous people. But they could not be satisfied to have so much done for them and not know to whom they should be grateful. So one night, after the shoemaker had left the pieces of leather on the bench, he and his wife hid themselves behind a curtain, and left a light in the room.

Just as the clock struck twelve, the door opened softly and two tiny elves came dancing into the room, hopped on to the bench, and began to put the pieces together. They were quiet, but they had wee little scissors and hammers and thread. Tap! Tap! went the little hammers; stitch, stitch, went the thread, and the little elves were hard at work. No one ever worked so fast as they. In almost no time all the shoes were stitched and finished. Then the little creatures whisked away out of the window.
The shoemaker and his wife looked at each other and said, “How can we thank the little elves who have made us happy and prosperous?”
“I should like to make them some pretty clothes,” said the wife.
“I will make the shoes if you will make the coats,” said her husband.

That very day they commenced their task. The wife cut out two tiny, tiny coats of green, two little pairs of trousers, of white, two bits of caps, bright red, and her husband made two little pairs of shoes with long, pointed toes. They made the clothes as dainty as could be, with nice little stitches and pretty buttons.
By Christmas time, they were finished.
On Christmas Eve, the shoemaker cleaned his bench, and on it, instead of leather, he laid the two sets of clothes. Then he and his wife hid away as before, to watch.

Promptly at midnight, the little elves came in. They hopped upon the bench; but when they saw the little clothes there, they laughed and danced for joy. Each one caught up his little coat and things and began to put them on. They were so happy. Then, when the clock struck two, they left smiling. They never came back any more, but from that day they gave the shoemaker and his wife good luck, so that they never needed any more help.

options.label: People who are not successful need to receive all the help they can get from others.
A great way to show your appreciation for what you have is to do kind things for others.
The best way to measure one's happiness is by how much money they have.
Hard work is only worth it if it leads to riches.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best describes a theme of this story?

options.label: "Once upon a time there was an honest shoemaker who was very poor. He worked as hard as he could, and still he could not earn enough to keep himself and his wife." (Paragraph 1)

"No one ever worked so fast as they. In almost no time all the shoes were stitched and finished." (Paragraph 6)
"They were so happy.…They never came back any more, but from that day they gave the shoemaker and his wife good luck, so that they never needed any more help." (Paragraph 13)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which phrase from the story best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It shows that the elves were very excited to see that shoemaker and his wife returned their favor.

It proves that the elves were poor themselves, and they helped the shoemaker because they know what it's like to have bad luck.

questions.prompt: How does the following phrase help develop the plot of the story: "They hopped upon the bench; but when they saw the little clothes there, they laughed and danced for joy" (Paragraph 13)?

options.label: angry that the elves entered their home without permission
surprised yet grateful and wanting to repay the elves
happy that someone else was doing their work so they could relax
greedy and excited that they could sell the shoes for more than they were worth

questions.prompt: Which phrase best describes the reactions of the shoemaker and his wife when they learned what the elves were doing?
Identify and explain the theme of the story. Cite evidence from the text in your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: What do the events of the story tell us about the shoemaker as a person? Is he hard- working or lazy? Grateful or ungrateful?
Why do you think such a fantastical fairy tale would still be remembered and told in modern times? Is there something about the story that might apply to everyday life?

How would you describe the last line of the story? Why is it significant that the elves no longer needed to return after they had given the shoemaker a bit of help? Is it better to help someone who wants to achieve success for him/herself, or to provide for someone entirely?

In "The Elves and the Shoemaker," the elves never interact directly with the couple they help, and yet they are still willing to offer their assistance. In the context of this story, what is good and how do we know? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Carving Stories in Cedar
articles.author: Kristine F. Anderson
articles.description: This article describes the First People's practice of carving trees into traditional totem poles.
articles.intro: The native, or first people, to live in the Northwest of the United States and Canada are known for making beautiful totem poles from cedar trees. In this article, Kristine F. Anderson describes the First People's practice of carving trees into totem poles.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why totem poles are called “silent books.”
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Everyone likes to hear family stories and legends. Many tales are passed down from generation to generation. The native people who lived along the northwest coast of the United States and Canada hundreds of years ago did not have a written language. These “First People” came up with a special way of preserving their stories and family histories.

Master carvers selected cedar trees from nearby forests. To record family histories and legends, they carved pictures and symbols into the soft wood. They also carved poles to honor their chiefs and important dead. These carvings have become known as totem poles .
“The poles helped us tell our stories,” says Israel Shotridge, a Tlingit (KLINK-it) carver from Ketchikan, Alaska. “They helped us preserve our culture and identity.”

The carvers topped many totem poles, sometimes called silent books, with the animal each clan or family used as its name and crest. The First People placed the towering sculptures in front of their homes to identify their clans and near waterfront areas to welcome visitors to their villages.
Carving and Raising a Pole
Shotridge, who has been carving for twenty years, makes many of his own tools, including some of his knives and small adzes, or axes. “Tradition is an important part of what I do,” he says.

Before he begins carving, Shotridge finalizes the design by talking with the person who wants him to carve the pole. Some of the poles are reproductions of old poles; others are new designs that are used for businesses, government buildings, and schools.

Next, Shotridge selects a tall straight cedar and sings special songs his mother — a tribal elder — taught him. He strips the bark off and draws the design on the wood with a special grease pencil. Sometimes he adds extra pieces of wood to show a bird’s wings or a fish’s fin.
An assistant or an apprentice may work with him on the larger poles. But even with a helper, it takes almost a year for Shotridge to complete some poles.

Shotridge paints his poles with traditional colors — red, teal blue, and black. Early carvers made their paints from local minerals, such as copper and iron ore, mixed with salmon eggs. But Shotridge, like most modern carvers, buys his paint.
When the pole is almost finished, plans are made to raise it. Cranes hoist big poles, which often weigh over a thousand pounds, into place. Smaller poles are raised by hand.
“We usually raise poles the old-fashioned way,” says Shotridge. “We use lots of human-power and ropes.”

When the pole is raised, the person who commissioned the carving often hosts a big celebration called a potlatch . Everyone feasts, and sometimes gifts are exchanged. The bigger the pole, the bigger the celebration.
If the pole was commissioned by one of the First People, guests sing songs from their family clan.
Once the pole has been raised, another silent book stands to tell the story of the people of the Northwest.

options.label: Totem poles are an important way for every family to share what is important to them.
The First People use totem poles to share their important stories and history.
There are not enough cedar trees left to build the totem poles people want.
Israel Shotridge wants to teach more people how to build totem poles.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: to drop
to lift
to make

options.label: describing why Shotridge is famous.

questions.prompt: Paragraphs 12-13 help the reader understand the text by —

discussionQuestions.prompt: The First People in the Northwest use totem poles to share stories that are important to their families and people. How does your family share stories? Do they share pictures, videos, or tell tales? What is your favorite family story?

Israel Shotridge carves totem poles that tell stories, so he is both an author and an artist. If you had a story to tell, how would you like to share it: through art or through words? Why? If you would share your story through art, which medium would you choose? Why?

At the end of the text, the author describes how people celebrate the totem pole being raised by having a potlatch. How do you celebrate important events with your family? How is your celebration similar to and different from the celebration described in the text?

articles.title: The Man Who Painted Truth
articles.author: Kathleen M. Hays
articles.description: Kathleen M. Hays describes how Oscar Howe changed the style of Native American art.
articles.intro: Oscar Howe was a Native American artist in the early 1900s who became known for his modern paintings. In this text, the author describes the art Oscar Howe created.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, find evidence that supports why “The Man Who Painted Truth” is a good title for this article.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Oscar Howe sat quietly at his drawing table, concentrating on a piece of blank paper. He was looking for what his Dakota Sioux ancestors called “points of beauty”. After a while, he picked up his pencil and began to connect the imaginary points with delicate lines. Gradually the picture in his mind appeared in the maze of lines on the paper.

Although points of beauty were an important part of an ancient Dakota ceremony, Howe’s paintings looked modern. Too modern, many people thought. All of his life he struggled to convince others that his paintings could be both modern and true to his heritage.
Lines of Magic and Beauty

Oscar Howe was born on South Dakota’s Crow Creek Reservation in 1915. Starting at age three, he began to draw lines on paper. “Each line had a fascination for me,” he said. “I thought of magic and beauty.”
Oscar’s parents did not understand his lines. The lines were abstract and did not seem to represent familiar objects like people and animals.

His parents took away his pencil and paper, so Howe began to draw with charcoal. When they forbade him to draw with charcoal, he drew on the ground outside. Though he was very young, Howe had already begun to develop his own artistic vision and style.
Studio Style

Howe attended high school in New Mexico, where he won a place in the Santa Fe Indian School’s painting program. The program, which became world famous at the Studio, was run by Dorothy Dunn. Dunn encouraged Howe and other students to paint subjects from their tribal backgrounds. She taught everyone to draw with firm outlines and little or no shading, to keep background details to a minimum, and to use natural colors. The style Dunn taught became the style all Native American painters were expected to use.
Finding His Own Truth

Early in his career, Howe painted in the Studio style. He painted pictures of Sioux boys on horseback, buffalo hunts, and deer bounding across the prairie. But as he became more confident, his work began to change.

He experimented with the traditional “point-and-line” technique of Dakota painters. Designs were created from point to point. Curved or straight lines connected each point. The pictures that emerged were filled with geometric shapes and bursting with color and emotion. They were not at all like the pictures he had learned to paint at the Studio.

Critics complained that his new style looked too modern to be Native American. Howe disagreed. “I have taken the straight line out of the Dakota past and used it as a part of my art,” he said. “Its meaning remains the same — the truth.” In Dakota pictographs and sign language, a straight line stood for truth. Howe had begun to paint his own truth.

Howe returned to the reservation often to talk to the old people. “I heard the truth from them and responded by painting them in like manner of their words,” he said. But his work continued to draw criticism. In 1958, he submitted a painting to a Native American art competition. The painting was rejected. The judges did not think it fit the rules for Native American art.
Howe wrote a letter of protest to the museum: “Are we to be held back forever...with no right for individualism, dictated to as the Indian has always been...?”
His words made museum directors, art critics, and teachers all over the world stop and think. Shouldn’t Native American artists be treated like other artists, free to paint as they wished?
Changing Native American Art

The next year, the museum changed its rules to allow different styles of painting for their competition. Howe was awarded the grand prize. In his independent way, he has won a battle as dramatic as any fought by his Dakota ancestors.

“In art I have realized a part of a dream,” Howe once said. “To present a true image of the Dakota Indian as I understood him and his culture.” By the time of his death in 1983, Oscar Howe’s efforts to paint his vision of the truth had forever changed Native American art.

options.label: by describing Oscar Howe's life from birth to death
by showing how different artists appreciate Oscar Howe's work
by using details to describe what one of Oscar Howe's paintings looks like
by telling how other Native American artists have faced struggles like Oscar Howe

articles.title: The Many and the Few
articles.author: J. Patrick Lewis
articles.description: A speaker describes Rosa Parks's refusal to give up her seat on a bus in Montgomery.
articles.intro: J. Patrick Lewis is an American poet and prose writer known for his children's poetry. In this poem, a speaker describes the effects of Rosa Parks refusing to give up her seat on the bus for a white passenger.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on who “the Many” and “the Few” are.
articles.permissions: "The Many and the Few" by J. Patrick Lewis. Copyright © 2001 by J. Patrick Lewis. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: For Rosa Parks, Part-time Seamstress, Montgomery, Alabama, December 1, 1955
It was an Alabama day
For both the Many and the Few.
There wasn’t really much to do;
No one had very much to say.
Until a bus, the 4:15,
Drove by. But no one chanced to see
It stop to pick up history.
The doors closed slowly on a scene.
The quiet seamstress paid her fare
And took the one seat she could find,
And, as it happened, just behind
The Many People sitting there.
The Many People paid no mind
Until the driver, J.P. Blake,
Told the Few of them to take
The deeper seats. But she declined.
Blake stopped the bus and called the police;
And Many a fire was set that night,
And Many a head turned ghostly white
Because she dared disturb the peace.
To celebrate the ride that marks
The debt the Many owe the Few,
That day of freedom grew into
The Century of Rosa Parks.

options.label: One person's action can impact many others.
It doesn't take much to create important change.
It takes many people to create positive change in the world.
Only certain people have the power to start a movement.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following identifies a theme of the poem?

"The quiet seamstress paid her fare / And took the one seat she could find" (Lines 9-10)
"And Many a head turned ghostly white / Because she dared disturb the peace." (Lines 19-20)
"The debt the Many owe the Few, / That day of freedom grew" (Lines 22-23)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It makes the events seem unimportant.
It emphasizes the importance of the events that took place.
It shows the events that took place as a small part of the Civil Rights movement.
It stresses how dangerous Parks' decision was not to give up her seat.

articles.title: Windows that Capture The Sun
articles.author: Anna Levine
articles.description: Marc Chagall was a famous Russian-French painter who designed stained-glass windows later in life.
articles.intro: Marc Chagall (1887-1985) was a famous Russian-French artist. In this text, Anna Levine describes how Chagall began designing stained-glass windows.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the events in Chagall’s life.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: At the age of 70, the famous painter Marc Chagall discovered that by designing stained-glass windows, he could breathe bright color and light into his artwork.
"I had to make stained-glass windows. I had to get myself into daylight," he said.

By the late 1950s, Chagall had already spent more than 50 years exploring the bold use of color. His paintings had made him famous and inspired countless artists. From then until his death in 1985, he created windows for churches, chapels, synagogues, and other buildings around the world.
Today, people still marvel at how his stained-glass creations flicker and change with the sun.

Born in 1887 in a Russian-Jewish town, Chagall started painting at the age of 19. He was frustrated by the neutral colors of traditional Russian art. He loved to paint with strong blues, reds, and yellows. In 1911, he moved to Paris, France. There, actors, writers, musicians, and artists from around the world were creating new art forms.
Bold Fantasies and Colors

Chagall learned new ways of painting but stayed true to his childhood memories. While others chose modern subjects, he painted scenes of fantasy and fairy tales based on an older, peasant-like way of life. Acrobats and green horses floating above rooftops, yellow skies, and red trees were all creations of his imagination. "My familiar sources remained the same," he said. "I did not become a Parisian, but now the light came from outside"

Chagall lived through turmoil and tragedy. When World War I began in 1914, he happened to be in his hometown. He and his new wife, Bella, were not able to leave for Paris for nine years. Then, in 1941, because they were Jewish, the Chagalls had to leave France to escape persecution by the Nazis during World War II. In 1944 came another devastating blow: Chagall's beloved Bella died of an infection.
Drawn to Stained Glass
By the 1950s, Chagall's life was calmer. The war had ended. He had returned to France and remarried. He continued to make his dreamlike paintings and was now world-famous.

One day, during a stroll through the French countryside, he discovered a beautiful chapel. He imagined replacing its walls with stained-glass windows. Some time later, in a 700-year-old cathedral, he saw a stained-glass window that contained a green donkey. It looked as if Chagall might have created it himself. Here was a sure sign, he thought, that he should create his own stained-glass windows.

He began collaborating with master glassworker Charles Marq. Many stained-glass windows are made of flat colors, but Chagall used various techniques to create the effects he wanted. He painted images on glass, then had them fired to high temperatures, making the lines and colors part of the glass. "Stained glass is not so easy," he once said. "You have to approach it as if you were trying to catch a mouse. Not in a cage... but with your hand. There are no foolproof methods, you either catch it or you don't."
Marq admired Chagall's energy. He said Chagall worked "with the precision of those tightrope walkers he loves, who fly in their weightlessness up there thanks to an immense amount of everyday work."

When Chagall was asked to design stained-glass windows for a synagogue in Jerusalem, Israel, he faced a new challenge. The Jewish religion forbids the drawing of human figures. Since Chagall was not allowed to include images of people in the windows, what could he do instead?
Bible Stories in Glass

He told the story of the 12 tribes of Israel with animals. He drew the animals described in the Bible stories to represent each of the tribes. He then chose a bright color for each window. When the sunlight shines through the glass, it sparkles as if made of rubies, diamonds, and emeralds.

At the same time, Chagall was creating huge stained-glass windows for the Roman Catholic Metz Cathedral in France. These works tell the stories of biblical heroes. Chagall's Jacob wrestling an angel flickers and glistens with life in the sun.

In 1964, Chagall designed a stained-glass window for the United Nations building in New York City. The United Nations works for international peace. Using a blue background as the symbol of peace and life, Chagall designed the window with an angel soaring from a bouquet of flowers, animals floating in the sky, and mothers cuddling their children.

Chagall had lived through two world wars and personal tragedy, but he still had hope for the future. He often refused payment for his stained-glass windows, especially those he made for places of worship. He created them as gifts for the world, gifts that capture the sun.

options.label: People have different feelings when they look at art, like happiness, joy, and sorrow.
Stained-glass windows are beautiful pieces of art that show religious images.
Marc Chagall was a famous painter who began making stained-glass art.
Many artists switch to different types of art forms later in their lives.

What would you say if they decided to try something new?

articles.title: Destination Endangered Species
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains how three animals have become endangered.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, DC. An endangered species is a species, a group of living things, that is very likely to become extinct in the near future. When a species becomes extinct, they no longer live on Earth. In this article, Pratt-Tuke explains why some animals are becoming endangered.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the reasons species are becoming endangered.
articles.permissions: "Destination Endangered Species" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: How do you spend your summers? Lazing on the couch? Visiting family? Sun-soaked days at the pool? A family trip?

Russell Laman spent his childhood summers on rather unconventional family vacations. After the school bell sounded for the last time in June, his family boarded a flight to the island of Borneo in Asia. Russell’s travel plan: Forty hours on a plane across the Pacific Ocean, a bumpy van ride to the edge of Gunung Palung National Park, and a boat ride upriver to a research camp where his parents study orangutans. Russell made his first family trip when he was less than one year old.

Russell’s mother is a biological anthropologist, and his father is a photographer for National Geographic . Russell, now 20 years old, joined the family business of researching orangutans. Flying drones into the treetops, Russell captures remote footage of orangutans nesting.

According to the World Wildlife Fund (WWF), orangutans on the island of Borneo are critically endangered with less than 105,000 left. Critically endangered is a title given to species with a high risk of extinction. In this article, we will take our own trip to visit a few critically endangered species, starting with one of Russell’s favorites, the Borneo Orangutans.
STOP #1: BORNEO ORANGUTANS

High in the treetops of the Borneo rainforest lives the shaggy, red-haired Borneo orangutans. In Malay, a native language of Southeast Asia, orangutan means “person of the forest” which is fitting given that orangutans share almost 97% of the same DNA as humans.

These tree-dwelling mammals swing from branch to branch with powerful arms that span 7 feet from fingertip to fingertip. That’s almost twice the arm span of a ten-year-old. They mostly eat fruit but also snack on leaves, nuts, bark, and insects. As they eat, orangutans spread seeds and support the rainforest ecosystem by planting the next generation of trees.

Over the last fifty years, the chainsaw and tractor reshaped Borneo’s rainforests. With the buzz of a chainsaw, companies leveled the rainforest to make space for logging and mining. Farmers cleared trees, and tractors plowed millions of acres to plant palm oil plantations. Deforestation at breakneck speed has destroyed over half of the orangutans’ rainforest home, according to PBS. With their homes and food supply in ruins, orangutans sometimes raid plantations for fruit, leaves, and nuts. Sadly, farmers poison or shoot orangutans that they think pose a threat to their crops.

Hunting and the exotic pet trade also endanger orangutans. Each year, hunters kill thousands of orangutans and sell their meat in Asian markets. In addition, an estimated 200 to 500 young orangutans are taken from their treetop homes and sold illegally as pets. Female orangutans only have babies every three to five years, so it takes years to replace disappearing orangutans. Scientists worry that if the decline in orangutans continues, they could be close to extinction by 2050.
STOP #2: AFRICAN FOREST ELEPHANT

Buckle up for a trip west to the Democratic Republic of Congo in central Africa, one of the last strongholds of the African forest elephant. African forest elephants are one of the two kinds of elephants that live in Africa. Unlike the famous African savanna elephants — think about the kind seen in The Lion King , African forest elephants are smaller and live in the rainforests of central and western Africa.

African forest elephants live with their family in herds. They munch on leaves, grasses, seeds, and fruit. Like the Borneo orangutan, African forest elephants help spread seeds and replant trees. Because of their tree-planting skills, scientists call them “mega gardeners of the forest.” African forest elephants also serve as the rainforest’s trashmen. They remove debris and fallen trees with their muscular trunks, helping the ecosystem by clearing paths for other animals.

Long ago, millions of elephants roamed the savannas and forests of Africa. But deforestation and poaching threaten elephants. According to International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN), scientists estimate that the elephant population has dropped by 86% with less than 40,000 African forest elephants left in the wild. With humans removing trees for agriculture, roads, and towns, between 1979 and 2007, forest elephants have lost 67% of the land they used to roam. As a result, these mammoth mammals have less land and less food.

Like the savanna elephants, African forest elephants have long, ivory tusks that frame their wrinkled faces. In exchange for money, poachers shoot or poison elephants and then chop off their tusks to trade illegally. Over half of the poached ivory travels to China to be made into jewelry and other accessories. The United States is the second-biggest consumer of illegal ivory. Despite laws to protect elephants from illegal hunting, poaching has reached an all-time high with roughly 100 elephants killed each day for their tusks according to Wall Street Journal .
STOP #3: HAWKSBILL TURTLE

In the warm waters along the equator, meet the Hawksbill turtle. About three feet long, hawksbill turtles are named for their hawk-like, pointy beaks. Using their beaks, hawksbills reach into the cracks in coral reefs to pull out sponges — their favorite food. By eating fast-growing sponges, hawksbills prevent them from overtaking slow-growing coral reefs. Coral reefs are homes to fish, offer safe and sheltered nurseries for infant organisms, and protect coastlines from crashing waves. Thanks to the Hawksbill turtles, coral reefs can thrive.

Hawksbill turtles are well-known for their red, brown, and yellow shells. But, their beauty has a price. Over the last 150 years, nearly nine million hawksbills were killed for their shells according to National Geographic . The tortoiseshell is fashioned into jewelry and other decorations which are illegally traded around the world. In 1977, hawksbills gained international protection from poaching; however, many turtles are still illegally caught and killed for their striking shells.

In tropical oceans, fishermen catch fish with wide nets and long fishing lines with thousands of razor-sharp hooks. Bycatch is when fishermen accidentally catch a marine animal that they do not want. Attracted by the fishermens’ bait, hawksbills get caught in fishing nets and lines. Tangled in nets and hooks, hawksbills cannot reach the surface to breathe and drown or suffer serious injuries. Conservationists estimate that commercial fishing is 2.5 times larger than what the ocean ecosystem can sustain, reports WWF . With too many fishing boats working the water, too many turtles are caught and killed unintentionally.

Beyond poachers and bycatch, turtles face another threat: climate change. When it is time to lay eggs, a female hawksbill waddles onto a beach and digs a hole in the sand, filling it with over 100 turtle eggs. Two months later the baby turtles hatch and scuttle back to the water to start their oceanic life. But rising water levels and stronger storms due to climate change erode beaches where hawksbills nest. Winds, rains, and crashing waves wash away nests of baby turtles hidden in the sand before they can even hatch. Warmer temperatures have another unexpected impact on hawksbills. The temperature of beach sand determines the gender of the baby turtle. Warmer beaches mean more female turtles are born. In 2019, a team of scientists found that female baby turtles now outnumber males 116 to 1, according to The Washington Post .

articles.title: First Eid in America
articles.author: Kinza Yasar
articles.description: A Muslim girl celebrates her first Eid in her new community in the United States.
articles.intro: Kinza Yasar was born in Pakistan, a country in South Asia, and moved to the United States with her family as a teenager. In this text, she writes about a girl named Farzeen who is celebrating her first Eid in the United States. Eid is a holiday celebrating the end of the Muslim holy month of Ramadan.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Farzeen feels.
articles.permissions: "First Eid in America" by Kinza Yasar, from Skipping Stones Multicultural Magazine, July - Sept. 2020, Vol. 32.

blocks.text: It had been six long months since our arrival into this quaint little town on the outskirts of Virginia and spring was just around the corner. Just as we were shedding off our winter coats and the gloomy memories of a long winter, the spring arrived with its full bloom. What a spring it was! So enigmatic, so pure and so full of life. Just by looking outside the French window of our small apartment, we would forget all our worries and every pinch of struggle felt worthwhile.

It was also the blessed month of Ramadan and tomorrow was Eid. Back home in Pakistan, Eid was my favorite holiday. Our entire household would get struck with the chords of excitement in the days leading up to Eid, reaching its peak on Chaand Raat . After breaking the fast, we would all gather at the rooftop, hoping to catch a glimpse of the tiny silver crescent that played peekaboo with us behind the clouds before revealing itself. We would then head to Meena bazaar to shop for bangles and get intricate henna patterns on our hands. Eid day was all about family visits, collecting Eidi money and stuffing ourselves with scrumptious food.

The next morning, after offering the Eid prayer at our local mosque, we came back and helped ourselves to the sheer khurma that Ammi had prepared the night before. There were no visitors and no one to visit. An emptiness loomed inside me, reminding me that Eid will never be the same anymore. After contemplation, I came up with a plan to make our first Eid in America a little festive. I followed Ammi into the kitchen.
“Ammi , can we please throw a mother-daughter Eid party for all our neighbors tonight?” I requested sheepishly.
Ammi reflected for a moment and then agreed.
“That’s a great idea! Most of our neighbors don’t celebrate Eid, but they can learn, and you might make new friends along the way!”

I hugged Ammi and went to my desk. After shuffling through a pile of supplies, I finally settled on a roll of green card stock, silver glitter foam, silver rhinestone stickers, scissors, and a glue stick. I made ten green cards displaying a silver crescent moon on top and tiny rhinestone stars sparkling around it. Inside the cards, I wrote my invitation notes.
“You are invited to a Mother and Daughter Eid Party. If you are in the mood for some mouth-watering food and keen to learn about Eid, please come join us at Apartment #401 at 5 PM sharp!
Thank You,
Ayesha (Mother) & Farzeen (daughter)
PS.: Farzeen is twelve years old.
I asked Ammi to accompany me and together we went door to door, sliding the invitation cards inside each mail slot in the door.

After returning, Ammi busied herself with preparing fancy Eid treats and I pretended to be her sous chef . She whipped chutneys, seasoned chickpeas, baked and fried. Finally, the food was prepared, and the dining table was adorned. The sight and smell of the mouth-watering food engulfed me with Eid nostalgia.

It was close to five and Abbu and my brother Amin left to get our car fixed at the workshop. I dressed into a peach-colored shalwar kameez and the glittery glass bangles from last year’s Eid clanked around my wrists. The only thing missing was the henna on my hands.
Just then, the doorbell rang. We opened the door and greeted an assortment of female faces. Everyone scooted and settled inside our tiny apartment.
The pretty lady with the blonde hair was the first to introduce herself: “Hi, I am Mary, and this is my daughter Lisa. We celebrate Christmas but would love to learn about Eid!”
Next was the shy-looking Hispanic woman: “Hi, my name is Joanna, and this is my daughter Marianna. We are from Peru.”
Then the Black lady in a lovely dress introduced herself: “Hi, I am Whitney, and this is my daughter Christie. We celebrate Hanukkah but want to learn about Eid!”

Lastly, the lady wearing the beautiful head scarf pitched in: “ Assalam-o-Alaikum and Eid Mubarak . I am Fariba, and these are my daughters Hania and Hira. We are from Morocco and just like you, we celebrate Eid.”

I was overjoyed to know that someone else in our building celebrated Eid! Soon after, we invited everyone to the table. All of them admired the food and ate until they could eat no more. Then Ammi started serving her special cardamom-infused milk tea. The tea cozy covering the teapot had a black velvet base with articulate silver mirror work done all over. The warmth of the tea relaxed the ambiance, and everyone started mingling. While Ammi and Fariba reminisced how they celebrated Eid back home, Mary, Joanna, and Whitney shared their Christmas and Hanukkah stories.

While we girls sat encircling the dining table admiring the bright henna on Hira and Hania’s palms. To my delight, their mom Fariba was a henna artist. The girls requested their mom to show us her henna skills. Fariba gladly opened her handbag and pulled out a cone-shaped piping bag.
“I never attend any Eid party without henna!” she said cheerfully.

Before long, everyone sat in a circle waiting for their turns. I made Lisa, Marianna and Christie go before me as they had never worn henna before. The earthy scent of henna permeated the air as Fareeba slowly drenched our palms with beautiful patterns. Our tiny apartment soon came alive with the sounds of giggles and laughter.

Just then, I remembered an old Islamic proverb that my grandma used to utter — A lot of different flowers make a bouquet . It dawned on me that all of us with our diverse backgrounds were the flowers that completed this beautiful bouquet called America. Best of all, I got an amazing henna design applied to my hands.
It was the best Eid ever; my very first Eid in America!

options.label: It is important to leave behind old ways of doing things.
Coming together with people who are different can be special.
When you are new you have to be patient before you can make friends.
If you do not understand how something is done, ask someone to teach you.

In paragraph 17 Farzeen is grateful to have lots of food to eat and in paragraph 3 she is disappointed there is no food.

Have you ever shared a holiday with people who were different from you? What happened and how did it feel? If you have not, what is a holiday tradition you would like to share and how could you share it with others?

articles.title: The Red List
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: The author explains how scientists use a Red List to determine if a species is endangered.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, D.C. Many animal species, or groups of similar living things, are in danger of going extinct, or no longer existing on Earth. In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains the purpose of the International Union for Conservation of Nature's (IUCN) Red List.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why the IUCN’s Red List is important.
articles.permissions: "The Red List" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Extinction threatens over 38,500 species. The possibility of disappearing impacts 41% of amphibians, 37% of sharks and rays, 26% of mammals, and 14% of birds. But how do scientists determine if an animal is threatened with extinction or not?
IUCN’s RED LIST

The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) — the first global environmental organization — publishes a Red List of Threatened Species. This list provides the best information about the conservation status of more than 138,000 of Earth’s species.

The Red List categorizes species based on how threatened they are. The categories include least concern , near threatened , vulnerable , endangered , critically endangered , extinct in the wild , and extinct .
LEAST CONCERN
Species named as least concern have widespread and healthy populations. Since wild populations of these species are abundant, the IUCN does not need to worry about protecting them.

The honey badger falls in the category of least concern . Honey badgers get their name from their favorite food: honey. However they really are not after honey; instead, they hunt bee larvae hidden in the sticky sweetness. They inhabit sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, and India, living in both in humid rainforests and cold mountains. Honey badgers are fierce for their size. They can take down and eat scorpions and venomous snakes, and will fight predators more than twice their size.
NEAR THREATENED
Species categorized as near threatened currently have healthy populations. Although not at risk right now, these species may become at risk in the future.

Weighing up to 2,000 pounds and running in thunderous herds, the IUCN lists thick-coated plains bison as near threatened . According to the World Wildlife Fund (WWF), up to 60 million bison once grazed North America’s grasslands. Unfortunately, westward expansion — starting in the 1800s and continuing today — has taken over land once roamed by North America’s largest animal. With land loss and population declines, plains bison could become vulnerable in the future.
WHAT ABOUT THREATENED SPECIES?
When determining if a species is threatened with extinction — either vulnerable , endangered , or critically endangered — the IUCN looks at the following factors:
Population size : how many members of the species exist in the wild
Population reduction : how much the population has decreased over time
Geographic range : the amount of land the species inhabits
Probability of extinction : how soon scientists predict a species will become extinct
VULNERABLE

Vulnerable is the first category on the IUCN’s Red List where an animal is considered threatened with extinction. A species is vulnerable if the population has less than 10,000 mature individuals — or individuals that can reproduce babies. A species also may be vulnerable if the population declines 30 to 50% over ten years or within three generations (whichever is longer). A generation starts at an animal’s birth and ends when it can reproduce. For example, giant pandas reproduce between the ages of four and six. For a giant panda, three generations would take roughly twelve years, so these black and white bears could be vulnerable if their population declined 30 to 50% over twelve years.

Third, a species is vulnerable if the total land that all species inhabit is less than 7,722 square miles. Think about adding the land that one group of pandas inhabits plus the land that another group of pandas lives on, plus the land of a third group. If the land for all the groups in one species is less than 7,722 square miles, IUCN may list the species as vulnerable . Furthermore, species may be vulnerable if one group lives on less than 772 square miles. For example, if a group of pandas loses their habitat due to deforestation and squeezes on a small plot of land smaller than 772 square miles, they also could be labeled vulnerable .

Finally, scientists use complex formulas and technology to figure out the likelihood that a species will go extinct without protection and help from humans. Vulnerable species face a high risk of extinction in the wild.

The IUCN classifies giant pandas as vulnerable . About 1,800 wild pandas live hidden in China’s mountain forests. These hefty bears mostly eat bamboo and lots of it — between 26 and 84 pounds each day. In 2016, pandas’ conservation status improved from endangered to threatened due to decreased poaching and more protected habitats. According to National Geographic, giant panda populations rose by 17% from 2003 to 2014. Yet,​​ growing cities and development continue to destroy panda habitats and bamboo. In addition, giant pandas reproduce slowly, only giving birth to a cub about every two years, keeping them vulnerable to extinction.
ENDANGERED

The IUCN classifies a species as endangered if the population has fewer than 2,500 mature individuals. Additionally, endangered species show population declines between 50 to 70% over ten years or within three generations. Third, a species may be endangered if its geographic range — or the amount of land that all species inhabit — is less than 1,930 square miles. According to scientific predictions, endangered species face a very high risk of extinction.

Blue whales are the largest animals on the planet, equaling the weight of 33 elephants. Interestingly, they are also the loudest. Their bellows can reach up to 188 decibels, even louder than a jet engine. The IUCN lists blue whales as endangered . From the 1860s until the 1960s, whalers hunted blue whales for their fat and body parts, killing up to 99% of the blue whale population.

Today, an estimated 10,000 to 25,000 blue whales inhabit Earth’s oceans. Ship crashes, entanglement in fishing nets, and climate change threaten these marine mammals. Another threat is noise pollution caused by ships, oil rigs, and coastal industry. Man-made noises in a normally quiet ocean interrupt blue whales’ communication, confusing them as they swim, feed, and breed with other blue whales.
CRITICALLY ENDANGERED

The IUCN classifies a species as critically endangered if the species has fewer than 250 mature individuals or if the population declines by 80 to 90% over ten years or three generations. In addition, a species may be critically endangered if its geographic range is less than 39 square miles or if a group is forced to live on less than four square miles of land. Critically endangered species have a 50% chance of extinction within ten years. Thus, critically endangered species face a very, very high risk of extinction in the wild.

The eastern lowland gorilla lives in the tropical rainforests of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) in Africa. Years of human war and violence in the DRC led to these gorillas being critically endangered . On top of the conflict, their habitat has been destroyed to make room for farms, ranches, and mines. According to WWF, the eastern lowland gorilla occupies only 13% of its native habitat. Scientists estimate that the gorilla population has declined by more than 50% since 1990.
EXTINCT IN THE WILD

After years and years of not finding a species in their native habitat, the species is declared extinct in the wild . Species classified as extinct in the wild only live in zoos and conservation centers. One example is Spix’s macaws. You may recognize this fluffy-headed, blue bird from the movie Rio.

Considered one of the world’s rarest birds, only 177 live in captivity. However, conservationists hatched a plan to release 52 macaws into their natural habitat. They hope that reintroducing Spix’s macaws to Brazil can grow a wild population of these birds.
EXTINCT

When the last individual of a species dies, the species is considered extinct . Scientists thought that the Pinta tortoise, found in the Galapagos Islands, went extinct in the 1900s after fishermen overtook the tortoises’ natural habitat. But in 1971, a scientist found one last Pinta tortoise. The scientist named him Lonesome George.

Lonesome George died in 2012, officially making the Pinta tortoise extinct. However, Yale University scientists have studied the genes of other Galapagos tortoises and found some that are half Pinta. Researchers hope to breed these tortoises and restore a half-sibling of the Pinta tortoises on Pinta Island.

options.label: The IUCN's Red List categorizes and tracks animal species depending on their threat of disappearing.
The IUCN's Red List categorizes animals to help scientists decide which should be placed in zoos.
The IUCN's Red List keeps many plants and animals from going extinct.
The IUCN's Red List shows scientists how to protect different animals.
