articles.title: Peaches
articles.author: Adrienne Su
articles.description: A speaker describes their experiences in America as the child of immigrant parents.
articles.intro: Adrienne Su is a Chinese American author who grew up in Georgia. In this poem, a speaker describes their experiences as the child of immigrant parents.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the significance of food to the speaker.
articles.permissions: Copyright © 2015 by Adrienne Su. Originally published in Poem-a-Day on October 23, 2015, by the Academy of American Poets.

blocks.text: A crate of peaches straight from the farm
has to be maintained, or eaten in days.
Obvious, but in my family, they went so fast,
I never saw the mess that punishes delay.
I thought everyone bought fruit by the crate,
stored it in the coolest part of the house,
then devoured it before any could rot.
I’m from the Peach State, and to those
who ask But where are you from originally,
I’d like to reply The homeland of the peach,
but I’m too nice, and they might not look it up.
In truth, the reason we bought so much
did have to do with being Chinese — at least
Chinese in that part of America, both strangers
and natives on a lonely, beautiful street
where food came in stackable containers
and fussy bags, unless you bothered to drive
to the source, where the same money landed
a bushel of fruit, a twenty-pound sack of rice.
You had to drive anyway, each house surrounded
by land enough to grow your own, if lawns
hadn’t been required. At home I loved to stare
into the extra freezer, reviewing mountains
of foil-wrapped meats, cakes, juice concentrate,
mysterious packets brought by house guests
from New York Chinatown, to be transformed
by heat, force, and my mother’s patient effort,
enough to keep us fed through flood or storm,
provided the power stayed on, or fire and ice
could be procured, which would be labor-intensive,
but so was everything else my parents did.
Their lives were labor, they kept this from the kids,
who grew up to confuse work with pleasure,
to become typical immigrants’ children,
taller than their parents and unaware of hunger
except when asked the odd, perplexing question.

options.label: Being the child of immigrants can lead a person to feel confused about their identity.
Food has the ability to bring people together, no matter what country they are from.
It can feel isolating when someone asks where you're originally from.
It is difficult for immigrants to feel accepted in a new country and new community.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement identifies the main theme of the poem?

options.label: "A crate of peaches straight from the farm / has to be maintained, or eaten in days." (Lines 1-2)
"But where are you from originally, / I'd like to reply The homeland of the peach" (Lines 9-10)

"but so was everything else my parents did. / Their lives were labor, they kept this from the kids" (Lines 31-32)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

articles.title: The Hubble Telescope: Photographing Worlds Beyond
articles.author: Marie Droual
articles.description: Marie Droual explains what scientists can learn about space from the Hubble Telescope.
articles.intro: Marie Droual is a former scientist who loves sharing the wonders of the natural world with everyone. She is based in New York City. In this informational text, Droual explains what scientists who study space, called astronomists, can learn from the Hubble Telescope.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Hubble Telescope is a “great achievement for astronomy.”
articles.permissions: "The Hubble Telescope: Photographing Worlds Beyond" by Marie Droual. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Have you ever looked up at the night sky and wondered what else could be out there? It seems dark, cold, and vast, full of potential. If you have, you’re not alone. For centuries, scientists have gazed upwards and wondered too.

Enter the telescope. A telescope is an instrument that allows the viewer to see faraway objects in space, like stars and planets. Telescopes have been used as far back as the 1600s to explore the great beyond. The first telescopes used lenses, curved clear pieces of glass, to focus the light from stars into an image your eyes can see. Just as a pair of eyeglasses makes things clearer and helps people see further away, a telescope does that on a much larger scale with a much larger lens. Nowadays, most telescopes are built with curved mirrors. Mirrors are much lighter than lenses and easier to make. A mirror in a telescope will catch even the dimmest light coming from a faraway star or planet and concentrate it into an image we can see.

But down here on Earth, we can only see so far, even with our telescopes. There are many natural and man-made elements that interfere with seeing into space, like rain clouds, lights, and the Earth’s atmosphere. Some scientists will go to the top of mountains where these earthly features aren’t as present. If only we could get high enough above all this interference…perhaps we could see further.

In the 1920s, scientists began to discuss the possibility of launching a telescope into space. After decades of research, fixing problems, and getting enough money, on April 24, 1990, the Hubble Telescope was put on a rocket and launched into space! Once the rocket got far enough away from Earth, it released the Hubble Telescope and it began to orbit the Earth. To orbit means to circle around an object, like how the Moon orbits the Earth. This was a great achievement for astronomy.

Every day since its launch, the Hubble Telescope has observed our universe as it orbits our planet. Scientists around the world can use the Hubble Telescope to take photographs. More than one million photographs taken by the Hubble have unlocked many secrets of the universe.

What are these secrets they are finding? One photograph from 1995 showed a part of the sky that looks empty to our eyes, but actually contained more than 3,000 galaxies! A galaxy is a massive collection of gas, dust, billions of stars, and all their planets, all held together with gravity. Gravity is what pulls you down to Earth. Without gravity, we’d all be flying around into space. So exactly how big are these galaxies? According to NASA , the galaxy Earth is in, the Milky Way, is 621,371,192,237,333,890 miles across, so…BIG.

Galaxies come in all sorts of shapes and sizes. Our Milky Way is shaped like a massive flat spiral. Just imagine how it would look if somebody spilled milk in the sky—that's how it got its name. Our Solar System exists in the Milky Way galaxy, and the Sun is one of its many bright stars. Although the Sun feels huge to humans, it's actually one of the smaller stars in our galaxy.

In one of the most iconic pictures captured, called the "Pillars of Creation," we see colorful gaseous blobs from which new stars are being born, something we’d never be able to see with just our naked eye. Thanks to the creation and advancement of telescopes, we can capture such wonders on film to study, share, and admire. With so many new advances in technology, who knows what we’ll discover in the years to come?

articles.title: Excerpt from "Inuit"
articles.author: Carol White
articles.description: Carol White describes how a group of people, known as the Inuit, live in the Arctic.
articles.intro: The Inuit are a group of people who have lived in the Arctic regions of Greenland, Canada, and Alaska for over a thousand years. In this excerpt from "Inuit," Carol White describes the Inuit people's way of life.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Inuit survive in their cold environment.
articles.permissions: The Kids' Site of Canadian Settlement, Inuit © Government of Canada. Reproduced with the permission of Library and Archives Canada (2018). Source: Library and Archives Canada/ www.collectionscanada.gc.ca

blocks.text: In one of the most remote places in the world, the Canadian Arctic, a people have survived over a thousand years. They are the Inuit. For the Inuit, the Arctic is a place teeming with life. Depending on how far north they live, the Inuit find everything from caribou herds and polar bears to beluga whales. The Inuit have adapted themselves to the various regions they inhabit. At one time they were considered to be among the healthiest people in the world. This is no longer the case; the Inuit lifestyle has changed dramatically over the past decades. The arrival of southerners and modern technology resulted in big changes to the Inuit diet and way of life.
Today, the Inuit are rediscovering their rich heritage and they are learning to govern themselves in a modern world.
Way of Life

When you live in an environment that has few plants, there is a very good chance you will become a hunter. The Inuit pride themselves on being great hunters. The Inuit had lots of sea and land animals to hunt. The most important of these were the caribou and the seal. These two animals provided the Inuit with food. Their skin was used for clothing, blankets, tents and boats and their oil was used for cooking and lamps. Bones, ivory and wood were used to make tools. Other animals the Inuit hunted were the walrus, whale, polar bear, musk ox, fox and wolf.
Food

Because edible plants are scarce in the Arctic, the Inuit ate mostly meat they got from hunting. They ate animals such as caribou, seals, walruses, polar bears, arctic hares, musk oxen, birds such as ptarmigan, and fish such as artic char, salmon and whitefish. In the summer they also gathered berries and other edible plants.
Clothing

Seal or walrus intestine is waterproof, and the Inuit scraped, cleaned, soaked and dried the intestines to make waterproof clothing. This kept the people dry, which was important since water freezes quickly in the North. People can get into a lot of trouble if they get wet in the Arctic and then freeze. Besides waterproof clothing, the Inuit also made parkas of caribou fur to wear in the cold winter.

At one point, scientists in Canada did a study to find out what the warmest winter clothes were. This included clothes that were sewn out of cloth, wool and other fabrics. The caribou jacket was the warmest by far. Even in winter, Inuit could not sleep with their jackets on because they got so hot that they would sweat. Sweat is dangerous in a cold climate because, like water, it freezes.
Shelter

At one time the Inuit had a summer home and a winter home. In the summer, the Inuit often lived in tents that they made from caribou hides with wooden frames. In the winter many Inuit lived in sod homes. They would dig a hole in the ground and pile rocks and sod all around the outside to make walls. Pieces of wood or whalebone were used as a frame for the roof, which the Inuit then covered with sod. In both the tents and the sod houses the Inuit built raised platforms at the back for sleeping.

The Inuit are famous for their igloos. An igloo is built of blocks of snow shaped into a dome. They were mostly used as temporary shelter during winter hunting trips. The igloo is one of the Inuit’s best inventions. It is warm and easy to construct. Most Inuit today have settled in villages and live in houses.

options.label: challenging
abandoned
plenty
modern

articles.title: Theseus and the Minotaur
articles.author: Retold by Stacey Lane
articles.description: In this retelling of an ancient Greek myth, the hero Theseus faces the Minotaur in a labyrinth.
articles.intro: In ancient Greece, people told myths to explain the mysteries of the world. In this ancient Greek myth, a brave prince must enter a labyrinth, a set of confusing and tricky connected passageways, to battle a monster known as the Minotaur.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Theseus’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Theseus and the Minotaur" by Stacey Lane, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The heavy gate slammed shut, blocking out the cruel cackle of King Minos. Alone, Theseus squinted into the silent darkness, the blood and braveness draining from his face. Why had he volunteered to be a hero? His father, King Aegeus, had tried to talk him out of it. The prince could be home in Athens right now, lounging in his palace and feasting on figs.

Instead, Theseus had set sail for the most feared place in all of Greece: the island of Crete, ruled by the wicked King Minos. Now the prince was locked in an endless labyrinth with more twists and turns than any maze in history. But being lost in the labyrinth forever was not what scared Theseus the most.

What lurked deep in the underground tunnels sent chills down the spine of every mortal in Greece: the Minotaur, a monster of unimaginable strength and fury. King Minos had built this labyrinth to contain the beast, but he could not control its hunger. So once every year, King Minos ordered fourteen Athenians to serve as his monster’s meal. Timid King Aegeus sadly obeyed to keep the peace between their warring cities. But Prince Theseus knew King Minos’s command would not stop until his monster was stopped. Daring Theseus vowed to be the first and last to go into the labyrinth and face the Minotaur.

As his eyes adjusted to the darkness, Theseus saw towering, winding walls branching out in every direction, creating narrow paths with shadowy corners. He felt his way along cool stone, careful not to make a sound.

Many had entered the labyrinth. None had made it out, but none of them had what Theseus had: a secret weapon from the princess herself. Fed up with her father sending innocent people to their doom, Princess Ariadne had come up with her own heroic plan. While King Minos was busy unlocking the gate, the bold princess had whispered simple instructions to the shaking prince. Then she slipped something to him. It wasn’t sharp. It wasn’t heavy. But it was clever.

Clutching the key to his victory, Theseus tiptoed his way into the heart of the labyrinth. Holding his head high and his shield higher, he turned and took a step. His shield banged into a wall and hit the ground with a thunderous clang. Staring at the dead end in front of him, Theseus did not see the glowing eyes pop open behind him, but he heard panting… and it was getting louder . . . getting closer. Theseus spun around.
From the shadows, a strange shape slinked toward him. Its face looked like a bull, but it stood on two legs, and its huge, hairy body was not quite like anything on Earth.

With nowhere to run, all hope lay in his secret weapon — a string. Princess Ariadne held tight to the other end of that string. She had whispered that by always keeping ahold of the string, Theseus could trace his way back to the entrance and escape the inescapable maze. It had seemed like the perfect plan, but now Theseus wondered what good it would do if he was stuck in the belly of the beast.

The Minotaur leaped at the prince, but right before it reached him, it tumbled to the ground. It had tripped on the string! Now was Theseus’s chance. He summoned all his courage and dove onto the beast’s back, grabbing it by the horns. The Minotaur rose. Theseus did not let go, determined to keep away from the monster’s chomping jaws.
Too huge to turn around in the narrow pathway, the roaring Minotaur charged forward, crashing into the dead end, again and again, trying to knock down the wall. Instead, it knocked itself out.
The nightmare was over. The heroic prince stepped around the motionless monster to scoop up the precious string. He followed it back to his freedom and freedom for all of Athens.

articles.title: Beautiful Breaks
articles.author: Stacey Lane Smith
articles.description: In the ancient Japanese art of kintsugi, artists repair cracked ceramics with special materials.
articles.intro: Ceramics are easily breakable pottery objects made from clay. In this text, Stacey Lane Smith describes how these pieces can be transformed into beautiful works of art.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes kintsugi special.
articles.permissions: "Beautiful Breaks" by Stacy Lane Smith, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: When something breaks, throw it away and buy a new one. Right? Gen Saratani aims to change that way of thinking one broken bowl at a time. This artist says that the idea that ceramics can be repaired is a shock to most people. Gen enjoys letting them know that “you don’t need to be sad when you have a chip or a break in a precious family treasure.” Not only can it be fixed, but it can be made more lovely with an ancient Japanese art called kintsugi. Gen’s family has practiced traditional Japanese lacquer art for three generations.

In kintsugi, chipped and cracked ceramics are repaired using the Japanese lacquer urushi. Urushi is the sap of a tree that grows in East Asia. The tree is a cousin of poison ivy. Its sticky sap acts like a natural glue that holds the pottery pieces together. Urushi makes the pottery shiny and strong. After it dries, real gold powder is put on the cracks to make them stand out, creating new bold designs that are truly one of a kind. The pottery is now so strong that Gen states it will stay together for thousands of years.

Although kintsugi has been practiced in Japan for over five hundred years, kintsugi has recently been growing in popularity around the world. Gen believes this is part of the movement to use materials that come from nature and to recycle and reuse.

Gen has never found any pottery that is too broken to be put back together again. However, the more tiny pieces there are, the trickier and more costly the repair will be. Major repairs can cost thousands of dollars, take months to complete, and require all the skills of a highly trained urushi master — and there aren’t too many masters still practicing.

To become a respected urushi master, one must study under a urushi master for twelve years. Gen began his study as a child helping out in his father’s art studio. His father learned from his father. Gen advises that a urushi master must be “patient and focused” because this art involves “always working alone with small pieces for a long time.”

Know what else takes patience? Waiting for a bowl to break on its own. Once pottery has been repaired with kintsugi, it is more detailed and more valuable than it was before. A boring old plain-white bowl can be transformed into a unique work of art coated with real gold. Some eager art lovers don’t like waiting around for gravity to take its course. They decide to take matters into their own hands.

Don’t think the conveniently clumsy can fool a urushi master. Gen can always tell the difference between ceramics that have been damaged by accident and those that have been smashed on purpose. Gen says that “it is hard to break in a nice shape.” When pottery is thrown to the ground or hammered, it usually ends up with “an ugly scar or crack.”

Gen finds it very strange that people come to his art studio to pay him to fix something that they just broke on purpose, but it actually happens quite often. Urushi masters have been called on to fix fake breaks for centuries. Still, Gen refuses to ever crack ceramics on purpose. Why? The most beautiful breaks are the ones that happen purely by chance.

options.label: Gen Saratani repairs broken pottery using an ancient Japanese art known as kintsugi.
It is actually quite easy to fix broken or chipped pottery if you learn from an urushi master.
Many people try to break their pottery so that they can learn how to fix it from Gen Saratani.
One cheap and beautiful way to mend a broken ceramic piece is to glue it back together with gold.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Gen shares that to be an urushi master one must be "patient and focused" (Paragraph 5). Do you think that you would enjoy being an urushi master and creating kintsugi pieces? Why or why not?

In kintsugi, broken pottery pieces are transformed into a new, beautiful work of art, rather than being thrown away. What is something else that you normally throw away when it is broken or damaged? How could you create art from this object that would otherwise end up in the trash? Does something need to look new or perfect to be beautiful? How can highlighting an object's flaws actually make it more beautiful? How can highlighting how a person has been broken or flawed make that person more beautiful?

articles.title: The Three Little Pigs
articles.author: Joseph Jacobs
articles.description: In this classic fable, three pigs each attempt to build houses to protect them from a hungry wolf.
articles.intro: Joseph Jacobs (1854-1916) was an Australian writer of folklore and literature. Some of his most popular versions of fairytales include Jack and the Beanstalk, Goldilocks and the Three Bears, and The History of Tom Thumb. Jacobs is credited for making story of The Three Little Pigs popular, though its earliest appearance in print seems to have been in The Nursery Rhymes of England by James Orchard Halliwell-Phillipps, which was published in 1886.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the way that the events of the narrative hint at the ending.
articles.permissions: "The Three Little Pigs" by Joseph Jacobs (1890) is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Once upon a time when pigs spoke rhyme
And monkeys chewed tobacco,
And hens took snuff to make them tough,
And ducks went quack, quack, quack, O!

There was an old sow with three little pigs, and as she had not enough to keep them, she sent them out to seek their fortune. The first that went off met a man with a bundle of straw, and said to him, “Please, man, give me that straw to build me a house.” Which the man did, and the little pig built a house with it.
Presently came along a wolf, and knocked at the door, and said, “Little pig, little pig, let me come in.”
To which the pig answered, “No, no, by the hair of my chiny chin chin.”
The wolf then answered to that, “Then I’ll huff, and I’ll puff, and I’ll blow your house in.” So he huffed, and he puffed, and he blew his house in, and ate up the little pig.
The second little pig met a man with a bundle of furze, and said, “Please, man, give me that furze to build a house.” Which the man did, and the pig built his house.
Then along came the wolf, and said, “Little pig, little pig, let me come in.”
“No, no, by the hair of my chiny chin chin.”
“Then I’ll puff, and I’ll huff, and I’ll blow your house in.” So he huffed, and he puffed, and he puffed, and he huffed, and at last he blew the house down, and he ate up the little pig.
The third little pig met a man with a load of bricks, and said, “Please, man, give me those bricks to build a house with.” So the man gave him the bricks, and he built his house with them.
So the wolf came, as he did to the other little pigs, and said, “Little pig, little pig, let me come in.”
“No, no, by the hair of my chiny chin chin.”
“Then I’ll huff, and I’ll puff, and I’ll blow your house in.”

Well, he huffed, and he puffed, and he huffed and he puffed, and he puffed and huffed; but he could not get the house down. When he found that he could not, with all his huffing and puffing, blow the house down, he said, “Little pig, I know where there is a nice field of turnips.”
“Where?” said the little pig.
“Oh, in Mr. Smith’s home field, and if you will be ready tomorrow morning I will call for you, and we will go together and get some for dinner.”
“Very well,” said the little pig, “I will be ready. What time do you mean to go?”
“Oh, at six o’clock.”
Well, the little pig got up at five, and got the turnips before the wolf came (which he did about six) and who said, “Little pig, are you ready?”
The little pig said, “Ready! I have been and come back again, and got a nice potful for dinner.”
The wolf felt very angry at this, but thought that he would be up to the little pig somehow or other, so he said, “Little pig, I know where there is a nice apple tree.”
“Where?” said the pig.
“Down at Merry Garden,” replied the wolf, “and if you will not deceive me I will come for you, at five o’clock tomorrow and get some apples.”

Well, the little pig bustled up the next morning at four o’clock, and went off for the apples, hoping to get back before the wolf came; but he had further to go, and had to climb the tree, so that just as he was coming down from it, he saw the wolf coming, which, as you may suppose, frightened him very much.
When the wolf came up he said, “Little pig, what! Are you here before me? Are they nice apples?”
“Yes, very,” said the little pig. “I will throw you down one.” And he threw it so far, that, while the wolf was gone to pick it up, the little pig jumped down and ran home.
The next day the wolf came again, and said to the little pig, “Little pig, there is a fair at Shanklin this afternoon. Will you go?”
“Oh yes,” said the pig, “I will go. What time shall you be ready?”

“At three,” said the wolf. So the little pig went off before the time as usual, and got to the fair, and bought a butter churn, which he was going home with, when he saw the wolf coming. Then he could not tell what to do. So he got into the churn to hide, and by so doing turned it around, and it rolled down the hill with the pig in it, which frightened the wolf so much, that he ran home without going to the fair. He went to the pig’s house, and told him how frightened he had been by a great round thing which came down the hill past him.
Then the little pig said, “Ha, I frightened you, then. I had been to the fair and bought a butter churn, and when I saw you, I got into it, and rolled down the hill.”

Then the wolf was very angry indeed, and declared he would eat up the little pig, and that he would get down the chimney after him. When the little pig saw what he was about, he hung on the pot full of water, and made up a blazing fire, and, just as the wolf was coming down, took off the cover, and in fell the wolf; so the little pig put on the cover again in an instant, boiled him up, and ate him for supper, and lived happily ever afterwards.

options.label: Angering one's enemies is not helpful and should be avoided.
Fear can encourage people to be particularly clever and practical.
The best way to deal with a bully is to confront him or her in a physical way.
Fear drives people to behave foolishly. E. The best way to test a person's abilities is to see how he performs when left alone. F. Hard work and serious effort will pay off.

options.label: It draws attention to the third pig's mean-spirited efforts to taunt the wolf and cause him harm.
It portrays the relationship between the wolf and the third pig, characterized by a friendly rivalry.
It shows that the third pig was able to forge a friendship with the wolf by showing him the error of his ways.
It demonstrates that the third pig was able to turn the tables on the wolf and actually cause him to be fearful.

questions.prompt: How does the following phrase help develop the plot of the story: "He went to the pig's house, and told him how frightened he had been" (Paragraph 28)?

options.label: He attempts to outsmart him by arriving at the field of turnips before the wolf and leaving before the wolf could trap him.
The third pig realizes that the wolf is trying to lure him outside and smartly decides to remain indoors so he cannot be attacked.
He tries to be considerate by collecting the harvest for both himself and the wolf so that the wolf doesn't have to make the trip.
The third pig tries to trick the wolf by collecting the apples from the apple tree before he arrives.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains how the third pig responds to the wolf's first attempt to lure him outside of his house in paragraph 13?
Explain how the structure of the story contributes to the development of its theme. Cite evidence from the text in your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Why do you think it was important that there were three pigs in the story? How would the story have been different if the details about the first two pigs had not been included? Do the failures of others make our accomplishments more meaningful?

Think about a time when you learned from the example of others. Did seeing someone get in trouble for something or experience negative consequences for their actions encourage you to avoid doing the same thing? Were you motivated by fear?
Do you think the pig would have been wiser to avoid the places the wolf pointed out to him altogether? What are the benefits of risk-taking?

In the context of the story, what are the effects of following the crowd? Would the story have ended differently if each pig had built his house of the same materials and taken the same approach to dealing with the wolf?

In "The Three Little Pigs," the third pig outsmarts the wolf despite being distressed over his fear of being eaten. In the context of this story, how does fear drive action? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Eyes Looking to the Sky
articles.author: Christopher Hallman
articles.description: A Polynesian boy travels in a voyaging canoe to settle Rapa Nui (Easter Island).
articles.intro: The ancient Polynesians, using large voyaging canoes that could travel great distances, settled the area of the Pacific Islands, from Hawai'i in the north, to Rapa Nui (Easter Island) in the southeast, to New Zealand in the southwest. The ancient Polynesian navigators, who were people who explored the sea, did not use instruments to help them find their way from island to island or across large spaces of the Pacific Ocean. Instead, navigators used the sun and stars, ocean currents and swells, and even cloud formations and other observations, such as the sighting of sea birds, to wayfind. In this story, a young boy and his family travel on a voyaging canoe to a new home on Rapa Nui (Easter Island).
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the relationship between the narrator, Makani, and Kilolani, the navigator.
articles.permissions: "Eyes looking to the Sky" by Christopher Hallman, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: To find your way in a voyaging canoe you must be able to feel the sea. That’s what Kilolani says. My mother says you need to have the magic — magic that comes from the stars above and the sea below. She says Kilolani has that magic. When I ask my father about this, he doesn’t answer. He just closes his eyes and shakes his head slowly. Father doesn’t speak of Kilolani, and he doesn’t want me to speak of him. I don’t understand why. I like Kilolani. I like the way he stands near the bow, feet planted wide apart, swaying with the up and down of the swells. The cold spray sometimes slaps him cruelly, dripping from his matted gray hair, creating little rivers on his shoulders and across the tattoos on his arms. He never flinches.

I want to talk with him. I want to ask him if it really is magic that he has. And maybe it is magic, because how else could he alone find the way to Mata ki te rangi , “Eyes Looking to the Sky” — the island that will rise alone from the deep ocean and become our new home.

Before we sailed, people talked with great certainty of this island we know only from our oldest stories. They spoke of its great volcano soaring into the sky, drawing a weary sailor’s eyes up to where it touched heaven. Ka’imi asked me if I believed in this island. I told her Kilolani says it is waiting for us, and he will find it. That is all I need to know.

What does Kilolani see and feel while he is standing there? I know I shouldn’t ask him. You never disturb the navigator. You never interrupt his concentration. If he fails, if he loses his way, then most likely we will all die. But still, I have to know. Because more than anything else, I want to be like him, like Kilolani. I want, more than I want the air that I breathe, to be the navigator of a voyaging canoe.
“Makani, go and help your mother with the fish,” my father says from behind me.

He must have watched me as I watched Kilolani. “Yes, father,” I reply, avoiding eye contact. Walking back to the stern, my feet matching the rhythm of the swells, I see the canoe of my cousins, its twin kino , or hulls, slicing through the deep blue water. I look for Ka’imi among the people on that side of the deck, but she is not there. My heart falls. Just seeing her across the swath of ocean that separates us is a daily joy.
I take a spot beside my mother, keeping well clear of the long oar that steers our vessel on the navigator’s commands. We kneel upon the rough matting beneath us and prepare to clean the fish.

My hands trace the movement of my mother’s as we begin our task. Her hands are sun browned, with smooth backs and wrinkled palms. Along the left index finger runs a jagged scar earned early in her marriage as she used the spiny tip of a stingray’s tail to pierce a knot of fibrous palm. The sharp spike slipped through bark and into flesh more easily than she had calculated. Her long black hair takes flight in the breeze and tickles my face. The air whispers around us, intent on dancing freely across the ocean swells, but draft after draft is trapped in the billow of our V-shaped sail. The wind, always a restless prisoner, spends some of its sun-born energy against the sail’s matting before it escapes, and in this way we move ever forward. The bow of the canoe rises, falls, and rises again. The rhythm of the sea, the faithful movement of sun and stars, define each day of our voyage. The navigator lives those rhythms.

As much as I love this canoe, I am homesick. Leaving our island was a hard thing; but harder still to remain in a place where there are too many families and too little food. Many days had passed since rain last fell on our crops of taro and breadfruit. How were we to feed so many mouths in our villages? Each night the king gathered our families for close examination of the natural signs that might recommend leavetaking. The men, with their voices low and their eyes turned to the eastern heavens, held such council until the morning of a particular day when the word went out that the wind now came from the west. I heard the king’s words of action: “Prepare the canoes. Prepare your spirit. It is time to go with this special wind.” The king chose each of us. Two canoes full of his closest family. Our leaving would ease the difficulties for those who remained.

Sometimes out here I feel separated from my body. I float just a little above the canoe and watch its activities as if it were my own dream. Perhaps this is all a dream and I will wake soon and stand on warm, firm sand beside Ka’imi. A cold spray from the sea, tossed into the air by our bow, reminds me that my fate is tied to this canoe, and my real dream lies where we are bound, and where we are bound rests with the spirit and skill of the navigator.
My mother smiles at me. She sees my mind is elsewhere.
“Your father was once a navigator, too,” she says.
I am unable to answer. Her words pierce my heart.

“You must hide your disbelief, Makani, for what I tell you is true,” she continues. “He was a fine navigator who lost course when the skies filled with clouds for days. The expedition almost perished from thirst. The king, with great sorrow, set him down from navigating the voyaging canoes. But understand my lesson: navigating alone is not the full measure of any man.”
She says nothing else and we gather our clean fish to take to the cook.

As we walk together, balancing our load against the swells, one of the sailors, high on the mast of the second canoe, calls to us in warning. Following his directions, my squinted eyes trace a low black line of storm clouds rising above the horizon, driving a feathery gray mist before them.

Mother and I hurry to secure our basket of fish and take our place near Father. Blue day turns to green-black night as the first gusts strike us. The wind flails the lines and tears at the great sail, making a noise that sounds as if a clan of spirits stands upon our vessel screaming encouragement to the chaos I cast spray stung eyes about for a thing to preserve my courage. There. On the foredeck, Kilolani bears the storm from his customary place, his legs bent at the knee, bracing for each gust and roll. Our king stands tall beside Kilolani, facing the onrushing clouds, shouting orders across to the other canoe to promote its safety. But his strong words are snatched away by the wind, heard by no one.

A wave sweeps under us from behind, and I pitch forward onto my face. My mother and father topple over me as the stern rises up and up and up. Our vessel groans and threatens to separate as the twin bows of our canoe angle sickeningly downward into the frothing green chasm beneath us. I brace now for the unnatural act of our great canoe falling onto us from above as we are upended. My mother’s arms hold tight around me. Her sea-drenched hair flows over my head and across my eyes, blinding me. For a moment I feel shielded by her and my father, cocooned as we are one around the other, until we are flung mightily rearward as the massive stern falls back into the sea. Our bow rises up again from the depths, bringing with it a freezing deluge of ocean water.

Then I hear her cry. Ka’imi! Slipping my mother’s grasp, I stand on shaky legs to answer this call. But I cannot see the second canoe. I turn in circles, thinking I am disoriented by the wind and waves. Was it swallowed by the same swirling maw that nearly gulped us down? I hear the call again, feeble and fading against the wind.
“Ka’imi!” I shout through cupped hands. “Ka’imi! Ka’imi!”

Then I see her outstretched arm, barely cresting the waves that pound at her. In a blink I am in the water, swimming hard for Ka’imi. The waves are too much for me. They batter my head beneath them. As I lose my breath, I see the arc of a man’s body leaving our canoe and knifing into the gray water. Rough, strong hands take hold of me, and of Ka’imi, too. We, all three, pull toward the rocking hull. Other hands grasp us along side and pull us from the jaws of an angry sea. The king himself draws Father upward and clasps his shoulder. My father bows his head in the presence of the king’s great favor. Kilolani stands at the rail and looks only at me, as if seeing my face for the first time.

Days pass, each one filled with our grief. We repair our canoe. Ka’imi, the second canoe’s only survivor, and I pledge to tell of this thing to every child born in our new land, to preserve the memory of this sacrifice.

During the twelfth night following the storm, I stand with Kilolani on the foredeck. The Milky Way blazes above us. Kilolani kneels beside me and says, “Makani, do you feel the hoa hua dele tai , the ocean swell, beneath your feet? That is the heartbeat of the sea.”
He then bids me look ahead, following his outstretched arm, toward a star burning clear and true before us. That is the kaveinga , our star path. Along that path lies our new island home.
“The storm is in our past, Makani,” he says. “Yon star beckons to our future. Regard the sea below and the sky above with care, for this is your first lesson.”

options.label: It is important to keep moving forward even after hard times.
Young people are able to act more bravely than adults.
We can never truly leave the place we call home.
Parents know what is best for their children.

Makani's father is not good at figuring out directions.

discussionQuestions.prompt: How do you think Makani's father would feel at the end of the story when Makani is starting to become a navigator? Why? Do you think that it is important to choose a career that your parents want for you? Why or why not?

In the story, Makani and his family are moving to a new, unknown land. Have you ever moved to a new place? If so, were your feelings about moving similar or different to Makani's? Why? If not, what do you think might be difficult about moving to a new, unknown place?
What skills does a great navigator need in the story? Which jobs today do you think might be most similar to Kilolani's job as navigator? Why?

articles.title: First Lady of Wildflowers
articles.subtitle: She helps keep America beautiful.
articles.author: Patricia Nikolina Clark
articles.description: "Lady Bird" Johnson helped to spread appreciation for wildflowers and natural beauty.
articles.intro: Claudia Alta "Lady Bird" Johnson was the wife of President Lyndon B. Johnson, the 36th President of the United States. As the wife of the President, her title was "First Lady." In this text, Patricia Nikolina Clark discusses Lady Bird Johnson's important work as First Lady.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Lady Bird Johnson’s actions.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Wildflowers in a mountain meadow are a gift of nature. But what about those wildflowers blooming along busy highways? Well, nature had a helper.

As the wife of our thirty-sixth President, Lyndon B. Johnson, Claudia Alta “Lady Bird” Johnson wanted beauty to be a national priority. “Americans spend a lot of time traveling the highways,” said the former First Lady. “That’s why it’s so important to make them as beautiful as possible.”

Lady Bird loved wildflowers. As a little girl growing up in “deep east Texas,” wildflowers helped her get through lonely times. Her mother had died when Lady Bird was five years old, and her father spent most of his time at the general store he owned. Her older brothers were away at school, so Lady Bird turned to nature for company.
“Nature was my friend and my teacher,” she says. “It was a joy to me, and it’s never failed me.”

Lady Bird loved to roam the woods. With the crush of pine needles under her feet and the wind whispering overhead, she would search for the wild violets and daffodils of spring. Then, in her own little ceremony, she would crown the first one “the queen.” Though she was alone, she wasn’t lonely; wildflowers were her playmates.

After high school, Lady Bird left home for the University of Texas in Austin. Again, she found joy in nature — in the vast amount of Texas bluebonnets that covered the fields and roadsides of Hill Country. She delighted in going on picnics and explorations with friends.
It was there that Lady Bird met and fell in love with a handsome young congressional aide named Lyndon Baines Johnson. He proposed to her on their first date.

Lady Bird was in Dallas, Texas, with her husband on November 22, 1963, when President John F. Kennedy was assassinated. Shortly after that terrible tragedy, Vice-President Johnson became President, and Lady Bird Johnson became First Lady of the land she loved so much.
The job of the First Lady is not defined in the Constitution. Each First Lady has to create that role for herself. Lady Bird wasn’t at all sure what her own role should be.
“The whole country selects the President,” Lady Bird said, “but only one man selects the First Lady — and it is highly unlikely that he was thinking of her as First Lady when he proposed!”

One day in May of 1964, Lady Bird was listening to her husband speak at the University of Michigan. He talked about the gradual disappearance of “America the Beautiful'' due to land development and pollution. “And once man can no longer walk with beauty and wonder at nature,” he said, “his spirit will wither and his sustenance be wasted.”

Listening to his words, Lady Bird remembered her childhood joy of playing in the woods with the wildflowers. She remembered how nature had been her friend. An idea began to form in her mind: As First Lady, she could do something to help keep America beautiful. She could make sure that children would always know the joy of wildflowers.

Lady Bird went right to work. She persuaded the President to propose a Highway Beautification Act that provided controls for billboards and junkyards along the highways and allocated money to landscape roadsides. The act was passed in 1965.

Then the First Lady started visiting wilderness areas. Reporters and photographers followed as she rafted down the Rio Grande and hiked in the Redwood National Forest. She wanted Americans to wake up to the beauty of their heritage — to see what would be lost if they didn't act to preserve it.

“Wherever I go in America,” said Lady Bird, “I like it when the land speaks its own language in its own regional accent. Texas should look like Texas, and Mississippi like Mississippi.” She realized that each part of the country has its own flavor — and its own native wildflowers.

Lady Bird pointed out that many wildflowers, like people, were immigrants. “They crossed the nation on the wheels of covered wagons, on the hooves of Spanish horses, perhaps even in the pockets of children.”

In 1969 a new President took office, and the Johnsons moved back to Texas. But Lady Bird didn't stop preaching about roadside beautification. Instead, she started the Lady Bird Johnson Award for Highway Beautification , which gave prizes each year to maintenance crews who planted and cared for wildflowers and native plants along the Texas roadways. Today more than a million miles of Texas roadways are edged with wildflowers.

On her seventieth birthday, in 1982, Lady Bird started the National Wildflower Research Center (now called the Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center) in Austin, Texas. The center encourages people to landscape with native plants and wildflowers. Information is given out regarding which plants are appropriate for planting in each region of the United States, along with advice on how to grow them.

But Lady Bird’s influence had an even farther reaching effect. In 1987 Texas Senator Lloyd Bentsen persuaded Congress to pass a bill requiring states to use part of their highway landscaping money to plant native wildflowers along their roadways.

Lady Bird is no longer First Lady of the land, but she is certainly our First Lady of wildflowers. She made us see that highways can be beautiful. And she is the reason why wildflowers now brighten our travels through almost every state.

options.label: Lyndon Baines Johnson proposed to Lady Bird after one date.
John F. Kennedy was President before Lyndon Baines Johnson.
Lady Bird's father worked at a general store when she was a child.
Lady Bird Johnson visited National Parks to show how beautiful they were.

discussionQuestions.prompt: If you were First Lady (or First Gentleman) what is a topic you would be interested in supporting? Why is this topic important to you? How would you try to get other Americans to care about this topic too?

Do you have a favorite place to be that is outdoors? Where is it? What makes it special to you? If you do not have one, describe a place in the outdoors you would like to explore if you got the chance.

Not everyone in the world is able to visit beautiful outdoor spaces. What are some of the reasons why someone might not be able to visit nature? What do you think the effects of not being able to visit nature are on a person? What could we do to make sure everyone is able to visit nature?

articles.title: Sumintra Celebrates Divali
articles.author: Meena Jagat
articles.description: Meena Jagat describes how a young girl celebrates the Hindu festival of Divali.
articles.intro: Divali is one of the most important festivals, or holidays, celebrated by Hindus. Hinduism is the world's oldest religion and the most common religion in India. In this text, Meena Jagat describes how a young girl celebrates the festival with her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people celebrate Divali.
articles.permissions: Copyright © Highlights for Children, Inc., Columbus, Ohio. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Sumintra Jagat lives in Maryland. Her family comes from Trinidad, a small tropical island in the Caribbean. About seventy years ago, Sumintra’s great-great-grandparents migrated from India to Trinidad to work on the sugarcane and rice plantations. Sumintra’s parents were married there in 1986 and soon came to the United States to live.

During Autumn, Sumintra and her family celebrate Divali (dee-WAH-lee), the Hindu festival of lights. Divali comes from the Sanskrit word Deepavali , meaning “row of lights.” It is observed along with the fall harvest, the season when pumpkins, mangoes, string beans, and other available fruits and vegetables are picked and eaten. Sumintra and her family celebrate Divali for one day; other people of the Hindu faith celebrate it for longer.

At least twenty-one days before the festival begins, everyone in the family is busy cleaning the house and all the windows. Sumintra washes the deyas , or clay lamps, and counts them. (As the time draws closer to Divali, Sumintra will cut the wicks of candles and place them inside the deyas. She will light one deya the day before Divali begins.)
At this time Sumintra and her family also begin fasting. This means that they do not eat meat, eggs, or fish, but lots of fresh and cooked fruits and vegetables.

On Divali, they prepare roti (pita bread), pumpkin, curried mangoes, curried chickpeas with potatoes, string beans, spinach, and rice. Sumintra’s cousins come over to her house, or she and her family go to their house to spend the day together.

Sumintra and her family continue the customs of her great-great-grandparents. Sumintra performs traditional Hindu prayer ritual. Her deya is lit and placed inside a taria , or plate. The round brass taria , which represents the world, holds clean water and fresh flowers for the Divali devotions. The clean water represents the ocean; the fresh flowers the food that grows on trees. Brass utensils are significant in the Hindu home because they are used only for serving food sacrifices to the gods. The deya is made of clay, and it symbolizes the human body. The light is the soul that flows in every direction, searching for inner peace and a place to live.

The white dot on Sumitra’s forehead stands for purity of heart. Every time she prays, she must light a deya to welcome the goddess Mother Lakshmi into her heart. Sumintra stirs a mixture of honey, milk, and rose water, called punchamrit , in a lota , or brass vessel. Milk represents the mother’s milk that nourishes her children. Milk is sacred in the Hindu homes because it comes from the most sacred animal, the cow. Mother Lakshmi appears in the form of a cow and is responsible for providing good health, wealth, and prosperity.

After the punchamrit is mixed, it is blessed by Sumintra’s parents and served during the ritual chanting of prayers and singing of devotional Indian songs. Sumintra and her family sing to wake up the gods and to invite them into their home.

The punchamrit is served to Sumintra first. She accepts with her right palm over her left. (Hindus associate God with the right hand and the devil with the left.) Sumintra then offers her parents sumintra because they are her providers.

When the ritual ceremony is over, Sumintra is excited about helping her family light the deyas and decorate the house with them. She turns each deya so that the spout faces east, the direction of the rising sun. During Divali, light, or goodness, chases away darkness, or evil.

Sumintra makes a mala , the Hindu word for garland or lei, out of fresh flowers. Marigolds and gardenias are used because their strong fragrances wake up the gods. Sumintra uses a needle and thread to sew the flowers together. She then places the garlands around the Hindu pictures in her prayer room.

The Hindus believe that Mother Lakshmi guides and protects those who pray to her and offer her sacrifices of foods, fruits, and sweets. As the goddess of good fortune, Lakshmi showers them with blessings and makes sure they don’t starve or become homeless. Although Divali is celebrated differently from country to country, certain important things remain the same. Divali is a time for people to share, give thanks, and appreciate one another.

options.label: by explaining why Sumintra loves the festival of Divali
by describing how one child celebrates Divali from start to finish
by showing how Divali is celebrated in different parts of the world
by telling how Sumintra learned to light the deyas from her parents

discussionQuestions.prompt: The author describes how Sumintra celebrates Divali. What is a special holiday that you celebrate? How do you celebrate it? Is there a holiday that you celebrate that is similar to Divali? Which one and why?

Sumintra celebrates Divali the same way as her great-great-grandparents. Are there any traditions that your family has passed down through the generations? What are they and how do you practice those traditions?

Divali is the celebration of good triumphing over evil. Can you think of any other stories you know that have this same theme? What are they? Why do you think people all over the world tell stories about this theme?

articles.title: Matthias and the Dragons
articles.author: Maggie Murphy
articles.description: In this medieval adventure, a clever boy must use his puppet theater training to outsmart dragons.
articles.intro: In this story, a boy named Matthias has an exciting adventure.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Matthias’ thoughts.
articles.permissions: "Matthias and the Dragons" by Maggie Murphy, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Striding quickly along, Matthias glanced uneasily at the black stone cliffs towering beside him. Ahead, a still lake swarmed with dark clouds of midges. Finally, he spied the faraway ocean. Now I know my way back, he thought. I'd better hurry. We're leaving the inn this afternoon, and Father won't be too pleased if I'm late.

Matthias spent happy days traveling around the kingdom with his father's band of puppeteers. On village greens at showtime, he joined the puppet masters behind the brightly painted theater. While the men pulled the puppets' strings and spoke for them, Matthias made the scenes even livelier by adding special sounds.

When the puppet princess knocked on the giant's castle door, Matthias rapped on a board. When the funny wizard brewed his potions, Matthias clashed pot lids. And when the prince — he had long brown hair like Matthias! — got lost in the forest, Matthias gave wonderful bird calls.

Matthias chirped, cheeped, and whistled so well because he spent lots of time listening to wild birds. Life on the road took him to many lonely inns surrounded by woods and meadows calling to be explored. Today, although he hadn't meant to break his father's rule about not going too far, he'd somehow wound up as lost as the puppet prince.

Now Matthias turned his steps south, eager to leave the forbidding cliffs and eerie lake, thinking, It's strange that I don't hear a single bird. An instant later, he understood why: a monstrous green dragon winged toward him, swooped, and circled.

Feeling like a mouse beneath a hawk, Matthias looked around desperately for a hiding place. The open ground offered no cover, and there wasn't time to dash to the lake and dive underwater. But in the nearby cliff face, he spotted a small hole.

With his heart pounding, he darted through the opening only to find he'd entered the smoke-scented cavern of a dragon twice as large as the first! This bright red creature lazed upon its hoard of riches like a great ship at anchor in a gleaming, golden bay. Before Matthias could flee, the dragon's long, scarlet tail thumped down, sealing the hole.

Matthias saw no other door to freedom except the cave's gaping mouth, which was located beside the dragon's mouth. For a terrible moment, he thought things were hopeless. But he soon realized the monster had merely changed its tail's position while stretching and hadn't noticed him...yet. Matthias crept behind two tall rocks. Peeking through a gap between them, he thought, When this dragon leaves to hunt, I'll —
The green dragon popped its head into the cavern's entrance. “Hello, dear,” it said to the red one. “I'm just checking on the adorable egg.”
“What a devoted mother,” said the red dragon, smiling.
Matthias followed the dragons' tender gazes to a jewel heap right in front of his rocky barrier. What he'd believed to be a huge pearl was in fact the shining egg.
“No joyful tap-tappings yet?” the mother dragon asked.
“Not one, my love,” said the father. “I'm guessing that our little darling won't crack out early.”
“It's so hard to wait.” The mother sighed happily, spewing flames. “Well, I'd best soar on to pick up lunch. Would you prefer deer or wild boar?”
“Pork sounds delicious.”
“Perfect. I'll grab six plump boars and flap straight back,” said the mother, and she flew away.

I have to sneak off before that lady dragon returns, thought Matthias. A protective new mother has eyes in the back of her head. He wanted to groan as the father, showing no signs of moving his tail, started sorting a treasure pile.

If only the egg would hatch, Matthias thought. That would get Papa on his feet. Suddenly, Matthias knew what to do. Imagining himself back at the puppet theater, he picked up a stone and struck it on the big rock sheltering him.
Tap.
The dragon paused, a diamond-encrusted crown dangling from one claw. Matthias forced himself to wait through a long minute of silence, thinking, Go slow. This performance must sound natural.
Tap.
“I did hear something!” cried the dragon. “Daddy's coming!”
As he lumbered toward the egg, his tail slid away from the hole. But Matthias couldn't risk tiptoeing off until he felt sure the dragon wouldn't look up.
Tap-tap.
“Old Dad's here, sweetie, ready to greet you,” the excited dragon told the egg. “And Mommy's flying home soon, bringing nice, fat piggy-wiggies!”

Matthias gave a last tap-tap before slipping out of the cave, scanning the empty sky, and running away. When he drew near the inn, he thought, my adventure's the kind of thing that happens to the puppet prince. Maybe we could…

A week later, Matthias sat behind the puppet theater's cave scene with his noisemaking tools ready. He grinned as the trapped prince told the audience, “I'll fool the dragon into believing the egg is hatching!”
The prince tapped with a fake stone; Matthias tapped with a real one. The crowd laughed loudly as the dragon puppet's pointed ears shot up and his head swiveled toward a wooden egg.
Matthias looked at his smiling father, thinking, Everyone loves our new show!
Tap-tap.

options.label: It is important not to judge people right away.

Always bring a map with you when you leave your house.

articles.title: Pelé's Beautiful Game
articles.author: Rachel Kehoe
articles.description: Brazil is home to one of the greatest soccer players of all time, Pelé.
articles.intro: Rachel Kehoe is a writer and longtime soccer fan. In this text, she tells about the life of one of soccer's greatest players, Edson Arantes do Nascimento, or "Pelé."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the words and phrases the author uses to describe Pelé’s soccer playing.
articles.permissions: "Pelé's Beautiful Game" by Rachel Kehoe, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Brazil is “o País do Futebol” — “the Country of Soccer.” It has one of the world’s best teams, holds an unprecedented five World Cup titles, and is the only country to have played in every World Cup tournament.

The game was introduced in 1894, when Thomas Donohue, a Scottish expatriate, organized the first official match. However, it was Charles Miller who sparked the country’s interest in the game when he brought the first equipment home after learning to play while he was studying in England. Known as the “Father of Soccer,” Miller helped set up the first league in Brazil. Excitement for the game spread like wildfire, and soccer was soon the most popular sport in the country.

Brazil takes great pride in its soccer culture, as the enthusiasm of the fans and the passion of the players shows. It has produced some of the world’s most famous players, such as Garrincha, Ronaldinho, and Neymar. But any discussion of Brazil’s soccer stars must begin with Pelé, a symbol for the sport itself.

Edson Arantes do Nascimento (nicknamed Pelé) was born on October 23, 1940. He grew up poor and worked as a shoeshine boy at the railroad station to help feed his family. However, as a young boy, Pelé showed amazing talent in soccer. Although small for his age, he was fast and had an extraordinary command of the ball. Pelé was playing for a local club when he got his first break. Santos FC, a professional club in the city of Sao Paulo, invited him to join the team. At only 17, he was the youngest player in the league and one of the top scorers. Throughout the country, newspapers and radio stations announced his victories. Pelé had become a national superstar.

By 1958, Brazilians eagerly waited for the World Cup, the international championship of soccer that is played every four years. Pelé was selected to play on the national team, and the question on everyone’s mind was: would this be the year of Brazil’s first World Cup victory?

The 1958 World Cup was held in Sweden. Pelé’s first game was against the Soviet Union. He felt a swell of pride standing in his bright yellow jersey, listening to Brazil’s national anthem. “When the band plays that song, all of us Brazilians feel a strange force within us.... All of us were living in a dream, but none more so than me,” he said. During the game, Pelé made several vital plays that helped put Brazil into the quarter-finals.

Against Wales, Pelé scored the game’s only goal, making Brazil one of four teams left in the tournament. They met France in the semi-finals, where Pelé scored a spectacular hat trick, a term used when one person scores three goals in a game. Brazil’s victory launched them into the World Cup final. They would play against the host nation, Sweden.

Sweden celebrated after scoring in the first four minutes of the championship game. Brazil quickly gained back control with two goals. In the second half, Pelé made one of the most extraordinary goals ever seen in World Cup history. After stopping a long pass with his chest, Pelé flicked the ball over the head of the Swedish defender and fired it past the goalkeeper before it hit the ground. Brazil had won the world championship. Pelé teammates lifted him onto their shoulders. They carried him around the field to the cheers of the crowd.

During his legendary career, Pelé accomplished everything a soccer player could imagine. In 1962, the Brazilian government declared him a national treasure. No other player has ever received such an honor. Pelé ended his career having scored 1,281 goals in 1,363 games, making him the most successful league goal-scorer of all time. He is the only three-time World Cup champion and has scored more goals for Brazil — 77 in 92 games — than any other player. In 1999, several organizations, including the International Federation of Football History and Statistics (IFFHS), named Pelé the best soccer player of the century.
In 1974, Pelé signed a two-year contract with the New York Cosmos to promote soccer in North America. He won over the crowds and attracted millions of Americans to the “beautiful game” of soccer.

After retirement, Pelé used his fame to help others. He raised money for UNICEF and created a worldwide series of workshops to help young soccer players. In 1995, he was named Minister of Sport for the government of Brazil. He used his position to create the “Pelé Law,” which guaranteed soccer players a fair wage and compensation if they got injured.

Pelé is hailed as a national hero for his achievements in soccer and for the positive difference he made in Brazil. He remains the greatest soccer star of all time and an inspiration to players everywhere.

options.label: Pelé helped bring together teammates who did not get along before.
Pelé became famous for doing things no other soccer player had done.
Pelé was popular in Brazil, but was not well-known elsewhere until now.
Pelé made soccer the most popular sport in Brazil by winning the World Cup.

Do you think soccer will ever become as popular in the US as it is in other countries? Why or why not?

articles.title: Sharks: Monsters or Misunderstood?
articles.author: Marie Droual
articles.description: Marie Droual explains many common myths and facts about sharks.
articles.intro: Marie Droual is a former scientist who loves sharing the wonders of the natural world with everyone. She is based in New York City. Sharks are some of the oldest living things on Earth. In this informational article, Droual explains there is a lot more humans can learn about these ocean creatures.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on myths and facts about sharks.
articles.permissions: "Sharks: Monster of Misunderstood" by Marie Droual. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: The sharp dorsal fin. The rows upon rows of razor sharp teeth. The large pitch-black eyes. The sharks you see on television in Jaws or Discovery’s “Shark Week” are not the friendliest looking creatures. They aren’t exactly an animal you want to find near you while swimming in the ocean. Despite their portrayals in movies and popular culture, sharks are complicated, misunderstood creatures that are more vulnerable than they appear.

Though they look like mammals, such as dolphins and whales, sharks are actually a type of fish. These large fish are not new to the world. They have been around for over 450 hundred million years, even older than dinosaurs. Unlike most other fish, sharks’ skeletons are made from cartilage and not bone. Cartilage is what your nose and ears are made of. This material allows them more flexibility, which helps them glide through the water. Most fish only have one gill slit, but sharks have five to seven, which all allow the shark to separate oxygen from the water.

Despite the continued portrayal of sharks as these large beasts with cold black eyes, there are many differences between sharks. There are over 400 different types of sharks! These range from the gentle giants called whale sharks which can grow to over 55 feet long, to tiny lantern sharks that are only 8 inches long. While some sharks swim deep in the ocean, others stay near the surface and in the shallow water. Some eat other fish, while others will go after small mammals like seals, and some sharks don’t even have any teeth! In popular culture and television, the only sharks that get a lot of attention are great white sharks and their rows and rows of sharp teeth.

One very common misunderstanding about sharks is their desire to hunt humans. When sharks attack humans, it is because they mistake a human for their normal prey, seals or dolphins. They don’t seek out humans on purpose. Despite what you might see in movies or on television, the truth about shark attacks is that they are very unlikely to happen to you. They are so rare, that statistically you are more likely to be struck by lightning than be bitten by a shark. Another common misconception is that sharks can smell a single drop of blood a mile away. While sharks do have strong senses of smell, their sense of smell is actually similar to that of other fishes. They notice smells between one part per 25 million or one part per 10 billion according to BBC’s Science Focus . This means for one drop of blood, there are 25 million drops of water around it to dilute it. This is equal to one drop of blood in a small swimming pool, not the entire ocean.

Sharks are very important to the ocean as an apex predator. They are at the top of the food chain, which means that they have few predators above them and eat animals below them. The food chain is the order in which food is moved from organism to organism. For example, the Sun allows plants like seaweed to grow, which are eaten by fish. Fish are then eaten by seals, and seals are eaten by Great White sharks. This is one example of a food chain that sharks are a part of. If you take away the Great White sharks, the seal population increases, which will then cause the fish population to decrease because there are so many seals to eat all the fish. Without fish eating seaweed, the seaweed grows out of control. As an apex predator, sharks help manage all the populations of organisms below them. If they are taken out of the chain, the effects are felt throughout the chain.

Some sharks even control the balance of an ecosystem through fear alone. Tiger sharks in Australia help protect seagrass meadows from turtles. Turtles eat the seagrass and without the sharks, will destroy these meadows. When tiger sharks are present though, the turtles are scared away, curbing their appetite for seagrass and protecting its growth.

Though sharks have a reputation for being very scary to humans, the sad truth is that they should be scared of humans. The number of sharks in the ocean is steadily dropping. There are a few reasons for this. For one, they mature quite slowly, over several years, and produce relatively few young and this makes them particularly vulnerable to overfishing. Though they have managed to live through four mass extinction events in the past, now humans are pushing them to the edge of extinction due to overfishing.

Secondly, overfishing of sharks is happening because more and more people want their fins. About 100 million sharks are killed every year according to National Geographic . Shark fin is considered a delicacy in many east Asian countries. It is a way for people to show off their wealth, like how wealthy people in many countries buy fancy cars or big mansions. Shark fins are also believed to have medicinal benefits, though there is no evidence or proof that they actually do. Both of these ideas are putting a strain on the number of sharks in the ocean. One practice in particular, called shark finning, is especially cruel, as the fishers will just cut off the fin and throw the rest of the shark back into the ocean. The fin is the most valuable part and this allows them to save space on their boats for more fins.

In response, some countries have taken steps to protect sharks by putting limitations on shark fishing. In China, where shark fin soup is very popular, the Chinese government has put a stop to serving shark fin soup at any official dinners, so as to change its cultural importance. These changes are small, but will hopefully push laws and culture in a direction to keep sharks safe. Despite their scary reputation, they are more vulnerable than they appear and they need our continued protection.

Jurassic Sharks: A History of Sharks

articles.title: Granuaile
articles.author: Fiona Waters
articles.description: Granuaile was a well-known female leader and pirate during the 16th century in Ireland.
articles.intro: Granuaile, also known as Grace O'Malley, was a well-known leader and pirate in Ireland over 400 years ago. In this informational text, Fiona Waters discusses the life and accomplishments of Granuaile.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Granuaile’s experiences compared to the experiences of the men of her time.
articles.permissions: "Granuaile" from Great Irish Heroes by Fiona Waters. Copyright © 2007. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Granuaile was the daughter of the chieftain Owen Dubhdara. She grew up in Belclare Castle, a fortified tower with a great hall where the clans would gather for huge feasts. She had been christened Grainne. From early childhood, she loved being with her father on board one of his great fleet of ships. Her father did his best to discourage her because she was a girl, but Grainne was determined. She cut all her hair off and borrowed some of her brother’s clothes. When she appeared dressed as a boy and demanding to be taken on the next voyage, her mother was angry, but her father laughingly agreed to take her with him. And so her life on the high seas began. From then on she was known as Grainne Mhaol, Bald Grace, and in time this name became shortened to Granuaile.

In those days, girls got married very young, and Granuaile’s husband was called Donal O’Flaherty. Her parents thought he might be able to tame their wild daughter, because he was known as Donal an Chogaidh, ‘Donal of the Battles.’ But although she bore three children, she became more and more involved in the adventures of the O’Flaherty clan. She had learnt much at her father’s side and men were impressed with her skill and her success. When Donal was killed in an ambush, Granuaile defended her castle successfully against her husband’s enemies.

Although the O’Flahertys admired Granuaile greatly, they were not prepared to accept her as their leader after Donal was killed. So Granuaile returned to her father who was very proud of his daughter’s exploits. He gave her the castle on Clare Island to live in and there she set up as a chieftain in her own right, with some two hundred followers. Granuaile then turned to piracy and soon became the most daring and most feared pirate on the west coast, her coffers overflowing with stolen treasure.

By now the English realized that she was making it too dangerous for them to travel by sea and that she had to be stopped. She finally met her match in the new governor of Connaught, Sir Richard Bingham. He chased her on the high seas, threatened to put her to death and finally imprisoned her. Two years in Dublin Castle almost destroyed her brave spirit, but she was released when she promised to give up piracy.

By now Granuaile was growing old and she decided to write to Elizabeth I, Queen of England, to ask for the Queen’s protection in the future. In time the Queen replied, asking several questions. Granuaile decided to travel to London to see her.
As she waited to be received by the Queen, she began to daydream about the exciting life she had led and did not hear the Queen’s servant speak.

“Madam!” he said. “I insist you accompany me to the inner chamber. Her Gracious Majesty Queen Elizabeth is ready to receive you.” Granuaile started back from her dreaming. She looked down her haughty nose at the impatient courtier. “Do not sneer at me, you popinjay,” she roared. “Do not believe that I am not an important person just because I do not wear fine clothes like the Queen of England. In my lands I am as powerful as your Gracious Queen.”
Granuaile brushed the courtier aside, and strode off to meet the Queen.

The two women looked each other straight in the eye. Each of them knew the other was strong, brave and fearless. “You are most welcome to my court,” said the Queen. “Pray sit here by my side and let us talk.” Granuaile bowed her head and took the chair beside the Queen. They made a strange pair, both no longer young, both proud, both truly remarkable. The Queen was dressed in a beautiful white brocade dress with rings on each of her fingers. She wore much make-up, and a huge red wig covered her head. Granuaile wore no make-up on her weathered face, her grey hair was her own and was simply tied back with a ribbon. Her dress was of plain wool, her velvet cloak was old and worn and the only sign of her wealth the brooch of heavy gold which was pinned on her shoulder.

The two women talked for a long time, to the amazement of the rest of the courtiers. And when Granuaile walked out of the court that day, she held her head even higher. The Queen had granted every one of Granuaile’s requests. Her brother and her son were released from prison, and she herself was provided with a living for the rest of her days.

Granuaile lived until she was in her seventies, a good age for those times. Even as an old woman, she remained a leader who was admired by all her followers. And by a strange coincidence, she died in 1603, the same year as Queen Elizabeth — two women who, despite their very different lives, had met and understood each other.

options.label: Granuaile and the Queen were unable to rise to power because of their gender.
Granuaile and the Queen were considered great leaders because they were women.
Granuaile rejected others' expectations of her and led an adventurous life.
Granuaile changed people's ideas about women in power with her success as a pirate.

"she died in 1603, the same year as Queen Elizabeth — two women who, despite their very different lives, had met and understood each other." (Paragraph 11)
