articles.title: Moving Home
articles.author: Heidi Stemple
articles.description: Heidi Stemple discusses a difficult decision for her family.
articles.intro: In this memoir, Heidi Stemple discusses the difficult decision her family had to make when her father was diagnosed with cancer.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the family decides whether or not to move.
articles.permissions: Copyright © 2015 by Heidi E.Y. Stemple. First appeared in Been There, Done That: Writing Stories from Real Life , published by Grosset & Dunlap. Used by permission of Curtis Brown, Ltd.

blocks.text: I have no stories of family drama in my childhood. My parents were hippies and we lived with a barn-full of artisans who created the most amazing silver, leather, and pottery items. My younger brothers and I had fields to explore and a pool tucked behind one of the barns. We had our own pottery wheels and the potters baked our pots in the kiln with theirs. My mom was a writer and my dad a professor who loved birds. I grew up and moved away. "Untitled" by Michal Balog is licensed under CC0. My family was different. Not bad, but different. My children are twelve years apart, and we lived in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina, on a cul-de-sac next to our best friends, the Napiers. Everything was fine until crisis struck. Back at home, my dad was diagnosed with cancer. Suddenly, there was too much distance between my family and my parents. I called my brothers — both equally far away. Someone needed to go home. I wanted it to be me. Both my brothers agreed that they were not able to pack up and relocate and loved the idea that I was willing — and ready — to do so. My husband agreed. Then we needed to talk to our daughters. They were reluctant of course. They didn’t want to leave their home.

So, as a family, we sat down to write a pros-and-cons list of moving. At first, the list was hard to figure out. I knew what my kids would gain by moving — their grandparents, full-time. Plus a 1. workers who create things mostly by hand 2. an oven used for drying and baking pottery 3. a street that is closed at one end hometown on a farm, surrounded by family and art and education. But my kids were dubious. All they could add to the list weighted the con side. For a week, we worked on that list. As the days went by, the cons all got crossed out, replaced by counterparts on the pro side. Changing schools, for example, had originally begun as a con, but as we talked it out, it started to look like an adventure. Leaving the beach, which we would miss, but rarely went to anyway, got turned into mountains and changing seasons. Pro, pro. In the end, nothing on the con side survived except leaving the Napiers. But Nana and Papa outweighed everything else. As we packed up and moved — my youngest and me first, then my elder daughter, then my husband with all our belongings in a big truck, we knew that it was family that was bringing us home, and the decision was right because we had made it as a family. No stories, especially true ones, have a perfect happily-ever-after. Though this story ends well, we do still miss the Napiers. Copyright © 2015 by Heidi E.Y. Stemple. First appeared in Been There, Done That: Writing Stories from Real Life, published by Grosset & Dunlap. Used by permission of Curtis Brown, Ltd.

How do you think the family can help each other through the difficult time ahead? Describe a time when your family came together when faced with a tragedy or a challenge.

Describe a time when you and your family decided on something together. Or, describe a time when you were not involved in a decision that your parents made and you wish you had been involved.

articles.title: A Touch of Genius
articles.subtitle: This artist sees with his hands.
articles.author: Highlights for Children
articles.description: Famous sculptor Michael Naranjo "sees" with his hands.
articles.intro: Michael Naranjo is a Native American artist known for his sculptures, or three-dimensional figures, of animals. In this text, the author describes his life and artwork.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes Michael Naranjo’s story remarkable.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Michael Naranjo is a Native American, a Vietnam War veteran, and “a sculptor who happens to be blind.” Behind this statement lies a remarkable story.

Michael grew up in the Tewa Indian pueblo of Santa Clara, New Mexico. As a boy, he roamed the scenic foothills west of the pueblo community and explored the Rio Grande, a river to the south and east. His world was enriched by the beautiful sights and sounds of the desert country.

Michael’s love of sculpting was born at the pueblo, too. “My mother was a potter, and I would help her fix her clay,” he recalls. “She gathered her clay in a place in the hills that only she knew about. Every potter has their own source of clay, and when they find that clay, they’re very secretive about it.

“My mother would bring in the clay and screen it to get out anything that didn’t belong, and then she would soak it in tubs. After that, she’d put the clay into a square of canvas cloth, and she’d sprinkle a different white kind of clay on top. Then she would fold this square of canvas and press on it this way and that way, and when she unfolded the canvas I could see this little log of clay inside.

“Then I would take off my shoes and perform a little dance with the clay. I would sidestep on this log of clay. I could feel the moist clay on the side of my foot and between my toes. And when I reached the other end, I’d step off the square of canvas, and she’d fold it and push it this way and that way and refold it, and I would have this little log of clay again. And once again I would perform my little dance.”
Michael’s dance served a very important purpose. He was blending the white clay and the brown clay to make it stronger. With this strong clay, his mother could make pots that would last a long time.

“That’s probably how I started sculpting…playing with clay,” Michael says. “Not long after that, I wanted to make figures of animals. And as they became more detailed, they became sculptures. So even way back then, I knew what I wanted to do was be an artist someday.”
Seeing with His Hands

Michael’s goal would not be reached easily. While serving with the Army in Vietnam, Michael was badly wounded in battle. He lost his sight and partial use of one hand. For the first time, Michael wondered if he could ever be a sculptor.
One day, while recovering in the hospital, Michael asked if he could have a small piece of clay. From it he made an inchworm.

The next sculpture Michael made, an Indian on a horse, was so good it was photographed by the newspapers. Lucky thing! Because when Michael decided to make his next sculpture, he found that the hospital didn’t have any more clay. So he reshaped the Indian on a horse into a bear with a fish in its mouth.

Today, Michael has lots of material to use to make his memories come to life. “I was able to see until I was twenty-three years old, so I have a very good idea of what most things look like,” he said. “So I sit, and I think about it, and I get a picture in my mind. If you close your eyes and think of…well, if you have a cat or a dog, you can picture this pet. The same process happens with me.

“Once you have the material in your hand that you can mold and shape, then you can carry it over from your mind to your fingertips; and your mind tells your fingers, ‘Make that bigger or smaller…’ until this whole process slowly starts happening.

“Nowadays, when I make animals, I sit there and think about the days when I’d take a moment sitting on a cliff side and look down and see a deer down there or watch some turkeys walk through the forest. Or the time I followed a mountain stream and a deer stopped in this pool of water and looked at me with his huge, brown eyes. It lasted just a few moments, but it’s one of those moments that I draw on for inspiration.”
Michael inspires others by leading sculpture workshops for children and adults, veterans and seniors, both sighted and visually impaired.
In 1999, Michael was named the Outstanding Disabled Veteran of the Year and received the LIFE Presidential Unsung Hero Award.
His sculptures can be seen in museums and public buildings across the United States, in the Vatican, and in the White House.
A Special Fan
Many people like to collect Michael’s work, but Michael fondly remembers one special young “collector.”

“It was maybe twenty years ago at the Indian Market in Santa Fe. One day there was this little boy who came, and he was looking at my work and I was telling him about it. Next year, he came back and said, ‘I was here last year. Do you remember me?’ And I said, ‘Yes.’ He said, ‘I want to buy that little buffalo.’ And I said, ‘OK.’ I told him how much it was.
“As he paid for it, he said, ‘I worked all last summer and this summer, and saved my money.’ I had no words to describe the emotion I felt. I still can’t describe what a moment like that feels like.”
Does Michael have one piece of sculpture that is his very favorite? Could it be the buffalo from the Santa Fe Indian Market? Or the bear with a fish in its mouth?
“You know, it’s the same as with children,” Michael said. “If you have more than one, you love them all equally. That’s how I feel about my sculptures.”

articles.title: Disease Detectives
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Epidemiologists do important work to prevent and respond to infectious diseases.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, D.C. In this informational text, Pratt-Tuke describes the important work that epidemiologists do.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the work that epidemiologists do.
articles.permissions: "Disease Detectives" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: In 2020, the world shut down as the infectious coronavirus spread. Families quarantined. Schools went virtual. Communities masked and gloved up.

But before the pandemic hit the United States, Michael Callahan was at ground zero in Asia, trying to understand this new virus. Callahan is an epidemiologist — or, as many in the field describe themselves, a disease detective. Epidemiologists study the causes and spread of diseases. As an epidemiologist, Callahan asks questions like, “What caused the coronavirus? How is the virus transmitted? Who is most at risk? And how can we prevent the virus from spreading?”

Callahan specializes in field epidemiology, which means he works directly with communities experiencing public health crises. Often covered from head to toe in a biocontainment suit, Callahan responds to disease disasters. He controls the outbreak, investigates the causes, and works with communities to put disease prevention strategies in place. For example, Callahan flew to Japan in February 2020 to safely evacuate four passengers from the Diamond Princess, a cruise ship suffering a coronavirus outbreak.

Callahan decided to pursue a career in epidemiology after working in a refugee camp in Africa, where he witnessed the uncontrollable spread of diseases. “I realized that it wasn’t the earthquake or tsunami that’s killing everybody, but it’s the malaria, the dengue, and waterborne diseases that come afterward. Infectious disease is a slow-rolling disaster. And it goes on forever,” Callahan shared.

After returning from Africa, Callahan earned a master’s degree in International Public Health and attended medical school at the University of Alabama. Over the last 20 years, he has worked in Ebola clinics in Africa and fought the SARS virus in Hong Kong. He has researched how to rapidly develop vaccines to fight diseases and worked for the Department of Defense to predict emerging diseases. At the height of coronavirus outbreaks, Callahan suited up and treated COVID patients at Massachusetts General Hospital.
Fighting Disease On Many Fronts

But, not all epidemiologists travel the globe and put themselves in disease disaster zones. Scientists in the field of epidemiology often specialize and concentrate their learning and efforts on a specific issue. More than half of epidemiologists work with government agencies, like the Center for Disease Control (CDC). About 15% work in hospitals to keep patients and doctors safe, and 11% work as researchers or teachers at universities.

For instance, medical epidemiologists study outbreaks of diseases and search for cures. They look for remedies in medicines and treatments and research how lifestyle changes affect diseases. Medical epidemiologists could analyze what types of drugs or diets prevent heart disease best.

Another kind of epidemiologist, pharmacoepidemiologist, spends a lot of time in laboratories. These researchers study how diseases react to medicines and calculate the possibility of harmful side effects of drugs. Then, they communicate these findings to hospitals, government agencies, and pharmaceutical companies.

Infection control epidemiologists work in hospitals and clinics. To help hospitals and doctors' offices control outbreaks, these epidemiologists create preparedness plans. In addition, they educate health care workers, investigate outbreaks of infection, and promote hygiene practices like hand washing to reduce infections and the spread of diseases.

Academic research epidemiologists work at universities to investigate the causes and effects of infectious diseases. They collect samples of blood and body fluids to study how viruses affect the human body. They also interview infected people to learn how lifestyle and people’s choices change how diseases spread.

Veterinary epidemiologists study how diseases spread in animals. They diagnose the cause of diseases in animals and devise solutions to stop it from spreading. They also protect humans from illnesses by limiting the spread of disease transmitted to humans through eating livestock like cows, chickens, and pigs.
The Skills of Disease Detectives

Whether epidemiologists work at the scene of an outbreak, for the government, or in a laboratory, they share some common characteristics. Like all detectives, epidemiologists must be strong observers. This means they notice details and gather lots of information. They also demonstrate critical thinking, analyzing data, spotting trends, and developing theories about the causes and spread of diseases. To present their findings to doctors, government officials, and the public, epidemiologists must communicate clearly. Finally, epidemiologists are problem solvers, coming up with solutions to health crises and the spread of disease.
Do you have what it takes to be a disease detective?

options.label: Epidemiologists work hard to keep animals from spreading diseases to humans.
Epidemiologists do important work to keep people safe from diseases.
Studying animals and their environments is called epidemiology.
Michael Callahan is the world's most famous epidemiologist.

articles.title: With Liberty and Justice for All?
articles.author: Dianne Swann-Wright
articles.description: A fictitious slave of Thomas Jefferson describes her life in slavery in this short story.
articles.intro: Thomas Jefferson (1743-1826) was an American Founding Father, one of the main authors of Declaration of Independence, and the third President of the United States. The Declaration of Independence was a document that declared the original Thirteen Colonies of the United States to be free from Great Britain. Jefferson also owned slaves. In this short story, a woman enslaved on one of Jefferson's farms describes her life of slavery.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the narrator, Hannah, describes Thomas Jefferson.
articles.permissions: "With Liberty and Justice for All?" from Appleseeds by Dianne Swann-Wright. Copyright © 2006 by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced by permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://cricketmedia.com/info/licensing2 for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Thomas Jefferson wrote about “liberty and justice for all.” He also was a slaveholder. Mr. Jefferson held about 200 men, women, and children in bondage at any given time. As enslaved people, they did not have the right to come and go as they pleased, and most of the time slaves were not paid for the work that they did. A woman named Hannah was one of the enslaved people who lived at Poplar Forest, a farm owned by Thomas Jefferson. Imagine that you could talk to Hannah. Listen to these imaginary memories Hannah might have shared. What questions would you ask her?

Mr. Jefferson will arrive in two days. It will take him just about this long to get from Monticello, the plantation where he lives most of the time to his large farm, Poplar Forest, which is my home. He will travel on horseback over land and cross a river to get here. Mr. Jefferson’s arrivals add excitement to everyone’s day. The men and women who work here in his fields, the children who live here with their parents, and the man in charge of the plantation when Mr. Jefferson is not here — all look forward to his visits.

My life as an enslaved person is very different from that of slaves who live on other plantations. Mr. Jefferson likes special foods, and I have learned to prepare them. Many of the vegetables I serve him have been grown here following Mr. Jefferson’s specific directions. My children — all 10 of them — either live with me here or at one of Mr. Jefferson’s other plantations such as Monticello or Bear Creek. I see them as often as I can but I still miss them very much. One fear that I have is that one of my children will break a law and will be punished or sent away. As an enslaved parent I am not in charge of them. Mr. Jefferson, their slave master, gets to tell them what to do and where to go.

I live in a community with some people I have known all of my life. My home does not look at all like one of Mr. Jefferson’s big houses. I live in a cabin with my husband and children. There is not a lot of room inside to work or play. I often cook for my family over an open fire in the yard. I wash my family’s clothes in a nearby stream. My children play outside whenever they can. Sometimes we even eat outside. Most of the time we sleep in the cabin — especially in the winter when it is very cold.

I have been working since I was a young girl. My first job was to look after the younger children and keep them safe from harm. I also gathered wood for the fire and helped carry water to be used in the slave cabins and in the main house. It was not hard work but it kept me very busy.

Most enslaved children do not go to school. Sometimes, but not very often, slaves learn to read and write. I learned to write and even wrote a letter to Mr. Jefferson one time when I heard that he was not feeling well. I told him that his house and furniture were all safe. I also said that he should be thankful for all of his blessings. I believe in God.

Mr. Jefferson said that all men were created equal and that everyone was supposed to have liberty and justice. He did not mean this for enslaved people like me or the other people he held in bondage. He meant it for men like himself. Slavery was a big problem for Mr. Jefferson. He said that it was like “holding a wolf by the ears.” It was dangerous to keep slavery and dangerous to let it go. He did speak out against slavery but not very much during my lifetime. (I am 27 years younger than Mr. Jefferson.)

In my letter to Mr. Jefferson, I signed my name “your humble Servant Hannah.” I have always tried to be a good cook and housekeeper. I have also tried to get my children to do the same. My hope is that one day my children and their children will have the liberty and justice that Mr. Jefferson wrote about in the Declaration of Independence.

options.label: The lives of enslaved people weren't so different from the lives of free people.
Slaves didn't have the freedom and equality that was promised to all men in the Declaration of Independence.
Even when slaves were freed, they still didn't have the same rights as people born free.
Slaves didn't have any rights because they were left out of the Declaration of Independence.

Despite these accomplishments, why do you think it's important that we also remember his wrongdoings, such as keeping slaves? Do you think the bad that he did erases the good, or vice versa?

articles.title: Secret Soldier
articles.author: Louise Purdy Monk and Andrew Matthews
articles.description: A brave and adventurous woman hides her true identity to fight in the American Revolutionary War.
articles.intro: The American Revolutionary War was fought from 1775-1783. American colonists, sometimes called Patriots, wanted to be independent from Great Britain. During this time, women were not allowed to serve in the army. In this text, a woman finds a clever way to help her fellow Patriots during the war.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the reasons behind Deborah Sampson’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Secret Soldier" by Louise Purdy Monk and Andrew Matthews, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Deborah Sampson (also spelled “Samson”) had a secret. In order to serve as a soldier in the Revolutionary War, she hid the fact that she was a woman. By all accounts, she fought bravely and well. But how did she do it? Surely her beardless face made the other soldiers wonder. Yet, boys as young as 15 years old were joining the army, so maybe they thought she was a young soldier. Deborah’s height may have been an advantage. She was more than five feet seven inches, which was several inches taller than the average man at that time. She didn’t have to worry about undressing because soldiers wore the same clothes from one day to the next, and opportunities to bathe were rare.

And why did she do it? There aren’t any clear answers, but Deborah’s childhood was difficult and was filled with work. As a single young woman without a home of her own and used to a hard life, a soldier’s life may have sounded like an adventure.

Deborah was born in Plympton, Massachusetts, in 1760. Her father was a sailor on a whaling ship. He was supposedly lost at sea when Deborah was five years old, and Deborah’s mother was unable to care for the family on her own. She sent the children to live with other families so that they all would not starve.

By the time Deborah was eight years old, she worked first for a relative and then for an elderly woman. When that woman became too sick for Deborah to care for her, the Thomas family took her in. She lived and worked as an indentured servant for eight years. Most girls were not formally educated before the 1800s. While living with the Thomases, however, she practiced reading and writing using lessons she borrowed from the 10 Thomas sons.

Deborah completed her indenture when she was 18 years old. She then worked as a spinner and weaver. She also taught school for a while, but she was restless. Sometime in either 1781 or 1782 (sources offer conflicting dates), she decided to join the Continental Army. At that time, women were not allowed to simply join the army. So Deborah hid her true identity.

She enlisted as “Robert Shurtliff ” (also spelled “Shurtleff ” and “Shurtlieff). She was assigned to West Point, New York. Her unit was charged with keeping an eye on British forces and material coming into and leaving from New York City. Some sources claim she served at Yorktown, Virginia, and participated in the attack that forced the British to surrender. She was back in West Point, when, during a reconnaissance march, her small group of soldiers clashed with patrolling Loyalists. She was wounded in the leg but treated herself without a doctor’s help.

In fact, no one ever knew that Deborah was a woman until she became ill and fainted while stationed in Philadelphia in April 1783. A doctor discovered Deborah’s secret while examining her when she was unconscious. Instead of turning her in, he sent her to his home to recover. When she was well again, the doctor wrote a letter to her commanding officer explaining the disguise. By that time, fighting in the war had ended, and many of the soldiers had been discharged. For her service, Deborah, known as Robert Shurtliff, was given an honorable discharge on October 23, 1783.

Deborah left the army, but she did not have a home of her own to which to return. An aunt and uncle in Stoughton, Massachusetts, took her in. While living with them, she met a farmer named Benjamin Gannett. Deborah and Benjamin were married in the mid-1780s. They settled down on a farm in Sharon and had three children.

Life was hard for the Gannetts and their growing family. In 1792, Deborah convinced the Massachusetts Legislature to release her army back pay, which the state had refused to give to her when her true identity was revealed. In 1802, to earn money, Deborah traveled around and gave talks about her time in the army. She also dressed in her soldier’s uniform and performed gun drills. A couple of years later, American patriot Paul Revere wrote a letter on behalf of Deborah, requesting a salary for her service, and in 1805, a military salary was approved.

Deborah died in April 1827, but the town of Sharon, Massachusetts, keeps her memory alive with its Deborah Sampson Street, Deborah Sampson Field, Deborah Sampson Statue, and Deborah Sampson Day, which is celebrated on May 23.

options.label: Although women could not fight in the war, Deborah Sampson helped in other ways.
Deborah Sampson hid her identity and fought bravely in the American Revolutionary War.
Although women did not have equal rights, Deborah Sampson was still a successful farmer.
Deborah Sampson disguised herself to become a business owner in Sharon, Massachusetts.

Should people of all genders be able to fill any role in society, or are there places where separation by gender makes sense? Explain your thoughts and reasoning.

Paul Revere had more power in society as a man, and he used that power to make sure Deborah was paid fairly for her brave service. Do you think what Paul Revere did was unusual for his time? Can you think of examples from other stories or from your own life of someone who has more power using it to help a person with less power?

articles.title: A Historic Rediscovery
articles.author: Robin Chalmers
articles.description: The discovery of an ancient city led to the establishment of Mesa Verde National Park.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Richard Wetherhill and his brother-in-law Charles Mason come across an ancient city now known as Mesa Verde, or "green table" in Spanish, in the mountains of Colorado.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the “historic rediscovery” Wetherill and Mason made.
articles.permissions: "A Historic Rediscovery" by Robin Chalmers, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Richard Wetherill and his brother-in-law Charles Mason were searching for stray cattle on December 18, 1888. Through the falling snow, they saw “a magnificent city” built into the alcove of the mesa across the canyon.

Almost 600 years had passed since the ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellers had left Mesa Verde in present-day Colorado. It is believed the Pueblo people lived in the area from 550 C.E. to 1300 C.E. The two ranchers knew that ancient sites had been found in the canyons. Still, they were awestruck by their view of clear outlines of walls, towers, windows, and doorways. They decided to explore.

The two men had found one of the largest cliff dwellings in North America. The alcove, which they named Cliff Palace, contained 150 rooms and 23 kivas. Parts of the structure were four stories high. The walls were plastered and painted with designs.

Archaeologists believe that Cliff Palace’s construction most likely started around 1200 C.E. and continued through the early 1270s. They also determined that about 100 people lived there. As the Pueblo families grew, they created more rooms. Fire pits were used for heating the rooms and cooking. Above the living areas, small rooms stored dried corn, beans, and squash. Those crops were harvested from the flat top of the mesa. To reach the farmlands and hunting grounds, the cliff dwellers used hand-and-toe holds carved into the steep sides of the mesa.

Wetherill and Mason spent two days exploring the site. They collected artifacts and searched for more cliff dwellings. They found Spruce Tree House, with 130 rooms and 8 kivas. They also walked through Square Tower House, with more than 80 rooms and 7 kivas.

In 1891, Swedish scientist Gustaf Nordenskiöld explored Mesa Verde. He excavated some sites and took more than 150 photographs. He published a book that described the buildings, tools, skeletal remains, and pottery he found. He also sent a collection of artifacts back to Sweden. At that time, there were no laws against treasure hunting or selling artifacts.

In 1906, President Theodore Roosevelt signed a bill into law creating Mesa Verde National Park “to preserve the works of man.” The act helped protect the park from ruin and from the further removal of artifacts.

Over the years, archaeologists have continued to learn more about the original people of Mesa Verde. In addition to cliff dwellings, the park includes reservoirs, dams, and farming terraces. In 1992, kilns were discovered at the edge of the mesa top. Refuse areas below the cliffs offered clues to how the ancestral Puebloans ate and lived. Wildfires also exposed nearly 700 new sites.

Today, the park contains nearly 5,000 archaeological sites. About 600 of them are cliff dwellings. Most of the sites have not been excavated. But the National Park Service has stabilized certain dwellings. Access to some of them has been made easier for visitors, mostly through guided tours. Other cultural sites can be viewed from overlooks. A visit to Mesa Verde is a journey back in time, offering a unique peek at how people lived more than 700 years ago.
Want to learn more? Go to www.nps.gov/meve .

options.label: Wetherill and Mason were wrong to explore the cliff dwellings.
Life in Mesa Verde during the 1200s was very difficult and dangerous.
Many people travel to Mesa Verde each year to stay in the cliff dwellings.
Visitors to Mesa Verde today can better understand the life of Pueblo Native Americans.

articles.title: Ida Lewis: Heroine of Lime Rock Lighthouse
articles.author: Timothy Tocher
articles.description: Ida Lewis became famous for her daring sea rescues in the 19th century.
articles.intro: Lighthouses are towers or other buildings that help ships sail to, from, and along shores safely. In this text, Timothy Tocher tells about the life of Ida Lewis, a woman who ran the Lime Rock Lighthouse in Newport, Rhode Island.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Ida Lewis’s acts of bravery.
articles.permissions: "Ida Lewis: Heroine of Lime Rock Lighthouse " by Timothy Tocher, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: On the afternoon of March 29, 1869, Zoradia Lewis was doing what she did many times each day, gazing out a window of the lighthouse on Lime Rock Island in Newport, Rhode Island. Choppy waves leapt to meet sheets of rain. The sun struggled to light the sky over Narragansett Bay. And yet, she could swear she saw an overturned boat with two men clinging to its slippery sides. Zoradia called her daughter, Ida.

Ida ran outside without pausing to grab a coat or boots. She and her younger brother Hosea pushed their lifesaving skiff into the surf. Again and again, the waves forced the little boat back to shore. Ida would not give up, and finally pulled the oars hard enough to launch. Despite the rough seas, they made steady progress. Though Ida weighed just over a hundred pounds, her arms were incredibly strong from years of rowing on the bay.

When they neared the overturned sailboat, Ida maneuvered the skiff so that the back, or stern, faced the men. While Hosea held the boat steady with the oars, Ida extended a pole to one man and pulled him over the stern. While he flopped on the floor, gasping for air, she reached for the second man. Once he also was safely on board, she struck out for the lighthouse.

Ida had rescued two soldiers, Sergeant James Adams and Private John McLaughlin. She and Hosea helped them into their home, where Zoradia had a roaring fire waiting. The soldiers could not believe that a woman had saved them. Once they recovered and returned to nearby Fort Adams, they spread the word. Newspapers and magazines took up the story. Suddenly, Ida was famous.

Ida’s father, Captain Hosea Lewis, had been named the keeper when Lime Rock Lighthouse was created in 1854. Its purpose was to prevent ships from running aground on the rocky little island as they sailed toward Newport Harbor, only a few hundred yards away. A cottage was later attached to the lighthouse so the Lewis family could live on the island.

Captain Lewis allowed fifteen-year-old Ida to help with his duties. She learned to handle the lifeboat as skillfully as her father. Ida loved hearing his stories of adventures at sea. Her favorite was about a lighthouse keeper’s daughter named Grace Darling who, in 1838, helped rescue eight people who had been shipwrecked during a storm off the coast of England. Grace’s father had hesitated to attempt a rescue while the storm raged, doubting that he would be able to control a small lifeboat on the wild sea. But Grace, overwhelmed by the pitiful plight of the survivors clinging desperately to the rocks, begged him to try—convinced that, if the two of them rowed together, they could save lives. Grace’s resolve to risk her life for others made her a symbol of female compassion and courage around the world.

The Lewis family had lived on Lime Rock for only four months when Captain Lewis suffered a stroke. No longer able to work, he depended on his wife and daughter to tend the light and offer aid to those shipwrecked in the harbor.

Wearing a linen apron to protect the lens from scratches, Ida climbed the steps to the light, fourteen feet above the water. She filled the lamp with oil, trimmed its wick, and polished the glass so that the beam could be seen for miles. It was Ida’s job to light the lamp each evening, check it at midnight, and extinguish it at sunrise.

In 1858, when she was only sixteen, Ida saved her first lives. Four boys dared each other to climb the mast of their small sailboat as they crossed the bay. When one of them took the dare, his weight atop the mast overturned the boat. The boat flipped upside down, and the boys could not right it. Ida rowed out and saved all four.

Other rescues followed, but the soldiers brought Ida recognition for her heroics. Harper’s Weekly , a magazine with over 100,000 subscribers, put a full-page drawing of Ida on the cover of its July 31, 1869, issue. Inside, a story entitled “Ida Lewis, the Newport Heroine” was accompanied by illustrations of Lime Rock and the interior of the Lewis home.
Ida’s newfound fame brought groups of visitors to Lime Rock. As Ida put it, “Boatloads of men and women… all talked to me at once and treated me as if I were a kind of real queen.”

Many of the richest families in the country owned summer estates in Newport. The Astors, Vanderbilts, and Belmonts brought their guests to meet Ida. Civil War General William Tecumseh Sherman came to pay his respects. Also visiting were Susan B. Anthony and Elizabeth Cady Stanton, leaders of the National Women’s Suffrage Association, a group determined to earn women the right to vote. They left with autographed photos of Ida.

When the President of the United States, Ulysses S. Grant, asked to meet her, Ida worried that he might ruin his shoes on Lime Rock, so she rowed to the mainland. Grant was quoted as saying, “To see Ida Lewis, I’d get wet up to my armpits.”

The townspeople of Newport were so proud of Ida that they declared July 4, 1869, Ida Lewis Day. They presented her with a lifeboat carved from oak, cedar, and walnut as part of their Independence Day celebration. It had copper oarlocks and red velvet seat cushions.

Ida was too shy to make a speech. A friend thanked everyone on her behalf. Though Ida kept the boat polished and on exhibition for visitors to admire, she continued to use her little skiff for work. It was lighter and easier for one person to operate.

Attention annoyed Ida. Captain Lewis, confined to a wheelchair, took charge of entertaining guests. He kept a log book listing visitors and told anyone who would listen about Ida’s adventures. One summer 9,000 people came to meet Ida.

Captain Lewis died in 1872. Ida’s mother was named lighthouse keeper, but it was Ida who performed the duties. When Mrs. Lewis passed away in 1879, Ida was officially recognized as keeper at a salary of $750 per year.

In 1881, Ida crawled across thin ice to save two men who had fallen through. That heroic deed earned her a silver medal from the Humane Society of Massachusetts and a gold medal from the United States Life Saving Service. In 1890, Andrew Carnegie, one of the richest men in America, awarded Ida thirty dollars a month for life in recognition of her service. Ida deposited the money in a bank as a legacy for her brother, Rud, who sometimes worked as her assistant. In 1907 the United States Congress presented Ida with the American Cross of Honor. She was the first woman to win the award.

Ida’s last rescue came in 1909, when she was sixty-seven years old. Four girls were sailing in the bay when a steamship named The Commonwealth passed by. The wake from the large ship washed over the sailboat and swamped it. Ida was able to drag the panicky girls into her skiff and bring them safely ashore. Over her career, Ida was credited with saving eighteen lives. She may have saved more, as she never sought publicity.
Ida was at work in the lighthouse on October 21, 1911, when she suffered a stroke. Rud found her and brought a doctor, but nothing could be done. They put Ida to bed where she died a few days later.

Ida was buried in a simple grave in the Common Burying Ground in Newport. A local high school student, fifteen-year-old Mary Jane Dewick, thought Ida deserved better. She collected money from her schoolmates and bought a tombstone engraved with an anchor and two oars. Installed in 1913, it bears this message:
Ida Lewis
Grace Darling of America
Keeper of Lime Rock Lighthouse
Newport Harbor
Born Feb 25, 1842
Died Oct 24, 1911
Erected by her many kind friends

Since her death, the United States Coast Guard has also paid tribute to Ida. They christened a search and rescue ship the Ida Lewis and have taken over the task of preserving her resting place. In 2018, a road through Arlington National Cemetery was named Lewis Drive in Ida’s honor, the first of the park’s forty roads to be named for a woman. The keeper known as “the bravest woman in America” will not be forgotten.

options.label: She died a very happy and wealthy woman.
She earned fame and awards from many people.
She traveled all over the country to share her stories.
She retired from the lighthouse and joined the Coast Guard.

articles.title: Pride and Premises
articles.author: Nola Bamcock
articles.description: A boy trespasses to get back a special possession.
articles.intro: Nola Bamcock is a writer based in Washington D.C. Premises are a piece of land and the buildings on it. In this story, a boy and his brothers are not allowed to go on the premises of a religious place by their house.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the important events that happen in the story.
articles.permissions: "Pride and Premises" by Nola Bamcock. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Luke and his brothers couldn’t be separated after school. You’d always find them together. Luke was the oldest, then it was the twins Lukas and Luther. Then there was the youngest, Lutz. He was only eight years old but wanted to do everything his older brothers did, so they often took him along with them.

They were known in the neighborhood as good kids. They never got up to no good. “Their mama raised them right,” neighbors would say. They never strayed too far either. Next to their house was a skateboard park, a basketball court, and a field. The boys spent hours there. They were allowed to do this after school, just so long as they obeyed the two rules their mom had given them. The first was to make sure they came home in time to finish their homework and their chores. The second was to steer clear of the Franciscan Monastery, the building where the local monks lived that stood beyond the field area. Its grounds were sealed from the play area by a tall iron fence that seemed to stretch for miles.
One evening their mom came into the bedroom the boys shared and told them that she had to work late tomorrow. Their cousin, Mitsy, was going to come and watch them after school.
“We don’t need Mitsy,” Luke replied. “We’re old enough to watch ourselves.”
“Yeah, right,” his mom said.

So Mitsy came over the following evening, and did what Mitsy usually does — she spent the whole evening on her phone, airpods in, either listening to music on Spotify, talking on the phone with her friends, or watching videos on Tik-Tok. She ignored the boys, and when they were hungry she pointed to a cupboard where the instant noodles were stacked. Luke made his brothers some PB&J sandwiches instead. But because Mitsy made the boys do their homework first and then their chores, the sun was already going down by the time they were ready to go outside and play.
“It’s late,” Lukas said, “the courts will be taken.”

“That’s fine,” Luke replied. He knew the lights from the monastery cast a glow over the field beside it. So maybe, he thought, they would have half an hour or so before it got too dark to stay out. “We’ll throw the football around.”
“We don’t have a ball,” Luther said. “We lost it over the summer.”
Luke looked over to the cabinet where, behind the glass window, a football stood on a stand. “We could use Dad’s.”
“I don’t think that’s to be played with,” Lukas said.
“What football do you know that’s not supposed to be played with?”

Luke walked over to the cabinet and slid the glass window open. He looked over at Mitsy, who sat on the sofa with her feet up on the coffee table, airpods in, looking down at the phone. He lifted the ball off the stand. They all knew the history of this ball. Their father had proudly spoken about it so many times. It had been signed by the former NFL player, Doug Williams, the first Black quarterback to ever win the Super Bowl. It was a prized possession.
“I don’t know…” Lukas said.
“What’s there to know? We are only going to use it for a little while,” Luke said. “What’s going to happen?”

So they went outside and began to throw the football to each other, the twins on one side and Luke and Lutz on the other. But Luke must’ve forgotten that the older boys tended to come out when it was late too, and were out in the skate park with some of the older girls, hanging around and talking smack to one another. They began to watch the boys, and sling comments at them.
“Put some spin on the ball.”
“Can’t catch to save your life.”
Eventually, a few of them came over. They were led by Marcel, who was tall and stocky and liked to throw his weight around. “Let me see that ball,” he said.
Luke’s heart sank. He already knew what Marcel was going to do. “We’re about to head home,” Luke said.
“Let me see that ball, shorty,” Marcel said again. “I won’t keep you from curfew.”
Lutz was holding the ball. Luke gave him a soft shake of the head.
No one moved.
“I said, let me see that ball .” He marched over to Lutz and snatched it from him.
The boys that were with him laughed.
“Want to see me put some spin on it?” Luke didn’t say anything. “How far do you think I can throw it? I’m telling you, I’ve got an arm like Mahomes. How far do you think I can throw it?”
“Far,” Luke said, and on that note, Marcel wheeled back, cocked his arm and threw the ball over the fence into the monastery grounds.
The way that the ball disappeared into the dark…Luke had to quietly admit to himself, Marcel did have a big arm.
The older boys laughed, before they turned back and headed to the skate park.
“Let’s go,” Lukas said, but Luke was still steaming at the sound of Marcel’s laughter.
“Come on,” Luther said, when he saw that Luke was just standing there, fists clenched.
“No,” Luke said. “I need to go and get the ball first. Dad’ll kill me if I don’t get it back.”
“You know we’re not allowed in there.”

The monastery was a grand building with sand-colored bricks. It had a dome and a small tower on its roof. People would visit the monastery gardens during the daytime, because of its fountains and ponds, the many colorful flowers and well-kept bushes and trees. But after dark, the monastery took on a scary feel. It was pitch black between the trees and there was a creepy quiet about the place. There were huge NO TRESPASSING signs marked in different areas along the fence. Workers from the monastery had put them up there after it was discovered that a lot of plants and flowers were being damaged. There was a rumor also that some of the meaner, older boys had vandalized a statue.
“No trespassing,” Lutz said.
“It isn’t trespassing,” Luke said, “if I am going in there to get what belongs to me.”
“Why don’t we just go and knock on the door?” Luther asked.
“For us to walk all the way around? Why bother with that? Besides, they’ll probably just tell us to get lost.”
“They’re monks,” Lukas said. “They’re not monsters.”
Luther shook his head. “I heard they have a security guard now, who is really mean and calls the police on whoever he catches in there.”
“Whatever,” Luke replied.
“How would you even get in?” Luther asked. “You can’t climb over.”
“But I can crawl under,” Luke replied and pointed to the foot of the fencing, where there was a gap between the ground and the fence.
“It’s not a good idea, Luke,” Lukas said.
“I’m getting that ball, before Dad notices it has gone missing.” Luke replied. “Wait here.”
“I’m gonna tell Mitsy,” Lutz said and then sped off towards the house.

Luke crawled beneath the fence, muddying his clothes a little. He stood up and brushed them off once he was on the other side. By now it was dark, and although the monastery was well lit, Luke couldn’t immediately see his ball. He began to walk around looking for it, but couldn’t help but to take in the wonder of the gardens around him. The tall plants, the flowers, the hedges, and the fountains. There were actual gardens too! Soil patches where it looked like they were growing food, radishes maybe, or even peas. He wasn’t sure, so he went to take a closer look and began to dig his fingers into one of the plants — you know, just to see the top of the vegetable, to see what it was they were growing here.
“Hey!” he heard a cry, and then saw a security guard running towards him. “You there!”
Luke leapt to his feet and started to run.
“Stop!”

He had never run so fast before, his heart thumping his chest, so fast that he lost one of his Nikes in the shrubs. He knew if he tried to run for the fence, by the time he crawled underneath the guard would be on top of him, snatching up his legs as he tried to wiggle through. So instead he headed towards the trees, where there was no lighting, where he could hide for a little while and shake off the security guard.
And he was running through the trees when he felt the ground disappear from beneath him. A ditch!

He tumbled as he fell to the bottom of the ditch. Now he was covered in dirt. He wanted to cry out, but knew this would alert the security guard. So he lay there, scared, wondering if he would make it home any time soon, and if he would ever recover his sneaker again. He wasn’t even thinking about the ball anymore.

He kept himself from sobbing, which was fortunate because at that moment he began to hear the heavy panting of the security guard. He heard him come to a stop by the ditch, out of breath. Then the security guard began to mutter to himself. “Come out,” he began to shout. “Make this easy on yourself. You boys have caused enough trouble around here.” The wand of light from the guard's flashlight fell into the ditch. Luke crawled away from it, and curled himself into a ball by the ditch wall. He covered his mouth. The light drew closer and closer. And then it stopped, just before it fell on Luke. He heard the security guard draw away.

He thought of his mother. She’d usually arrive home in the evenings with food, the likes of vegan lasagna, and vegan mac and cheese, greens and spicy kale — on Fridays it was Chinese food — and the boys would be so happy to see her and to smell the aroma of the food as it drifted into the house. But before any of them could eat, she’d ask them about their day, about school and what they got up to at home. And, as always, she’d remind them with a warning: I hope you did not go into the monastery gardens. There were rumors, you see, that some of the older boys had recently been caught by groundsmen who had them arrested for trespassing.

He sat in the ditch for a little while until he caught his breath. Then he climbed up and out the ditch. It was pitch black in this area of the gardens. He could hardly see the trees in front of him. He looked up to try and find the moon, but it was hiding behind the treetops. He began to walk between the trees until the forest ended and he came out onto a field again. There was a shed. He could hide there, he thought. Maybe until the morning, when he could explain himself to one of the monks. They would understand. He was not a vandal. He just wanted his ball.

He knew the thought was silly. He couldn’t stay until the morning. His mom would be so worried about him. She’d call the police and everything. He’d be in even more trouble than what he knew he was already in.

He kept walking, he had no idea what direction the fence was in, but he knew he had been walking the wrong way when he came to the monastery building. There was a side door. He could find a monk, he thought, and tell them about his ball and shoe.

He tried the door. It was locked. That figures, he thought. There must be a main door. So he began to walk around to the front. There was a fountain, and he could see a boy was sitting on its edge. The boy must’ve been just a little younger than Luke was.
The boy jumped at the sound of Luke treading across the gravel, but then sat back down and didn’t seem very surprised he was there.
Luke was surprised to see another boy there. “What are you doing here?” he asked.
“What are you doing here?” the boy asked.
“I asked first,” Luke said.
“My dad works here,” he replied.
Luke breathed a sigh of relief. He sat down beside the boy. “Oh man, you got to help me.” He explained that he had lost his ball and now, also, his shoe.
“I was thinking,” Luke said, “If I could just speak to one of the monks and explain it to them. Can you help me do that?”
The boy shook his head. “I don’t know any of the monks. I’ve never met them.”
“I thought you said your dad works in the monastery.”
“Monks can’t have children,” the boy replied.
The two of them looked at each other for a moment.
And then: footsteps.
“DAD,” the boy suddenly blared. “HE’S OVER HERE!”

Luke jumped up and took off again, just in time to see the security guard running down the main steps. But what he noticed this time was just how out of breath the guard was, and since he was now back under the lights, he could see the fencing. The guard would never catch him in time. It meant no shoe, no ball, but maybe making it home would mean less trouble for him than if his mom had to pick him up from the police station for trespassing. He decided he had caused enough trouble for one night and bolted for the fencing. He slipped to the ground and crawled under it, the security guard shouting warnings that if he should ever come back again…
Luke never stopped running or looked behind him until he got home. He was so tired that he flopped down on the sofa. His brothers heard him enter and came running into the den.
“What happened?” Lukas asked.
Luke mumbled a reply.
But then his mother came into the room and asked him what he had done with his clothes and where his other shoe was.
Luke was shivering cold and tired and didn’t have the heart for anything but the truth. He told her what happened.
“Yes,” she replied. “Mitsy told me before she left. Your brothers told me about the ball. I was starting to get worried. I warned you not to go in there.”

Luke explained that he didn’t want Dad to get mad at him when he came home from work. He knew now that playing with a signed ball had been a silly idea. Besides, after Marcel laughed at them, he thought that getting the ball back would show Marcel and the others that they weren’t going to get the best of him.
“You don’t have anything to prove to boys like that,” his mom replied. “Remember that next time. Don’t go chasing after trouble, not even when it arrives on your doorstep. You hear me?”
“Yes, mom,” Luke replied.
“Now go take the clothes off, take a shower and go to bed. Tomorrow, we’ll head over to the monastery and explain, and hopefully get your shoe and the ball back.”
Luke rose from the sofa and began to make his way to the door.
“What about dad?” Luke asked.
His mom winked. “Please! Your dad comes home, eats at the kitchen counter and heads right on to bed. I think that will at least buy us a day to get the ball back to where it belongs.”
Luke smiled.
“Luke,” his mom said just before he left the room. “All we ever have to do in life is knock on the front door. If you’d done that, you’d at least still have your shoe.”
Luke looked down at his feet, his sock was dirty and wet. He looked up at his brothers and then at his mom again. “And my pride, Mom. I’d still have my pride too.”

options.label: People will usually help us if we do things the right way.

questions.prompt: As it is used in paragraph 34, "rumor" most closely means —
How does the author use paragraph 2 to set the reader up for the rest of the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do your parents or the people that you live with have any rules that you do not agree with or understand, like Luke's mom does in the story? What are the rules? Do you still follow these rules, even if you do not understand why they exist? Why or why not?

In the story, Luke decides to sneak into the monastery after Marcel makes fun of him and throws the ball over the fence. Thinking about this text, why do people do bad things or make poor decisions? Have you made a poor decision because of something someone else said or did? How did you feel when they said or did this thing to you? Looking back, do you think you reacted in the right way or would you react differently if you could do it again?

Luke's siblings tell his mom what he has done. In your opinion, should siblings be more loyal to each other or to their parents? Why? Thinking about this text, what makes a family? Why is it important for families to both support each other and to hold each other responsible for following the rules?

Luke tells his mother that if he had not snuck into the monastery he would still have his pride. Describe a time when you made a decision that made you lose your pride. How would you act differently in the future?

articles.title: Learning How to be Patient: the Marshmallow Experiment
articles.author: Melissa L. Weber
articles.description: This text discusses the findings of a famous experiment that tested the behaviors of young children.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Melissa L. Weber discusses the results of a famous experiment that tested the behaviors of young children.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what scientists learned from the Marshmallow Experiment.
articles.permissions: "Learning How to be Patient: the Marshmallow Experiment" by Melissa L. Weber. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: If someone offered you a marshmallow now, but promised you two marshmallows if you just waited 15 minutes more, what would you do? There’s always the chance you don’t like marshmallows. But let’s say you love them. Would you prefer to snack on one right away, or remain patient enough to double your treat?

More than 20 years ago, a group of boys and girls, between the ages of three and six, were presented with this choice as part of an experiment. It was nicknamed the Stanford Marshmallow Experiment. No one paid much attention to it at first. But about 10 years later, the researchers contacted the children who took part in the test. They asked both the kids and their parents some questions and learned something interesting! Some of the kids who chose to wait 15 minutes (to get the extra treat) were doing better on tests. Could there be a connection between waiting and future success?

Some scientists believe there is no connection, but other researchers believe patience made the difference. The students who waited for the extra treat were more patient. That extra patience helped students in other parts of their lives too, like studying and learning.
What else could make a difference?

Since the Marshmallow Experiment, other scientists have conducted similar tests on more kids. Sometimes the kids get to choose a different snack, like animal crackers or pretzels. But the basic test was the same for everyone involved. They could choose to eat one treat right away. Or, if they waited for 15 minutes they got extra treats. When scientists checked in with the kids and their parents years later, some of the kids who waited for the extra treat were doing better on tests, but not as many as with the first marshmallow experiment.

Those researchers looked at other factors that might make a difference. In some cases, kids who didn’t wait for the extra treat had a good reason not to wait. They believed they would not get the better treat later. They didn’t trust the people who asked them to wait! Researchers realized children’s attitudes made a big difference.
Does patience help you plan for success?
If, when you first started reading this, you thought you would rather eat your snack right away, that’s OK. The good news is that we can all learn how to be more patient.
“Patience is like a muscle,” says Dr. Parker Huston, a psychologist who works with kids. He says you can stretch your patience and make it stronger.

“People get frustrated from three main areas of their lives,” he says. First, people who are ill get frustrated by the sickness. (That’s pretty reasonable.) Second, friends or family maybe say or do things that seem frustrating. Third, daily hassles like when things go wrong can be frustrating.

For example, you may be mad at your friend who said something hurtful. When you get frustrated by friends, do you say something hurtful in response? You may get upset if you can’t find your homework. You may feel frustrated if your favorite necklace is tangled, and you can’t get the knot out.

“Think about the times you get most frustrated and write a list,” suggests Dr. Huston. This way, you’ll be prepared for those frustrating times. Then, the next time something happens that makes you lose your patience, try taking two deep breaths and count backwards from ten to one. By learning to wait before you respond, you will increase your patience. Athletes get stronger by lifting a small amount of weight at first, and then gradually increasing the amount. You can strengthen your patience in the same way.
Take a breath and keep an open mind.

In a book called Mindset , the author Carol Dweck says some people get frustrated when they try something new. If they can’t do it right away, they give up! Instead of giving up, Dweck says people need to realize some things require practice. She explains that open-minded people ask questions, learn more, and try again. Many activities, from playing a sport to learning to play a musical instrument, require practice. When you practice, you will make mistakes. By asking for help and trying again, you will get better. Learning to try again is another way to increase your patience.

But back to the marshmallow. If you’d choose to eat it right away, that’s fine. Maybe you’re the type of person who doesn’t think you’ll really get a second treat later. Or maybe marshmallows are your favorite snack. If you’re the type that would choose to wait, perhaps it’s because you’re not hungry. Or you prefer your marshmallows toasted into a s’more. Whatever your choice, stay open minded and learn to practice patience. No matter where your future takes you, patience can help you get there.

options.label: Learning how to be more patient takes practice.
People who are patient are more happy than those who are not.
People are impatient by nature and must learn how to be more patient.
The findings of the Marshmallow Experiment do not support how people behave in real life.

articles.title: Dust of Snow
articles.author: Robert Frost
articles.description: A speaker describes snow falling on them from a tree branch.
articles.intro: Robert Lee Frost (1874-1963) was a popular American poet, well-known for his depictions of rural life in New England. In this poem, a speaker describes snow falling on them from a tree branch.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens to the speaker and how it affects them.
articles.permissions: "Dust of Snow" (1920) by Robert Frost is in the public domain.

blocks.text: The way a crow
Shook down on me
The dust of snow
From a hemlock tree
Has given my heart
A change of mood
And saved some part
Of a day I had rued.

articles.title: The Girl Who Changed the Universe
articles.subtitle: Astronomer Cecilia Payne blazed a trail.
articles.author: Rachel Bithell
articles.description: Cecilia Payne faces challenges as a woman on her journey to become a groundbreaking astronomer.
articles.intro: Astronomy is the science of studying stars, planets, and other objects in outer space. In this text, Rachel Bithell tells the story of Dr. Cecilia Payne, a groundbreaking astronomer from the 1900s.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on challenges Cecilia faces.
articles.permissions: "The Girl Who Changed the Universe" by Rachel Bithell, Muse , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Cecilia clutched her best gloves, adjusted her earmuffs, and rushed to meet the other girls in her dormitory. She did not want to risk being late and having to walk past the male students seated in the dining hall. She feared that the boys who stomped in time to her steps each time she attended physics class would do the same at tonight’s lecture. It would be a price worth paying to hear Arthur Eddington recount the details of his recent eclipse expedition, a scientific triumph that would surely make 1919 a turning point in the history of astronomy. But if she didn’t have to go through that embarrassment, all the better.

She was lucky to be going at all. Of the 500 tickets available, women received only four. Even those had come at the last minute, and not to her. But one of the women fell ill. She thoughtfully gave her ticket to Cecilia, who only just obtained permission to be out past curfew. With relief, she found her companions, cocooned in coats and scarves and ready to brave the December weather in Cambridge, England.
A Complete Transformation
The girls found seats together, almost unnoticed amid the excited chatter in the hall. When Eddington approached the podium, the crowd fell silent.

From Eddington’s first sentence, Cecilia was entranced. He elegantly explained Einstein’s new theory of relativity. He recounted the expedition to an island off the west coast of Africa and their struggles with thieving monkeys, vicious mosquitoes, and stormy weather. When he presented evidence that confirmed Einstein’s theory, Cecilia felt as if she had summited a mountain.

Back in her room, Cecilia didn’t bother to shed her coat before setting pen to paper to record the lecture, word for word. She would want her coat anyway. She had used her day’s allowance of coal. There would be no fire.
Seeing light beneath Cecilia’s door, her best friend, Betty Leaf, knocked. Cecilia, intent on astronomy, didn’t hear. Betty knocked again, then finally let herself in.
“Well,” Betty asked, “What did you think of our Mr. Eddington and his stars?”
“Oh, Betty,” Cecilia said, “It was like a thunderclap but so acute and so personal. A complete transformation of my world view. I am done with botany. I am going to be an astronomer.”
“As good as that?” Betty asked with a laugh.
“And better.”
Seeing that Cecilia was entirely serious, Betty grew concerned.
“But Cecilia, you can’t change your shop to astronomy. You’re a science student. Astronomy is part of mathematics. It’s not allowed.”

“You are perfectly right,” Cecilia admitted. “But I can take all the physics Cambridge has to teach, which is the next best thing. And they can’t stop me from attending the astronomy lectures and reading as much astronomy as I can pick up on the side.”

Over the next few months, Cecilia ransacked the libraries at Cambridge University, reading everything she could find about astronomy. Her presence at astronomy lectures sometimes invited stares, but she was undeterred. She was learning so much, but she longed to get her hands on a telescope.
Access to the Observatory

The opportunity came a few months later when the Cambridge observatory held a public viewing. Propping her bicycle against the stone wall and ascending the narrow stairs, Cecilia joined the small crowd circling the telescope. A rather plain young man introduced himself as Mr. Green, the observatory’s second assistant, and invited them to view a binary star system.
As Cecilia put her eye to the telescope, Mr. Green pointed out what Cecilia could readily see. The two stars were different colors.
“How can that be,” she asked, “If they are of the same age?”
Mr. Green looked alarmed. He cleared his throat. “You might consult Mr. Eddington’s book on stellar movements.”
“But I have read it,” Cecilia replied, “and it does not address…”
“Um, as tonight is a public viewing,” Mr. Green interrupted, “perhaps we should leave that discussion for another time. Let’s turn our attention to the Andromeda Spiral.”
Mr. Green pulled the iron weights that drove the telescope and began to adjust the focus.
“Mr. Green,” Cecilia said, “are you familiar with estimates of the distance to the Andromeda Spiral?”
Mr. Green stared at Cecilia, panic in his eyes.
“I leave you in charge. I will return in a moment.” He fled down the stairs.

Not wanting to waste a moment of their time with the telescope, Cecilia focused the image, then boosted a young girl to the eyepiece. Presently she heard a chuckle behind her. Turning, she found Eddington himself looking on from the top of the stairs. Mr. Green had fetched him from his study to deal with the girl asking questions.
For a heartbeat, Cecilia was too stunned to speak, but just as quickly realized she had an opportunity that might never come again.
“Mr. Eddington,” she blurted, “I should like to be an astronomer.”
Eddington raised one eyebrow, silently considering. Finally, he spoke.
“I can see no insuperable objection.”

Over the next three years, Cecilia followed her ambitions at Cambridge. Eddington suggested readings, answered questions, helped her learn necessary mathematics, and gave her access to the observatory. She finished her course of study in 1923 but was not awarded an official degree. Cambridge did not grant degrees to women until 1948. Finding no astronomy jobs open to women in England, Cecilia followed an opportunity in the United States. For decades, the Harvard College Observatory (HCO) had employed women as “computers.” Cecilia asked the director of the HCO, Harlow Shapley, to accept her on a fellowship. He agreed.
A Wider Horizon

Cecilia became the first student to earn a doctorate degree in astronomy at Harvard, but her degree read “Radcliffe College” because Harvard did not grant degrees to women. Almost 40 years later, her paper describing her research would be called “the most brilliant PhD thesis ever written in astronomy.” Cecilia found that spectral classes of stars had different temperatures and pressures, not different compositions as astronomers then thought. More importantly, she showed that stars, and in fact, the visible universe, were made mostly of hydrogen. Her discovery fundamentally changed scientists’ understanding of the universe.

Even after such a brilliant start, Cecilia faced many difficulties making a career in astronomy. For years, she did the work of a professor but was called a “technical assistant.” Her small salary came from the HCO’s equipment budget. She taught many classes, but her name wasn’t included in the course catalogs. Harvard’s president had declared that, as a woman, Cecilia “should never have a position in the University.” In 1933, she helped astronomer Sergei Gaposchkin escape Nazi Germany. They married a few months later and worked together at the HCO. Finally, at age 56, she became the first woman promoted to full professor and then the first woman to become a department chair at Harvard.

When Cecilia died at age 79, her obituary called her “probably the most eminent woman astronomer of all time,” though she had received comparatively little recognition during her life. But she hadn’t chosen astronomy to become famous. As she told aspiring scientists, “Your reward will be the widening of the horizon as you climb. And if you achieve that reward you will ask no other.”

articles.title: Flags and Flowers: Honoring Veterans Every Day
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Ten-year-old Preston Sharp honors veterans by placing flags and flowers on their graves.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James describes the way one child has chosen to honor veterans.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Preston’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Flags and Flowers: Honoring Veterans Every Day" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: It was Veterans Day in 2015, a day when the United States celebrates people who serve, or once served, in the military. Preston Sharp wanted to honor his late grandfather, who was a veteran of the U.S. Navy. So Preston went with his parents to the cemetery where his grandfather was buried. He put a flag and flower next to his grandfather’s grave, but as he looked around him he noticed that the other graves of veterans didn’t have any decoration.
“I was really surprised,” Preston said. “And really disappointed.”

Preston was ten years old, but that didn’t stop him from deciding to do something about it. He started small, by earning money from his parents, family, and friends. Then he started working odd jobs and asking for donations. He reached out to others in his community. Eventually, he raised enough money to buy a flag or flower for the grave of every veteran in his grandfather’s cemetery. Preston didn’t stop with just his grandfather’s cemetery, either. He continued raising money and began visiting other cemeteries as well. Soon, his Flags and Flowers program had attention from national news. President Trump even honored him during the State of the Union address in 2018.
Preston has been visiting cemeteries for more than 10 years. He’s been to every state to lay flowers and flags at more than 300,000 graves, and he’s done it all to honor veterans.
Honoring Their Service

National cemeteries are places where veterans and their families may choose to be buried. More than 5 million people are buried in national cemeteries. Most national cemeteries are within the United States, but there are 26 American cemeteries and monuments in countries outside of the U.S.
Doug Ledbetter is the director of the Dayton National Cemetery in Dayton, Ohio. Doug believes it’s important to have national cemeteries, and for us to pay respect to the men and women buried there.
“You look out across the cemetery and you see what the cost of freedom is,” Doug said.

It’s also important to make sure that veterans are not forgotten. Sometimes people call being forgotten a kind of ‘second death.’ “One of our missions at the national cemeteries is to make sure that no veteran dies that ‘second death,’” said Doug.
Preston agrees. When he lays down flowers and flags he makes sure to say the veteran’s name. “Saying a veteran’s name keeps their spirit alive,” Preston said.
Honoring veterans is a responsibility that Preston also carries out beyond the cemeteries, too.
Recognizing Veterans Every Day

Preston makes sure to also honor men and women who currently serve in the armed forces, as well as those still alive who used to serve. One of his favorite sayings is “honor veterans every day, not just on a holiday.”
Veterans can face unique challenges. Coming back home after being far away and having participated in war can make going back to regular life difficult.

“It’s not just about what happens when they serve and all the good that they do then,” Preston said. “They risk their lives for their country, but then they come home and they don’t have anyone to talk to. I try to talk with as many veterans as I can…”
Never Too Young
Being young never stood in Preston’s way. He saw something that needed to change, and he found a way to make a difference.
Preston encourages other kids to talk with veterans as well.

“I try to get this message across to everyone, especially younger people, that if you see a veteran, tell them ‘thank you for your service.’ Just saying that can change their life, even their day,” he said.
Come rain or shine, Preston will continue putting flags and flowers at the graves of veterans who have died and talking with those who are living. He hopes that you do the same as well.

options.label: Preston Sharp's Flag and Flowers program was unpopular in his community and he had to work hard for it to be accepted.
Preston Sharp created a news program that drew attention to the issues that veterans face after returning home from service.
Preston Sharp's decision to take action when he recognized a need in his community led to a movement that encouraged others.
Preston Sharp believed that the Flags and Flowers program gained popularity due to luck and that it would be hard for others to achieve something similar.

articles.title: Feathers
articles.author: Anonymous
articles.description: A woman doesn't understand the harm in spreading a rumor.
articles.intro: In this short story by an anonymous writer, a woman spreads a rumor, unaware of the consequences of her actions.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the woman’s understanding of rumors changes throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Feathers" by Anonymous is in the public domain.

blocks.text: A sharp-tongued woman was accused of starting a rumor. When she was brought before the village rabbi, she said, “I was only joking. My words were spread by others, and so I am not to blame.” But the victim demanded justice, saying, “Your words soiled my good name!” “I’ll take back what I said,” replied the sharp- tongued woman, “and that will take away my guilt.” When the rabbi heard this, he knew that this woman truly did not understand her "feather" by Jo Andre Johansen is licensed crime. under CC BY-SA 2.0. And so he said to the woman, “Your words will not be excused until you have done the following. Bring my feather pillow to the market square. Cut it and let the feathers fly through the air. Then collect every one of the feathers from the pillow and bring them all back to me. When you have done this, you will be absolved of your crime.”

The woman agreed, but thought to herself, The old rabbi has finally gone mad! She did as he asked, and cut the pillow. Feathers blew far and wide over the square and beyond. The wind carried them here and there, up into trees and under merchants’ carts. She tried to catch them, but after much effort it was clear to her that she would never find them all. She returned to the rabbi with only a few feathers in her hand. Facing the rabbi, she said, “I could not take back the feathers any more than I could take back my words. From now on I will 1. a Jewish scholar or teacher 2. Soil(verb)to make dirty 3. Absolve(verb)to free from guilt or blame be careful not to say anything that would harm another, for there is no way to control the flight of words, any more than I could control the flight of these feathers.” From that day, the woman spoke kindly of all she had met.

Think before you speak.
