articles.title: What We Eat
articles.author: Joseph Bruchac
articles.description: The speaker shares a lesson from his grandfather about the value of soil.
articles.intro: Joseph Bruchac is the author of dozens of books for children and young people that celebrate and teach about Indigenous people and culture. In this poem, the speaker shares words about soil from their grandfather.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the poem says about soil.
articles.permissions: "What We Eat" by Joseph Bruchac from HERE WE GO: A Poetry Friday Power Book, copyright © 2017. Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: It has been said
we are what we eat.
Perhaps that is so
but what I think now
is that in the end
it all comes down
to the simple words
my Indian grandfather
spoke one late summer
as we knelt
in the garden
to pull up
sun-gold carrots.
Brushing them
on his sleeve,
Grampa said to me
“We all got to eat
a ton of dirt” —
his words taking me
back to the soil
that feeds us,
that we feed.

options.label: We should respect how parts of nature are connected.
It is important to wash your hands before you eat.
Everyone should learn how to grow vegetables.
Eating healthy helps you live a long life.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the poem?

options.label: they think they are funny.
they believe they teach a lesson.
they find their meaning confusing.

options.label: The speaker is afraid of the soil and does not like to touch it.
The speaker is disgusted by the soil and does not want to eat it.
The speaker is curious about the soil and wants to learn more about it.
The speaker is grateful toward the soil and wants people to take care of it.

articles.title: The Mystery at Earth's Center
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: This informational text explains the different layers below the Earth's surface.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains what scientists know about the Earth's center.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how scientists study the Earth’s center.
articles.permissions: "The Mystery at Earth's Center" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: People have climbed to the top of the tallest mountains. People have gone to the deepest part of the ocean. People have even been to space! But there’s one place people have never been able to reach — the center of Earth.
Why Not?

Earth has layers, kind of like an onion. We live on the crust. It’s the thinnest layer, but it still goes 25 miles (40 km) deep. Underneath the crust is the mantle. The mantle is the thickest layer. It makes up most of Earth’s interior and is about 1800 miles (2900 km) thick. The outer core is below the mantle. Then there’s one more core below that, called the inner core. Put it all together, and the center of Earth lies about 3,959 miles (6,378 km) below your feet!

The deepest hole ever drilled went about 7.6 miles (12.2 km) deep. That’s only a third of the way through the crust and nowhere near the core. In 2019 a research ship tried to drill through a thinner part of the ocean floor. The drill only made it about 2 miles (3.2 km) down before the tunnel collapsed. One scientist has suggested using a lot of explosives to crack open a spot in the crust, but so far nobody’s tried that.

Not only is Earth’s core far, far down, but as the layers go deeper, they also grow hotter. The temperature at the bottom of the crust is about 1832°F (1000°C). It’s a toasty 6332°F (3500°C) at the bottom of the mantle. In fact, it’s so hot there that the rock stays melted into liquid. The core is even hotter. It’s almost as hot as the sun’s surface! So, even if we had a drill that could reach that deep, we’d need a way to keep it from melting.
The good news is that even though we can’t reach Earth’s center, we still have a pretty good idea of what’s there.
But If We Can’t Reach It, How Do We Know?

We may not be able to make it to Earth’s center, but there’s something that can… earthquakes! Earth’s crust is made up of gigantic plates that move very slowly. Sometimes the plates bump into each other. Other times they get stuck and then snap apart. The plates’ bumping or snapping releases energy that spreads as waves. These waves can move through the entire planet like ripples across a pond.

We feel some of the waves as an earthquake’s ground-shaking vibrations. Others travel deep into Earth. Scientists measure how fast the waves go and what paths they take. Then they use that information to build a picture of Earth’s insides. Think of it like a doctor using an X-ray to look inside a patient, but instead of X-rays, scientists use earthquake waves.
So What’s Down There?

We know Earth has a solid inner core. Scientists first thought the whole core was liquid. Then in 1929, a scientist named Inge Lehmann discovered that earthquake waves were bouncing off the inner core. She knew that if the core had been liquid, they would have moved through it. It had to be solid.

Lots of measurements and new computer models have told us more. The inner core is almost as big as the moon, and it’s mostly made of iron. The outer core is also made of iron, but it’s liquid. Like Earth itself, the cores spin. But they don’t move in the same direction. The inner core turns to the east. The outer core turns to the west. Scientists think the cores’ opposite turning is what creates Earth’s magnetic field, which protects the planet from the sun’s harmful energy.
Now for the Mystery

New research hints there might be something else at the deepest part of Earth’s center. In 2020, a team of scientists announced that they’d looked at how 100,000 earthquake waves moved through the inner core. Their study shows that how the waves move through the very inner part doesn’t match the rest. That could mean there’s something different there. It might have something to do with the way the center is changing as the core around it cools and expands. But nobody knows for sure.
Earth’s center remains a mystery.

options.label: It is difficult for humans to study the Earth's core, but scientists use earthquake waves to learn more.
The Earth's center was puzzling for scientists for many years until they were able to drill deep into its surface.
There are many places in the world left to explore and one of the most fascinating places to study is the Earth's center.
Scientists think that starting small earthquakes and recording where their waves go could help us learn more about the Earth's core.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

"Earth's crust is made up of gigantic plates that move very slowly." (Paragraph 6)

options.label: The mantle is deeper beneath the Earth's surface than the inner core.

options.label: to speed up.

articles.title: Picturing Volcanoes
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Shield volcanoes, stratovolcanoes, and cinder cone volcanoes are the three main types of volcanoes.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James explains how volcanoes erupt, or explode.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes each type of volcano special.
articles.permissions: "Picturing Volcanoes" by India James. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: When you picture a volcano, what do you imagine? You probably think of a tall, pointed mountain. Perhaps you imagine red-hot lava spewing from the top or gushing down the sides. Dark towers of ash and smoke might jet into the sky.

All of these things can happen, but this image is only one type of eruption from one type of volcano. There are many different kinds of volcanoes and eruptions that happen all over the earth. Shield volcanoes, stratovolcanoes, and cinder cone volcanoes are three of the main types.
Shield Volcanoes

Shield volcanoes sometimes don’t look like volcanoes at all, but these are the largest volcanoes on the planet! Instead of a pointed peak, these volcanoes are wide with gentle, sloping sides. They get their name from a warrior’s shield; people thought these volcanoes were shaped like a shield laying on the ground.

The Hawaiian volcanoes of Mauna Loa and Kīlauea are both active shield volcanoes. This means that both of these volcanoes are still erupting. Mauna Loa is the largest active volcano on Earth. It reaches all the way to the bottom of the ocean. Less than half of its height is above the water. If Mauna Loa and Mount Everest were placed side-by-side, Mauna Loa would be taller!

Eruptions on Mauna Loa and Kīlauea are slower than you might think. Lava on these volcanoes can flow up to 35 miles per hour. That’s fast for a car driving around your neighborhood, but it’s slow for a volcanic eruption. What shield volcanoes lack in speed, they make up for with their long lives. Shield volcanoes can erupt for millions of years.
Stratovolcanoes

Stratovolcanoes come closest to the cartoon image of a volcano. These volcanoes are tall and may have pointed, snow-covered summits. They have steep sides. When these volcanoes erupt, they tend to put on a dramatic show.

Mount St. Helens is a famous stratovolcano in Washington State. It erupted on May 18, 1980. Hot ash, steam, dirt, rocks, and gasses rushed down the side and out of the top of the volcano at hundreds of miles per hour. Mudflows, called lahars, flooded the river valleys around the volcano. The eruption was so violent and blew away so much dirt and rock that Mount St. Helens went from being the 5th tallest mountain in the state to the 92nd. Fifty-six people died in the eruption.

There are many other stratovolcanoes beside Mount St. Helens. Mount Rainier in Washington State is also an active stratovolcano. Mount Vesuvius, which buried the ancient city of Pompeii in 79 CE is a stratovolcano, and so is Krakatoa in Indonesia which had a devastating eruption in 1883.
Cinder Cone Volcanoes

Cinder cone volcanoes are the most common type of volcano on Earth. They’re also the smallest kind of volcano. Most are only a few dozen or a few hundred feet tall. Like stratovolcanoes, cinder cone volcanoes have the shape you might think of when picturing a volcano: steep sides and a summit with a crater at the top.

When a cinder cone volcano erupts, hot gasses shoot out and mix with lava as they rise. Pieces of lava are thrown into the air. These pieces of lava cool as they fall back to earth. They land on the ground as solid rock. These rocks are called cinders. Cinder cone volcanoes are piles of these small pieces of rock.

Sunset Crater near Flagstaff, Arizona is one of the most famous cinder cone volcanoes in the United States. You can hike part of the way up this 1,000-foot-tall volcano. You should always be careful when exploring volcanoes, but Sunset Crater isn’t active. It formed about 1,000 years ago and hasn’t erupted since.

Shield volcanoes, stratovolcanoes, and cinder cone volcanoes all bring magma, hot fluid from inside the earth, to the surface as lava which hardens into rock. Big or small, volcanoes help shape our planet. Even if they look different than what you might think of as volcanoes, each type is important.

options.label: Shield volcanoes are the most dangerous type of volcano in the world.

articles.title: How Earth's Air Came to Be
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Earth's air is breathable thanks to organisms called cyanobacteria.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains how Earth's present atmosphere came to be.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how cyanobacteria affect the air.
articles.permissions: "How Earth's Air Came to Be" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: If you could step into a time machine and travel back about 3 billion years, you’d have a big problem. No, it wouldn’t be meat-eating dinosaurs — they didn’t show up until much, much later. Your problem would be the air. You wouldn’t be able to breathe. In fact, the air would be like poison to you.

When Earth formed, it didn’t have the kind of air we breathe. Our atmosphere today is a mixture of gasses, including nitrogen and oxygen. Your body is made to use oxygen. But 3 billion years ago, the atmosphere was full of carbon dioxide and methane. Those are not gasses that today’s people or animals can breathe.

There also wasn’t much land back then. In fact, some scientists believe there might not have been any dry land at all. It’s thought that the ancient oceans had two to three times more water than they do today. The continents were down too deep to rise above the sea.
What kind of life could survive a water world without breathable air? Bacteria. They were simple life forms, but they did something really important. They completely changed Earth’s air.

The kind of bacteria that lived in the ancient oceans are called cyanobacteria (sai-a-now-bak-TEE-ree-uh). They were the main life form on Earth for more than 1.5 billion years. Like all living things, they needed food to live. They used sunlight to change water and carbon dioxide into sugars. Then they released oxygen as waste. It’s a lot like the way today’s plants take in sunlight and carbon dioxide and release oxygen.

The planet’s oceans were full of cyanobacteria, and they all released oxygen. There was so much oxygen in the water that some escaped into the air. This happened over and over for millions of years. Finally, somewhere around 2 billion years ago, there was enough oxygen in the air that it became a major part of Earth’s atmosphere.

That was bad news for cyanobacteria. All that extra oxygen was deadly to them. They began to die off. Sad for them, but good for us. It took another 1.7 billion years, but life forms that could breathe the new kind of air developed. Eventually, that led to animals — like the dinosaurs — and finally, us.

Cyanobacteria didn’t die off completely, though. Today’s cyanobacteria are sometimes called blue-green algae. (That name isn’t quite right, because they’re bacteria, not algae.) Too many of them in a body of water sometimes end up as the slimy stuff known as pond scum.

We can also see some ancient cyanobacteria today, in layered rocks called stromatolites. Cyanobacteria often clump together. Their stickiness traps bits of minerals, rocks, and soil into layers. The layers build up and up. Over time, the layers turn to rock. A stack of cyanobacteria layers is called a stromatolite. It can take a long time for one to form. It may take 2,000 to 3,000 years to make one 3-foot (1 m) tall stromatolite.

If you visit the shallow waters of Hamlin Pool in Western Australia, you can see stromatolites. Some of the rounded, rocky stacks sit underwater. Others have grown tall enough that their tops are above the water’s surface. Some are thought to be 3.5 billion years old! Even though the bottom layers are ancient, there are still living cyanobacteria on top. The Hamlin Pool stromatolites are considered to be living fossils.
Life as we know it today wouldn’t exist without those long-ago bacteria. The next time you take a breath, thank the cyanobacteria that started it all.

options.label: Cyanobacteria are the living things responsible for making Earth's air breathable.
Cyanobacteria are underwater rocks that turn oxygen into carbon dioxide.
Cyanobacteria are the reason dinosaurs are no longer on the planet.
Cyanobacteria are a life form that uses sunlight to make sugar.

articles.title: How (Not) To Stop A Volcano
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Scientists are working to find a way to protect people from natural disasters.
articles.intro: A volcanic eruption is when a volcano suddenly bursts or explodes. In this text, India James explains how scientists are trying to stop volcanoes from erupting.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author feels about different ways scientists have tried to stop volcanoes.
articles.permissions: "How (Not) To Stop A Volcano" by India James. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Volcanic eruptions are dangerous. The hot lava that spills from a volcano can burn through anything in its way. Super-hot gas can rush out at hundreds of miles an hour. Landslides can bury everything in their path. Even the ash that rises from a volcano can cause major problems. It can cause people to suffocate. It clogs engines in cars and planes. And if there’s enough of it in the air, ash can even block sunlight so that food can’t grow for hundreds or even thousands of miles around a volcano.

Naturally, with all this disaster, scientists have been trying to work out for years how to stop volcanoes from erupting and prevent all the destruction. Over time ideas have ranged from dropping bombs to using cold water to try and cool lava.
Drop the bomb!

Mauna Loa is a slow-moving volcano in Hawaii. Lava usually flows at around twenty-five miles an hour. That’s the speed a car drives in neighborhoods. It’s slow enough for people living nearby to have time to react before any lava reaches them. In 1935, lava from Mauna Loa was getting close to the Wailuku River. This river supplied water for the town of Hilo. People were desperate to stop the lava before it reached the river.

Engineers and scientists from the US Army suggested that bombs could be used to stop the volcano. They hoped explosions from bombs could collapse the lava tubes (the places where lava flows inside of a hardened lava shell), as well as channels where the lava flowed. They thought that this might open up other paths for the lava away from the river. They dropped 20 bombs to try and redirect the flow.

Unfortunately, the bombs landed too far away from the lava flow. The explosions didn’t change the course of the lava at all. Luckily, the lava stopped soon after the bombs were dropped. It never reached the Wailuku River.

Using explosives to change a volcano might work, if the explosives landed just right. But bombs don’t often fall on their targets. This makes it dangerous to use them in an area where there are people. Detonating bombs in a populated area isn’t a good way to keep people safe! Most scientists and engineers have given up on the idea of using explosives to protect people from volcanoes.
A Cold Shower!
Another way that engineers can try to protect people from lava is by spraying it with cold water. This method mostly worked in Iceland in 1973.

The Eldfell volcano on the island of Heimaey erupted near a town full of people. The residents were able to get away safely, but they wanted to preserve their town. They pumped seawater from around the island directly onto the lava. The water had to be sprayed continuously. It took a lot of seawater and a lot of effort by many people. Even with all this water, the residents couldn’t always stop the lava. One area of lava flow moved too fast to be stopped. The homes and buildings in that area were destroyed. Other areas of the town were buried in ash. But after four months, the volcano stopped erupting. Many people returned to their homes within a year.

Using seawater to stop lava was mostly successful. However, this idea won’t work for all volcanoes. The Eldfell volcano required a lot of water to cool the lava, and many volcanoes are not near an ocean.
New plans

Scientists and engineers are studying yet another way to try and stop a volcano. They’re working on plans to drill down inside a volcano. Magma, the name given to the hot fluid beneath the earth that lava forms from, gathers in chambers far below the surface of the earth. If engineers could reach these chambers, perhaps they could relieve some of the pressure that causes a volcano to erupt. Or, perhaps they could create new paths for the magma to flow that would be out of the way of harming people.

There are many challenges to this plan. It’s hard to dig far down into the earth, and magma chambers are often buried deep. Magma is also so hot that special tools would have to be designed for the high temperature. The whole project would be very expensive. But scientists and engineers are working on ways to overcome these obstacles.
For now, staying far away from a dangerous volcano is the best plan.

options.label: Scientists are trying to understand what causes volcanoes to erupt.
Scientists want to find a way to protect people from the dangers of volcanoes.
Scientists attempted to stop lava flow from a volcano in Hawaii by using sea water.

articles.title: Tornado Coming!
articles.author: Dick Donley
articles.description: A boy must act quickly when he hears a tornado warning.
articles.intro: Dick Donley has written from Highlights . In this short story, a boy must act quickly when he hears a tornado warning.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Matt's actions when he realizes a tornado is coming.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: The siren’s wail pierced the muggy afternoon and echoed off the Ozark hills. Tornado warning! Matt Morrison swallowed hard. What a time for his folks to be off in the city.
“We should be back before school is out,” his dad had told him. “If we aren’t, be sure to feed the chickens.”

But school had let out early because of the storm watch. Matt fought panic. He remembered what the TV weatherman had said when he spoke last month at the school assembly: “If you hear that siren, head for shelter immediately.”

“Come on, Buster,” Matt called to his dog, huddled under the table. He scooped up the terrier and pushed open the back door. Matt hated the outside storm cellar. There would be water on the floor and mice scurrying around the corners.
The wind stopped. Off to the southwest, churning black clouds swirled against a sky turned eerie yellow. The stillness was scarier than the wind.
Buster whined and struggled in Matt’s arms. “Steady, boy.” Matt tugged open the heavy cellar door with one hand.
To the west, a ragged corner of cloud swooped lower. Dust flew up to meet it. The breeze returned to rattle a loose shutter on Mrs. Laney’s house just up the road.

Mrs. Laney? Should he check on her? But what could he do? With her arthritis, she probably couldn’t walk down to the storm cellar in time. Besides, he wasn’t sure she’d even want him checking on her. She was always yelling at him because Buster had dug up her flower beds or something.
But if there was a tornado… she didn’t have a basement or a storm cellar. He had to make sure Mrs. Laney was OK.
“Sorry, Buster.” He set the trembling dog on the steps in the storm cellar and let the door drop shut. Buster would be scared in there by himself, but he’d be safe.

Matt ran up the road. Swirling dust stung his eyes, and the wind was growing louder by the minute. He raced up Mrs. Laney’s porch steps and pounded on her door. No answer. Matt turned the knob and stepped inside.
“Mrs. Laney?” he called.
“Who is it?” The quavery voice came from the back of the house.
“It’s Matt Morrison.”
“What? Speak up.”
Matt dashed down the hall. Mrs. Laney was sitting in the rocking chair in her bedroom.
“Matthew Morrison? What are you doing in my house?”
The wind howled outside.
“It’s a tornado!” Matt shouted. “We have to take cover!”
“Nonsense.” Mrs. Laney un-hooked her cane from the chair arm and struggled to her feet. “Just a nasty thunderstorm.”
“No, ma’am.” Matt grabbed her arm. “Please come with me,” he said, pulling her toward the hall.
“Don’t you hustle me, boy,” she said, but she hobbled along beside him. “Where are we going?”
“Here.” He led her into the bathroom and closed the door behind them. “You’ll be safer in the bathtub, Mrs. Laney.”
“I am not getting in there.”
Matt looked at the old woman, her lips pursed together, her eyes defiant. “Please?” he asked.
Mrs. Laney sighed. “Oh, all right,” she said. She dropped her cane and, leaning on Matt’s arm, stepped into the tub and sat down.
“Good,” he said. “Now put your arms over your head.”

The weatherman had said to pull a mattress over you, too, but there was no time for that. Matt lay down on the bathroom floor next to the tub. He reached an arm over the edge and clasped Mrs. Laney’s hand.
Something crashed outside. The weatherman had been right. A tornado sounded just like a freight train, and this one was thundering toward them.
The whole house shuddered, and Matt felt rain on his face. Dazed, he raised his head. The roof and ceiling were gone, and he looked up at an ugly, gray sky.
Soon the roaring subsided and the house stopped shaking. The storm had passed.
“Sakes alive!” Mrs. Laney’s face, white with fear, appeared over the edge of the tub. “That was a tornado!”
“Yes, ma’am.” Matt stood up, checking to make sure he was still in one piece.
“Can you please help me out of here?” Mrs. Laney asked.
Getting her out took all of Matt’s strength, but he finally managed. Mrs. Laney smoothed her skirt.
“Well, Matthew,” she said as she took her cane, “let’s go see the rest of the damage.”
Matt pulled the door open, then gulped. The big old pin oak on the west side of the house had fallen squarely across the bedroom, smashing both the bed and Mrs. Laney’s rocking chair.
“Oh my.” Mrs. Laney put her hand to her throat.

As Matt stared at the mess, he suddenly thought of his own house. Hardly daring to look, he peeked down the road. The windrow of poplars was gone and debris littered the yard, but the house still stood. He heard Buster’s bark from the cellar and the squawk of chickens from the coop behind the house.
“You — you’d better come to our place, Mrs. Laney,” he said.
“Yes,” Mrs. Laney said. “I expect you’re right. Well, at least we’re still alive, thanks to you.” She patted his shoulder. “If the gas is still on in your kitchen, I’ll fix us some hot chocolate.”
“That would be great,” Matt said. But first he had to let out Buster and feed those noisy chickens.

options.label: Always have a plan and stick to it during an emergency.
If you can help someone during an emergency, you should.
Make sure you're safe during an emergency before helping others.
First impressions of people are not always accurate.

articles.title: Justice for All
articles.author: Lynn Rymarz
articles.description: Lynn Rymarz discusses how Ida B. Wells confronted racial injustice on a train.
articles.intro: Ida B. Wells (1862-1931) was an African American journalist and early leader in the Civil Rights Movement. In this informational text, Lynn Rymarz discusses an incident in which Ida refused to give up her seat on a train when asked to move because of the color of her skin.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Ida reacts to her treatment on the train.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: One day in May 1884, twenty-one-year-old Ida Bell Wells boarded a train bound for Woodstock, Tennessee, and the school where she taught. Ida sat in the first-class ladies’ coach and opened her book. Minutes later the conductor collected passengers’ tickets.
“Can’t take your ticket here,” the conductor told Ida. “You will have to go to the other car.”

In 1884, African Americans, particularly in the South, did not have the same rights as whites. They could not attend the same schools or drink from the same water fountains. African Americans were expected to sit in a separate railroad car, one that was usually dirty and smoke-filled, though they were charged full fare.
Having paid for a first-class ticket, Ida refused to budge. It was unfair, she thought. “The other car is a smoker,” she told the white conductor. “I propose to stay.”

When the conductor grabbed Ida’s arm to drag her out, she bit his hand. Furious, he disappeared, quickly returning with two other railroad men. They yanked Ida from her seat, ripping her sleeve. As they hauled her down the aisle, the white passengers stood and applauded.
Rather than ride in the smoking car, Ida got off the train. The railroad had not heard the last of her. She would fight for equal rights. She would stand up for justice.

Ida was born a slave in Holly Springs, Mississippi, in 1862, the second year of the Civil War. When the war ended three years later, so did slavery. Freedom brought newfound rights and hopes for equality. Education, which had been denied to Ida’s parents, was now available to their children. “Our job was to go to school and learn all we could,” Ida remembered.

But in the summer of 1878, life changed for sixteen-year-old Ida. While she was away from home visiting her grandmother, a yellow-fever epidemic raged through Holly Springs, killing her mother, father, and infant brother.
As the oldest of six children, Ida became the head of her family. In need of work, she passed the teachers’ examination and found a job in a one-room schoolhouse, a mule ride away.

It was difficult to raise a family and work. When Ida’s aunt offered to help out, Ida moved to her aunt’s home in Memphis, Tennessee. Ida’s new teaching position took her to Woodstock. A train brought her to and from work.

Ida soon saw “efforts all over the South to draw the color line on the railroads.” Hopes of equality were fading. The railroads’ attempt to separate African Americans and whites was demonstrated that day in 1884 when Ida was forced out of her seat. Fuming mad, she sued the Chesapeake and Ohio Railroad.

When the case went to court, the judge decided in Ida’s favor. Her ticket, he said, entitled her to a first-class seat — a seat that the smoking car did not provide. He awarded her five hundred dollars in damages.

But Ida’s victory did not last long. The Chesapeake and Ohio Railroad appealed its case to the Tennessee Supreme Court. When the verdict was reached, the victory went to the railroad. It determined that the “smoking car was a first-class coach for colored people,” thereby providing separate but equal accommodations. Ida had to return the five hundred dollars and pay more than two hundred dollars in court costs.
Hugely disappointed, Ida wrote in her diary, “I had hoped such great things from my suit for my people… I have firmly believed all along that the law was on our side and would… give us justice.”

Ida had lost her battle with the railroad, but not her determination to stand up for what was right. After losing her court case, Ida wrote an article about the railroad’s suit against her. It appeared in a number of African American newspapers. Readers quickly recognized the power of Ida’s words.

Ida began a new career as a journalist. Her reputation took her to Washington, D.C., in 1913 as a delegate to the National Equal Rights League, a committee formed to address discrimination. The league appealed to President Woodrow Wilson to end segregation.

Ida did not live to see the end of segregation. When she died in 1931, African Americans still attended separate schools, drank from separate drinking fountains, and sat in separate sections on buses and trains. Ida’s battle for civil rights would now be fought by others.

One day in December 1955, Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat to a white passenger on a bus in Montgomery, Alabama, bringing the Civil Rights Movement to the attention of the nation. In speaking about the courageous woman who went before her, Rosa Parks said, “Ida B. Wells is an inspiration to everyone who knows about her. She did not only fight for her rights but she won. I am sorry I did not know her personally.”

Ida B. Wells helped lead the way in bringing about justice for all. A journalist and organizer, she helped create the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), which still fights racial injustice today.

options.label: Public transportation would have never been desegregated if it wasn't for Ida's hard work.
Ida bought her train first-class train ticket knowing she would be asked to leave, in order to bring attention to segregation.
When people saw Ida violently removed from her seat on the train, they realized how unjust racial segregation was.
Ida fought for racial justice for herself and others, and moved future generations to fight for civil rights.

articles.title: Girls of the Crescent: Meet the Two Teenagers Fighting for Better Representation in Books
articles.author: Girls of the Crescent
articles.description: Two Muslim girls start a non-profit dedicated to increasing diversity and representation in books.
articles.intro: Mena and Zena, two Muslim girls living in Michigan, discover the importance of making books with female Muslim characters available in schools and libraries.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why Mena and Zena decided to create the Girls of the Crescent organization.
articles.permissions: "Girls of the Crescent: Meet the Two Teenagers Fighting for Better Representation in Books" from BookTrust, published 28 January 2019. Reprinted with permission.

blocks.text: When teenagers Mena and Zena realized they never saw characters like themselves in books, they decided to do something about it. Here, they tell us about founding their organization Girls of the Crescent...
We are Mena and Zena, two Muslim girls living in Michigan, aged 14 and 15. We go to the same high school and are interested in science, math, and art — but most of all, we love to read.
We've both visited our local public library countless times, checking out book after book. They've given us information, fun, adventure, humor, and understanding.
But one thing was missing from all of the books that we read — we never saw ourselves represented.

When reading picture books, we never saw characters that looked like us; when reading chapter books, we never read about characters with our names; when reading novels, we never felt personally connected to the characters.

Then, when we were in fourth grade, we were given a school project to research a person we looked up to. We both went to our public library with female Muslim women in mind, but we couldn't find any books about them.

Later, we began to realize that the same thing was happening in other genres, too — that there was a shortage of books about Muslim girls. In fact, the first time we saw ourselves represented was this year when we read The Lines We Cross by Randa Abdel-Fattah.
The feeling that we experienced when reading about characters like us was indescribable and we were astonished that we never experienced it before.
Making a change

Books have an immense impact on how children behave, socialize, and see themselves in the world, and if young kids don't see themselves represented, there is a certain feeling of not belonging or not fitting in. We knew that we had to address this problem and do something to help empower Muslim girls like us.

We decided to start our own non-profit organization, Girls of the Crescent — we collect books with female Muslim main characters through donations, and then gift them to schools and libraries around the community.
We have compiled a list of books on our website that includes strong Muslim female protagonists, ranging from early books to young adult and adult fiction.

People are always surprised to see how long the list of books is, because most of them are not included in our local library or school collections. We are hoping to provide awareness for these books that are so important for diversity.

We started off in our schools, talking to our school PTAs and asking them for donations. Using this approach, we managed to collect almost 200 books that have been added to the library collections of 21 schools in our district.
We have also received monetary donations from neighbors, friends, and other organizations, and authors have even got involved by donating copies of their own books with female Muslim main characters.
What's next?

Now, we hope to get our books into other schools around the community and present our endeavor to mosques. And we want to hold a fundraiser in a high school and middle school in our school district to get more funds for buying books.
Word is spreading and we were even featured in Amy Poehler’s Smart Girls. And in our spare time, we are writing our own book about 50 great Muslim women who have achieved inspirational things.
We hope that the work we are doing will help Muslim girls feel included and give them role models.

And our plan is to expand to other races, religions, cultures, and people of other backgrounds, so we can make sure everyone gets represented. We want our work with Girls of the Crescent to spread awareness about the importance of diversity in books.

options.label: Libraries should have lots of books.
Donations are important to help libraries grow.
Researching in public libraries can be challenging.
Diversity in books can help young people feel included.

articles.title: Inside the Eye of the Clumsy Dragon
articles.author: Patrick Harris II
articles.description: A teenage boy learns an important lesson about perseverance after participating in a martial arts.
articles.intro: Patrick Harris II is a Black queer writer, storyteller, and middle school humanities teacher. He is the author of "The First Five: A Love Letter to Teachers," and has won multiple national teaching awards. In this short story, a teenage boy named Mehki takes part in martial arts, a form of fighting that mainly comes from East Asia. Forms of martial arts include karate, taekwondo, judo, and more.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Mehki feels.
articles.permissions: "Inside the Eye of the Clumsy Dragon" by Patrick Harris II. Copyright © 2023 by Patrick Harris II. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0.

blocks.text: I’ve always wanted to be a sports guy. But my toothpick shaped arms, my inability to physically multi-task, and my badly-timed laughter skewed me away from sports. At age 5, I was kicked off the T-ball team because I struck out too much. At age 7, I walked off the court during tennis because my hand-eye coordination was off. At age 9, the boys said I was too skinny for football. Basketball? Well, I just can’t master dribbling and running at the same time.
But maybe, at age 13, I could master karate?

It all began one evening at home. I was channel surfing on the couch and stopped at two foreign men battling one another. They kicked, punched, did back flips and broke boards. My cousin Lauren, who was already in martial arts classes, came over to me and tried to battle me like in the movie. I flapped my hands to shoo her away. I couldn’t take my eyes off the TV screen.
I called my best friend Brittany to tell her the good news. “I’m taking martial arts!” I said. The phone went silent. All I could hear were weird grunts. “Hello?”
“You know you Black right?” she finally replied, and then she busted out with a Black cookout laugh. “Well, you know, Black people… ” she laughed even harder.
Click . I hung up.
My face straightened.

My nana and grandad had tip-toed their way into the living room like spies, peeking their head around the corner from the kitchen. I saw my nana’s mini-afro and my grandad’s fedora before they fully revealed themselves. They always somehow appeared right as my phone conversations ended.
“Baby. Now… you say you taking what?”
“I’m taking martial arts, nana. Karate.”

Nana shifted to grandad. “Joseph, honey! Your grandson is trying to… take… martial… arts.” She was laughing uncontrollably and could barely get the sentence out. My grandad fell out laughing and spilled that night’s dinner.
I looked at the beef stew slipping and sliding onto the floor. I was going to master martial arts anyway.
“Come on and help clean this up,” my nana said.
I arrived to three small characters lit in green and gold: Kuk. Sool. Won. Korean. Martial. Arts. I could’ve sworn this was karate.

I walked in the door with my gym bag and my gym shoes, ready to train. But Lauren stopped me. You could not just walk onto the mat. She said it would be best to watch and learn first. She shooed me over to the empty seats. My eyes were glued to her bright yellow belt.

I took a step towards the empty seats in the rustling audience of grown ups, but just before I hopped into a seat, I was halted by a man in a black Dobok. It was decorated with the Korean flag and Korean script. He was bald with a beard, like my grandpa. Gold shimmers hung off his uniform. Without speaking a word he walked towards me and pointed towards the floor. “NO SHOES ALLOWED” it screamed in duct tape. My eyes looked at him and everyone else. I rewinded my steps and kicked my shoes off.

Something was different about this martial arts studio. The students, all small brown bodies with white belts, stood tight for attention. Heads forward, eyes focused, arms tightly attached to the hip, feet together. Afros tight, waves on swim. You could hear the beads clanking with every movement. This was no ordinary studio. Everybody looked like me.
Like experienced soldiers in an army, Lauren and the other kids delivered high kicks, swift punches, and loud “kihap!”
“Go baby!” the moms in the crowd shouted.

Lauren was matched up against a boy her height. Like a snake, she slithered underneath his arm. I blinked. Smack! The audience gasped. The boy was face first on the ground, and Lauren’s knee was pinned on his armpit.

A tall bearded Black man and a caramel-colored teenage girl were in shiny silver uniforms. They both cradled thick wooden planks. Lauren was up first. She sprung up and marched forward. I blinked. Crack! The wooden plank stood no chance. Lauren bowed stoically.

Before the students were dismissed, the master instructor stood and the room fell silent. “Thank you all for supporting our students and attending our belt test and demonstration.” He smiled for the first time that evening. Then he walked a little closer to us, towards the edge of the mat, and it looked almost as if he was staring directly at me. “If any of you are interested and ready, we would love you to join the Kuk Sool Family.”
I slowly raised my hand.

I was nervous when I showed up next week to participate. There were people who looked like me who were doing things that I knew I could never do. Lauren and the same kids I saw at the belt test were completing aerial cartwheels. The master instructor greeted me at the door. I looked at the sea of white belts. I was the only one taller than five feet and with peach fuzz.
“I’m here for class sir,” I whispered.
He asked my name. “Mehki,” I replied. He introduced himself as Pyung Kwan Jahng Nim Marlin Godfree.
“Take your shoes off. Hop on the mat,” he ordered.
“Cha Ryeo,” the entire room echoed. Everyone stood to attention and faced forward. Head forward, eyes focused, arms tightly attached to the hip, feet together.

Our task was to follow the lightning fast speed of our Pyung Kwan Jahng Nim. We put our left foot out when he did, but somehow I ended up hugging my hamstring. All I could hear was my grandparents’ laughter.

I limped behind the energetic kids who whipped around the mat. I got closer and closer to the front of the mat where I was to complete a cat roll. I put my hands out in front of me, closed my eyes, and began to lean forward but stopped.
“Take the risk! No risk, no reward,” Pyung Kwan Jahng Nim yelled. I toppled over.
BAM. SPLISH. I opened my eyes and Lauren had two heads. “Try not to land on your head, next time. Get up,” exclaimed the instructor. I wiped off the sweat from the puddle I landed in.
Next, we were partnered up with someone our same age and rank. It was time for sparring.

“Tyshawn. Mehki. Respect to your partners,” our master ordered. We faced one another. Tyshawn was at least a foot shorter than me, light bright with a Huey Newton fro and a scary scowl. Before I could think twice, I was on the floor with my hand underneath his knee.
“For real?” I accidentally said aloud.

Three gallons of sweat later, class was over. We kneeled in front of the Korean flag, The Kuk Soo Won flag, and the United States of America flag. We gave honor to each flag and then to our instructors. We ended the class with a deep breath.
As I struggled to stand and to carry my soaking wet suit, Pyung Kwan Jahng Nim approached me smiling. “How’s your first day?”
“Hmmm,” I limped over to grab my shoes. “Kuk Soo Won, another sport that I thought I could conquer and like normal, I didn’t. At least, I’m consistent.”
He smiled. “Time shall pass and you will conquer challenges and inherit new things to learn.”
Brittany called later that night. I let it ring for a little while. I did not want to admit she was right.
“Hey,” I answered.
“How’d it go?” she asked genuinely.
“Chile, I cannot.”
We both busted out laughing.
“You know you ain’t no Bruce Lee or nothing! Did you have fun?”
“You don’t really think about having fun when your pressure points are being jumped on.”
“I know you did. If you had fun and you learned something new then it was worth it. I’m not going to let you quit just because you, ‘cannot.’ You feel me?”
“I guess,” I said, and told her I had to go. I still had knots in my stomach. I walked in the kitchen.
My nana looked over at me. “How was your first martial arts class booka bear?”
“You know what, nana. I don’t know about all this.”
“What you mean?”
“Now you know I’m not athletic in the least.”
“We all know that. But you get better every day that you try.”
I nodded in agreement.
“Plus, I paid for it,” she added.
“Okay nana,” I replied and chuckled as I looked outside the window.

Five weeks later. We were lined up again, by rank. By now it was different. When the lightning speed round began, I kicked towards the fluorescent lights. My hamstring remained intact. I punched far and fast. With each kick and each punch, I yelled until I felt my vocal cords vibrate.
And still, no sweat yet.
Our master would come and adjust my feet, my hands, and tell me to aim higher. I was up for the challenge.
“Keep it up,” Pyung Kwan Jahng Nim said.
I grinned and then went right back to a serious face. Aerial flip. Suddenly, my knee felt the concrete through the six-inch mat.
My instructor noticed my face and my now lazy attempts to flip. He approached me and asked to go into the backroom to talk.
“Talk to me, Mehki.”
“I’m just trying to catch up to everyone. I don’t want to be the only older kid in a lower rank. I want to be the best.”

“This is only your 5th class, Mehki.” He sighed with a slight smirk. “And everyone moves at their own pace. We want to make sure that you understand the moves and techniques. Not that you just know them. You’re different.”
“What do you mean by that?” My face scrunched up.

“Well, you’re one of the most athletic students here. And you just got here. You have a long way to go. Yes, martial arts can be frustrating. It’s a solo journey. But, I’ll do my best to make sure you don’t feel alone.”

As the days moved forward, I did my best to take on a new attitude. In the basement of my house, I kicked high, punched fast, and gave loud kihaps. I showed up to the next few classes with a new focus.

Then the day arrived. My first belt test. The sun was out and the parking lot was full. Brittany and my grandparents sat next to each other. Brittany was beaming, and my grandparents kept an encouraging face. Lauren wished me good luck as she walked out with her new blue belt.
I stood up at attention. It was time to begin the test.

We marched into the martial arts studio, lined up, and were ready to go. I punched, turned, kicked, forward, back, turn, punch, kick, jumping crescent kick, X block, Kuk Soo. I was finally keeping up.

Techniques were next. I partnered up with a kid that was actually my size. I’d never seen him before. He stared me right in the eyes. I stood in my attack stance. Right arm grab, I pulled my opponent to the floor, I yelled aloud… I put my knee on his arm and finished him. My smile was wide and my eyes were lit. For once, I proved myself wrong.
We sat in a line. The instructor brought out thick, wooden boards. My eyebrows scrunched. I was unsure my toothpick arms and lanky limbs would come in handy here.
“Mehki!”
I jumped up in attention. I practiced kicking the board. I could feel how thick the board was with the ball of my foot.
“Kick right in the middle.” The instructor whispered to me. I practiced again. I took a deep breath.
I closed my eyes, turned my body, and lifted my right leg. Crack.
My eyes burst open and I looked at my instructor. He said, “Kiop.”
I responded. “Kiop.”
My nana’s eyes screamed.
We made a straight line with our broken boards. We did it.
One at a time we ran to the front to receive our yellow belts. I shook hands with Pun Kwon Ja Nim and I bit my lip into a smile.
“Thank you, sir,” I told him with a smile.
“This is only the beginning,” he said. “I only make grand champions. You will be one of them. The hard work begins from here.”
I smiled. I was doing high-fives inside as I turned and walked away.

I grabbed my coat and shoes, and joined the flock of people filing out of the door. I was just about to jump into the car with my grandparents, when I spotted a kid with his hands as binoculars stuck on the glass. I told my grandparents to hold on. I ran up to him, whipping through the people filing out.
“Hey,” I put my hand on his shoulder. “Try it out, take the risk.”

Being a great athlete comes naturally to most people.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In this short story, Mehki learns an important lesson about taking risks. articles.title: Pioneer Doctor of the Prairie
articles.subtitle: Helena Knauf Wink served her patients with skill and devotion.
articles.author: Karen Blumhagen
articles.description: Dr. Helena Knauf Wink (1854-1936) became the first female doctor in the Dakota Territory.
articles.intro: In this text, Karen Blumhagen describes how Dr. Helena Knauf Wink (1854-1936) became the first female doctor in the Dakota Territory, the area of the United States now known as North and South Dakota.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Dr. Knauf responds to problems.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: It was the winter of 1888. Dr. Helena Knauf Wink steered her exhausted team of horses towards Jamestown, her home in the Dakota territory. Her sleigh had overturned twice in the wild morning ride through falling snow, but she arrived in time to save the baby. When she finally reached home, a settler from miles out on the prairie was waiting. A family was sick and needed her.
Dr. Wink restocked her medical bag, packed her sleigh with food and blankets, and hitched up her best horses. She pondered the rising storm, then turned her team onto the buried trail.

By now the drifts were two feet deep and the wind was howling. It took all of Dr. Wink’s energy and courage to guide her blinded horses through the swirling snow. Somehow she managed to reach the sick family’s farmhouse.

A young boy stabled her horses and helped unload her sleigh. He was the only one of the family of six who was well enough to lend a hand. Dr. Wink laid out medicines and blankets, then served milk and her home-baked bread.
While the blizzard raged outside, she tended her patients. After three days the wind died down, and she said good-bye, satisfied that they could care for themselves.
Early Life

Young Helena Knauf always wanted to be a doctor. Her parents had taught their children to value education and service to others. In a time when many farm children did not attend school, the Knauf children learned to fit school lessons with work on their Michigan farm. Helena earned a teaching certificate, taught in country schools, and saved her salary to pay for medical college.

In 1880, female doctors were not accepted by society. Medicine was considered too unrefined and intimate for women, and they were often criticized for considering such a career. But twenty-six-year-old Helena enrolled in the medical department of the University of Michigan and graduated in the spring of 1883 with sixteen other women. These seventeen were the only women out of 117 graduates.

After Helena’s parents died, ten of her brothers and sisters moved west to the rich farmland near Jamestown, Dakota Territory. Helena joined her family in August 1883. She began her practice shortly after and became the only female doctor in the area.
Serving the Plains People

The plains people wrestled a living from the land. They worried about livestock and crop losses and unpaid bills. Their sod homes, cabins, and dugouts barely kept out winter’s frigid blasts. Unsanitary living conditions and unclean drinking water caused outbreaks of typhoid fever. Many people contracted diphtheria, scarlet fever, and the flu.
Dr. Knauf believed her mission in life was to assist these struggling people. Soon she knew all the trails leading to their homes on the prairie.

Her years helping on her parents’ farm proved useful. She had learned horsemanship and could drive a team of horses well. She even broke her own team of strong, young sorrel horses so she could answer her patients’ calls in any kind of weather.

Any doubts her patients had about her being a doctor were soon replaced by respect and appreciation. She came without excuse, day or night, foul weather or fair. She only charged what people could pay and many times didn’t send bills. She stayed with patients until they were better. She baked bread, did housework, and cared for small children- chores many doctors would not do.

During the early years of her practice, Dr. Knauf lived in rented rooms and kept her teams of horses at Jamestown’s Capital Hotel Livery Stable. One day while unhitching her buggy, she met Mathias Wink, a local teamster who sometimes worked at the stable. He admired the way she handled her horses and soon grew to like her. She felt the same way. In 1886, they married.

Mathias was a benefit to his wife’s practice. He kept her equipment in good condition and sometimes accompanied her on the long drives to visit patients. When their son, Walter, arrived in March 1891, he also rode with his mother, nestled in a basket at her feet.
Dr. Wink’s Skill

Dr. Wink became skilled in treating many kinds of ailments. In the late 1800s, before Jamestown had any hospitals, she brought nine-year-old Lizzie Stuff to her home to treat her ruptured appendix. With the help of three other doctors, Dr. Wink performed one of the earliest appendectomies in the Midwest while the girl lay on her dining-room table. Tending her young patient, Dr. Wink didn’t sleep for the next three days and nights. Because of her skill and vigilance, Lizzie survived.

Dr. Wink worked until her death in 1936 at the age of eighty-one. During her fifty-two years of practice, she delivered more than five thousand babies. Each year on high-school commencement day, she presented a silver spoon engraved with a Jamestown historical site to each of the graduates she had delivered. These spoons became treasured family heirlooms. They were not only reminders of the privilege of attending and graduating from high school, but also of Dr. Wink, who bravely nurtured the families of the prairie.

options.label: Dr. Helena Knauf Wink was the best doctor in Jamestown because she was able to deliver more than five thousand babies.
Dr. Helena Knauf Wink was a female doctor who got married to a man named Mathias and had a son named Walter.
Dr. Helena Kauf Wink faced many challenges to become a doctor and worked to help people in her community.
Dr. Helena Knauf Wink wanted to become a doctor, but could not because she was a woman.

articles.title: Clinging to Tradition, Mattaponi Fight Reservoir
articles.author: Ellen Nakashima
articles.description: Representatives of the town of Newport News and the Mattaponi Indians debate a new reservoir.
articles.intro: In this informational text, written in 1997, representatives from the Mattaponi Indians and from King William County and Newport News discuss their positions on plans to build a new reservoir. The Mattaponi Indians, in 2009, would eventually win the fight to stop the reservoir from being built, but only after a long legal battle with the city.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what each side believes.
articles.permissions: From The Washington Post © 1997 The Washington Post. All rights reserved. Used under license.

blocks.text: On nights when the shad were running, so many fishing lanterns would bob and twinkle on the Mattaponi River, you’d think you were in New York City.
Or so said Mattaponi Indian Chief Webster Custalow, whose eyes glow when he recalls how he and his father fished on the river many decades ago.
“The river was just lit up with lights,” said Custalow, 84. “It was beautiful to look at.”

For more than 300 years, Custalow and his ancestors, back to the great Chief Powhatan, have fished for shad on the Mattaponi — as his sons now do during shad season each spring. There may be fewer lanterns on the river today, but the several dozen tribe members left on Custalow’s tiny reservation are clinging to a tradition they consider vital to their heritage: shad fishing in the tidal river that flows into the Chesapeake Bay.

Now, they say, their tradition is threatened by a $150 million plan to build a 1,500-acre reservoir a few miles from their reservation. King William County and Newport News want a larger water supply to serve growing populations and boost development.

Part of the reservoir project would involve construction of a pumping station that would draw fresh water from the Mattaponi. The Indians fear the diversion would make the tidal river too salty for the shad to spawn there.
“It’s inevitable that it would be the end, that it would mean the demise of the tribe,” said Carl Custalow, known as Lone Eagle, the chief’s second son.
But the Mattaponi are, the younger Custalow said, “standing up for our rights.”

They have brought forward a 320-year-old treaty, signed by England’s King Charles II, that guarantees that nothing shall be built within three miles of the reservation. The Indians contend that part of the dam would encroach on that three-mile buffer.
They have asked Virginia Attorney General James S. Gilmore III (R) to review the treaty and halt the project. Gilmore has not issued an opinion on the matter.

“Believe me, we are not totally against progress,” said Carl Custalow, 53, who has built himself an airy ranch house on the high riverbank. “But we need to do things in a manner that is not destructive… People think of Indians as history, something you read in the books, but we’re not. We’re living here today. We really want to recapture our culture.”

As he spoke, his eyes darted to a bald eagle, swooping down from the dusky sky to nab some dinner. “There he goes. Watch him,” Custalow said, as the bird nose-dived toward a shad. “He’ll catch one before dark.” But for Custalow, it was too windy to fish that day. The gusts would have prevented the proper throwing and setting of nets.

The river is giving up fewer shad. The state banned sale of the fish several years ago to preserve the species. But the Mattaponi, many of whom have office jobs in nearby communities and live in modest wood houses and trailer homes, have dreams.

They are hoping to expand a shad hatchery and open a museum and cultural center. Their goal is to enlarge a reservation that once stretched from the York River to Fredericksburg. They want to offer young people jobs and homesteads so that they’ll stay or return. Although the tribe counts 450 members, only 60 live on the reservation.

The tribe sees the dam as an obstacle to expansion and thus to preservation of its culture. The reservoir — which still needs approval from the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and state regulators — would flood more than 400 acres of wetlands, where Carl Custalow said the Mattaponi camped 8,000 years ago and left tools and other artifacts.

But the immediate threat, Custalow said, is the proposed pumping station about three miles upriver, which would be able to suck up 75 million gallons of fresh water a day to feed the reservoir. He fears the withdrawal of all that fresh water would raise the salinity of the river and destroy the shad spawning ground, along with the tribe’s hatchery plans.

But officials in Newport News and King William say the project would not harm the Mattaponi. They cite a study by the Virginia Institute of Marine Science that concluded that the pumping of fresh water would not “significantly increase” the river’s salinity.

They resent the David-vs.-Goliath, white-vs.-Indian overtones the issue has acquired. “It’s certainly not a position we like to be in,” said King William Administrator David Whitlow. “We think the reservoir can be built without upsetting their culture and the environment. We would not have pursued it to this length if we do not feel that was the case.”

Newport News Mayor Joe Frank agreed. He also said city officials are working with the Mattaponi to ensure that any artifacts are preserved. “Native Americans have been mistreated over the centuries in this country,” he said. “Certainly we don’t want to be a party to doing that.”

The region is growing, and it needs water, officials said. Right now, the Newport News water system provides water to 400,000 people in eight cities and counties. In the next 50 years, it will be serving 600,000.

“When we look at the needs of this area, it is clear that the reservoir is critical,” Frank said. “We’re working very hard to diversify our economy, to bring in new businesses, new jobs. We have to prepare for the future. If you don’t start today, you wait until it’s too late.”
The dam has been in the works for a decade, and the reservoir is not expected to yield its first glass of water until 2005, officials said. The city has spent $8 million to $10 million on planning.

King William is looking forward to higher property taxes around the reservoir and as much as $150 million in payments over 50 years from Newport News, which will lease the land from the county. It also plans to buy the land around the dam and build at least five boat ramps or recreation centers.

David Morris, project director for Newport News Waterworks, disputes the meaning of the 1677 treaty, which states: “ Noe English shall seate or plant nearer than three miles of any Indian Towne; and whosoever hath made or shall make any encroachment upon their lands shall be removed from thence. ”

The three-mile ring was more of a “demilitarized zone” between the Colonists and the Indians, Morris said. Those days, he said, are long gone. He ticked off “hundreds” of structures within the zone: schools, cemeteries, a courthouse. “A lot has been built within that buffer area,” he said.

But the Mattaponi view the treaty differently. A spokesman for Gilmore said an opinion is expected within a month. The project still needs state and federal approval, and those reviews could take months. “I don’t believe they have to take all the water from this river to put water somewhere else,” Chief Custalow said. “When God made the world, he made enough water for everybody. He put nature here to take care of it. If you let nature have its way, it can replenish itself. We think we can do it, but we can’t.”

options.label: The city of Newport News wants to rewrite a treaty from 1677.
The city of Newport News wants to respect the heritage of the Mattaponi Indians.
The city of Newport News wants to fish for shad in a river that is a cultural home to the Mattaponi.
The city of Newport News wants to build a reservoir, but the Mattaponi Indians want to stop the project.

articles.title: Our Parents Are Soldiers
articles.subtitle: Daniel and Isabella Gomez's mom and dad are in the Army. What's life like for the kids when their parents are serving overseas?
articles.author: Joe Bubar
articles.description: A brother and sister describe the experience of having two military parents deployed overseas.
articles.intro: There are nearly 2 million children who have parents in the U.S. military. In this informational text, a brother and sister describe what it is like to be part of a military family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Daniel's feelings towards his family.
articles.permissions: "Our Parents are Soldiers" by Joe Bubar from Scholastic News Edition 4, November 6, 2017. Copyright © 2017 by Scholastic Inc. Reprinted by permission of Scholastic Inc.

blocks.text: In July 2016, 10-year-old Daniel Gomez and his 5-year-old sister, Isabella, said goodbye to their mom and dad. Their parents, Jose and Elizabeth, are in the U.S. Army. They were being deployed to Kuwait, a country in an area called the Middle East. It’s about 7,000 miles away from their home in El Paso, Texas. They wouldn’t be returning home for nine months.
“The hardest part about it was that I couldn’t see them in person and I couldn’t hug them,” Daniel says.

Daniel and Isabella are among the nearly 2 million kids in the U.S. who have at least one parent in the military. While their parents have the difficult job of keeping America safe, these kids face challenges of their own.
Parents On Duty
Last year’s deployment wasn’t the first for either of the Gomezes. In fact, Jose has been deployed overseas five times.
In the Army, Jose uses maps and computers to locate enemy targets. Elizabeth’s job is to keep track of everything that needs to move with her unit. That includes food, supplies, and even tanks.

Last year marked the first time that both parents were sent overseas at the same time. Their Army units were sent to Kuwait in case they needed to assist with military operations in other countries. After a few months, Jose’s unit was sent to Iraq.
A World Apart

Back in the U.S., Daniel and Isabella had to adjust to life without their parents. They went to live with their grandparents in Tampa, Florida. Daniel missed going to the movies and playing basketball with his parents. He even missed just knowing they’d be home to greet him after school. He also sometimes worried for his parents’ safety.
“I get nervous, like I don’t know what’s going on,” he says.

Talking to his parents on FaceTime on his smartphone helped him deal with the stress. Daniel and Isabella chatted with their parents every couple of days while they were away. Because of the time difference, when it’s morning in the U.S., it’s evening in Kuwait and Iraq. So Daniel and his parents had to talk early in the morning or late at night. Daniel says he treasured those moments.
Still, he says the happiest day of his life was when his mom and dad made a surprise return home last March.
On the Move

For many military kids, life can still be difficult even when their parents aren’t overseas. They have to move frequently, any time their parents get assigned to a different base. In addition to living in Texas and Florida, Daniel has lived in North Carolina and Virginia.
Daniel’s dad knows what that’s like for his kids. His father was in the military too.
“You make friends for two or three years, you become comfortable, you know the people, you know the area, and then you just pick up and move and start from scratch,” he says.
But Daniel says he has gotten used to the moves and making friends in new places. Despite the difficulties, he’s proud of his parents. He even wants to join the military when he grows up.
“I’m glad they have this job and that they’re serving this country,” says Daniel.

options.label: The military tries to avoid deploying parents of young children.
Children in the military often travel overseas with their parents.
Life as part of a military family can be both challenging and rewarding.
Military families enjoy making friends with people from all over the world.

articles.title: The 16th Street Baptist Church Bombing
articles.author: Jessica McBirney
articles.description: A exploration of how the bombing of a Black church highlighted racial tensions in 1960s America.
articles.intro: In the 1950s and 1960s the American people, backed by civil rights leaders like Martin Luther King Jr., were speaking out against racial inequality and discrimination. In 1954 the Supreme Court ruled the separation of black and white children at school as unlawful in the Brown v. Board of Education case. While the ruling was met with resistance in many Southern white communities, many African Americans saw the case as a stepping stone to ending other racial inequality problems, and it eventually sparked a series of non-violent protests carried out by black communities that would continue for over a decade. The protests triggered outrage among police and many white community members, and soon some areas (mainly in the South) were experiencing frequent crime and violence on both sides.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify evidence that shows how the 16th Street Baptist Church Bombing had an impact on the way Americans viewed racial discrimination.
articles.permissions: "The 16th Street Baptist Church Bombing" by Jessica McBirney. Copyright © 2016 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 2.0.

blocks.text: In the 1950s and 1960s, racial conflicts were a big problem in Birmingham, Alabama. A lot of people thought of it as the most racist city in America. Twenty-one bombs were set off at African American churches in the city between the years of 1955 and 1963. People gave the city the nickname “Bombingham.”

Many civil rights leaders were trying to help fix the problems in Birmingham. One of those leaders was Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. The activists worked to plan protests and events. The people who went to the protests often got arrested. The police in Birmingham did not allow the protests to happen without trouble.
Civil rights leaders and community members went to the 16th Street Baptist Church to plan meetings. It became well known as a place where the activists met.
Sunday Morning
Until Sunday, September 15th, 1963, no one was killed in the bombings. On that day, the 16th Street Baptist Church became the site of another explosion.

At 10:22 a.m., the teenage Sunday School Secretary got a phone call. The person delivered an ominous message, just saying “Three minutes.” Less than one minute later, the bomb went off. Fifteen sticks of dynamite had been hidden under the church steps.

The explosion hit a back room in the church. Inside, five young girls were getting ready to sing in the church choir. Witnesses said the bomb caused the girls to fly through the air “like rag dolls.” The blast also tore a seven-foot wide hole in the wall. In fact, the explosion was so powerful that a man who was driving by was blown out of his car.

The bomb killed four of the girls: Addie Mae Collins, 14; Carol Denise McNair, 11; Carole Robertson, 14; and Cynthia Wesley, 14. The fifth girl, Sara Collins, was badly injured. Pieces of glass had flown into her eye and blinded her. Beyond the five girls, the explosion injured 19 other people.

Black people in Birmingham reacted quickly to the bombing. Many people rushed to the church to help look for bodies in the church’s ruins. Hundreds of others stormed the city. They burned buildings that white people owned and protested violently. It took 300 extra state policemen to help Birmingham’s police get the city back under control.
Public Support

The funerals for the four girls got a lot of attention around the U.S. Thousands of people, including more than 800 clergymen of all races, attended. Martin Luther King, Jr was also there and spoke at the event. He encouraged people to keep working towards civil rights. He hoped that if they worked hard enough crimes like the bombing would no longer happen.
Investigations

The investigation of the crime was done poorly and took a long time. At first, the state of Alabama offered a $52,000 reward for the bomber’s arrest. Twenty-five FBI agents came to work on the case. Part of what made the investigation hard was the lack of evidence. A lot had been destroyed with the church. Finally the FBI discovered that the bombers were four members of the Ku Klux Klan. Their names were Thomas Edwin Blanton, Jr., Herman Frank Cash, Robert Edward Chambliss, and Bobby Frank Cherry. Shockingly, the FBI closed the case in 1968 without arresting the four men.
Late Arrests

There was a new Attorney General in Alabama in 1971. He started looking into the case again, digging up old evidence. His efforts helped convict the group’s leader. Robert Chambliss was sent to jail in 1977.
The FBI reopened the case in 2000. Herman Cash had died in 1994, but Bobby Cherry and Thomas Blanton, Jr. were convicted in 2002. Both received sentences of life imprisonment.
A Call to Action

The bombing at 16th street was terrible. But it was also very important. People were able to see how bad racism really was. The event was part of why President Lyndon Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act in 1964. After the act, it became harder to treat people poorly based on the color of their skin.

options.label: A significant number of bombings took place in Birmingham, Alabama during the 1950s and 1960s.
Churches in Birmingham were often used as meeting places for civil rights leaders to organize and hold events.
Lyndon Johnson passed the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which made it illegal to discriminate against people for their race, gender, religion, or nationality.
The deaths of four young girls in a Birmingham church bombing sparked massive protests against racial discrimination and gave racism national attention.
