articles.title: Cafeteria Craze
articles.author: Lynette Samuel
articles.description: A group of friends work together to solve a problem in their school cafeteria.
articles.intro: Lynette Samuel is a writer based in Augusta, Georgia. Her work often tackles some of the challenges that students face at school. In this short story, a boy observes his peers eating lunch in the school cafeteria.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Syd’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Cafeteria Craze" by Lynette Samuel. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Syd sat in the school cafeteria surrounded by his noisy classmates. The room buzzed with students chatting, chewing, and toting trays laden with items from the lunch menu. He sniffed, twisted his nose from side to side and breathed in the odors floating in the air. Salty, sweet, cheesy, sour, fruity flavors all mixed together.

Max sat at the table beside him. Max ate with the same two friends every day. They chowed down on hot dogs topped with sauerkraut and ketchup. They bragged about their home runs, grounders, and outfield catches during their little league game on Saturday.

Syd looked to his left and watched Lily skillfully eat her sushi rolls with soy sauce and wasabi. Ever since a classmate had watched Lily eat with chopsticks and said, “Ewww! You’re eating with sticks. That’s gross,” Lily and her friend Kim sat by themselves. Today they were whispering, giggling, and planning what they would wear to school the next day.

Syd looked over at Zach, sitting alone, and felt a wave of sadness. Zach was new to the school and was having trouble making friends. Was it his braids? His accent? He just didn’t know. Zach took a couple of bites of his peanut butter and jam sandwich, ignored the apple, and gobbled down his chocolate chip cookie. He tilted his head way back to finish the container of chocolate milk.
Syd breathed a long, deep sigh and munched on his vegetarian lunch of lentil pancakes and chickpea curry.
You don’t eat meat? Dude! That’s wild!
You’ve never had a cheeseburger?

Syd, too, had experienced impolite and hurtful remarks from classmates about the meals he brought from home. Sometimes the feeling of being alone was hard to take. He looked around the cafeteria. Something had to be done.

On their way back to class, Syd approached Lily, Zach, and Max. “Listen guys, I have an idea,” he said. “Let me see if I can talk to Mrs. Wellington and I’ll meet you in the principal's office after school.”
“What’s going on?” Lily asked.
“Who’s in trouble?” Max's tone matched his puzzled look.
Zach raised his eyebrows. The look of concern was clear.
After school, the students found their way to the office where they found Principal Reynolds and Mrs. Wellington, the cafeteria lady, waiting.
“Come in, have a seat,” the principal said and sat behind his desk.
Mrs. Wellington looked troubled. She nodded at each of them.
“We’d like to take over the cafeteria,” Syd began.
We would??!!
Zach, Lily, and Max were wide-eyed with surprise. They had no idea. But Syd had their attention, and they wanted to hear him out.
Mrs. Wellington stiffened a bit and sat up straighter in her chair.
“Well, let’s hear it,” the principal encouraged, folding his arms across his chest.
Syd went on to explain what he saw during lunch in the cafeteria, how some students sat alone and how others appeared not to enjoy their meals.
“I believe that we,” he began waving his hand to include his friends, “can plan a lunch that will bring students together and provide tasty meals that everyone will enjoy.”
Mrs. Wellington tilted her head to the side and seemed to be giving the idea serious consideration. “I think you’re onto something here,” Principal Reynolds said. “Let’s give it a try.”
“You can have access to the kitchen. It’s fully stocked. I’m here to help. You’ll be responsible for cleaning up when you’re done,” said Mrs. Wellington. She had a stern look on her face.
As they left the office and exited the building, the boys gave each other high fives. Lily seemed to be deep in thought, already planning a menu. They were excited and eager to get started.
“Max, you’re up first on Monday, come up with something good,” said Syd as they left the school building.
“Don’t worry, I will.” Max gave a thumb’s up and boarded the school bus.

The next week, when they entered the cafeteria, the students instantly pinched their noses and covered their mouths. Some made gagging sounds as if they were going to throw up. The smell was just awful, and no one wanted to inhale the terrible odor.
“What is that smell?” one student asked.
“It smells like stinky feet in here,” another said. Some students tried to hold their breath.

As they passed through the line with their empty trays, the students noticed Mrs. Wellington. They’d never seen her like this. Her forehead was wrinkled, her lips were pursed, and her arms were crossed. She slowly and rhythmically tapped her left foot. Max stood next to her with the biggest grin on his face and pronounced, “Liverwurst and limburger cheese sandwiches for everyone!”
Each sandwich was dripping with globs of mayonnaise, slices of raw onions, and pickles.
“Come on guys, eat up. It’s so tasty,” Max urged his classmates.
“Uhhhh, no thanks.”
“That’s okay.”
“I’ll just have milk please.”
“I’m allergic.”
No one tried Max’s sandwich.

Liverwurst, limburger cheese, onions, and pickles may not be so bad by themselves, but not mashed together in a sandwich , Syd thought. What a disaster . He hoped Lily would do a much better job tomorrow.

The next afternoon the students entered the lunchroom cautiously, not knowing what to expect. Some held their nose — just in case. Lily’s menu featured yellowfin tuna, salmon sashimi, and sushi rice. The platters were beautifully decorated with pickled ginger, edible flowers, and green onions. There was matcha mochi for dessert.
“Is that raw fish?” someone exclaimed loudly.

One by one, the students quietly turned around and made their way out to the playground without having any lunch at all. No one ate a bite. Mrs. Wellington stood behind the counter — her lips pressed tightly together, shaking her head from side to side.
Lily stretched her eyebrows and dropped her jaw.
“What!” she said out loud. Lily was totally surprised and confused.
No one tried the matcha mochi.
Syd smacked his forehead and groaned, “ Ohhhh noooo. ”
“Don’t worry,” Zach said. “I’ve got this. Tomorrow we’ll have the best school lunch ever.”
“Thanks,” said Syd. “We just can’t have another bad lunch day. I’m counting on you.”
On Wednesday when the students entered the cafeteria, they were thrilled. There were cheers of yeahs and hoorays.
“Now this is lunch!” someone voiced loudly.

Every table was brimming and overflowing with cupcakes, cookies, caramel popcorn balls, lollipops, ice cream sundaes, fizzy sodas, apple pies, donuts, strawberry shortcake, chocolate bars, jellybeans, milkshakes, snow cones, gum drops, marshmallows, and dozens of flavors of bubble gum.

“Where’s the protein, the complex carbohydrates, the whole grains? The vitamins! The minerals!” cried Mrs. Wellington. Her arms were stretched wide as if she was carrying armfuls of fruit, vegetables, meats, and breads.
The students went from table to table tasting their favorite treats. Syd plopped down on a lunch stool and buried his head in his hands.

News traveled fast and word got out. The parents heard all about the limburger cheese and the sashimi. The total dessert menu was the last straw for many of them. The office phone did not stop ringing. Principal Reynolds and the school secretary were not successful in getting to all the calls.
Many parents began arriving at school, and Principal Reynolds assembled everyone for a meeting.

Amid the chaos, Mrs. Wellington and Syd scurried around the lunchroom attempting to clean up before anyone came back for seconds when an announcement blared over the PA system. “Syd Patel, please come to the principal’s office immediately!”

Syd jumped at hearing his name. He turned and headed toward the office, head down, shoulders slumped. Mrs. Wellington followed, confident that this chaos in her usually calm and ordinary lunchroom would soon be resolved.
“Come in,” Principal Reynolds motioned for Syd to enter. “There have been some complaints from parents and students about the lunch menu recently,” he said.
Syd nodded.
He became even more uncomfortable when he noticed that the adjoining conference room was jam packed with parents searching their phones, scribbling on notepads, and humming with conversation.

“Syd, take a look at this, tell me what you think,” one dad called Syd over and shared his screen so Syd could see his notes and bullet points. The parents were already generating a new plan and organizing a schedule. Mrs. Wellington stepped in, talking with parents about the layout of the cafeteria, the equipment in the kitchen, and the contents of the pantries, refrigerator, and freezer.
“Mrs. Wellington,” came a voice from the crowd. It was one of the parents, Mr. Allen.
Recognizing him, Mrs. Wellington responded, “Yes, John, you have a question?”

“May I suggest that we keep the sashimi and the other items on Lily’s menu. After all, it was the best of all the choices that were presented.” Tapping his forefinger on his chin, Mr. Allen continued, “I think we can encourage the students to be open to foods from other cultures. Let’s give it a try.”
There were nods of agreement and a chorus of “Good idea!” “Let’s do it!” and “Sounds like a plan.”
“Syd, we’ll need you and your team here early tomorrow, at least an hour before school starts,” another parent interrupted. “Can you make that happen?”

“Yes, yes, I can,” Syd responded, already thinking about sharing the plan with Lily, Zach, and Max. He was relieved to have the parents’ support. He realized that his idea needed some extra help and supervision.
Raising his voice to be heard over the chatter, Principal Reynolds interjected, “Let’s get to work.” He then went around shaking hands, thanking the parents for stepping in.
On Friday, when the students entered the cafeteria, there were gasps and plenty of ooohhs and aaaahhs. Was this the same lunchroom they ate in everyday?

The parents were dressed in clothes from different countries. Lily’s mom was dressed in a lovely flower print kimono. Zach’s dad wore a green boubou gown and a kufi cap to match. Max’s parents had on matching blue plaid outfits. His mom wore an ankle length tartan skirt and his dad wore a kilt and carried bagpipes. Syd caught a glimpse of his mom in the crowd. With part of the fabric draped over one shoulder, she was beautiful and graceful in her multi-colored sari.

The entire room was decorated with streamers, balloons, banners, and flags representing places all over the world. Mexico, Africa, Italy, Japan, India, Israel, and the Caribbean Islands were just some of the interesting places. The entire room seemed to sparkle with all the colorful garments and festive accessories.
“Come on in,” Syd’s voice boomed into the microphone. “This has been prepared especially for you. Enjoy your lunch today.”

The students filed through the lunch line. There were all kinds of foods to choose from. It was an international smorgasbord! There were egg rolls and dipping sauces, tortillas filled with meat, jollof rice, soups, salads, and fruit — mangos, bananas, and kiwi. There was baklava and assorted cookies for dessert. Syd was happy to see his classmates enjoying his mom’s lentil pancakes and chickpea curry.
Noticing a classmate walking directly toward her, Lily stiffened and held her breath.
“Uh, Lily, I’m sorry about what I said before.”
Who’s she talking to? Lily turned to see who might be behind her.
“No, really,” the classmate insisted. “I shouldn’t have said those things and anyway, this is kinda yummy,” she said, munching on sashimi.
Lily hesitated, “I, I’m glad you tried it.”
“I was thinking about trying the rumbledethumps next.”
“Me too.”
Together they headed toward the sound of bagpipes playing softly.

Syd looked around the lunchroom and saw that Mrs. Wellington was finally smiling again. Principal Reynolds was talking with teachers and parents, agreeing that this had been an unusual school lunch week.
“I guess everyone doesn’t like liverwurst,” Max confessed to Syd when lunch was almost over.
“Nope, not everyone,” Syd replied. Including me , he thought.
“Sometimes we can get too fancy,” Lily mentioned.
“Yep,” said Syd. “Perhaps we can try new foods a little at a time.”
“I guess there needs to be balance when preparing a meal,” said Zach. “Not too much sugar.”
“I think you’re right,” Syd agreed. “But most of all, we’ve gotten to know each other better. Sharing is the best way to experience other cultures, and a great way to do that is with food.”
As the parents were cleaning up and preparing to leave, Syd found his mom in the crowd and gave her a big hug.
“Thanks for helping out, Mom,” he said.
“You’re welcome, honey, any time,” she responded.
The following week, Max and his baseball buddies made it a point to sit with Zach, and soon there was so much laughter coming from their corner of the room that they had to be asked to quiet down.

Lily offered to show some girls in her class how to pick up French fries with chopsticks. They were awkward at first, but they had such fun trying to get the fries in their mouth before the potato dropped back on to the plate. They soon gave up, used their fingers, and spent the rest of their time talking about fashion and hairstyles.
Mrs. Wellington could not have been more pleased.

options.label: Only adults know what is best for children.
Solving problems is easier for kids than adults.
Following the rules can sometimes get you into trouble.
Sharing new experiences is a great way to develop friendships.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the story?

options.label: They explain why Syd does not enjoy trying new foods.
They show why Syd is only friends with people who eat the same food as him.
They describe the reason Syd's friends think he is lying about the food he eats.
They share that Syd knows what it is like to be teased because of the food he eats.

questions.prompt: What do the following paragraphs reveal about Syd? You don't eat meat? Dude! That's wild! You've never had a cheeseburger? (Paragraphs 6-7)

options.label: They introduce a problem and then present a possible solution.
They highlight the setting and then show how it affects the characters' actions.
They describe a fight between the characters and then share how they make up.
They share an event from the past and then describe what happens in the present.

questions.prompt: How do paragraph 8 and paragraph 22 add to the story?

options.label: They are fed up with the new school lunch menus.
They are excited that the new lunch menu includes drinks.
They are pleased that the students enjoyed the dessert menu.
They are angry with Principal Reynolds for not listening to their menu ideas.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "last straw" suggest about the parents? (Paragraph 54)
How do Syd's actions in the story support the idea that working together is a good way to solve a problem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: During the "international smorgasbord" in the cafeteria, students and families wear traditional clothing and share traditional foods from their varied cultures. The word "culture" refers to beliefs, values, traditions, and customs shared by a particular group. Describe your culture. Is it similar to any of the cultures from the story? What type of food or clothing is special to your culture, family, or community? How does it make you feel when people want to learn more about you or your culture? Why?

At the end of the story, Syd's school shares an "international smorgasbord" where they are able to sample food from all over the world. Do you enjoy trying new foods? Would you be excited if something like this happened in your school cafeteria? Why or why not.

The characters in the story work together to solve a problem in their community. Have you ever worked together with your friends, family members, or people from your community to solve a problem? What was the problem and how did you work together to solve it? Were your efforts successful? Why or why not?

articles.title: Workers' Rights and the History of Labor Unions
articles.author: Jessica McBirney
articles.description: This text provides a history of labor unions, including tactics and advances in workers' rights.
articles.intro: What is a labor union? In the past, when workers were mistreated by their employers, there was nothing they could do about it. Labor unions were the workers' solution to this problem. This article sets the scene for the dawn of labor unions, explores the history of workers' rights, and explains the strategies they still use today.
articles.annotationTask: As you read this text, identify 3 strategies that union members used to seek justice and create change.
articles.permissions: © 2016. Workers' Rights and the History of Labor Unions by CommonLit is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 2.0.

blocks.text: Have you ever heard the phrase “There’s strength in numbers”? For many years, people who worked on farms, in factories, or in other low-wage positions were sometimes forced to endure terrible conditions with little pay. But they soon found, with “strength in numbers,” that they could take charge of their circumstances. That’s where labor unions come in.

A labor union is a group of workers who join together to ask their employers for better payment, working hours, and working conditions. Labor unions are very common today, and many workers in the United States are members of different kinds of unions. However, unions did not always exist. Modern labor unions didn’t appear in the United States until the late 1800s.

The United States experienced an Industrial Revolution from about 1860 until about 1920. People started to use machines and factories to make products instead of making them by hand. Factory owners became rich very quickly, but they did not treat their workers with kindness or respect. Employees had to work up to 12 hours per day for only a few dollars. They did not always get weekends off. In many industries, workers faced inhumane conditions such as having to bend over all day, or breathing in smoke from the machines.

One of the worst industries to work in was the coal industry, where workers spent all day deep underground in coal mines. The mines were very dark and usually damp and cold. Workers had to breathe coal dust and poisonous gases. They had to crawl through the mine in narrow tunnels and pull carts full of coal behind them. There was always a chance that the whole mine could collapse from an explosion. Because coal miners were paid so little, many families could not afford to buy enough food. They had to send their children to work in the mines too, just so the family could have enough money to live on. Children worked six days a week just like their parents, so they could not go to school.

Workers decided they could not keep living such hard lives. They knew they had to work together to negotiate with the rich and powerful factory owners, so they grouped together to form labor unions. Labor unions used 3 different strategies to protect workers:

The first strategy was called “collective bargaining.” Workers chose a few leaders to represent them in a meeting with the factory or mine owner. In this meeting, everyone would agree to a new contract that gave workers shorter hours, higher pay, and safer working conditions. If the factory owner did not agree to the contract, all the workers would strike, or stop working until they got what they wanted. Many strikes occurred during the Industrial Revolution, and workers still go on strike today.
The second strategy was giving benefits to union members. If any worker got hurt on the job, the union would pay for the doctor’s visit, medicine, and sometimes food.

The third strategy was working with the government to pass more laws. Union leaders could make deals with politicians to pass laws that helped workers. For example, child labor became illegal and the government began to send inspectors to make sure factories were safe.

Unions were very successful in making sure business owners did not take advantage of their workers. Unions still serve many of these same functions today. Workers still go on strike when their employer will not agree to a better contract, and unions still work with the government to get laws passed. Many people across the country rely on unions to keep their jobs safe and their families fed.

questions.prompt: Explain how the Industrial Revolution relates to the history of workers' rights. Cite evidence from the text in your response.

options.label: By forming unions, workers protected their rights and made important social changes.
Children were the first members of labor unions before the trend spread among adult workers.
Workers acted illegally and outside the law, so their strikes were not always respected.
Labor unions were a popular trend in the past, but after the Industrial Revolution, workers have moved beyond them.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best identifies a central idea of this text?

options.label: "Labor unions are very common today, and many workers in the United States are members in different kinds of unions. " (Paragraph 2)
"Many strikes occurred during the Industrial Revolution, and workers still go on strike today." (Paragraph 6)
"If any worker got hurt on the job, the union would pay for the doctor's visit, medicine, and sometimes food." (Paragraph 7)
"Many people across the country rely on unions to keep their jobs safe and their families fed." (Paragraph 9)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which phrase from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: Not human; animal
Difficult yet rewarding
Unequal
Cruel and brutal

options.label: Paragraph 2
Paragraph 4
Paragraph 5
Paragraph 9

discussionQuestions.prompt: Without unions, workers have to do whatever their employers say, including working without benefits or in dangerous conditions, or they could be fired. A labor union helps the workers have a little power so that their rights can be respected. How can unions and employers come to fair compromises? What rights should workers demand? What rights belong to employers?

How do people create change? Can strategies like collective bargaining, working with the government, or agreeing to benefits help people create change in other scenarios? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: The Desperate Ride of Caesar Rodney
articles.author: Candace Fleming
articles.description: Delegate Caesar Rodney rushes to Philadelphia to participate in the vote for independence.
articles.intro: On July 2, 1776, delegates from the thirteen colonies voted in favor of their independence from England and formed the United States of America. In this short story, Caesar Rodney, a delegate from Delaware, rushes to Philadelphia to participate in the vote to ensure that the colonies gain their independence.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why Rodney’s vote matters.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: On a black July night in 1776, a dust-caked messenger yanked his horse to a stop in front of a Delaware farmhouse. The messenger leaped from his mount, sprinted to the house, and pounded on the door. He had urgent news for the man inside.

Skeleton-thin and wearing a green veil to cover a tumor on his face, Caesar Rodney opened the door. “They are voting on independence tomorrow, sir,” the messenger breathlessly reported. There was no time to lose. Rodney dressed quickly, then saddled and mounted a horse. They galloped into the inky darkness toward Philadelphia, eighty miles away.

Since May of 1775, delegates to the Continental Congress in Philadelphia had been debating whether to break away from England. Rodney, one of the three delegates from Delaware, had been in his seat on June 7, 1776, when Henry Lee of Virginia boldly suggested total independence. Rodney quickly backed him.

Several delegates did not agree with Lee, and they angrily flung their arguments at those who pressed for independence. How could the young colonies survive without England’s protection? Wouldn’t England’s army crush the colonies’ untrained militia? Without England to maintain law and order, they insisted, mobs would run wild in the streets. The discussions raged for weeks.
In the heat of the debate, Rodney received an alarming report that a thousand English supporters were rioting near Dover, his hometown. Local authorities begged Rodney to return immediately.

Rodney didn’t want to leave before the Congress voted on independence, but other delegates assured him that the debate would drag on for days. Satisfied, Rodney traveled home only to find that the rioting had ended. Exhausted, he decided to rest overnight before returning to Philadelphia. Rodney had been asleep only a few hours when his much-needed rest was disturbed by the messenger.
As his horse sped across the sleeping Delaware countryside, Rodney thought about the situation he was in. He knew it was vital that every colony vote yes when the roll was called for independence.

But the Delaware delegation was split and dangerously close to voting no. One delegate was not yet ready to break ties with England, and the second wanted independence. Rodney’s vote would decide whether Delaware voted yes or no. If even one colony voted no, the resolution would fail. Fearing the worst, Rodney spurred his horse on.

A fierce thunderstorm broke with the dawn. Lightning flashed and rain poured down, turning the road into a sea of mud. Rodney refused to slow his pace, and by 11 a.m. he was only fifteen miles away. But his horse was exhausted, and Rodney was forced to stop for a fresh mount at a roadside inn. He paced anxiously for thirty excruciating minutes while a horse was saddled. At last Rodney pounded once more toward Philadelphia.

As Rodney rode toward the city, rain slashed at the windowpanes of the Philadelphia statehouse. Inside the assembly room, the air crackled with tension as the delegates listened to arguments for and against independence.

Among them, Thomas Jefferson fidgeted nervously. If Lee’s resolution didn’t pass, Jefferson’s Declaration of Independence would be forgotten. John Adams, who was a staunch supporter of independence, sat tight-lipped and silent. Only seventy-year-old Benjamin Franklin looked confident. He felt sure that a vote for independence would come sometime that day.

Finally the debate ended, and the delegates decided to vote after lunch. That afternoon they returned, and John Hancock, president of the Continental Congress, banged his gavel to quiet the delegates.

Just then, hooves clattered over the cobblestones in front of the statehouse. All eyes turned toward the door. In burst Rodney — dripping wet, spattered with mud, and still wearing his riding boots and spurs.

“He was the oddest looking man in the world — tall, thin, and slender as a reed, with a pale, veiled face no bigger than a large apple,” John Adams wrote of Rodney’s dramatic appearance. “Yet there is sense and fire, spirit, wit, and humor in his countenance.”
Ignoring the stares, Rodney strode up the aisle and proudly took his seat. Hancock cleared his throat and began to call the vote.
“New Hampshire.”
“Aye.”
“Massachusetts.”
“Aye.”
“Rhode Island.”
“Aye.”
“Pennsylvania.”
“Aye.”
“Delaware.”
Caesar Rodney rose to speak. “As I believe the voice of my constituents and all sensible men are in favor of independence,” he said, “I vote for independence.”
Rodney’s vote broke Delaware’s deadlock. The resolution for independence had passed.

options.label: Independence was endangered when some delegates refused to support the vote for independence.
People were inspired to vote in favor of independence after seeing how passionate Rodney was to vote.
While Rodney was not sure if independence was the right answer, he trusted his fellow delegates to make the right decision.
Rodney's determination to take part in the vote allowed the colonies to finally become independent from England.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best expresses the main idea in the passage?

options.label: "Since May of 1775, delegates to the Continental Congress in Philadelphia had been debating whether to break away from England." (Paragraph 3)
"Rodney's vote would decide whether Delaware voted yes or no. If even one colony voted no, the resolution would fail. Fearing the worst, Rodney spurred his horse on." (Paragraph 8)
"Only seventy-year-old Benjamin Franklin looked confident. He felt sure that a vote for independence would come sometime that day." (Paragraph 11)
"'As I believe the voice of my constituents and all sensible men are in favor of independence'" (Paragraph 25)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: The text follows Rodney's journey then shifts to explain the events in Philadelphia.
The text explains the issues in Philadelphia and then shifts to describe Rodney's journey.

options.label: Paragraph 1
Paragraph 6
Paragraph 10
Paragraph 12 E. Paragraph 13 F. Paragraph 26

questions.prompt: PART B: Which TWO paragraphs from the text best support the answer to Part A?
Describe the relationship between Caesar Rodney's views on the vote for independence and the views of the other delegates discussed in the text. Use evidence from the text to support your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Caesar Rodney races to Philadelphia because he knows his vote matters in the decision to declare independence. Why is America unique in its election process? Do you believe in the power of one vote? Explain. Do you think your vote can have as much power as Rodney's when you turn 18?
Rodney hits several setbacks in his ride to Philadelphia. Why was he able to succeed? Describe a time when you were successful even though it was doubtful. What helped you to succeed?

articles.title: The Poppy Lady
articles.author: Barbara Elizabeth Walsh
articles.description: Barbara Elizabeth Walsh discusses how Moina Michael became known as the "Poppy Lady."
articles.intro: Moina Belle Michael (1869-1944) was an American teacher who was known widely as the "Poppy Lady." In this informational text, Elizabeth Walsh discusses how Moina got her famous nickname.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why Moina felt motivated to wear red poppies.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: And now the Torch and Poppy red We wear in honor of our dead. Fear not that ye have died for naught; We’ll teach the lesson that ye wrought In Flanders Fields.
— from a poem by Moina Belle Michael

One afternoon in November 1918, Moina Belle Michael hurried through the streets of New York. She wore a bright, red silk poppy on her coat collar and carried a bouquet of 24 smaller poppies. World War I was raging overseas, and Moina was determined to do her part.
Maybe the red flower would remind people of the American soldiers fighting in Europe and the sacrifices many had made.

Moina was a teacher at the University of Georgia when war first broke out. Friends, neighbors, and students had gone overseas to fight. Moina planned to do more than knit socks or sweaters, or roll bandages for the Red Cross. She wanted to encourage the soldiers before they left for war.
Volunteering for the War

Moina traveled to New York City to care for soldiers at the YMCA headquarters at Columbia University. There was nothing she enjoyed more than sitting and chatting with the soldiers. She looked at photographs of their families and sweethearts and listened to their hometown news. She bought fresh flowers to brighten the room when the weather outside turned dreary.

Earlier that November morning, one of the soldiers had left a copy of Ladies’ Home Journal on Moina’s desk. She turned to the marked page of the magazine and found a well-known poem, which began, “In Flanders fields the poppies blow / Between the crosses, row on row…”
Moina had read the poem before. She knew that Lieutenant Colonel John McCrae, a Canadian physician, wrote it while treating soldiers on the battlefields of Flanders in Belgium.
Remembering the Soldiers

Lieutenant Colonel McCrae’s poem, remembering those men, always touched her. But that morning, Moina was struck by the last verse and accompanying illustration. Soldiers with sad eyes stared at a battleground blanketed in white crosses and bright red poppies. The soldiers seemed to speak to her. “Remember us,” they asked.
Moina thought about her soldiers at the Y, and how they had left their families and homes to protect the freedom she enjoyed each day. To Moina, they were heroes.
Silently, she made a promise. She would always wear a red poppy to remember the soldiers. She wrote her promise in a poem.
Just then, three YMCA war workers stopped by her desk with a check for $10. They thanked her for all she did to brighten the room.
There was no doubt about what Moina would do with the money. “I shall buy red poppies,” she told them.
Creating a Symbol
Moina showed them Lieutenant Colonel McCrae’s poem and her poem. Word spread quickly. Before long, everyone wanted to wear a red poppy.

It wasn’t easy finding silk poppies on the streets of New York, but Moina was successful. Hurrying back to the Y, she handed them out. She dreamed of the day when every American would wear a poppy to remember the soldiers.

Two days later, World War I ended and Moina returned to her students in Georgia. True to her promise, she wrote letters to leaders around the country, suggesting they adopt the poppy as a national memorial symbol.

But, as she taught returning servicemen, Moina soon realized another purpose for the small flower. Many of the servicemen had no jobs. No money. Some were wounded or too ill to work. Maybe the poppy could also help the veterans and their families.

Her hard work paid off. In 1921, the American Legion Auxiliaryadopted the Poppy of Flanders Fields as its memorial flower. Other national and international veterans organizations followed. Disabled veterans who were members of the patriotic groups made crepe-paper poppies, offering them for donations. Pennies, nickels, and dimes poured in.

During the years that followed, the poppies raised millions of dollars in the United States and England to benefit veterans, war widows, and orphans, and the flowers reminded people of soldiers everywhere. To Moina’s delight, the message of the poppies spread to 50 other countries. At last, her dream of helping soldiers was coming true.

For the rest of her life, Moina Belle Michael was known as the “Poppy Lady.” She received citations,awards, and words of praise from around the world. But the Georgia schoolteacher did not feel like a hero.
“What I did, and am doing is no more than any other person would have done,” Moina insisted. “I only thought of it first.”

options.label: While Moina's use of poppies raised awareness about the war, it did little to offer actual support to returning soldiers.
Moina's commitment to remembering soldiers changed into a movement that helped veteran organizations.
John McCrae used red poppies to express his respect for soldiers long before Moina began wearing them.
Moina didn't fully realize that she was making a statement about soldiers when she first started wearing red poppies.

articles.title: Free at Last: A Kurdish Family in America
articles.author: Karen O'Connor
articles.description: Karen O'Connor describes talking to a Kurdish family about their experiences as refugees.
articles.intro: Karen O'Connor has written for Highlights . In this memoir, O'Connor describes meeting a Kurdish family who was forced to leave their home in Kurdistan, a region in the Middle East that spans multiple countries but is not a country of its own, and come to America.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the challenges the Ahmet family faced on their journey.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Amina Ahmet squatted on the floor of her kitchen in El Cajon, California, mixing flour and water and yeast into large balls. She pushed and pulled the bread dough, then flattened it into a pan for baking. As soon as the loaves were fresh from the oven, she handed one to me, then another, and another, until I had a stack from my waist to my nose.
“Enough! Enough!” I said, laughing. “This is more than my husband and I can eat in a month.”
“No, no, no!” she scolded. “This bread — a present for you.”
I gave in and tucked the warm golden pita loaves into a bag to take home that evening.

The memory of that day in 1994 is still clear in my mind. I can picture the Ahmet family gathered together in the small living room of their apartment — Amina and her husband, Taha, along with their five children: Khamger (age 19), Ferhat (16), Warvin (9), Zendon (5), and baby Susan, who was born in the United States.
I was a visitor, soon to be a friend. I had come to speak with the family about people who leave their homeland during a war and start over in a new country. These people are called refugees .

Taha and Amina had brought their children to America to get away from the bombs and the fighting in their homeland of Kurdistan during a war with Iraq. Kurdistan, a mountainous region in the Middle East, extends over parts of Turkey, Iran, Iraq, Syria, and the former Soviet Union. Because of the region’s rich oil reserves, many countries have wanted to control it. Kurdistan’s people have fought for many centuries to make the region their own country but haven’t been able to do so.
The family had fled their small village of Binaviah in 1988. “We were eating breakfast one morning,” said Ferhat. “Then suddenly soldiers ran through the streets shouting and throwing gas bombs.”

Taha gathered his family. They ran for safety with only the clothes they were wearing, a little food, a camera, a few blankets, and their horse. Their journey to freedom took them over mountains and along rivers. At night they huddled under trees to keep warm. During the day, they walked with other villagers. Finally, they came to a refugee camp in Turkey.
There they lived in a small tent for four years. Ferhat remembered freezing winters and hot summers. They sometimes had to wait in long lines for water — even during snowstorms.
“Life was hard,” said Taha. “We did not have enough food or medicine or warm clothes.”
“I missed my parents,” said Amina, with tears in her eyes.

Then in 1992 they received good news. Amina’s brother, who lived in San Diego, California, arranged for them to come to the United States. He promised to help them get settled. Today, Amina, Taha, and their children are thankful for a new place to call “home.”

Nine-year-old Warvin enjoyed telling me how happy he is. He said he liked seeing his mother smiling again. And his father no longer woke up in the night afraid for his family. “My cousins taught me to speak English,” he said proudly, “but I taught myself to ride a bike.”

Amina and Taha’s children learned American ways quickly. But Amina said she preferred to follow many of the customs of her homeland. For dinner during my visit, she set a plate of eprakh on the mat on the living-room floor. There was not enough room at the kitchen table for all of us. The mixture of rice, meat, and vegetables wrapped in grape leaves was delicious. Amina had picked the grape leaves herself. Next she served a salad of chopped tomatoes, onions, lettuce, cucumber, pickles, and salt. And finally, she carried in a platter of chicken, rice, and warm pita bread.

We gathered on the floor and shared the feast. After dinner, we talked more about life in Kurdistan as Taha pointed to photos of the country’s leaders displayed on the wall. One of the children put on a video of a family wedding.
Our meal ended with traditional hot tea, sweetened with sugar, and served in small glasses. A cookie called kade , made with sugar and walnuts, was part of dessert.
Then it was time to say good-bye. I picked up my bag of home-made bread, hugged everyone, and kissed baby Susan on the cheek.

Before I left, Amina asked me to visit again. She whispered something to Ferhat. A smile lit up his face. “She said you are like our grandmother,” he told me. “She misses her mother very much. Maybe the children can call you their American grandmother.”

I liked that. I would come again and again for three more years until the family moved to Tennessee, where Taha had found a new job. No matter how far away they are, the Ahmet family will always have a place in my heart.

options.label: Karen O'Connor didn't understand what a refugee was before she learned about the Ahmet family's experiences.
The Ahmet family was surprised by how difficult it was to relocate to America and the less than friendly welcome they received when they arrived.
Karen O'Connor developed a close friendship with the Ahmet family and learned about their challenging experiences as refugees.
While Karen O'Connor was hesitant to make friends with the Ahmet family, sharing a meal brought them together.

articles.title: After the Winter
articles.author: Claude McKay
articles.description: A speaker describes a hopeful future.
articles.intro: Claude McKay (1889-1948) was a Jamaican American novelist and poet who played an important role in the Harlem Renaissance. In the following poem, a speaker discusses their feelings about the seasons.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker describes the seasons.
articles.permissions: "After the Winter" by Claude McKay (1971) is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Some day, when trees have shed their leaves
And against the morning’s white
The shivering birds beneath the eaves
Have sheltered for the night,
We’ll turn our faces southward, love,
Toward the summer isle
Where bamboos spire to shafted grove
And wide-mouthed orchids smile.
And we will seek the quiet hill
Where towers the cotton tree,
And leaps the laughing crystal rill,
And works the droning bee.
And we will build a cottage there
Beside an open glade,
With black-ribbed blue-bells blowing near,
And ferns that never fade.

options.label: Summer is the most pleasant time of year.
Looking towards the future can bring us joy.
Remembering the past brings us closer together.
Nature is beautiful if we take the time to appreciate it.

questions.prompt: What is the main theme of the poem?

options.label: hopeful.
humble.
insistent.
regretful.

questions.prompt: The speaker's tone is mostly —

options.label: a difficult past
a beautiful day
the speaker's love
the changing seasons

questions.prompt: Which of the following describes what the "shivering birds beneath the eaves" (Line 3) represent?

options.label: the future will be peaceful.
spring does not last forever.
summer is a beautiful time of year.
the past holds important memories.

questions.prompt: Lines 9-12 help the audience understand that —

discussionQuestions.prompt: Why does the poet use the seasons to represent feelings? If you were to think of a feeling that you associate with each season, what would it be and why?

Who do you imagine the speaker is speaking to and what is their relationship? What lines from the poem make you think this?

articles.title: The Painted Elephant Parade
articles.author: Jennifer Buchet
articles.description: The Painted Elephant Parade is a tradition that begins the Hindu festival of Holi.
articles.intro: Jennifer Buchet is an award-winning children's author. In this article, Buchet provides information about the Elephant Parade which is part of the Hindu festival of Holi.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the main ideas of each part of the text.
articles.permissions: "The Painted Elephant Parade" by Jennifer Buchet, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Large crowds gather in the streets of Jaipur, India, toward the end of March. The air is thick with anticipation and excitement. Delicious aromas of samosas, malpuas (sweet pancakes), savory kabobs, and spicy kachoris drift around the boulevard. The rhythmic pounding of traditional wooden drums called dhols starts to reverberate across town. The beating announces the beginning of one of the most majestic and colorful parades in the world — the annual Elephant Parade that marks the beginning of Holi.

It’s a jaw-dropping, eye-catching line of color and sound as these massive moving canvases stroll down the street. Trunks swinging, tails swishing, the female pachyderms proudly strut their stuff. Their trunks are dusted with gold and decorated with rings and tie-dyed scarves, their ankles bedecked with loads of jingly bells. Their mahouts, their personal elephant caretakers, ride atop vibrant-colored jhools , or saddle cloths. They too are dressed in their finest, often carrying silken parasols, muttukuda , and waving white tufts of vencamaran . After the gala, many of these outsized beauties will compete in a modeling pageant, a game of tug-of-war, and even polo.

Several festivals throughout Asia celebrate the elephant, but none are quite as colorful as Jaipur’s Elephant Parade. The parade is the first event of Holi, an ancient Hindu celebration also known as the Festival of Colors or Festival of Love. Holi marks the end of winter and the beginning of spring and celebrates the triumph of good over evil. During this riotous and highly colorful festival, people let go of any anger and resentments toward others and unite in forgiveness, love, and fun.

With all this joyous color, it’s no wonder that the painted elephants play a colossal role during Holi. But how do people paint elephants? Just like any art project, they need a smock, plenty of paintbrushes, and water. But these artisans must be extra careful because their canvas tends to move and has extremely heavy feet. A soft voice, gentle hands, another person to help, and some tasty snacks for the elephants are all important elements, too.

The painters often plan the motifs, or designs, for days before they even touch their giant friends. Designs are drawn on paper or laptops, and then, of course, enlarged. Lotus blossoms, peacocks, tigers, and mandala symbols are all common patterns. A good artist can “hide” the elephant herself behind all the detailed artwork.

Once the artist is ready to paint the pachyderm, the elephant is cleaned. Any hay, dirt, and dust are brushed off, and hoof nails are thoroughly scoured and buffed pre-pedicure. The initial designs are typically outlined in white paint across the elephant before they are filled in with the flamboyant colors. Hours later, the moving models are outfitted with their special jewelry. These elephants sometimes wear large, dangling earrings covered in faux jewels, glamorous gold- and silver-plated caparisons that rest on their foreheads, and loads of silver and gold rings along their tusks and ankles.

These sensational elephants usually participate only in religious festivals, but they can sometimes be seen strolling in the crowded market streets offering rides right alongside cars, bikes, and people. Of course, not everyone can see a real-life painted elephant, but you may be able to see an incredibly life-like ceramic one. In 2006, a father and son were inspired to help the wild elephant population after seeing a baby elephant with only three legs; the fourth was lost to a landmine. Banding activists and artists together, they created the world’s largest art exhibition of decorated elephant statues. Based on the actual height and size of baby elephants, these stunning statues were created to bring awareness to the declining wild elephant populations. Each one is unique, and these elephants parade around the world, in cities such as Bangkok, Rio de Janeiro, and London.
If you’re inspired to paint an elephant, whether real, ceramic, or on paper, you just have to answer one question — how do you really paint an elephant?
One brushstroke at a time, of course.

options.label: Elephants are gentle animals that many people do not really understand.
It takes time and imagination to decorate elephants for the celebration of Holi.
The festival of Holi is celebrated each year as a way to protect elephants from harm.
Baby elephants take part in the elephant parade but older elephants are not a part of Holi.

articles.title: Louis Riel
articles.author: Britannica Kids
articles.description: Louis Riel was a Métis leader who tried to protect his people's rights when the land that was their home became part of Canada.
articles.intro: Indigenous people are the people who first lived in a particular country or place. This is a biography of Louis Riel, a member of the Métis people.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what kind of person Louis Riel was.
articles.permissions: Excerpted with permission from Encyclopædia Britannica, © 2023 by Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.

blocks.text: Introduction
Louis Riel was a leader of the Métis, a people of mixed Indigenous and European descent. He tried to protect their rights when the land that was their home became part of Canada.
Early Life

Riel was born on October 23, 1844, in Saint Boniface, Assiniboia. Assiniboia was a district that was owned by the Hudson’s Bay Company in what is now the Canadian province of Manitoba. It was also called the Red River Settlement. Riel went to Montreal to study and later held several jobs in the United States. He returned to the Red River area in the late 1860s.
Rebel Leader

In 1869 the Hudson’s Bay Company arranged to transfer the Assiniboia lands to the new country of Canada. The Métis were afraid that this would bring outsiders who would harm their way of life. They formed a group to protest the changes, and Riel became their leader.

The Métis established a new government with Riel as president. He sent a group to the capital city of Ottawa to push for Métis rights. They came to an agreement with the Canadian government, and Assiniboia became the province of Manitoba in 1870.

Riel won recognition and praise from some Canadians. The government, however, did not trust him. In 1873 he was elected to the Parliament, but he did not take his seat. The following year he was expelled from the legislature. In 1879 he moved to Montana in the United States, where he married and started a family.
Northwest Rebellion
Back in Canada the Métis continued to struggle against the new government. In 1884 the Métis of the Northwest Territories asked for Riel’s help.

Riel returned to Canada and asked the government to address the problems of the Métis. But the government did not respond. In 1885 some of the Métis therefore began an armed rebellion. Government troops quickly crushed the rebellion, and Riel surrendered. He was put on trial and found guilty of treason. Riel was sentenced to death and was hanged on November 16, 1885.

options.label: Louis Riel started the Hudson Bay Company to help the Métis people.
Louis Riel started the Hudson Bay Company to help the Métis people.
Louis Riel helped Canada win an important battle against the United States.
Louis Riel was a leader who fought to help the Métis and keep their rights safe.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

"The Métis established a new government with Riel as president." (Paragraph 4)

articles.title: Happy New Year in Spring?
articles.author: Joan Issari
articles.description: Nowruz, the Persian New Year, is celebrated at the start of spring with many different customs.
articles.intro: A tradition is a practice that is passed down over many years. In this text, Joan Issari teaches about the traditions of New Year in Iran, a country in southwestern Asia.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the traditions the author explains.
articles.permissions: "Happy New Year in Spring?" by Joan Issari, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Of course! In many areas of the Middle East and Central Asia, New Year’s is celebrated, not in the dead of winter, but on the first day of spring, a tradition dating back more than five thousand years to ancient Mesopotamia. Called Nowruz, or New Day, in Iran, it is a long, happy holiday when you visit friends and family, receive gifts of money and special things you want, wear new clothes, and are served cookies and sweets everywhere you go.

Nowruz is not a religious holiday but celebrates the rebirth of earth and all life on it. The thirteen days of festivities begin the Tuesday evening before the spring equinox, on what is traditionally called Chaharshambeh Souri, which means Wednesday Evening (or Wednesday Eve). In ancient Mesopotamia, each day ended, and a new day began, at six o’clock in the evening, instead of at midnight as we now observe. By this reckoning, Wednesday evening came before Wednesday morning — on what we now call Tuesday evening! But the holiday name hasn’t changed.

On Chaharshambeh Souri you jump over a little bonfire to purify yourself for Nowruz. The flame is supposed to remove all your sickness and bad luck. The fire is usually lit outside the house, using a few sticks, but sometimes it’s just a candle placed on the floor inside. As you jump over the glowing flame you shout, Zard-i man az toe , Sorkh-i toe az man , or in English, “My paleness to you, your healthy glow to me.” There’s a lot of joking and laughter. Be sure to jump high!

Nowruz is a time of peace and good will. Ashtee, making up and forgiving grudges held against another person, is important. The house has been cleaned top to bottom. You and your family put on brand-new clothes. To celebrate ta’ feel , the moment Nowruz arrives, you sit around the Sofreh Haft Seen , a tablecloth set with seven or more symbolic items whose names begin with the letter S in the Persian language, Farsi. The New Year is greeted with prayer for the welfare of each other, the country, and the world.

The traditional Haft Seen, or Seven S’s, are sabzi (green seed sprouts, symbolizing new life), seeb (apples, symbol of beauty), seer (garlic, medicine and good health), serkeh (vinegar, age and patience), senjed (fruit of the lotus tree, for love and happiness), samanoo (a creamy sweet pudding, prosperity), and sumagh (red sumac berries, joy). There are usually also sekeh , gold and silver coins, often in a small bowl of water, symbolizing wealth; sombol , or hyacinth, a spring flower for life and beauty; and perhaps a highly polished samovar for boiling water and making tea.

The sofreh includes a holy book for whatever religion your family observes. Sometimes there’s a book of poems by Hafiz or Ferdowsi, the most revered poets of medieval Persia. A small bowl of live goldfish, a samak , is supposed to bring good luck, especially if you’re looking at it the moment Nowruz arrives. There is a bowl of hard-boiled eggs, usually colored by the children — at least one egg for each person present. A mirror reflects light, representing goodness; two or more candles are lit in front of it.

The Iranian New Year begins at the exact second of the spring equinox, when the sun crosses the equator and the length of day and night are about equal. (“Equinox” means “equal night.”) Thousands of Iranians around the world celebrate Nowruz at the same instant, but at different times of the day in each time zone. Since it takes 365 days for the earth to circle the sun, the time of the equinox moves by about six hours — a quarter day — every year.

Some families lay the mirror flat on the table and place an egg on it. They watch at the moment of equinox to see if the egg moves. It used to be said the earth rests on one horn of the bull Taurus, one of the twelve constellations of the zodiac. The bull becomes tired of his burden and once a year shifts the earth to his other horn. This creates a jolt that may make the egg roll on the mirror.

At the moment of equinox you sing out Aid-e-shoma mobarak ! (Congratulations on your holiday!) and Sal-e-noh mobarak ! (Happy New Year!) You jump up to hug and kiss everyone. Older members of the family give a coin to younger ones. In the past, before the Iranian Revolution, this was a gold coin with a picture of the shah on it; such coins are now rare and very expensive. In the United States many banks at this season have rolls of new quarters or silver dollars that have been gold-washed. You can buy these to give to friends and family at Nowruz. It is said that as long as you keep the coin, your purse or wallet will never be empty — which is true! Other gifts are also exchanged, especially for children.

Dinner on the first day of the new year is a rice pilaf with dill and lima beans, served with fried whitefish. The meal also may include chicken and lamb kabobs, salad, yogurt with chopped cucumber, mint, and spring onions, and many other tasty Persian dishes.

Everyone tries to spend the final, thirteenth day of Nowruz out of doors in a natural setting like a park. This event is called Sizdah Bedar , meaning Thirteen Outside. The number thirteen is unlucky in Iran, so everyone goes on a picnic to keep the bad luck outside the house. You take with you the bowl of sabzi, the green sprouts, from the Haft Seen. By now the sprouts are tall and probably turning yellow because, it is said, they have received all the bad luck in the house. Traditionally, they would be placed in a flowing stream or river to be carried away, but today many families just put the sabzi in the trash.

Nowruz is a time of peace and joy, when everyone can celebrate the end of winter and the coming of spring. This Nowruz, why not join the New Year festivities by serving tea and sweets to your family and friends? And don’t forget to wish them a Happy New Year in spring!

articles.title: Mary Had a Little Turkey
articles.author: Greta Burroughs
articles.description: Greta Burroughs discusses the history of Thanksgiving and similar holidays around the world.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Greta Burroughs discusses how Thanksgiving came to be celebrated as a holiday.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the main ideas of the article.
articles.permissions: "Mary Had a Little Turkey" by Greta Burroughs, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: When you think about Thanksgiving, what’s the first thing that comes to mind: food, football, parades? How about lambs?

What do lambs have to do with Thanksgiving? The connection between the two is the poem “Mary Had a Little Lamb,” which was written by Sarah Josepha Hale. She’s the lady who persuaded Abraham Lincoln to proclaim Thanksgiving a holiday.

Today, most people are familiar with Thanksgiving. Kids get off school and many grownups get off work to spend the day as they wish, whether feasting on turkey or a large pepperoni pizza with extra cheese.

However, back in the 1800s, Thanksgiving was not widely celebrated. Some people living in farming communities observed harvest festivals, while other folks had small family gatherings at various times of the year. Several northern states, including New Hampshire where Hale grew up, observed Thanksgiving every year but celebrated it on different days.

Hale envisioned a holiday set on a specific date that would promote a sense of community and shared experience. She hoped it would help ease the tensions caused by the ongoing Civil War. So began Hale’s campaign to make Thanksgiving an annual national celebration.

Hale used her talent as a writer to get others excited about Thanksgiving. She drew upon her childhood memories of celebrating Thanksgiving in her 1827 book Northwood; A Tale of New England . In this book, Hale stated there should be one day a year set aside to show our joyful gratitude for all our blessings.

She also wrote magazine articles, stories, and poetry about folks enjoying big meals. She included recipes for roasted turkey, pumpkin pie, and other goodies that many now enjoy eating on Thanksgiving.

Hale penned letters to senators, governors, presidents, and anyone else she could think of. It took thirty-six years and a lot of writing, but in 1863, President Lincoln finally passed the proclamation declaring the last Thursday in November as a “Day of Thanksgiving and Praise.”

Hale’s holiday dream came true, but there was one glitch. Some years November has five Thursdays, instead of four. That caused problems when some people wanted to celebrate on the fourth Thursday, while others preferred to wait the extra week. The disagreements increased during the 1920s, when large cities, such as Philadelphia and New York City, held their first big Thanksgiving Day parades, and in 1934, when the National Football League played its first Thanksgiving game.

President Franklin D. Roosevelt put an end to the date debate when he signed a bill in 1941 setting the date for Thanksgiving. Anyone wishing to observe the holiday could celebrate it on the fourth Thursday of November.

That’s how Thanksgiving became the family feasting tradition the United States has today. It’s all due to Sarah Josepha Hale not giving up on her dream. For all her hard work, Hale is often called the mother of Thanksgiving in America.
Are days set aside to express gratitude for a plentiful fall harvest around the world?
Yes. In Canada, Thanksgiving is celebrated by relaxing and sharing a meal with family on the second Monday in October.
In England, the Harvest Festival takes place in late September or early October with special services at local churches to give thanks for a successful crop yield.

The Chinese celebration of giving thanks is called the Mid-Autumn Festival. It is held during the eighth month of the lunar calendar. Families spend the evening gazing at the moon and honoring the moon goddess. They eat mooncakes, baked treats with sweet or rich filling.

In Germany, Erntedankfest is a harvest festival often celebrated on the first Sunday of October to give thanks for a good year and good fortune. Harvest crowns made of grain, flowers, and fruit are paraded to churches or presented to the Erntekönigin , the honorary harvest queen.

People in Japan celebrate hard work and being involved with the community during the Kinrō Kansha no Hi holiday on November 23. Students make cards and gifts to thank hospital staff, firefighters, and other workers who help their community.

In Liberia, the west African country settled by formerly enslaved people from the United States, baskets of fruits like bananas, papayas, mangoes, and pineapples are brought to church on the first Thursday of November. After the Thanksgiving service, the fruit is auctioned off.

A person can go to any part of the world and find a celebration to give thanks. Holidays go by different names and may be celebrated in different ways, but they’re always a special time to get together with family and friends.
How do you express gratitude?

options.label: The holiday of Thanksgiving was important in the ending of America's Civil War.

articles.title: The Chinese Zodiac
articles.subtitle: The Rat Race
articles.author: Sarah Novak
articles.description: The Lunar New Year celebrates a new year in the 12-year cycle of the Chinese zodiac.
articles.intro: A tradition is a practice that is passed down over many years. In this informational text, Sarah Novak teaches readers about the tradition of the Chinese zodiac.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the main ideas of each section of the text.
articles.permissions: "The Chinese Zodiac" by Sarah Novak, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: A line of animals had gathered on the riverbank — a pig, a dog, a rooster, a monkey, a goat, a horse, a snake, a dragon, a rabbit, a tiger, an ox, and a rat. “How will we get across the river to the Jade Emperor?” they asked each other. “He invited us to race to his Heavenly Palace!” “How will I get to the other side first?” thought the crafty rat. The powerful ox, confident in his strength, plunged in and began to swim across. The others followed, except for the rat. He had already climbed up on the ox’s back for a free ride. As soon as the ox arrived at the opposite bank, the rat leaped off and scampered to the Jade Emperor, first in line. The ox was second, followed by the other animals one by one, with the slow pig last. But where was the cat? He had missed the race entirely. He was still napping by the warm stove because the rat had forgotten to wake him as promised. Or did that rat really forget? That is why the cat and the rat are enemies, and why there are 12 animals in the Chinese zodiac, with the rat first and the pig last.

That paper placemat under your bowl of wonton soup at the Chinese restaurant probably has pictures of those 12 animals, along with some dates and descriptions. They are the Zhongguo shuxiang , or Chinese zodiac. Unlike the Western zodiac and its constellations, the Chinese zodiac is based on an ancient calendar. However, this calendar’s cycle includes 12 years, not 12 months. Each year in a cycle is named for one of the zodiac animals, and the animal is said to “rule” that year. For example, the year 2016 was the Year of the Monkey, and 2017 was the Year of the Rooster.
Happy New Year, Chinese Style

The traditional new year in China officially begins not on January 1, but on the first day of the Lunar New Year, a date that varies from year to year. A lunar year is measured by the recurring phases of the moon. According to Chinese reckoning, the new year begins at the second new moon after the winter solstice (the shortest day of the year). The exact day varies, but always falls between late January and the middle of February. This date marks the beginning of spring and is celebrated with parades, feasts, and fireworks as the most important holiday in China and for Chinese communities worldwide. Many people, however, when figuring out their Chinese zodiac animal sign simplify the process by using the corresponding Western year that begins in January.
Mysterious Origins

Other than the folktale version, no one knows exactly how the selection and order of the zodiac animals began. Some scholars think they may have originated with ancient Chinese clans that chose an animal they hunted, farmed with, or worshiped to identify their tribe. According to tradition, the legendary Yellow Emperor established a zodiac system, along with bestowing fire, medicine, weapons, and agriculture on his people. Adding up the years since his reign (said to be 2697-2597 B.C.) makes the Western year 2016 equivalent to Lunar Year 4714.
Tombs and Travelers

Tomb paintings dating from the 6th century B.C. depict the zodiac animals, and a pottery set fashioned in the shape of figures with animal heads and human bodies was discovered in a tomb dating to the Tang dynasty (A.D. 618-90). Some historians suggest that the concept of the animal zodiac was not originally Chinese but was brought by Buddhist monks and other travelers on the Silk Road, the ancient trading routes that connected China with India and the Mediterranean Sea.
Year of Destiny

The Western calendar was established officially in China in 1912 at the end of the Qing dynasty. The old calendar was not abolished officially until 1949, with the creation of the People’s Republic of China. Many Chinese people, however, still enjoy the traditions of their zodiac animal. Every recurring 12th year of a person’s zodiac sign is considered a benming year, or “year of personal destiny.” A 60th birthday — five sets of 12 years — completes the cycle, and is celebrated as a special occasion. In Chinese tradition, the Jade Emperor is the ruler of heaven. A lunar year is an interval of 12 lunar months. The average time between new or full moons — that is, a lunar month — equals 29 days, 12 hours, and 44 minutes. The Yellow Emperor was known as Huangdi. The Chinese honor him as a legendary ruler who brought culture and civilization. Dynasty refers to a family or group holding power for many years. Buddhists are followers of Buddha (563?-483? B.C.), the Indian founder of Buddhism, whose followers seek enlightenment, a blessed state in which a person rises above desire and suffering and attains Nirvana — an ideal condition of rest, harmony, stability, or joy. Oracle refers to a prediction or advice from a divine source. Solar year refers to the period of time it takes the earth to make one complete revolution around the sun — 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes, and 45.51 seconds. Yin and yang are Chinese words for complementary but opposite forces that make up the life force.

options.label: The Chinese zodiac restarts every 12 months.
The Chinese zodiac is based on the constellations.
The Chinese zodiac was invented by the Jade Emperor.
The Chinese zodiac is a tool that people use to mark time.

articles.title: Tết: Celebrating the New Year
articles.author: Christine Graf
articles.description: People observe different traditions to bring good luck for Tết, the Vietnamese new year.
articles.intro: A tradition is a practice that is passed down over many years. In this text, Christine Graf explains the traditions of Tết, the new year in Vietnam, a country in southeastern Asia. The traditions of Tết include many superstitions, which are beliefs not based on fact.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Tết superstitions.
articles.permissions: "Tết: Celebrating the New Year" by Christine Graf, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Tết takes place sometime between the end of January and the middle of February and usually lasts for half the month. It is the most important holiday of the year and a time for all Vietnamese people to return to their home villages to share peace and love with one another.

Tết preparations begin weeks before the holiday arrives. Houses are cleaned and painted, new clothes are purchased, debts are paid, disputes are settled, and food is prepared. Shoppers crowd the streets and purchase food, merchandise, flowers, and small fruit trees. Flowers are believed to make homes more welcoming to good spirits, and fruit trees symbolize fruitfulness in the coming year.

The Vietnamese people are superstitious and are concerned about keeping evil spirits away during Tết. They believe that the three Kitchen Gods, the good spirits that protect their households from evil, return to heaven several days before Tết to report to the Jade Emperor on the family’s activities from the prior year. Ceremonies are held on the 23rd day of the 12th month to say goodbye to the gods. They will return again at Tết.

Cay Neu poles are placed in front of homes to provide protection from evil spirits while the Kitchen Gods are away. These bamboo poles have been stripped of most of their leaves, and their tops are wrapped with red paper — red symbolizes good luck and happiness, and is believed to scare away evil spirits. Poles are decorated with objects that are intended to attract good spirits, and the poles are removed when Tết is over.

On the day before Tết, stores are closed, streets are empty, and families are gathered in their homes. At midnight, firecrackers are set off and drums and gongs are sounded at local temples to mark the beginning of the holiday and to welcome back the Kitchen Gods.

Food is an important part of the holiday, and families celebrate with special meals that include banh chung — rice cooked in sweet coconut milk, filled with pork and beans, and boiled in a banana leaf. Legend says that banh chung was created thousands of years ago by the daughter of King Hung. Because she could not afford a gift to honor her father during the New Year celebration, she created the special food for him instead. Today, eating banh chung is a way for the Vietnamese people to express gratitude to their ancestors. Other holiday foods include dried fruit and vegetable candies, sweetened lotus seeds, and pickled pork snacks.

During Tết, children receive “lucky money” in red envelopes in hopes of bringing them good fortune. Some Vietnamese people do not acknowledge individual birthdays and consider everyone to be a year older on the first morning of Tết.
It is believed that whatever happens during Tết affects the person’s luck for the entire year ahead. The first visitor to a home during the New Year can bring good or bad luck to a family.

There are many other superstitions associated with the holiday. For example, homes should not be cleaned during Tết because the good luck could be swept out. Bad luck can also be avoided by not working, fighting, arguing, talking negatively about people, lending anything, breaking anything, or digging in the ground.

Everyone is on his or her best behavior during Tết, and the brightly decorated streets are a place for celebration. There are many festive parades that include dragon dances — acrobatic dances performed by dragon-costumed dancers on the third day of the new year. In Vietnam, dragons symbolize blessings and prosperity, and the dragon dance is considered to be lucky.
At the end of Tết, the Vietnamese people hope that the New Year will bring them good luck. Thoughts of Tết will never be far from their minds, and they will anxiously await next year’s celebration.

options.label: Banh chung is a rice dish invented by the king to celebrate Tết.
People try to bring good luck on Tết in many different ways.
On Tết people clean out their homes to sweep out evil.
Tết is celebrated on the 23rd day of the 12th month.
