articles.title: A Salute to Memorial Day
articles.author: Brian S. McGrath
articles.description: Brian S. McGrath explains the history behind the United States' annual Memorial Day celebration.
articles.intro: Memorial Day is celebrated on the last Monday in May. In this informational text, author Brian S. McGrath explains the history behind the United States' yearly Memorial Day holiday.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Memorial Day is celebrated.
articles.permissions: "A Salute to Memorial Day" by Brian S. McGrath ©TIME For Kids. All rights reserved. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: On Memorial Day weekend, families across the United States enjoy warm weather, backyard barbecues, maybe even a trip to the beach. The school year may not be over, but it sure feels like summer.

Yet Memorial Day is not just a time for fun. The national holiday began with a solemn purpose: to remember those who died while serving in the U.S. military. Over time, it took on a more celebratory tone. How did this happen?

Starting in the mid-1800s, Memorial Day was observed on May 30. It was known as Decoration Day. Its purpose was to remember the 620,000 people killed in the Civil War, which ended in 1865. The first-ever memorial service at Arlington National Cemetery, in Virginia, took place on May 30, 1868. Around 5,000 people were there. Ohio congressman and future U.S. president James Garfield delivered a speech. The soldiers gave their lives “for love of country,” he said, “and made immortal their patriotism and their virtue.” Visitors decorated soldiers’ graves with flags and flowers.

The serious mood didn’t last long. In 1873, the state of New York declared Decoration Day a day off from work. Businesses were closed. This allowed families to take trips to the beach. Sports fans could go to afternoon games. Soon, other states gave workers the day off too.
Not everyone was happy about the change. Some worried Memorial Day would lose its meaning. In 1887, President Grover Cleveland made headlines after he was said to have spent the day fishing.

In 1889, Congress made May 30 a national holiday. As the years passed, fewer American families had ties to the military. A big shift came in 1971, when Congress put the Uniform Holiday Act into effect. The act designated Memorial Day as the last Monday in May instead of May 30. That created a three-day weekend — a mini-vacation. It put people in mind of the upcoming summer. It also put them in the mood to spend. Businesses offered sales to attract shoppers.

But some Americans have not forgotten the original meaning of Memorial Day. Many Gold Star families still visit a cemetery to pay respects to service members who sacrificed their lives. Krissy Stewart lost her husband, Marine sergeant David Stewart, to war in Afghanistan. He left behind two children, McKenzie and Marshall.

“Memorial Day for Gold Star families is every day,” Stewart says, referring to families who’ve lost a relative in war. “But I’m thankful that we have this day on the calendar. I hope that through our family’s loss, people will pause and reflect that freedom is not free.”

options.label: The Civil War was a time of great loss for the United States.
The way Memorial Day is celebrated has changed over time.
Memorial Day is important because it helps businesses make money.
Holidays to honor soldiers who died in war can be found throughout history.

articles.title: The Great Woman at the Bottom of the Sea
articles.author: Kyra Teis
articles.description: A shaman asks a spirit for help to assist their people.
articles.intro: Kyra Teis writes and illustrates children's books and graphic novels. A shaman is a religious or spirit expert in an indigenous community who serves as a healer, tribal elder, and keeper of cultural customs. In this story, a shaman wants to save his community.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why the shaman approaches the spirit.
articles.permissions: "The Great Woman at the Bottom of the Sea" by Kyra Teis, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. blocks.text: In the far north live a group of native people who call themselves Inuit, or “the People.” For hundreds of years, their ancestors occupied the land of ice, snow, and sub-zero temperatures that stretches from Russia to Greenland. Before the coming of modern technology, life in such a climate was dangerous and unpredictable. People’s well-being was tied to the availability of game, upon which they depended for subsistence, and the weather, which could disrupt a whole hunting season, leaving the Inuit in danger of starvation, sickness, and death. The world of the Inuit was harsh, and they believed that it was inhabited by supernatural beings of many kinds, some friendly, some evil, and some indifferent to the activities of people. There were personal “helper” spirits who accompanied and protected individuals, spirits of dead ancestors, animal spirits, and spirits in rocks, sleds, rope, kayaks, and even in names. There were also spirits with more far-reaching influence, including Sedna, the Great Woman at the Bottom of the Sea . Her magical transformation from human to spirit explains the origin of seals and reveals the code of ethics by which the Inuit lived. Through contact with spirits like Sedna, the Inuit had a hand in determining their fate in a precarious world. Many tales and legends tell of this most important, though delicate, relationship.

The first one awake, I stand outside my snow house and turn my face to the east. I raise my hands to the sunrise toward the streaks of gray in the black sky. Toward Sila, spirit of the air and of breath.
“I am awake! I am alive! I greet you with the life-giving light!” I call out to her, taking great gulps of icy winter wind into my lungs. I will need her to help on my journey today.
Today I go to visit Sedna, the Great Woman at the Bottom of the Sea.

I am shaman and I must go. I alone of all my people speak with the spirits who inhabit our world. I speak with them in my dreams and they tell me of the future. I speak with them in my thoughts, and they tell me the secrets I need to know to keep my people safe and warm and to keep our caches full through the long Arctic winters. The spirits tell me where the seals will come up from the watery depths to breathe, when the winter winds will break, or how illness might be cured. I listen to them not with my ears but with my soul which visits them in the places where they hide.
The spirits spoke to me last night. They pulled at my ears, tugged at my hair, pushed my arms and legs so I flopped in my sleeping sack like a doll. Their message was important.
“Your people will starve this winter,” they said. “Have you noticed? The hunting animals have gone from this place! Sedna has stolen them!”
“Your food stores will not last.”
“Your dogs will freeze.”
“Your homes will be destroyed.”
“Be wise!” She is sending bad storms. Dangerous times are coming, and you are not prepared.”
Ordinarily midwinter is a time of great feasting and game playing, but no winter in memory or in story was ever as bad for my people as this!

Except one, a long time ago. That was the winter when Sedna was a human maiden like myself, waiting on this very shore to go on a journey of her own. Like my people, hers were starving. While they crowded onto rafts to head for more abundant hunting grounds, no one heard this orphan fall into the ocean.

“Aiaiai!” she screamed, the icy water biting into her skin. She seized the side of a raft to save herself and cried for help. But no one cared about one more hungry belly. She caught the glance of an old man and called out to him, “Help me!”
He did not answer but instead lifted his axe and brought its cruel blade down on her grasping hands.

As she slipped under the waves forever, a miracle occurred: her severed fingers turned into seals. Newly born, sleek and shiny, they bobbed and dove among the rafts that carried Sedna’s people away from her. The animals watched as people hurried off without looking back or asking after her. The people did not know that the orphan they left to die would become such a powerful spirit watching over everything they did.

Now she lives on the ocean floor in a house like mine and watches us like a great judge, deciding our fate. Will we have enough hunting animals to feed us through the long winter? It is she who will decide. Will we have sufficient caribou hides to keep us warm? It is up to Sedna. Will the wind blow ice? Will storms destroy our homes? Will the sea overturn our boats? Sedna is the one who knows. And why should she care after what she suffered?

Our wrongdoings collect like dirt in her hair. When we tell lies, cheat our friends, break the rules our ancestors established long ago, our offenses nearly suffocate her. They hang in tangles, greasy and black, for she cannot groom herself with her fingerless hands. It is no wonder she becomes enraged at us! She broods there, on the floor of the sea, hoarding game animals into the pools that surround her house; seals, bearded seals, walruses, and whales all gather there at her will. They are no use to us there, and she knows it. But what else can she do to make us mend our ways? What else means as much to us as the hunting animals?

I go to Senda today to set right all the bad things we have done. The spirit will speed me to her, making the great tunnel through land and sea to the ocean floor. I will take with me a sacred comb as old as the memory of people, and like my ancestors before, I will run it through her hair, easing out the knots. Gently, as if I were helping my grandmother, I will comb and comb until her hair lies as straight and shiny as my own.
When she is calm and happy again, she will turn to me and give me a wish.

“Great Mother, I ask only for the seals, sprung from your hands, to return to the shores of my camp and that my father may have caribou to hunt and my mother may have hides to sew. Spirit of the Deep, I ask only that you give us life.”
These magic words I will sing, and Sedna will turn misfortune away from us.
I go now to pay a visit to the Great Woman at the Bottom of the Sea.

options.label: "'Have you noticed? The hunting animals have gone from this place!'" (Paragraph 6)
"Sedna is the one who knows. And why should she care after what she suffered." (Paragraph 16)
"When we tell lies, cheat our friends, break the rules our ancestors established long ago, our offenses nearly suffocate her." (Paragraph 17)
"These magic words I will sing, and Sedna will turn misfortune away from us." (Paragraph 21)

articles.title: Meet Your Mighty Microbes
articles.author: Nicola Davies
articles.description: Nicola Davies explains how microbes keep the human body healthy.
articles.intro: Microbes are tiny life forms that can only be seen with a microscope, a special tool with a lens to make very small objects larger so that they can be seen by the eye. In this informational text, Nicola Davies explains what microbes do for your body.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the jobs that microbes do for your body.
articles.permissions: "Meet Your Mighty Microbes" by Nicola Davies, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Think you’re alone reading this article? Think again. Everywhere you go, a hundred trillion living things go with you.
They may be too small to see — thousands could fit into the size of this dot. But they are all over you, inside and out. They outnumber the cells of your body by 10 to 1.
You Are Habitat

Most are bacteria. Some are fungi, like molds and yeasts. And some are viruses, so tiny and so simple that they lie on the boundary between living and not living. All of these tiny organisms are much, much smaller than your body’s own cells. Together, they make up what is called your microbiome, a complex web of life that lives in, on, and with you. Scientists are only just beginning to understand how it works. But one thing is clear: unlike the bad germs that can cause diseases, your microbiome works hard to keep you healthy. In fact, you couldn’t live without it.

From the moment you were born, microbes began to make you their home. For them, your body is a huge world with different habitats, from the dry deserts of your arms to the moist rainforest of your gut. Each part of your body has its own group of species adapted to live there, each with a role to play in your health.
The Home Guard

Your skin is your body’s largest organ. It protects your delicate insides. And it has help! Almost 200 different kinds of bacteria, plus fungi and viruses, naturally live on human skin. Because the skin is always picking up new microbes from the air, the mix varies over time and from person to person. But like a tourist town, the native skin residents stay around, while visitors come and go. Your regular skin microbes help protect you from infection, like an invisible power suit. Your skin is their home, so they guard it from harmful germs that might make you sick. One skin resident, Staphylococcus epidermidis , can even alert your body’s own anti-disease defenses (called the immune system) when it senses harmful microbes. Then it helps your immune cells to kill the invaders.

Each region of your skin has its own special mix of microbe species. The bacterium Propionibacterium acnes , for example, loves faces. It eats the oily goo that oozes from pores (the tiny holes that cover your skin). It was once blamed for causing pimples. But now scientists have found that at least one kind of acne bacterium helps to keep skin healthy and spot free. Other skin microbes make a natural lotion to help your skin stay soft and supple.
Mouth Islands

Wet, warm, and regularly supplied with food, your mouth is another great place for microbes. So it’s no surprise that hundreds of different kinds live there. Though usually only around 200 kinds live in each mouth at a time. Every tooth can have slightly different microbes, like the different animals on ocean islands.

Many mouth microbes are out to feast on the sugars in your food. They produce acid as waste. This acid eats away at tooth enamel, making cavities. Some kinds that live on tongues or between teeth make stinky gases that cause bad breath. But other mouth residents, such as the bacterium Streptococcus salivarius , come to the rescue, killing the harmful, stinky bacteria.
Microbe City

The center of microbiome operations for your body is the last part of your intestine, the colon. This long, squishy tube is packed with microbes, constantly dividing to make more to replace those that have died. Half your poop is made of millions of their microscopic dead bodies.
Up to 1,000 different kinds of bacteria are found here. But a home team of just 30 or 40 different species makes up 90% of your gut microbiome.

The list of jobs these microbes do is huge! They digest your food. They make vitamins. They send information to your body to help it run right. And best of all, they protect their turf (your gut) against harmful invaders that can make you sick.

In the huge microbe city of your colon, billions of microbes compete for food and space. The best competitors are the helpful, regular gut residents. When this community of good microbes is strong, harmful microbes just don’t stand a chance.

Your guts also hold lots of immune cells, keeping watch. Gut microbes constantly talk to this immune system. This helps the body to know when it should send in germ-killing cells and when it should calm down.

When good gut bacteria are weak, baddies can take over and make people very sick. Doctors have discovered that one surprising way to fight some stubborn gut infections is with a dose of good gut bacteria from a healthy person. The new good microbes move in and out-compete the killer microbes, curing the infection.
One Big Happy Family
Does it make you feel squirmy to think of yourself as a habitat for microbes? It shouldn’t.

Our microbiomes are a living part of us. And doctors are discovering that many health problems, including diabetes, obesity, allergies, and even bad moods can happen when the microbiome gets out of balance. But if you look after your microbes, they will keep you healthy. So stand up and say it proudly — We are here!

options.label: Microbes do more harm than good.
All humans have the same microbiome.
Humans should take care of their microbiomes.
No one knows how important microbes are to us. E. Microbes live all over our body and keep us healthy. F. The more microbes you have, the healthier you will be.

articles.title: Two-Thirty Crossing
articles.author: Leslie J. Wyatt
articles.description: A young boy tries to save his father's life.
articles.intro: This story takes place in the 1800s, a time when many settlers, people who settle in a new country, moved to the American West in search of land and opportunity. The push westward was fueled by the Homestead Act, which gave settlers 160 acres of land to live and work on for five years and eventually own. While the Homestead Act gave settlers an opportunity for a new life, Native Americans lost their homes and were forced to move elsewhere. In this story, a young boy risks his life to save his father.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how John helps his father.
articles.permissions: "Two-Thirty Crossing" by Leslie J. Wyatt, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Sleet pounded against the windowpane of the Nebraska soddy while John Maxwell’s father lay in his bed by the wall. His throat sounded terribly raw as he coughed from deep in his lungs. John covered his ears to escape the sounds. Each day Papa grew worse. Now he was burning with fever, his blue eyes clouded and confused. Fear crept up John’s back with icy fingers.

It was just he and Papa here on their homestead claim. The nearest neighbor lived across four miles of rolling prairie, while the town of Grand Island, Nebraska, lay fully ten miles southwest of the Maxwell homestead. How could he ever get word to the doctor there without leaving Papa alone? Mama would know what to do.

But Mama and the girls wouldn’t join them until the following spring of 1869. That had sounded like such a great plan when they’d all been together in their little house back in St. Louis, Missouri. John shivered. Then it had seemed so easy to homestead — just live on the land and grow crops for five years to become its owners. But the question chipped away at the back of John’s mind — could grown men die from cough and fever? What good would free land be to them without Papa?
The feverish man’s breath rattled in his chest. He muttered strange phrases and thrashed as if to drive back his terrible sickness.
“Papa!” John knelt by the bed. “Can you hear me?”
But Mr. Maxwell only moaned.

The ticking of the little mantel clock they had brought from back home pounded in John’s ears. Mechanically he noted the time: five after two. In less than half an hour, the afternoon train to Grand Island would roar through the crossing more than three miles away.

Then an idea pierced his fear-clouded mind. John jerked on his Mackinaw and grabbed his cowboy hat off the table. Papa had given the hat to him that past summer when he had turned twelve. “A man needs a good hat in this country,” his father had said. John swallowed around the lump in his throat. Back then the gift had made him happy. Today it was small comfort.

Turning to his bed, John yanked the white case from his pillow and tucked it inside his shirt. Then, stuffing the matchbox in his pocket, he snatched the kerosene lantern from its hook and dashed out the door without looking back.

Icy rain hit him with a cruel fist. The whole world seemed gray and dripping as he sprinted for the sod barn, and the wide sweep of surrounding prairie seemed cold and forbidding. Once inside the low building, the familiar bulk of their milk cow calmed him somewhat, and the two plow horses looked up with mild surprise. For a moment John stared at their huge heads and bulging muscles. Papa had been plowing in the October cold when he had started to cough. By the time he had finished breaking enough ground to plant a crop next spring, he could hardly walk.
“Got ’er done, John,” he had rasped. “Come spring, you an’ me’ll plant our first crop.”

Papa hadn’t been up since. A knife of fear twisted in John’s belly. He turned from the workhorses and made his way past the animals to where the Maxwells’ saddle horse stood. She was a game little black mare he and Papa had named Ozark after the low, wooded mountains of southern Missouri. Conscious of swiftly flying time, John grabbed a bridle and slipped the bit between Ozark’s teeth. Smoothing the saddle blanket onto her back, he heaved the saddle on and cinched it tight.

Next John tied the lantern to his belt. Then he led the horse from the quiet of the barn into the cruelty of the sleety afternoon. Dancing, Ozark laid her ears back as John got a foot in the stirrup and threw himself up into the saddle. The lantern banged awkwardly against his thigh, but the pillowcase was a comforting warmth against his belly.

John threw a glance over his shoulder. The little soddy where his father lay seemed so lost and helpless as it perched on the small knoll of their homestead — like an island in the midst of an infinite ocean of brown grassland. Turning from the sight, John took a deep breath to steady his hands and clapped his heels into the mare’s flanks. She leaped ahead like a rock from a slingshot, and he leaned forward to put more of his weight over her powerful shoulders, helping her the only way he knew how.

The railroad tracks lay an eternity beyond him. Normally John loved to wander over the long, slow roll of the Nebraska plains, following the brush-and-tree-lined coulees to where the vast land met the broadness of the sky. Now, instead of exciting him to see how the land stretched forever, it filled him with despair.

The tough, brown grass of the prairie seemed to drag at Ozark’s pounding hoofs. How could a horse’s thin legs hope to cover the expanse in time? If the great, black engine steamed past as they raced toward it, what would he do then? Tomorrow might be too late for Papa. John thudded his heels into the mare’s flanks again, and she flattened out like a racehorse, pitting muscle and bone against a cold, iron monster.

Never had the prairie seemed so infinite. Never had time seemed to fly on such swift wings. The straining horse beneath him lathered and steamed while the frozen rain cut at John’s face. Then ahead and to his left came the whistle of the train! It would soon be at the crossing. John nearly lost a stirrup as he prodded the mare to an ounce more speed. He had to make it. He just had to.

Moments later, Ozark pounded up to the crossing as if a pack of wolves were at her heels. John threw himself from the saddle, glad the mare had been trained to stand still when her reins were dropped. He stumbled the last few feet, willing himself not to watch the locomotive bearing down upon him, no more than a mile away. Kneeling by the tracks, he fumbled with his matches and lantern. Again the whistle blew — a blaring trumpet in his ears.
Once, twice, the matches sputtered in the sleety drizzle. The third one caught for a moment but fizzled out as he touched it to the lantern’s wick.

Toooooo WHOOOOOOO . Too close. John dropped the lantern. Jerking the pillowcase from under his shirt, he jumped between the rails and whipped the cloth back and forth with all his strength. Would the engineer see this little white blur in the gray afternoon? In the back of his mind, he recalled Papa saying it could take a train more than half a mile to slow down and stop.
Half a mile? The train loomed closer and closer, and John couldn’t tell if it was slowing at all.

WHOOooo WOOOOOO . The giant eye of the headlight cut through the sleet. John stood his ground, a rabbit in the path of a great predator. If he couldn’t stop the train, no doctor would come, and Papa might die. John could think no further than that.
He whipped off his hat and waved it, too — hoping, praying. “Stop! Please, stop! Don’t you see meeeeeeee?” he screamed as the locomotive barreled down upon him. One last wave. One last plea. “STOP!”

He leaped from between the rails and hit the ground hard before rolling away from the tracks. Moments later he felt the suck of the engine and caught a glimpse of the angry, red face of the engineer. John’s panting was loud in his ears as the hissing monster screeched its way to a stop nearly a quarter of a mile beyond him.

In a daze, John stuffed his pillowcase back under his shirt, gathered up his lantern and Ozark’s reins, then tottered on suddenly weak legs to where the locomotive had halted. His horse stayed by his side. The engineer leaped from the cab and rushed toward him.
“You crazy boy!” the man bellowed. “You could’a got yerself killed right there! More than one man has got crushed flaggin’ a train like that.”
John tried not to burst into tears. “P-please, sir. I’m sorry, sir,” he stammered. “I just had to stop you — my papa’s real sick. Would you take word to the doctor in Grand Island for me?”
The engineer’s voice was suddenly gentle. “What’s wrong with ’im?”
John tried to keep his voice from shaking. “He’s coughing real bad, talking crazy, burning with fever.”
“Could be pneumonia.” The burly man cleared his throat. “Where do you live, boy?”
“About three miles northeast of here — in a soddy on a small knoll.”

“I’ll tell you what. When we hit town, I’ll start Doc Matthews in that direction. He should be to your place before dark.” The man turned to go, then looked back and said, “Hope your pa will be all right, son.”
The lump of tears in John’s throat was too big to let words squeeze by, so he nodded and tried to smile.

Moments later, the train pulled away like a massive serpent. Twin streaks of warmth ran down John’s icy cheeks, but he smiled into the sleety Nebraska wind as he raised his hat in a salute to the disappearing train. Then he mounted Ozark and turned the tired, black mare back toward the sod house to find that his father was more ill than before.
Doc Matthews arrived on horseback as the gray daylight faded to grayer twilight.
“Well, son, he’s mighty sick,” the silver-haired man said. “It’s pneumonia, all right.”
John stared at his hat, now hanging next to his father’s on the post in the center of the room. “But, Doctor,” John said, swallowing hard, “will he —?”
The doctor’s hand was warm on John’s shoulder. “That’s in the good Lord’s hands. All I know is that tonight is critical. If we can get him through till morning, he should be on the mend.”

Papa thrashed and moaned worse than ever, wild-eyed and racked with deep coughing. Doc Matthews rolled up his sleeves. “I’m going to need some hot water, John, and a couple of extra blankets. And how are you at making coffee?”
“I’ve watched Papa a lot,” John said, glad to have something — anything — to do that might help.
As he stirred up the fire in the cook-stove, he tried not to think of all the mornings Papa had smiled at him across the rim of his cup.
“Here’s the water, sir.” John watched as the doctor rummaged in his black bag. “I’ll just set your coffee here on the table. Can I get you anything else?”
“Nothing right now, son. Thanks.” Doc spread the blankets over the sick man. “I’ll let you know if I need anything later.”
John vowed he would stay awake to help, but the next thing he knew, the morning sun streamed across his face. Doc Matthews drowsed in a chair near Papa’s bed.

The sour taste of fear flooded John’s mouth as he crossed to the quiet form on the other bed. The moaning was over, the restless hands still. “Oh, Papa,” John whispered, reaching down and stroking his father’s work-worn hand. “We were going to plant our land, come spring.”
The hand beneath John’s stirred.
“And so we shall, son.” Papa’s voice was very weak, but as he gazed up at John, his eyes were as clear and blue as the Nebraska sky. “So we shall.”

articles.title: Beetles with Brawn
articles.author: Melissa Stewart
articles.description: Melissa Stewart describes the strength of rhinoceros beetles.
articles.intro: Rhinoceros beetles are brawny, or strong, insects. In this informational text, Melissa Stewart explores what these interesting beetles do.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes the rhinoceros beetle special.
articles.permissions: "Beetles with Brawn" by Melissa Stewart, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: As dusk falls in a Brazilian rainforest, a male rhinoceros beetle crawls out of a hole and takes a quick look around. No enemies in sight.
Pushing forward with his huge horn, the beetle plows a path across the forest floor. He’s searching for food — and a good nesting spot for his mate.
The beetle stops at an old tree stump. It’s prime real estate — the perfect place for a female beetle to lay eggs. When the hungry larvae hatch, they can feast on the rotting wood and grow quickly.

As the male explores his new home base, he’s startled by a noise from behind. He spins around to face the intruder. It’s another male rhino beetle, and he has his eye on the same territory. The insects lock horns and push. The attacker is strong, but not strong enough. He backs away and then scurries into the forest.
Record-Breaking Beetle

Male rhinoceros beetles’ hefty headgear resembles a rhinoceros, giving them their name. But the beetle’s real claim to fame is its super strength. For its size, the male rhinoceros beetle is one of the strongest animals in the world.

An elephant — the largest land animal — can carry about one-fourth of its body weight. Rodger Kram, a scientist at the University of Colorado in Boulder, has proved that a rhinoceros beetle can lift much more.

“I’m one of those people who likes to read The Guinness Book of World Records for fun,” says Kram. As he was flipping through the book’s record-breaking entries, he found one that intrigued him. “It said rhinoceros beetles can support 850 times their own body weight,” recalls Kram. “I found that hard to believe, so I decided to test it.”
How Strong Really?

Kram ordered rhinoceros beetles through the mail. Then he rigged up a Velcro weight strip for the beetles to pull, and tested them with different weights. He found that the beetles can lurch or crawl when carrying 100 times their weight, but only for short distances.

“There may be some species that really can lift 850 times their weight,” says Kram, “but not the ones I worked with.” The scientist tried to find out where the Guinness Book got their number, but wasn’t able to track it down. Nonetheless, he was still impressed with the strength of the brawny beetles in his laboratory. How, he wondered, could they do so much work without collapsing in exhaustion?

To answer that question, Kram let the beetles pull weights on a tiny treadmill, at different speeds. He found that at a walking pace, the insects could haul 30 times their own weight for 30 minutes before slowing. That would be like an eight-year-old carrying two cows for half an hour — without getting tired!

Next, Kram placed a box over a beetle and attached a small suction pump. Using this setup, he measured the amount of oxygen the little insect was inhaling. From this, he calculated how much energy it used as it pulled weights at different speeds.

What did Kram learn? Rhinoceros beetles require much less energy than humans and other animals to move large loads. Think of it like this: a small car can travel 50 miles on the energy it gets from 1 gallon of gas. But a truck might need 3 gallons of gas to go the same distance. Like a small car, the body of a rhinoceros beetle is more efficient than the bodies of other animals. As a result, it can survive on very little food. That’s good news for the hungry rhino beetle that just fought off a rival.

Before he has a chance to gorge on rotting fruit, however, he senses more trouble. As a bat swoops out of the sky, the beetle ducks for cover. Using his super strength, he quickly burrows underground and disappears from sight. Phew! That was a close call.

articles.author: Time for Kids
articles.description: Students weigh in on the debate over school uniforms.
articles.intro: School uniforms are a set of clothing that students must wear every day when they go to class. In this TIME for Kids article, two students share their opinions on school uniforms.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Yuna and Emerson support their opinions about school uniforms.
articles.permissions: " Should Students Wear Uniforms " ©️TIME For Kids. All rights reserved. Reproduced with permission. blocks.text: Some schools have a policy stating that students must wear uniforms to class. Normally associated with private schools, uniforms have become more common in public schools, too. According to the National Center for Education Statistics, uniforms are required at about 22% of U.S. public schools.

Many schools say uniforms make it easier for students to focus in class, and that uniforms promote inclusion. But some students think uniforms are uncomfortable, and that kids should have the freedom to express their personality through their clothing.
Are uniform policies the best way to keep kids focused on learning? Or should students be allowed to wear what they want?
TIME for Kids asked readers to weigh in. Here, two of them share their opinion about whether or not students should be required to wear uniforms at school.
YES!
by Yuna Chong, 9
Santa Clara, California

Uniforms are as much about safety as they are about inclusion. Teachers don’t have to worry about losing sight of students at the playground or on a field trip. Also, when kids wear uniforms, both the older and younger students feel like they are part of the same group. I wear a uniform and feel like my school is one big team. I feel included.

I think it is harder to bully someone about his or her clothes if you are all wearing the same thing. When everyone is dressed the same, you don’t have to worry about how you look or about wearing the latest trend. Parents can save money, as well! And in the morning, I can save time because I don’t have to choose what kind of clothes I should wear.
NO!
by Emerson Sperling, 12
Sterling Heights, Michigan

I don’t think kids should have to wear a uniform. If students are wearing the exact same thing, they can’t express themselves. Kids should feel like they can be their own person. They shouldn’t have to match. During school hours, we should also be comfortable.

If we’re in scratchy shirts and uncomfortable dress pants, we’re not going to focus very well. I get that the teachers don’t want us focusing on everyone else’s clothing, but it’s still uncomfortable to be in a uniform all day. Also, parents have to go shopping and spend a lot of money on the uniforms.
If students could wear normal clothes, we’d be able to express ourselves, be comfortable, and not spend money on outfits that we never wear at home.

options.label: Some students see the benefits of school uniforms, while others do not.
Parents want students to wear school uniforms because they prevent bullying.
Schools are debating whether school uniforms or field trips are more important.
Students want school uniforms because they help them get ready for school more quickly.

discussionQuestions.prompt: After reading the text, do you think schools should have a uniform policy? What evidence or reasoning supports your answer? Do you have school uniforms at your school? If so, do you find them helpful or not? Why? If you don't have school uniforms at your school, do you think having them would be helpful or not? Why?
What advice do you have to either Yuna or Emerson to make their arguments stronger?

Consider the claims that both Yuna and Emerson make in the text. Would it be possible to develop a school uniform policy that both students would agree to? Based on their arguments, what changes to a school's uniform policy might work best for both students?

In the text, Emerson states, "If students are wearing the exact same thing, they can't express themselves. Kids should feel like they can be their own person." What does he mean by this? How can the clothing we wear be a part of our identity? What are some of the other ways people can express themselves?

articles.title: Impromptu Performance
articles.author: S. Q. Eries
articles.description: A student finds the courage to take action on stage after a setback nearly ruins the performance.
articles.intro: In this short story, a student gives an impromptu, or unplanned, performance in his school play.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the narrator feels about his acting skills.
articles.permissions: "Impromptu Performance " by S. Q. Eries (Melissa Ng), Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. blocks.text: Some people think being in the ensemble of a play is boring. I disagree. To me, any chance to stand onstage is a thrill. And sometimes ensemble members do cool stuff. For instance, in Mayflower Middle School’s upcoming production of The Legendary Cowherd , eight of us play a sixteen-legged dragon, with me bringing up the rear.

As Team Dragon waits for our cue at dress rehearsal, I swing our tail, enjoying the swish of its Mylar streamers. We might be unrecognizable beneath the glitter-draped framework, but it’s still fun bringing a fantastical creature to life.
A shout rings from the stage. “I will defend this realm!”
The stagehand signals us, and Team Dragon files onstage only to be blocked by Vijay, the lead actor. “Halt!” he cries.
Where there’s a dragon, there’s usually a hero to vanquish it, and this play about a peasant who becomes a warrior sticks to that classic storyline.

Music blares, colored lights flash, and the fight begins. While Vijay swings his spear, we circle, zigzag, and serpentine across the stage. Fortunately, the only one who has to remember the complicated choreography is Vijay’s sister Priya at the dragon’s head. The rest of us simply follow.
At last, Vijay — er, the legendary cow-herd — deals the final blow. Our dragon collapses, starting with Priya and dropping one by one until I flop down, legs thrashing.
The music stops. “Nice, Team Dragon,” calls Ms. Park, the theater teacher. “Priya, that was perfect. And Ramon, good energy with the tail. OK, everyone, places for the finale.”

We immediately clear the stage. This next and final scene, where the triumphant hero returns to his village, involves the entire cast, and Team Dragon hurries to hand our framework to three parent volunteers. As we straighten the villager outfits we wore underneath, other actors head onstage. Some move set pieces from the wings as they go. Others bring props, the most impressive of which are five cows. Each is mounted on wheels so a single actor can roll it along.

As Tyler takes his cow’s rope, he eyes his twin, Maddie. “Race you.” I groan. The twins are fun but forever causing trouble. Yesterday, they raced to see who could finish their makeup fastest and spilled powder all over their costumes.
Maddie smirks at her brother. “You’re on —”
“Not with my cows you don’t!”
The siblings flinch as Mr. DeSouza, the props team lead, glowers down. Instantly on best behavior, the pair meekly wheel their cows onstage.
“Yikes,” I mutter, glad I didn’t get Mr. DeSouza’s evil eye.

“They deserved it,” says Priya, tying a kerchief over her rumpled curls. “The props team spent weeks on those cows. It would mess things up for us all if they got ruined right before opening night. Now hurry up, Ramon. The scene’s about to start.”
Rehearsal ends, and after changing, I look for Priya and Vijay. We’re neighbors, so we usually walk home together.

I find Priya in the eighth row of the auditorium. “We’re stuck a while longer, Ramon.” She points to the stage where Vijay stands with a sound tech who fiddles with the tiny mic clipped to his hat. “The crew needs to tweak stuff for the soliloquy.”
“I don’t mind waiting,” I say, sitting beside her. “It’s cool watching the techs work.”

Up until last year, the school’s only stage was a wooden platform in the cafeteria with no stage lights or speakers. Then our auditorium got built. Now we’ve an honest-to-goodness theater with state-of-the-art equipment, and The Legendary Cowherd will be its very first show.

Needless to say, it’s got us all fired up. From the way the leads practice lines between classes to the way Mr. DeSouza’s team fusses over props, everyone, onstage and off, is determined to make the performance a success.
The sound tech finishes and steps aside. “Go ahead, Vijay.”

Vijay launches into his monologue. It takes place right before the dragon battle, and the hero, tempted to run from the impending fight, wrestles with his fears and ultimately scrounges the courage to face the monster.

As the crew adjusts lighting and sound levels, I admire Vijay’s acting. His expressions are perfect. His gestures are perfect. Voice building, he raises his spear and bellows the hero’s signature phrase, “I will defend this realm!” Goosebumps prick my skin. I love that line, and Vijay delivered it flawlessly. No wonder he’s the star.

Priya sniffs. “I think you would’ve been better as the hero, Ramon.” I gape. Priya, Vijay, and I have done theater together for years, but while Vijay’s won several roles and Priya’s had some bit parts, I’ve only ever been in the ensemble. For good reason. I’m terrible at memorizing dialogue. No matter how hard I try, I always end up forgetting a word. Or twenty. “You’re kidding, right? You saw how I completely bombed the audition.”

We boys all auditioned on the soliloquy Vijay just performed, and unlike Vijay who nailed it, I botched it. I started OK, but around the third sentence I got so caught up in the role, I forgot where I was and ended up ad-libbing the rest.
“You didn’t completely bomb it,” says Priya. “You got the first and last lines right.”
I groan. “You’re just making fun of me.”
“I’m not.” Priya leans over the armrest to look me straight in the eye. “There’s more to acting than reciting lines, Ramon.”
“Fine,” I retort, irked by her know-it-all tone. “Explain how my audition was good and Vijay’s wasn’t.”
“I never said his was bad. Vijay’s audition was solid. But yours… you really looked like you were psyching yourself up to fight a dragon.”
“Even though I was just ad-libbing Gibberish?”
“It might’ve been ad lib, but it wasn’t gibberish,” says Priya. “If I didn’t know better, I would’ve thought it was the actual script, it sounded that good.”
I blink. “Thanks.”
“On top of that, you had amazing stage presence. Even from the back of the auditorium, I heard every word.”

“That just means I have a big voice. Which doesn’t matter much with mics,” I say, pointing to the sound techs. I heave a sigh. “Priya, even if what you say is true, that doesn’t mean I deserve a speaking role, let alone the lead. Improvising is fine for acting exercises but not a play. There’s a script for a reason. Without it, everything falls to pieces.”
“I guess,’’ Priya concedes. “Still, that doesn’t change the fact that you’re a good actor, and I know you’ll get to show it someday.”

Opening night is electric. The instant the curtain rises on The Legendary Cowherd , energy charges the entire theater. The crowd’s applause makes the action scenes livelier; their laughs make the funny bits giddier. And when Vijay steps out for his soliloquy, the air grows thick with tension.
At least it does for the audience. Backstage in the left wing, a different drama’s unfolding.
“Whoops,” say the twins.
“My tail!” My eyes bug out at the sight of the dragon’s Mylar streamers snarled in the wheels of a cow.
The rest of Team Dragon levels a death glare at the twins. The two had been horsing around the props and rolled the cow right over our tail.
I give the streamers a tentative tug and shake my head. “They’re really stuck,” I say, getting up to look for help. “Where’s Mr. DeSouza —”
“No!” Tyler shoves me aside to yank at the streamers. “They’ll come loose if you try harder.”
“Yeah,” chimes Maddie, grabbing the Cow.
“Stop that,” I cry, as they strain to pull cow and streamers apart. “You’ll break —”
The streamers snap. The twins fall, losing their grip. The damaged Mylar flutters down. The cow, however, goes flying. Priya lunges to stop it, but she’s too late.
The cow barrels onstage — smack into Vijay! He chokes mid-soliloquy. As he gapes at the prop that interrupted him, panic hits backstage.

Our star’s been literally knocked off script by a wayward cow. Even if Vijay picks up where he left off, there’s no graceful way for him to get the cow off. Worse, the large prop is completely in the way of the upcoming dragon battle.

Those thoughts flash through my mind in a millisecond, and the next instant, I’m darting onstage. As my feet race, so does my brain. A cow crashed into the hero. I’m in a peasant costume. So the reason we’ve burst in is… “A dragon’s coming!”
Suddenly, all eyes are on me. I don’t have a mic, so I can only hope my voice carries as I continue shouting. “Run for your life!”
I grab the cow’s rope. As I start hauling it stage right, Vijay’s mouth flaps like a landed fish. “Wha—?”

“Don’t just stand there!” I face Vijay, willing him to play along. “A monster’s heading this way. It ate my herd, and it’ll eat you if you don’t run!” As I yell, the left wing stagehand hangs on my words, waiting for the right moment to cue Team Dragon.
Vijay’s eyes flash, and he snaps back into character. “I’m not running,” he declares, thumping his spear. “Because I will defend this realm!”

The stagehand signals, and a fourteen-legged dragon with a somewhat raggedy tail charges in. As hero and monster face off, the cow and I exit stage right. Once I’m safely in the wings, my legs turn to rubber, and I crumple to the floor. The music for the fight booms, and below its thundering beat, I laugh, giddy with relief and exhilaration.
The Legendary Cowherd concludes to a standing ovation. The cast bows, and the curtain descends on a successful opening night. From the outside looking in, everything’s gone without a hitch.
The cast and crew know better.
As Mr. DeSouza chews out the twins after curtain call, Ms. Park pats me on the back. “That was an amazing save, Ramon.”
Others cheer in agreement, and my face warms. “I only did what anyone else would’ve done,” I mumble, embarrassed by the praise.
“But not anyone could’ve handled it as well as you,” says Ms. Park. “That wasn’t just quick thinking, that was excellent acting.”
“She’s right,” says Vijay, striding over. “You were awesome.”
Surprise ripples through me. Never in a million years did I think I’d hear that from Vijay. And when he offers me a high-five, I grin so wide my cheeks feel about to split.
As my palm smacks his, I sense Priya smirking in the crowd. No doubt she’s got an I-told-you-so waiting for me. But this is one instance I don’t mind being wrong.

options.label: to be a hero
to disappear
to beat in a battle
to become friends with

articles.title: Sometimes, History is Sadness
articles.author: David White
articles.description: This piece explores the human impact of the tragic historical events of September 11, 2001.
articles.intro: This text discusses the events of September 11, 2001, when a series of terrorist attacks were carried out across the country.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the title connects to the article.
articles.permissions: "Sometimes, History is Sadness" © 2015, David White. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: History is full of beautiful and wonderful things. You can probably think of many, like your first birthday and the day you got your latest favorite thing. People in your family probably remember great days like the day men walked on the moon or the day the Berlin Wall came down. Everyone you know has favorite days, days that they remember happily.
September 11, 2001 was different. On September 11, a great many people died.

You probably already know the details. The big picture is that the World Trade Center, one of the most famous symbols of New York and of the United States, was destroyed. One airplane hit each of the two towers of the World Trade Center. Not long after those crashes, both towers collapsed.The Pentagon, the center of the U.S. Defense Department, was also hit by an airplane, and many people died there, too.
Farther away, another airplane that had been hijacked crashed in Pennsylvania. Everyone aboard was killed.
In total, 2,996 people died and more than 6,000 others were injured.
This is very sad. It’s horrible. It’s terrifying. It makes us all wonder what’s next.
Many people (children and adults) looked up in fear whenever they heard a noise. Was it another airplane? Was it coming for them?

This terrible tragedy affected everyone in different ways. Some people knew someone who was there. Many had family members who were visiting New York or Washington who couldn’t get home. The sadness extended far beyond the city of New York or Washington, D.C.

Sometimes, history is sad. History is full of terrible things, like long wars and terrible natural disasters. History is full of people dying and people being injured. It’s a part of life, just as much a part of life as happiness and great things.

Older people you know can probably remember the explosion of the Space Shuttle Challenger or the disaster at the Chernobyl nuclear plant or the assassination of President John F. Kennedy. Those were terrible, horrible things, and people were terribly afraid for many days after each of those things happened. Now, many years later, we can look back on them and learn from them. Security in the space shuttle program, in nuclear power plants, and around the U.S. president is greater today.

When all is said and done, September 11 was certainly one of the most terrible tragedies in history. But as people listened to the news and talked to their friends and family about this terrible day, it became easier to remember that time can help ease the pain that people feel. People recover, and heal, and the grief of the survivors lessens.
Sometimes, only time lessens pain and grief.

options.label: We are all responsible for improving national security and helping to prevent future disasters.
It is important to make sure one's friends and family members are safe following a tragic event.
Tragic events will always be a part of history, but the pain that they cause may lessen over time.
The World Trade Center was an icon of New York and a symbol of American values before it was destroyed.

questions.prompt: What is the central idea of this passage?

options.label: to emphasize that both happy and sad events are a part of life
to show that children remember more good events than adults do
to show that tragedies can destroy the progress that societies make
to encourage readers to focus on these events in order to live happily

questions.prompt: Why does the author include a discussion of happy events in paragraph 1?

options.label: Like September 11, the events mentioned in paragraph 10 are tragedies that have also caused feelings of fear and sadness.
The author mentions the events in paragraph 10 to show that other tragedies are usually not as horrible as September 11.
The tragedy of September 11 causes the most sadness today because it happened after all of the other horrible events mentioned in paragraph 10.
September 11 and the other tragedies mentioned in paragraph 10 are proof that older people only like to remember and talk about horrible events.

questions.prompt: What connection does the author draw between September 11 and the events mentioned in paragraph 10?

options.label: by explaining how America changed after the September 11 attacks
by stating that grief will never completely go away no matter how much time passes
by describing how many people were scared and wanted to give up after the September 11 attacks
by giving examples of the changes that have made society safer after other tragedies have occurred

questions.prompt: How does the author support the idea that society can learn and grow from tragedies?
How does the title, "Sometimes, History is Sadness" connect to the main idea of the article?

articles.title: The Orb
articles.author: Rebecca Birch
articles.description: In an alternate reality, a young girl refuses to have her future determined by a machine.
articles.intro: Rebecca Birch is a science fiction and fantasy writer who has written for Cricket Magazine. This short story is set in a future where a machine called the Orb determines each young person's future on their twelfth birthday. In this story, a young girl named Margie prepares to enter the Orb.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Margie’s watch is important to the story.
articles.permissions: "The Orb" by Rebecca Birch, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Margie Callahan stood before the Orb, her mother on one side, First Minister Chao on the other. The metal walls gleamed like ice, and everything smelled vaguely of sanitizer. She’d been inside the shiny, silver room before, on a tour with her class when schooling had only just begun, but this was different. This was real.
“Mom, do I really have to go?”

The first minister pressed her tortoiseshell glasses up her nose with one long finger and glanced at Margie’s mom before looking back down and giving Margie a tight smile. “Everyone goes, Margie. You know that. I did when I was your age. So did your mother.”
Mom’s fingers — callused from years of assembling standardized furnishings for schools and childcare centers — trembled in Margie’s grasp. “She knows.”

“There are others waiting their turn,” the first minister said, her lips smiling but an edge of impatience coloring her voice. Every child who had turned twelve in the past month would face the Orb that day.
Margie bit the inside of her cheek. Her mom was scared, but trying to put on a brave front. She could manage at least as much. “I’m ready.”
Her mother exhaled quietly, slipped her hand out of Margie’s, and gave her shoulder a gentle squeeze.

First Minister Chao pressed her palm against a sensor on the Orb’s surface. Rainbow light pulsed to life within, swirling behind the plex-glass door so that purple, blue, and green slid like an oil slick over the first minister’s shiny dark hair and glasses. A low hum shook the floor.
Margie swallowed convulsively.
“You remembered the rules? You didn’t bring anything with you?”

Margie shook her head and hoped nobody could hear her heart pounding. She didn’t want to answer the question out loud, because it would be a lie. The ceremonial jumpsuit they’d given her had no pockets, but she’d tucked one small item in the band of her underwear: her father’s watch. The one thing he’d left behind just for her. He’d made it in his workshop with his own hands, with both their names engraved on the underside. She hadn’t taken it off since the day he’d died. Not until that morning, when her mother made her do it.
Margie had cried, even though at twelve she was too old for tears, but her mother had insisted, not leaving the changing room until the watch lay like a dead thing on the counter.

Her father always told Margie that he’d help her be strong — all she had to do was keep the watch with her always. When her mother had stepped out to let her finish changing in private, the almost inaudible ticking proved too much of a temptation.

Margie had dried her tears and put on the jumper, the watch tucked away where no one would think to look. Her mother had checked her wrist to be sure she hadn’t put it back on again, and been satisfied.
One little watch couldn’t make a difference to the Orb, could it? It was powerful enough to know a person’s destiny. Surely it wouldn’t be swayed by such a small thing.

The first minister knelt down, meeting her eye to eye. “This is the most important day of your life, Margie. You’re going to learn who you’re meant to be.” Her expression hardened. “Now, you’ve heard that sometimes people don’t come back out. Those are people with evil in their hearts. People destined to commit the worst of crimes. The Orb prevents those crimes from happening. But you’re a good girl, right? So there’s nothing to be afraid of.”

The weight of her lie pressed down on Margie’s breastbone, making it hard to breathe. Was she really a good girl? A good girl wouldn’t break the rules. But the steady, almost imperceptible tick-tick-tick against her belly was the only thing making her brave enough to keep from running.
She nodded and tried to smile.
First Minister Chao squeezed her hand, then rose and touched a button on the Orb’s side. The plex-glass door lifted.

The hum intensified, vibrating under Margie’s bare feet. Dancing waves of color washed through the door, turning her white jumpsuit from orange to gold to green. An earthy, damp scent wafted over her.
“Go on,” said the first minister.
Margie forced herself to take a step. Another. One more step and she entered the Orb.

The ground was cold and gave way underfoot, as if she were stepping on molded gelatin. She hesitated, but a soft hiss warned her the door was closing, and she took the last step that brought her fully within.
The door sealed with a thud and Margie spun around to look back, but the plex-glass was opaque from the inside. She could see nothing.
She pushed against the wall. “Mom!”

Her voice vanished quickly, consumed by the Orb. Unlike the exterior with its shiny metal walls, the interior of the Orb felt almost like fabric. The rainbow colors pulsed to a loud heartbeat thrum — da-dum, da-dum .

Margie pressed her palms against her ears and squeezed her eyes shut. It dimmed the sound enough that she could turn her attention instead to the tick-tick-tick of her father’s watch she sensed against her skin. Focusing on the watch’s rhythm, Margie cautiously opened her eyes.

The Orb was bigger on the inside than it should have been, with rounded hallways, which had not been visible on the outside, branching off in all directions. The colors began to coalesce, turning one pathway red, another blue, and on through the spectrum.
Margie inched toward the yellow pathway. Ahead, she heard the sound of a young woman’s laughter. Her own? Was she supposed to follow it?
The blue pathway smelled of the sea. She’d visited it once with her parents when her father was still alive. A good, happy memory.
A shriek echoed down a crimson pathway, followed by the sound of running feet. Margie flinched away.
And behind it all, the heartbeat thrum. Her own heart stuttered, as if trying to match the pulse, but Margie focused again on the watch. Her heart was her own. She wouldn’t let it be overwritten.

More pathways branched from the original six, colors melting into a multitude of hues, and from each pathway, a different sound, a different feeling. Some called to her so loudly she nearly followed without thinking, but the watch anchored her in place, fighting the hypnotic heartbeat.

How long did she have? Was there time to think? What if she chose the path for those who weren’t good girls? Any path could be the wrong one, and who was to say that any of them was right? Her mother, for all her good work in the assembly room, never seemed truly happy. Not the way her father had been.
The laughter down the yellow pathway was tempting, and so was the sea. But why did she have to choose now? Why not wait until she had a better idea of who she was?
Margie sank to her knees and closed her eyes, concentrating only on the watch’s rhythm. Slowly, everything else faded away. No more laughter. No more heartbeat. No more sea.
Only the tick-tick-tick and the rhythm of her own breathing.
She hardly noticed when the plex-glass door slid open, opening her eyes only when First Minister Chao touched her shoulder. “Margie?”
The colors were gone, leaving the Orb a strangely bare white. “Yes?”

The first minister gave her a real smile, the kind that crinkled the corners of her eyes. “Your mother was right. There’s a lot of your father in you.” She offered Margie her hand and helped her to her feet.
“Is that a good thing or a bad thing?”
She stepped out of the Orb and her mother raced toward her, gathering her into an embrace. “Oh, baby girl, that is the best thing. I’m so proud of you.”
Margie blinked, her cheek pressed against her mother’s ribs. “I don’t understand.”
“It means you’ll be your own person, Margie,” the first minister replied. “Now, if your mother doesn’t mind, I need to take you for a short exit processing.”

Margie detached herself from her mother’s arms and followed the first minister into a small office just down the hall. When the door closed, the first minister rested one hand on the mahogany desk and turned her deep black eyes on Margie. “What was it you brought with you?”
“I —” Margie stammered. “I don’t understand.”

“Yes, you do,” the first minister replied, a smile tugging at the corner of her mouth. “The Orb couldn’t make the choice for you, but nobody can resist the Orb without help. Only the bravest break the rule. Like your father, and like me.”
Margie bit her lip and looked down at her feet. “My father’s watch. Before he died, he made me promise to have it with me always.”

“Your father was a good man. His innovations have helped shape the future. He’d have been proud of you, Margie Callahan. I am. The world is a better place when we can make it for ourselves. You will have that chance.”
“So I’m a good girl?”
“Better,” said the first minister. “You’re a strong girl. But you must never tell anyone what you did. Do you understand? Not even your mother.”
Margie nodded, though she wasn’t so sure. If her mother had been able to choose her own path, would she be happier today?
“The Orb is the glue that holds our society together. Everyone trusts its judgment. If they were to begin to doubt it, can you imagine the chaos that would follow?”
Margie nodded again. “I understand, First Minister.”

She would keep the secret, at least until the time she could really understand, one way or the other. The tick-tick-tick of her father’s watch would count the hours, months, and years. And when she was older, when she’d learned what there was to know, then she would decide.
Because she was her own person.
No one could tell her no.

articles.title: Fighting to End Injustice: Thurgood Marshall's Legacy
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Thurgood Marshall fought against segregation and became the first Black Supreme Court justice.
articles.intro: In this biography, India James writes about the life of Thurgood Marshall (1908-1993), a famous U.S. lawyer, judge, and Supreme Court justice.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Thurgood Marshall’s early experiences in law school and as a lawyer.
articles.permissions: "Fighting to End Injustice: Thurgood Marshall's Legacy" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Thurgood Marshall climbed the tall white steps of the U.S. Supreme Court in Washington, D.C., one cold morning in December. He was a lawyer and was there to present a case before the Supreme Court justices.

At the time, Black children went to different schools than white children. Keeping Black and white people separate was called segregation. Schools for Black children were often given fewer books and supplies than those for white children. That meant Black children couldn’t have the same kind of education as white children. Ending segregation is what brought Marshall to the Supreme Court.
Marshall thought this separation was against the laws of the U.S. Constitution. He said that Black children and white children play together, and they should go to school together, too.

“They play in the streets together. They play on their farms together. They go down the road together. They separate to go to school. They come out of school and play ball together,” he said. It didn’t make sense, and segregation harmed Black children’s education.

All nine justices agreed that Marshall was right. They decided that segregation was against the law in a very famous case called Brown v. Board of Education . Marshall saw something wrong, and he used his voice to change it.
Fighting for change
Marshall was born in Baltimore, Maryland on July 2, 1908. It was less than 50 years after slavery had been abolished. He grew up in a time when Black people were not treated the same as white people.

The University of Maryland Law School wouldn’t take Marshall as a student because he was Black. He had good grades, but they wouldn’t let him study with their white students. He went to Howard Law School instead, a historically Black school. While he was there, he learned how making and changing laws could help fight inequality.

Marshall began fighting for change as soon as he graduated law school. He was even part of a case against the University of Maryland. He said that the school could not keep out students because of the color of their skin. He won the case.
“Where you see wrong or inequality or injustice, speak out, because this is your country,” he said. “This is your democracy. Make it. Protect it. Pass it on.”

He joined the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) as one of their lawyers. He won 29 of the 32 cases he argued in front of the Supreme Court. One of those cases was fighting to end school segregation.
He was successful, but Marshall’s time as a lawyer was almost over. He was about to become a judge.
Justice Marshall

President John F. Kennedy chose Thurgood Marshall to be a judge in September, 1961. But being President Kennedy’s choice wasn’t enough. The U.S. Senate also had to vote to give him the job. Unfortunately, several senators didn’t want him to become a judge. It took months to convince them that Marshall should serve.

Marshall worked as a judge for six years. In 1967, President Lyndon B. Johnston chose him to be a justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. The U.S. Supreme Court has the final ruling over any court in the country. It is the highest court in the country.

The U.S. Senate had to vote again for Marshall to become a Supreme Court judge. This time the vote went quickly. Thurgood Marshall became the first Black justice of the U.S. Supreme Court on August 30, 1967. He spent his career fighting to end discrimination.
“I wish I could say that racism and prejudice were only distant memories,” he said. “America has no choice but to do better.”

articles.title: Cleaning Up the World's Highest Junkyard
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Tracey Vonder Brink describes the impact of trash on Mount Everest, and the efforts being made to clean it up.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracey Vonder Brink describes a problem on Mount Everest, the tallest mountain in the world.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how some people are dealing with the trash on Mount Everest.
articles.permissions: "Cleaning Up the World's Highest Junkyard" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The temperature on Mount Everest often falls below freezing. Gusts of wind can reach over 100 miles per hour. The air at the top is too thin to breathe. Mount Everest is the highest mountain in the world and one of the most difficult to climb. Hundreds of mountain climbers try to reach the top every year. They travel from around the world to the mountain, which lies between Nepal and Tibet. But making it up the mountain takes training, hard work, and some luck. The climbers who do reach the top find an amazing view. They also find something else along the way — trash!
Trash on the Mountain

Mount Everest is too tall to climb in one day. A climber’s body needs weeks to adjust to the height. Climbers bring tents and sleeping bags to set up camp along the way. They pack food and drinks. They carry oxygen bottles to help them breathe. But there are no trash cans. Climbers just leave their empty oxygen bottles, food wrappers, old tents, and other trash behind.

Trash on the mountain is not a new problem. Even Sir Edmund Hillary, one of the first people to reach the top in 1953, admitted that he and his team left their trash behind. Thousands of climbers have been on the mountain in the 70 years since. Each climber makes about 18 pounds (or 8 kilograms) of trash, some of which they leave behind. Add it all up, and you’ll understand why some climbers call Mount Everest the world’s highest junkyard.
Why It’s a Problem

Trash doesn’t break down when the temperature is below freezing. Not even human waste breaks down. Snow and ice might hide some trash, but it’s still there. It reappears when the snow melts. Trash left years ago is being uncovered as climate change melts the ice.

Why does this matter? After all, no one lives on the mountain. But Mount Everest, which is covered by glaciers, is part of the Himalayas mountain range. Ten major rivers begin in the Himalayas. Some are fed by the water from Everest’s ice and snow. These rivers supply more than a billion people with drinking water. Trash and waste from the mountain ends up in the water. Garbage on Mount Everest is more than just ugly. It harms the health of the people and animals who depend on its water.
Cleaning Up Everest

Dawa Steven Sherpa is a mountain climber who wanted to do something about the trash. It cost too much for him to pay a big team to clean up Everest, so he came up with another plan. He saw climbers carrying supplies up the mountain. But when they came back down, they weren’t carrying anything. He explained in a TED talk that he went to climbers and said, “You’re coming down empty anyway. If you see any garbage on the way, put it in your backpack. Bring it to me; I’ll weigh it. For each kilo, I’ll give you a dollar.” In the first six years, his program collected 33,000 pounds (15,000 kilograms) of trash. That’s more than 16 tons!

Dawa Steven isn’t alone. Other groups have formed to help clean up the mountain. The government of Nepal now charges climbers a $4000 garbage fee before they climb. They get their money back if they bring all their trash down with them. In 2022, climbers returned about 75,000 pounds (34,000 kilograms) of garbage. Thanks to people like Dawa Steven, Everest is on its way to being cleaned up. “The world is full of problems,” Dawa Steven said in his TED talk. “I believe that problems are just opportunities for us to change the world.”

options.label: More tourists would visit Mount Everest if there was less trash left behind by climbers.

The trash left behind on Mount Everest is harmless to the wildlife and people living nearby because it remains frozen on the mountain.

articles.title: Slinky Science
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Tracy Vonder Brink explains the science behind the Slinky, a popular toy.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains the science behind the Slinky, a popular toy for children.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how a Slinky moves.
articles.permissions: "Slinky Science" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Have you ever played with a Slinky? Maybe you launched it from the top of the steps and watched it march down. Or you might have stretched it way, way out and let it spring back together. As you played with that Slinky, did you notice it had two kinds of energy? It’s true! A Slinky can be more than just fun. It can teach you science.
Two Kinds of Energy

James Valles is a physicist at Brown University. That means he studies energy. He says you can learn about different kinds of energy with a Slinky. One kind is called potential energy. That means stored energy. Another kind is kinetic energy. That’s the energy of motion. “You store energy, and then you release it, and it’ll give you motion,” Valles explains. Think of it like stretching a rubber band to make it fly across the room. Pulling the rubber band back loads it with potential energy, and when you let it go to sail through the air, that’s kinetic energy.

The way a Slinky works is similar. “When a Slinky is walking down stairs,” Valles points out, “you see potential energy turned into kinetic energy.” A Slinky at the top of the steps has potential energy. “Potential energy is giving something the power to move on its own later,” Valles says. So, the Slinky at the top of the stairs can’t move yet. It doesn’t start to go on its own until you pull one end down to the first step. Then gravity takes over. Gravity is the force that pulls everything toward Earth’s surface. It’s what keeps your feet on the ground. Gravity also pulls on the Slinky as it moves down the steps.
How A Slinky Walks

Gravity’s pull gets the Slinky moving when you let the toy go. This movement is kinetic energy. The Slinky’s coils stretch as it moves. They pull as they stretch, loading up with stored energy again. When the coils bunch up, the Slinky flips over. “If you look very closely,” Valles says, “you’ll see the coils get closer together as it starts to snap over. That’s the spring pulling on itself. It has the stored energy that allows it to pull on itself to fling it over. It’s stored energy turning into motion energy.”

Stretching and bunching up moves the Slinky, but it also needs gravity’s pull to keep going. That’s why it stops when it reaches the bottom of the steps. That’s also why it works on stairs but not a flat floor. What do you think might happen if you put a Slinky on a ramp? Would it still walk down?
More Slinky Science

You can use a Slinky to understand potential and kinetic energy even if you don’t have stairs. Hold a Slinky up in the air by one end. Wait until it stops moving. Then pull the bottom of the Slinky down and let go. Did you see the Slinky go up? Valles explains what happened: “When you pull it down, you give it stored energy, then it turns into motion when you let it go.” A moving Slinky is potential energy turning into kinetic energy over and over again.

Now hold one end of a Slinky in each hand on top of a table or the floor. Spread your hands apart and stretch it as far as you can. What kind of energy does the Slinky have while it’s stretched out? If you said potential energy, you’re right! Now let the Slinky go. What kind of energy did you see as the Slinky snapped back together? If you said kinetic energy, right again!
Sometimes you can learn a lot from a simple toy!

options.label: The Slinky helped scientists discover how gravity affects potential energy.
The Slinky can help demonstrate potential and kinetic energy in many ways.
The Slinky can help demonstrate how potential and kinetic energy are the same.
The Slinky helped scientists discover how potential and kinetic energy are different.

questions.prompt: What is a main idea of the text?

options.label: The Slinky is a simple object that should only be used by children.
