articles.title: Honest Abe
articles.author: Barbara Radner
articles.description: In this informational text, the author shares several well-known stories about Abraham Lincoln.
articles.intro: Abraham Lincoln (1809-1865) was the 16th President of the United States. He served from March 1861 until he was famously assassinated in 1865. He is best remembered for his characteristic honesty and his steady leadership of his country throughout the Civil War. During his time as president, he strengthened the United States government and abolished slavery.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the ways the author supports the idea that Lincoln was an honest person.
articles.permissions: "Honest Abe", © 2005, Barbara Radner. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: This history tells about what happened while Abe Lincoln had a job working in a store in a small town. Every day people came and bought things they needed there. It was a frontier community. People did not have any extra money to spare. This story tells you about his character. As you read, think about the traits he shows by what he does. Abe Lincoln could not endure the thought of cheating anyone, even though it had been done unintentionally. He took great care of the money at the store. But some days there "Abraham Lincoln Memorial, Washington DC" were mistakes. He always made sure to by Samir Luther is licensed under CC BY-SA 2.0. correct them. One day a woman bought goods in Offutt’s store amounting to something over two dollars. She paid Abe the money and went away satisfied. That night, while going over the sales of the day, Abe found that he had charged the woman six and one-fourth cents too much. After closing the store, though it was late, he could not go home to supper or to bed till he had restored that sixpence to its proper owner. She lived more than two miles away, but that did 1. "Frontier community” refers to an area on the outskirts of developed society, in which not many people live. 2. Unintentionally(adverb)unplanned or accidentally 3. Denton Offutt owned and operated a general store in New Salem, Illinois during the 19th century. 4. Restore(verb)to return to a former condition, place, or position not matter to Abe Lincoln. He walked all the way there—and back. Another evening, as he was closing the store, a woman came in for a half-pound of tea. He weighed it out for her and took the pay. But early next morning, when he came to “open up,” he found the four-ounce weight instead of the eight-ounce on the scales, and inferred that he had given that woman only half as much tea as he had taken the money for. Of course, the woman would never know the difference, and it meant walking several miles and back, but the honest clerk weighed out another quarter pound of tea, locked the store and took that long walk before breakfast.

J. G. Holland wrote, in his book Life of Abraham Lincoln, of the young man’s progress during his first year in New Salem: “The year that Lincoln was in Denton Offutt’s store was one of great advance. He had 6 7 made new and valuable acquaintances, read many books, won multitudes of friends, and become ready for a step further in advance. Those who could appreciate brains respected him, and those whose ideas of a man related to his muscles were devoted to him. It was while he was performing the work of the store that he acquired the nickname, ‘Honest Abe’—a characterization that he never dishonored, that he never outgrew… He was everybody’s friend, the best-natured, the most sensible, the best-informed, the most modest and unassuming, the kindest, gentlest, roughest, strongest, best fellow in all New Salem and the region round about.” "Honest Abe", © 2005, Barbara Radner. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

articles.title: Election Choices: 2008
articles.author: Barbara Radner
articles.description: This informational text details the election of President Barack Obama during the 2008 election.
articles.intro: The 2008 U.S. presidential election was a historic one. After Republican President George W. Bush served his two terms in office, the Republican party nominated John McCain and the Democratic party nominated Barack Obama.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the significant events of the 2008 presidential election.
articles.permissions: "Election Choices: 2008" by Barbara Radner. Copyright © 2009. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: A convention is a very big meeting. Many organizations have conventions. Every four years there is a national election in the United States. The summer before the election, there are two conventions. One convention is for the Democrats. The other is for the Republicans. They choose leaders for the next four years. They hope the people they choose will win the election. At the 2008 Democratic convention, there was a big change. Never before had the Democrats chosen an African-American as their candidate for president. They did. It was "Obama unity" by Garfld986 is in the public not a surprise, though. Barack Obama had domain. been running for president all year. He had won primaries. A primary is an election in a state. The people of the state vote for the person they want to be president. Then at the convention their representatives vote for that person. At the convention, there was a problem. Many people had voted in primaries for Hillary Clinton. They wanted her to be the first woman president. It was not because she was a woman that people voted for her. They thought she would do a good job. She was a senator. 1. The Democrats and the Republicans are the two major political parties in the United States. 2. In 2008, Hillary Clinton was serving as a senator from New York. She is also the wife of former President Bill Clinton, who served in the White House from 1993-2001. Hillary Clinton ran for president again in 2016. 3. In the U.S., each state sends two senators to the Senate. These senators serve for six years each term and vote on laws in the branch of government known as Congress. She had met many world leaders. She was a leader, herself. More Democrats thought that Barack Obama would be a great president. So they chose him. She was disappointed, but Hillary Clinton helped him. She gave many speeches telling people why they should vote for him. She said, “He is the person who can change our country.” “Yes, we can” was their slogan. It meant that people could change what was happening in the United States. At the Republican convention, they had to make a choice. They chose John McCain. Then they surprised everyone. They chose a woman as vice president. People wondered if they did this to get more votes. The woman was Sarah Palin. She made many speeches. She got some people to vote for the Republican Party. Some people thought she was the opposite of Barack Obama. Some people thought it was a good idea for the Republicans to choose her. Other people thought it wasn’t. They said that she would only get votes from one group of people.

Barack Obama won the national election. He got votes from many different groups. More people voted than in the last election. It was important to them. After the election, he met with Hillary Clinton. He asked her to be Secretary of State. That is a very important job. The Secretary of State meets with world leaders. The Secretary of State helps solve problems with other countries.

options.label: to describe the events involved in the 2008 presidential election
to persuade readers to support one presidential candidate
to explain what a U.S. presidential convention is like
to describe how the 2008 vice presidential candidates were chosen

questions.prompt: PART A: What is the author's main purpose in writing the article?

options.label: "A convention is a very big meeting. Many organizations have conventions." (Paragraph 1)
"At the 2008 Democratic convention, there was a big change." (Paragraph 2)
"She said, 'He is the person who can change our country.'" (Paragraph 3)
"She got some people to vote for the Republican Party." (Paragraph 4)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which sentence from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: Hillary Clinton was a better candidate than Barack Obama.
Barack Obama was elected because he was the hardest working candidate.
The Republicans lost the election because their candidates were too surprising.
The 2008 presidential election was historic because it featured unique candidates.

questions.prompt: Which statement best identifies a central idea of this text?
In the last paragraph, the author states that more people voted in the 2008 election than in the election before it. How did Barack Obama's campaign contribute to this growth?

options.label: She was more popular than Barack Obama.
The job usually goes to whomever lost the election.
She was more familiar with the White House.
She had experience meeting with world leaders.

questions.prompt: According to the text, why did Barack Obama likely select Hillary Clinton as the Secretary of State?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In paragraph 3, the author mentions that Barack Obama's slogan, "Yes, we can," "meant that people could change what was happening in the United States." What are some things you think people wanted to change in the United States at that time? Are those things similar or different from the things people want to change in the United States now?
The idea of democracy is often what people say sets America apart from many other countries. Why is it important for citizens to have a say in their government?

articles.title: E Train Talks: Literacy Advocate
articles.author: Rebecca E.F. Barone
articles.description: Ten-year-old Ethan "E Train Talks" is dedicated to increasing literacy in communities that lack access to books.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Rebecca E.F. Barone describes the work of a young advocate. An advocate is a person who publicly supports a particular cause. The advocate in this text supports literacy, which is people's ability to read and write.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on E Train Talks’ actions.
articles.permissions: "E Train Talks: Literacy Advocate" by Rebecca E.F. Barone. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Do you remember the pandemic? Were you lonely when everything closed and no one went to school? Ten-year-old Ethan missed his friends during quarantine. He joined book clubs on Zoom as a way to connect with other readers his age. Soon, he began reviewing the books he read, and he even started a YouTube channel and podcast where he interviewed authors.

One day, Ethan — who goes by the name E Train Talks — interviewed Dr. Molly Ness. Dr. Ness is an author who also works to end book deserts . E Train Talks had never heard of book deserts before, and he couldn’t believe what he learned.

“Book deserts are a lot like food deserts,” E Train Talks explained. “They’re places where books are scarce… Lots of cities and communities have a ton of areas where books are scarce. Kids never have a chance to enjoy reading and learn to love it.”
E Train Talks was shocked when he learned that there are kids who grow up without access to books.

“Books bring you a lot of joy and companionship, if you’re going through a tough time and feel alone. Not only is there always going to be a book that [shows] your struggles on the page that you can relate to, but there’s always going to be a friend that you can look to in a book,” said E Train Talks.
E Train Talks missed his friends, but at least he had books to keep him company. It made him sad to learn that there were kids without books. He knew he had to do something about it.
Literacy Advocate

E Train Talks decided to raise money and collect books to give to kids who lived in book deserts. His enthusiasm for books quickly caught on. Lots of people began to help him by raising money and donating books.
“I tried to incorporate book drives and opportunities for others to join in and help me give back,” he explained.
E Train Talks is now a 13-year-old 7th grader. In the past three years, he’s donated more than 1,500 books himself! He’s arranged for even more books to be donated by setting up book drives.

One of his favorite memories is meeting author Stuart Gibbs. Gibbs writes books like the Moon Base Alpha series and Spy School series. E Train Talks loves these books, and he was excited when he and Gibbs were able to do a book event together. They went to an elementary school where they spoke to more than 700 students in kindergarten through fifth grade. Together, they talked about their love of books, as well as what writing and reading means to them. At the end of the event, they gave each student a signed copy of a book that Gibbs had written!
Book Joy

“It’s so important for kids to have access to stories,” E Train Talks said. “Books make you compassionate as you grow to love characters and understand their struggles. You grow to be empathetic, as well.”

Books have always been a big part of E Train Talks’s life. When he was a little kid, his mom would read picture books to him. Then, as he grew up, he found that he loved to read nonfiction books. Talking with others in book clubs helped him find fiction books that he enjoyed as well.
“Stories are so important and can change your life in so many ways,” E Train Talks said. “It’s been an incredible experience. I hope it can keep giving kids joy for many years to come.”

options.label: E Train Talks hopes to encourage other kids to advocate for changes that are important to them.
Driven by his love of reading, E Train Talks created a movement that is helping kids gain access to books.
Driven by his love of reading, E Train Talks wrote a children's book series that encourages young kids to read.
E Train Talks' YouTube channel and podcast were so popular during the pandemic that he had little time to work on other projects.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?

options.label: by telling facts about E Train Talks' family
by giving the reader examples of other famous YouTubers
by sharing an experience that readers may be familiar with
by explaining why E Train Talks had a hard time making friends in school

questions.prompt: How does the author introduce the text in paragraph 1?

options.label: "E Train Talks had never heard of book deserts before, and he couldn't believe what he learned." (Paragraph 2)
"In the past three years, he's donated more than 1,500 books himself!" (Paragraph 9)
"Together, they talked about their love of books, as well as what writing and reading means to them." (Paragraph 10)
"'Books make you compassionate as you grow to love characters and understand their struggles.'" (Paragraph 11)

questions.prompt: Which quote best highlights a benefit of reading according to E Train Talks?

options.label: concern
confidence
excitement
struggle

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of the word "enthusiasm" as it is used in paragraph 7?
How do paragraphs 2-6 add to the central idea of the text?

discussionQuestions.prompt: This text supports the idea that kids are capable of doing really important things. Have you ever participated in an activity to create positive change? If so, describe it. If you haven't, what kind of positive change would you like to see in your community? How might you be able to make this change? As an extension activity, students can research other young people who have made an impact through their actions, and then present their findings to the class.
E Train Talks loves books. How do you feel about books? Think about the people in your life — how do they feel about books? Why is reading important?
What inspired E Train Talks to create his podcast and YouTube channel? Does this inspire you to want to do something to help out your community? Why or why not?

E Train Talks felt isolated and lonely during the COVID-19 pandemic, but he managed to respond positively by joining an online book club, creating a YouTube channel, and creating a podcast. Has there ever been a time when you were able to turn a negative experience into something positive? Explain.

articles.title: Columbus and the Egg
articles.author: Barbara Radner
articles.description: In this story, explorer Christopher Columbus teaches his critics a lesson about discovery.
articles.intro: Christopher Columbus (1451-1506) was an Italian explorer, navigator, and colonizer. During his voyages, he claimed to discover North and South America, where millions of native people already lived. Afterwards, many Europeans traveled to settle in North and South America.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, note how people reacted to Columbus’s success.
articles.permissions: "Columbus and the Egg", © 2005, Barbara Radner. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: This is a story about Columbus. He was an explorer. He is the explorer who came to the Americas from Spain in 1492. This is a story about what might have happened long ago when he was back in Spain.

When Columbus came back from his trip to the Americas, many people praised him. He was made an admiral. That means he was a leader. People said what a great thing he had done. But not everyone liked him. Some were jealous of all the attention he got.

One day Columbus was at a party that a Spanish gentleman gave in his honor. People were saying, “What a great discovery you have made.” Several persons were present who were jealous of the great admiral's success. They were proud, conceited fellows, and they very soon began to try to make Columbus uncomfortable.

“You have discovered strange lands beyond the seas,” they said, “but what of that? We do not see why there should be so much said about it. Anybody can sail across the ocean; and anybody can coast along the islands on the other side, just as you have done. It is the simplest thing in the world.”
Columbus made no answer; but after a while he took an egg from a dish and said to the company: “Who among you, gentlemen, can make this egg stand on end?”
One by one those at the table tried the experiment. When the egg had gone entirely around and none had succeeded, all said that it could not be done.
Then Columbus took the egg and struck its small end gently upon the table so as to break the shell a little. After that there was no trouble in making it stand upright.
“Gentlemen,” said he, “what is easier than to do this which you said was impossible? It is the simplest thing in the world. Anybody can do it—AFTER HE HAS BEEN SHOWN HOW!”

options.label: Success is possible once you overcome your fears and doubts.
Solutions to problems can seem easy once someone has already discovered them.
People are usually not supportive of others who succeed because they are jealous.
The most important quality in a leader is the ability to stand up to criticism.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best identifies a theme in this story?

options.label: "He was made an admiral. That means he was a leader." (Paragraph 2)
"Several persons were present who were jealous of the great admiral's success." (Paragraph 3)
"When the egg had gone entirely around and none had succeeded, all said that it could not be done." (Paragraph 6)
"'Anybody can do it—AFTER HE HAS BEEN SHOWN HOW!'" (Paragraph 8)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which phrase from the text best support the answer to Part A?

options.label: It proves that the Spanish gentleman only threw the party to embarrass Columbus and make him feel unimportant.
It shows that the people who were jealous of Columbus only went to the party to take credit for his discovery.
It shows that the party guests were trying to make Columbus' achievements seem easy and unimpressive.
It proves that Columbus did not deserve to be honored by the Spanish because he had not accomplished anything special.

questions.prompt: How does the phrase "'Anybody can sail across the ocean...It is the simplest thing in the world'" from paragraph 4 contribute to the development of the main ideas of the story?

options.label: to provide an example of how successful people think creatively
to encourage people not to be too proud of their accomplishments
to tell the true story of Columbus' discovery of the New World
to illustrate the importance of respecting leaders

questions.prompt: What is the author's main purpose in writing the article?
How does the author describe the relationship between Columbus and the Spanish gentlemen at the party? Cite evidence from the text in your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Why do you think Columbus allowed each attendee to try to balance the egg for himself before revealing how it could be done? What effect does this have on the story?
Have you ever interacted with people who were jealous of your success? How did that make you feel? How did you deal with the situation?

The story showcases Columbus's confident response to people who questioned the importance of his accomplishments. In the context of this article, why do people succeed? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.
What does the author think of Columbus? Do you think everybody would agree with how she describes him? Cite evidence from the text and your personal experience in the answer.

articles.title: Feeling Weightless: Forces and Motion on a Roller Coaster
articles.author: India James
articles.description: India James explains the science behind how roller coasters work.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James explains the science behind roller coasters.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how roller coasters are designed.
articles.permissions: "Feeling Weightless: Forces and Motion on a Roller Coaster" by India James. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: People love riding roller coasters. Amusement parks become crowded as soon as the weather is warm enough to ride super-fast rides. The only thing louder than the sound of the roller coasters are the happy screams of the people riding them.

Roller coasters are huge machines that change how forces are applied to our bodies. Forces are anything that push or pull. Forces move an object by speeding it up or slowing it down. These changes in speed are called accelerations .

Do you think roller coasters are scary because they go so fast? Well, think again! It’s not speed that makes people scream. Roller coasters are fun and scary because they make our bodies feel different. It’s all about forces!
Feeling Weightless
Larry Giles is the Vice President for Engineering at Busch Gardens amusement park in Williamsburg, Virginia. He’s spent a lot of time thinking about how people react to forces and motion.

“There’s a lot of different things you can use to create that ‘wow’ factor and that fun factor,” he says about amusement park rides. But one of the things people enjoy most is the feeling of weightlessness.

Gravity is one of the most important forces in our lives. Larry says it’s important to understand that we all feel gravity every moment of every day. Gravity is the force that pulls you down to Earth. It’s scary when we feel a change from that normal “pulling down” force.

Scientists and engineers refer to the force of gravity that we experience normally on Earth as “1 g.” Anything less than 1 g means a person feels lighter than they do normally. “People get really excited anytime they get anything less than 1 g of gravity, which is what you’re sitting at right now,” says Larry.

“When your body gets below 1 g, it starts perceiving that ‘hey! I’m falling!’” Larry said. “It automatically transmits a scare factor to your whole body going, ‘hey, this is not normal!’... So we try to create places in our rides that have less than 1 g and get close to 0 g,” he says.
But how do the engineers create this feeling?
That First Big Drop
“Where do you feel is the scariest part of the ride?” Larry asked. “It’s that first drop, right? Everybody says, ‘Oh wow, that first drop is so exciting.’”

Engineers design roller coasters to have different types of curves. Tight curves will put different forces on the riders than wide curves. One of the biggest curves on a roller coaster usually happens after the first hill.

The force of gravity is always pulling down. Engineers design this first curve to make a rider feel a force pointed up. “Vertical accelerations mean you’re getting uplift,” Larry said. “And that’s giving [a rider] airtime.” When the up force from the roller coaster matches the down force from gravity, the riders feel weightless.
“Those are the kind of things we try to create,” said Larry. “Things you aren’t used to feeling, and if you’re not used to feeling them, they do create excitement.”
On a roller coaster, forces are more than just physics. They’re a lot of fun!

options.label: It is more important that roller coasters are designed to be weightless than safe.
Riding a roller coaster is an experience that everyone should have once in their life.
Engineers need many years of training to design roller coasters using force and motion.
Engineers use their knowledge of science and the human body to design roller coasters.

questions.prompt: What is a main idea of the text?

options.label: The author asks questions about how roller coasters have changed over time and then answers them.
The author explains the science behind roller coaster design and then highlights why people enjoy them.
The author tells a story about how roller coasters were invented and then describes roller coasters of the past.
The author compares roller coasters to other amusement park rides and then describes her personal favorites.

questions.prompt: How does the author organize the information in the text?

options.label: "Amusement parks become crowded as soon as the weather is warm enough to ride super-fast rides." (Paragraph 1)
"'There's a lot of different things you can use to create that 'wow' factor and that fun factor'" (Paragraph 5)
"'So we try to create places in our rides that have less than 1 g and get close to 0 g'" (Paragraph 8)
"One of the biggest curves on a roller coaster usually happens after the first hill." (Paragraph 11)

questions.prompt: Which quote best highlights the way science is used when designing roller coasters?

options.label: grabs
stops
sends
gathers

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of "transmits" as it is used in paragraph 8?
How do roller coaster engineers create rides that are exciting?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Some engineers help design machines like roller coasters. Would you like to have a job like this? Why or why not? As an extension activity, have students work together to design a roller coaster based on the information shared in the text.

Do you enjoy roller coasters, or do you think you would enjoy roller coasters? Why or why not? What do you find fun or scary about riding a roller coaster? Do you think things can be both fun and scary at the same time? Explain your thinking.
After reading about how roller coasters are designed, what are some additional questions you have? What steps could your class take to find the answers to these questions?

articles.title: Red Dirt
articles.author: Angela Johnson
articles.description: A speaker talks about a special keepsake.
articles.intro: Angela Johnson is an award-winning American children's book and poetry author. In this poem, a speaker talks about a special keepsake.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker feels.
articles.permissions: "Red Dirt" from The Other Side: Shorter Poems by Angela Johnson. Copyright © 2000. Used with author's permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Got me some red Alabama dirt I keep
on the bathroom shelf in a heart-shaped
bottle.
Dug it up from the back of a cheap motel
in central Alabama me and Mama stayed at
when our car broke down driving south.
Was going to show it to whoever
up north.
But never have yet — can’t think of anyone
who’d understand.
Still look at it and miss where I used to
wander.
Red, red dirt of Alabama.

options.label: We value objects more when they connect us to a special place or time in our lives.
Adults understand the importance of creating memories more than children do.
We must learn to let go of the past if we want to enjoy the present.
It is hard to make friends after moving to a new place.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the poem?

options.label: "Got me some red Alabama dirt I keep / on the bathroom shelf" (Lines 1-2)
"Dug it up from the back of a cheap motel / in central Alabama" (Lines 4-5)
"Was going to show it to whoever / up north." (Lines 7-8)
"But never have yet — can't think of anyone / who'd understand." (Lines 9-10)

questions.prompt: Which line explains why the speaker has kept the bottle of red dirt to themselves?

options.label: They share that the speaker enjoys looking at nature.
They explain why the speaker enjoys traveling to new places by car.
They highlight the speaker's strong personal connection to Alabama.
They show that the speaker likes spending time alone rather than with others.

questions.prompt: How do lines 11-13 add to the reader's understanding of the poem?
How does the poet's repetition of the word "Alabama" add to the meaning of the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you think the speaker is happy or sad in their new home? Explain your thinking.

The speaker of the poem describes a memory they have of spending time with their mother in Alabama. Do you have any memories of special times spent with a friend or loved one? What do you remember about the time you spent together and why has the memory stayed with you?
In the poem, the speaker describes Alabama as a special place. What is a place that holds strong memories for you? What are the foods, people, or activities that you remember about this place?

In the poem, the speaker talks about an item that holds a special memory for them. What items do you own that remind you of special memories or stories? What memories do they remind you of? Do you think it is important for people to hold on to objects that remind them of special times? Why or why not?

articles.title: Danger at Scituate Lighthouse
articles.author: Marlene Bateman Sullivan
articles.description: During the War of 1812, a girl must protect her town from a British invasion.
articles.intro: The War of 1812 was fought between the United States, Great Britain and Spain from 1812-1815. Native peoples offered support to the United States and Great Britain. During the war, the British landed a warship filled with soldiers in Scituate, Massachusetts. In this story, a girl must protect her town from the British soldiers.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Rebecca’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Danger at Scituate Lighthouse" by Marlene Bateman Sullivan, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Rebecca stood by her father, looking out the windows of Scituate Lighthouse at the glitter of the surf and beyond to the deep blue swells of the ocean. Low sand dunes, crowned in spots with sea grass, topped the Massachusetts beach. There were no clouds in the sky — nothing at all to give warning of the danger that was sailing toward them.
Earlier that day, Mr. Bates had decided that he would take most of Rebecca’s younger brothers and sisters to town to pick up food and supplies, which were dangerously low.
“Now, Rebecca,” he began, gazing out across the open sea, “I want you and Abigail to help your mother and take care of things while I go to town with the others. I won’t be gone long.”
“We’ll help,” Rebecca promised.
“I know I can count on you.”

Still, Mr. Bates looked a little worried about leaving the lighthouse. After all, peace had not yet been declared, even though the War of 1812 was nearly over. Although English warships used to frequently raid towns along the coast — including Scituate — no English troops had been sighted for a long time. Besides, Rebecca thought as she watched her father set off, the family would soon starve if he didn’t go for food.

Rebecca didn’t mind watching the lighthouse. She was used to helping her father polish the lamps and trim the wicks. Her father, brothers, and sisters hadn’t been gone long when Rebecca happened to look out the window at the ocean. What she saw nearly made her heart stop. It was an enemy ship: a small, light-gunned British raider. Calling for her mother, she grabbed her father’s naval telescope and could just make out the name painted on the side: La Hogue .

Rebecca, Abigail, and their mother stood together, frightened, as they watched the ship sail closer. Mother told the girls to watch the ship while she ran outside to find their brother. She had to send him to the nearby village to sound the alarm and alert the home guard. The girls watched helplessly as the great ship dropped anchor just off Cedar Point. Two small boats were lowered off the side, and Rebecca shivered in fear as they began to fill with soldiers. She knew the boats would come to shore quickly with the strong, sweeping strokes of the oarsmen.

It was time to run. As they bolted down the lighthouse stairs, Rebecca suddenly stopped when she saw her fife. Oh my! she thought. It just might work...Rebecca grabbed the fife and the drum that Abigail had been learning to play.

She and her sister hurried out of the lighthouse and over to the large sand dunes that lay behind it. They stopped in a small gully and hid among the cedar trees. Peering through the trees, they saw soldiers in their bright red coats coming closer and closer. Stopping to catch her breath, Rebecca held her sister’s trembling hand. She looked toward Scituate. The village seemed far away, though she could see the tall, white steeple of the church in the distance. She wondered if her older brother was there yet, alerting the villagers that British soldiers were coming.

Rebecca handed Abigail her drum. “We’ve got to play ‘Yankee Doodle.’” Abigail opened her mouth to argue, but her sister silenced her with a stern look. “You know that the home guard always marches to a drum and fife. If the British soldiers hear the music before they get ashore, they’ll think our troops are coming to fight.” Abigail nodded. Her face was white, but she gripped her drumsticks firmly.
The lively rhythm of “Yankee Doodle” soon filled the air. When it reached the ears of the men in the rowboats, they stopped rowing.

The sailors rested on their oars, listening to the proud and defiant music. They looked nervously at one another — the music had to be coming from the drummer and fifer that always accompanied American soldiers. Faint strains of the music reached the skipper, who was still aboard the La Hogue . He listened tensely, thinking, too, that the music had to be coming from confident American soldiers who were hiding behind the sand dunes.
The wind was strong and cool, picking up the fine golden sand and tossing it high, but Rebecca and her sister played on.

The captain feared that if his men landed on the beach, they would be walking into a trap, so he fired a cannon as a signal for them to return to the ship. The oarsmen quickly turned the rowboats around.

When the home guard from the village arrived at the lighthouse, they were amazed to find that the La Hogue had already set sail. They congratulated Rebecca on her quick thinking and for tricking the British into leaving. Rebecca and Abigail’s bravery and lively music had defeated the enemy...without a single shot having been fired.

options.label: Do not sneak up on people.
Be brave in the face of danger.
Work together to reach your goals.
Protect people who are not as strong as you.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the story?

options.label: to convince the British soldiers to come closer so that the American soldiers can trap them
to make the British soldiers think that the American soldiers are waiting for them on shore
to stop the American soldiers from the other town from coming any closer
to help the American soldiers find where the sisters are hiding

questions.prompt: Why do the sisters play "Yankee Doodle"?

options.label: it gives important information about the setting
it introduces the main character and her family
it shows how the characters solve their problem
it teaches the reader a lesson they can use in their own life

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 6 help the reader understand the story?

options.label: annoyed
anxious
sneaky
relieved

questions.prompt: In paragraph 13, the word "tensely" shows that the skipper felt —
What do Rebecca's actions show about her character?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Rebecca must think quickly to protect her town. Have you ever been in a situation where you had to think quickly? What was the situation and how did quick- thinking help you?
Rebecca and Abigail play the fife and the drum. Do you play any instruments or would you like to? What are they? Why do you like these instruments?

In the story, Rebecca's father puts her in charge of watching the lighthouse. How is this an important job for her? Do you have any important jobs in your family? What are they? How and why are they important?

articles.title: Benjamin Lay: The Quaker Comet
articles.author: Ruben Brosbe
articles.description: Abolitionist Benjamin Lay was famous for his impassioned dedication to justice.
articles.intro: Quakers are members of a religious group who are known for their belief in peace and unity. This informational text describes the life of Quaker abolitionist Benjamin Lay.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Lay’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Benjamin Lay: The Quaker Comet" by Ruben Brosbe. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Benjamin Lay wasn’t like his fellow Quakers. First, he was just a little more than four feet tall and had a very curved spine. But it wasn’t Lay’s appearance so much as his ideas that truly set him apart from his community. That’s because Lay was a white man who was an abolitionist , which means he believed in ending slavery.

Lay was born in England in 1682. From an early age, he didn’t take the easiest route. He was supposed to take over his family’s farm. Instead, he moved to London when he was 21 and got a job as a sailor. Working on ships was not easy for a person Lay’s size but he worked hard. While working as a sailor, Lay began to hear stories from fellow sailors about the horrors of slavery.
Years later, Lay traveled to the island of Barbados with his wife, Sarah. They were shocked by the treatment of the enslaved people there. They left Barbados and eventually moved to Pennsylvania.

Everywhere Lay lived he got into trouble with his fellow Quakers. The Quakers felt peace and unity were two of the most important ideas of their group. But Lay felt that fighting for the truth, that all humans were equal, was more important than getting along with others. He was not afraid to shout at people he believed were wrong. This included those white Quakers who were rich because of slavery.

Lay tried lots of different ideas to show his fellow Quakers how evil slavery was. He gave speeches and wrote essays. In 1737, he published an anti-slavery book with help from his friend Benjamin Franklin. Once, he stood outside a Quaker meeting house in winter without a coat and with one foot bare. People who saw Lay standing in the cold were worried, but he told them that people who were enslaved were forced to work outdoors dressed just as he was.

One day, he tried something truly outrageous. He brought a hollowed-out bible with him to the meeting house. The bible was filled with berry juice. Lay stood up and gave a speech against slavery. It was like many speeches he had shared before. But this time, when he finished, he pulled out a sword and stabbed it into the bible. The juice inside was supposed to look like blood, and it splattered everywhere. His fellow Quakers were stunned. Then they kicked Lay out of the meeting house.

Lay continued to fight against slavery for the rest of his life, no matter how much trouble he got in. After his wife died, Lay went to live in a cave. He wanted to live a simple life as free from wrongdoing as possible. He refused to eat meat. He even made his own clothes so that he did not have to wear clothes made from cotton that was picked by enslaved people.

In 1758, a friend of Lay’s came to the cave where he was living with good news. He told Lay that Quakers in Philadelphia were planning to kick out members of their group who were a part of the slave trade. When Lay heard this, he paused for a little while. Then he told his friend that he could finally die in peace. Lay died a year later, on February 3, 1759, at the age of 77. Almost 20 years after Lay died, Quakers in Philadelphia voted to ban its members from enslaving others.

After Lay died, a man named Robert Vaux wrote a biography of his life. He wrote that Lay was not like a calm, peaceful moon but more like a dangerous comet that threatened the planets it flew by. That’s because throughout his life Lay did a lot to shake up the world around him. His actions and words made many white people uncomfortable and upset. Lay knew that the world he threatened was a harmful and unequal one. He hoped his actions would help to build a new one where everyone was free.

options.label: Benjamin Lay is known for his protests against slavery and his belief in equality.
Benjamin Lay was a peaceful person who inspired other Quakers through his gentle words.
Benjamin Lay's stunts against slavery made him popular with Quakers throughout the United States.
Benjamin Lay used the success of his family to help him in his fight against the unfair treatment of enslaved people.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?

options.label: kind.
dangerous.
humorous.
shocking.

questions.prompt: As it is used in paragraph 6, "outrageous" most closely means —

options.label: "Lay was born in England in 1682." (Paragraph 2)
"While working as a sailor, Lay began to hear stories from fellow sailors about the horrors of slavery." (Paragraph 2)
"Lay continued to fight against slavery for the rest of his life, no matter how much trouble he got in." (Paragraph 7)
"When Lay heard this, he paused for a little while." (Paragraph 8)

questions.prompt: Which line from the text shows that Benjamin Lay was determined?

options.label: Paragraphs 5-7 explain the actions Lay took, and paragraph 8 explains the later effect of his actions on the Quaker community.
Paragraphs 5-7 describes how slavery was wrong, and paragraph 8 describes why people refused to accept this as true.
Paragraphs 5-7 explain how Lay was different from others in his community, and paragraph 8 explains why being different is important.
Paragraphs 5-7 show how Lay disturbed the Quaker community, and paragraph 8 describes how people punished him for his actions.

questions.prompt: How do paragraphs 5-7 connect to paragraph 8?
How does the title of the article add to the overall meaning of the text?

discussionQuestions.prompt: The text states that "Lay knew that the world he threatened was a harmful and unequal one." Explain what you think this means. How did Lay's choices show that he did not want to participate in inequality? What are some ways that you think the world is unequal or unfair today? What are personal actions or choices you could take to show that you disagree with something? How could these actions or choices make a difference?

Benjamin Lay aggressively stood up for what he believed in, even when many people disagreed with his opinions. Why do you think it is important to stand up for what you believe, even if it means going against the way things are done in one's community? Do you think that Lay made the right choice to disturb the "peace and unity" of the Quaker community by arguing for his beliefs? In your opinion, is it easier to make change by getting along with and inspiring people or by arguing with people? Explain your thinking.

Who are some leaders or public figures today that are like "comets," who shake up the world around them? Do you think good leaders and public figures should make people feel uncomfortable and upset, as Lay did? Why or why not?

articles.title: Steam Power
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Tracy Vonder Brink describes the science behind geothermal energy.
articles.intro: Tracy Vonder Brink loves true stories and facts — especially the weird ones! She writes articles and books for kids from her home in Cincinnati, Ohio. In this article, Tracy Vonder Brink describes a special kind of energy.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the benefits of steam power.
articles.permissions: "Steam Power" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Earth’s core is about as hot as the sun’s surface. It’s so hot that it melts the rock above it into a liquid called magma. That’s a lot of heat! Energy made by using Earth’s heat is called geothermal energy. Some areas in the United States are able to use geothermal energy as a source of power. In California, The Geysers power plant uses it to make enough electricity for 725,000 homes. They do it with steam.
Steam to Electricity

The Geysers is the world’s largest geothermal field. It is in the Mayacamas Mountains, in an area where magma is closer to the surface. The magma’s heat boils water that collects underground. The boiling water makes steam, and the steam is trapped in rock layers. The Geysers drilled 319 wells to reach it! The areas where the wells reach the steam is called the steam field. How much steam flows out of the steam field? About twelve and a half million pounds per hour!

John Avery is an engineer who has worked at The Geysers for more than twenty years. He explained how it works: “The steam comes out like steam from a tea kettle. Except instead of steam just coming out of the kettle, we have pipelines that take it to the power plants.” All that steam flows into turbines in 13 power plants. The turbines are connected to generators. The steam spins the turbines. The turbines turn the generators, making electricity. Then the steam goes into cooling towers. Cooling the steam turns it back into water. The water is returned to the ground.
A Cleaner Form of Power

Other kinds of power plants also use steam to create electricity, but they make steam by burning fuel, such as coal. Burning coal and other fuels like it causes pollution and releases the greenhouse gasses that cause climate change. The Geysers use steam that’s already in the ground, so its power plants don’t need to burn fuel. It’s a cleaner form of energy that creates little pollution.

The Geysers also recycles. The underground water that becomes steam needs to be refilled so the steam can keep flowing. Some of that water comes from turning the steam back into water in the cooling towers. But The Geysers also takes treated wastewater from nearby towns. How much recycled wastewater do they use? About 20 million gallons per day!
Good for Some But Not for All

If geothermal energy is cleaner, why don’t we use it everywhere? John Avery explained that geothermal power plants like The Geysers “can only occur in certain places that have heat and water and a certain type of rock.” Not all rock lets water and steam flow through it. Plus, those three things need to be close enough to the surface to reach by drilling. “Ours is only two miles deep,” he said. The farther a company has to drill, the more it costs.

But in places where heat, water, and the right kind of rock can be reached, geothermal energy can be a good source of power. California, Nevada, Utah, Oregon, Idaho, New Mexico, and Hawaii all have at least one geothermal power plant. Around the world, geothermal energy is used in parts of Indonesia, the Philippines, Japan, New Zealand, Mexico, Italy, and Kenya. Geothermal energy can’t power the whole world, but every bit of cleaner energy helps.

options.label: The Geysers have failed to use less fuel when creating electricity.
The Geysers create less pollution by helping cities use less electricity.
The Geysers have started using wastewater as a way to create power.
The Geysers create a cleaner form of energy using steam from the earth.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses a main idea of the article?

options.label: It describes the steps engineers take to create geothermal energy at The Geysers.
It introduces a problem caused by using steam power in geothermal power plants.
It explains the differences between geothermal power plants and other power plants.
It asks an important question about using the earth's heat to create geothermal energy.

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 4 contribute to the reader's understanding of the article?

options.label: "Some areas in the United States are able to use geothermal energy as a source of power." (Paragraph 1)
"Burning coal and other fuels like it causes pollution and releases the greenhouse gasses that cause climate change." (Paragraph 4)
"Not all rock lets water and steam flow through it." (Paragraph 6)
"California, Nevada, Utah, Oregon, Idaho, New Mexico, and Hawaii all have at least one geothermal power plant." (Paragraph 7)

questions.prompt: Which detail best explains a challenge of using geothermal energy as a power source?

discussionQuestions.prompt: The article describes an innovative way of using steam to generate electricity. Why do you think this type of innovation is important, especially for the environment? What other actions can individuals, communities, and governments take to help keep our environment clean? As an extension activity, have students research and present on additional methods of environmental conservation that are being utilized around the world.

The article describes how using geothermal energy can offset the effects of climate change and pollution. What are the benefits of preserving the environment for future generations? Imagine not being able to visit the sea or other natural places because of pollution. How would you feel? How would you feel if future generations were not able to experience this?

articles.title: A Totem Pole Comes Home
articles.subtitle: A piece of history makes a long journey from Sweden to a small village in Canada.
articles.author: Kathleen Curry
articles.description: Kathleen Curry describes how the Haisla people solved a mystery about their missing totem pole.
articles.intro: Throughout history, some important objects from different cultures have been removed from their original location and put in museums, often without the permission of the people of that culture. The Haisla people are the native, or First People, from the North Coast of British Columbia, Canada. They are known for their totem poles, or large carved cedar poles, that tell stories. In this article, Kathleen Curry describes how the Haisla people solved a mystery about their missing totem pole.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the actions of the Haisla people.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Dancers wind through the crowd, their mother of pearl buttons sparkling against their black-and-red cloaks. The cedar straw hairs of their carved masks move as they jump, dance, and beat their hands against brightly painted drums.
It is a hot July day in Kitamaat Village in British Columbia, Canada. The Haisla people pack the gymnasium, fanning themselves with paper plates while they listen to rhythmic chanting.
This is a celebration. It also ends a 62-year-old mystery.
Along the gym wall lies the guest of honor. It is thirty feet long and 2,645 pounds. It is hiding under a white sheet covered with evergreen branches.
At long last, the G'psgolox (gups-GOLAX) totem pole has come home.
“It disappeared in 1929. Our people came back from a fishing trip and it was gone, cut down at its base,” says Gerald Amos, a former leader and Haisla elder.
For 50 years, no one in Kitamant Village knew where the G’psgolox had gone or if it had even survived. It became a thing of legend.
And then the Haisla people found a clue.
In the late 1970s, a village elder saw a picture of what looked like the pole in a book and learned that it might still exist in a Swedish museum.
The Pole Reappears
But it wasn’t until 1991 that members of the Haisla band followed the clue and traveled to Sweden.
There they found the G’psgolox pole, weathered gray with age, its wood cracked but the carved eyes of the bear and other spirits still staring out at the world.
“We were the first of our people to see it after 60 years. I cannot describe the feeling,” Amos says.
Amos also learned why the pole had disappeared. In 1929, the Canadian government had allowed a representative of the Swedish government to take it to his home country without Haisla permission.
“We knew we had to bring it back,” Amos adds.
But getting a long and heavy totem pole from a foreign country and across the Pacific Ocean was no easy task.
“First we talked to the Swedish government. We needed them to agree to its return,” says Amos.

Anders Björklund is the director of the museum where the totem pole was found. He says that the talks about how to return the pole helped the Swedish people and the Haisla develop a strong relationship. After many years, they figured out how to move the pole and how best to look after it following its return.
As part of this agreement, the Haisla promised to give Sweden an identical pole. This decision gave 17-year-old Tricia Robertson a very unusual job.
“I worked with my grandfather, who is a master carver, and my uncles, and we created two new poles identical to the G’psgolox pole,” Tricia says.
Indeed, carving is nothing new for the Robertson family. Tricia’s great-great-grandfather carved the original pole.
“I’ve always wanted to carve, even when I was a small child. I can’t imagine doing anything else,” Tricia explains.
And Tricia got her fill of planning, chipping, and chiseling. “We live in Vancouver so we go to Kitamaat Village and work for three weeks and then take one week off,” Tricia adds.
Later, all the carvers went with the pole to Sweden to finish the detail work.
“This was very challenging. Usually, a carver like my grandfather would listen to the log’s spirit, but for this project we had to work from numbers and measurements,” Tricia says.
The new pole was finished and given to Sweden in 2001. The second pole was erected at the totem’s original site, where it will eventually weather and return to the earth.
The Pole Goes Home
The Haisla people waited another five years before a final agreement was reached and the money was raised for the original pole’s trip across the Pacific Ocean.
Finally on July 1, 2006, Kitamaat villagers put on their traditional dress, cooked, and baked until the gym smelled of bread and salmon. Then they celebrated the return of the G’psgolox pole.
“This pole is important to the Haisla people. It connects us to the past. It comes from a time of our strength, when our culture was strong,” explains Amos.

Already Haisla youth are connecting with the pole when they view it enclosed in a special glass-fronted crate at a local shopping mall. It will stay there until a special building can be constructed at Kitamaat Village.
For the Haisla band, the G’psgolox pole is special not only because of its past but because of its future. Amos sums up the pole’s importance: “It is about hope and healing.”

options.label: How to Carve a Totem Pole
Understanding Haisla Culture
History of the Native People of British Columbia
An Amazing Achievement: Finding The G'psgolox Pole

questions.prompt: Which alternate title best expresses the main idea of the passage?

options.label: They explain the steps for how to make a new totem pole.
They describe the first hint that Haisla people found about the totem pole.
They tell the reader how the Haisla people's totem pole had been destroyed.
They show how the Haisla people solved the mystery of their missing totem pole.

options.label: carved
raised

torn down

questions.prompt: In paragraph 25, the word "erected" means —

discussionQuestions.prompt: There are many pieces of art throughout the world that have been removed from their original owners without permission and placed in museums. Some people think that this is the right thing to do, because more people can see this art and learn from it, while others think that this art should be returned to where it is originally from. What do you think should happen to this artwork? Why?

The author describes how people celebrate the totem pole being returned. What is an important event that your family or community has celebrated? Why was it important to your family or community? How did you celebrate?

The Haisla people solved a mystery that had puzzled them for a long time. Do you like to solve mysteries or puzzles? Why or why not? If you do, do you have a favorite puzzle or mystery? What is it? Why is it your favorite?

articles.title: Eating in Silence
articles.author: Pamela Huber
articles.description: A speaker describes making lasagna with their mother.
articles.intro: Pamela Huber is a writer based out of Washington, D.C. In this poem, inspired by Li-Young Lee's "Eating Together," a speaker describes making lasagna with their mother.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify the figurative language the speaker uses to describe cooking.
articles.permissions: "Eating in Silence" from American Literary Magazine by Pamela Huber. Copyright © 2015 by Pamela Huber, reprinted with permission of author and American Literary Magazine ( amlitmag.com ). All rights reserved.

blocks.text: From my mother’s hands
I take sheets of pasta,
lay them down in a tomato
sauce bed, and we sprinkle
parmesan and asiago on top —
it falls like pollen on a field.
Layer on layer, we laugh
and splatter sauce on our shirts.
My father climbs the stairs,
says the lasagna looks like dog
meat, but he eats two servings,
fills up his plate as I stack
my grandmother’s china
in my head, mentally sorting
the silence of the table,
wondering if they remember
when grandma taught her the recipe.

options.label: Silence can be comforting in times of tragedy.
Cooking can be hard during difficult times.
Tragedy can cause people to change how they act.
Cooking can bring family members together.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best identifies the theme of the poem?

options.label: "parmesan and asiago on top — / it falls like pollen on a field." (Lines 5-6)
"Layer on layer, we laugh / and splatter sauce on our shirts." (Lines 7-8)
"says the lasagna looks like dog / meat, but he eats two servings" (Lines 10-11)
"in my head, mentally sorting / the silence of the table" (Lines 14-15)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: They are considering what their parents are thinking, since their parents aren't speaking.
They are uncertain about the silence and wish to fill it with conversation.
They are upset by the silence, as the parents don't appear to remember this is a recipe from the speaker's grandmother.
They are comforted by the silence, as they are certain that everyone is thinking about the speaker's grandmother.

questions.prompt: What does the speaker mean when they say they are "mentally sorting / the silence of the table" (Lines 14-15)?
Reread lines 4-6: "and we sprinkle / parmesan and asiago on top — / it falls like pollen on a field." How does the simile contribute to the tone of poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you have a recipe that has been passed down in your family? How does it make you feel when you help prepare that meal and eat it with your family?
Think about the way the speaker chooses to discuss their grandmother. What are the moments of silence that you share with family and friends as you think about the loved ones you have lost?
What is the relationship between meals and family? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Becoming A Junior Ranger
articles.author: India James
articles.description: This text discusses the Junior Ranger program in national parks.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James explains the Junior Ranger program at the U.S. National Parks. National Parks are protected areas set aside by a national government to keep nature safe.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on who benefits from the Junior Ranger program.
articles.permissions: "Becoming a Junior Ranger" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Go to any national park, and you’ll see park rangers out and about. They wear wide-brimmed hats, gray button-up shirts, and moss-green pants. They’re on trails and at visitor centers. They help guests find their way around, and they run classes and programs about the unique plant, animal, and geological features of each park.

Park rangers are important to the national parks. They patrol each park’s trails, campgrounds, and wild areas to make sure the visitors are safe and the area is being taken care of. Park rangers might answer questions from guests, or they might help enforce park rules. When there’s an emergency, park rangers help with search and rescue efforts.
Junior Rangers
Park rangers are adults with specialized training, but did you know that you can be part of the ranger program, too? You can be a Junior Ranger!

Junior Rangers are people of any age who have completed activities and lessons about each park. Every national park has a Junior Ranger program. Each park’s program is different. In some parks, visitors might have to go on a scavenger hunt to find items around the park. In other parks, visitors might have to read information and answer questions about the park’s history.

After completing all the tasks and reciting the park’s oath, visitors are official Junior Rangers! Each park has a unique Junior Ranger badge that illustrates some of the park’s features. The badge is a special way to show off the hard work it took to become a Junior Ranger. Collecting Junior Ranger badges from lots of different parks can be a fun challenge.
Fun in the Parks

Batty and Falcon are 11 and 12 years old, and they blog with their parents about traveling. Visiting national parks and becoming Junior Rangers is one of their favorite ways to explore. They’ve visited many national parks. Each park is different.
“One that I especially like is Olympic National Park because it has such a variety of ecosystems — beach, rainforest, mountains, and lakes,” said Batty.

Falcon enjoys seeing places without a lot of people. “My favorite type of parks are ones that not many people visit but are still gems. Then I can ask the rangers lots of questions without feeling like I’m hogging their time. One memorable park was Hopewell Furnace National Historic Site in Pennsylvania. We planned for a short visit, but ended up staying for hours and hours. We had the site almost all to ourselves because no one else wanted to brave the cold. The ranger spent lots of time answering our questions and talking about national parks and Junior Rangers.”
Batty and Falcon get to take the Junior Ranger Oath at each park after visiting, talking with rangers, and completing activities.

“I have a feeling of accomplishment when I take the Junior Ranger oath, especially when... I learned more than I expected to and had to work through some extra challenges,” said Falcon. “At most parks, the oath is very similar, but each park puts its own twist, depending on what the park is protecting and preserving.”
Getting to see places they might have missed and learning about people and parks they didn’t know about is one of the things they love most about the program.

“Recently I completed the Mary McLeod Bethune Council House National Historic Park Junior Ranger program,” said Batty. She wasn’t excited to visit this park at first because she didn’t know anything about it. Some national parks are famous, but others are small and not as well known. The activities to become a Junior Ranger — and the challenge of getting as many Junior Ranger badges as possible — help people to get to know lots of parks. “If it hadn’t been for the Junior Ranger program, I may never have gone there!”
Oldest “Junior” Ranger
Just because many of the people who become Junior Rangers are kids and teens, that doesn’t mean they all are! Junior Ranger programs are open to everyone.
Rose Torphy was 103 years old when she became a Junior Ranger at Grand Canyon National Park. She was three years older than the park itself!
Becoming a Junior Ranger is a great way to learn about the national parks. Perhaps, one day, you can level up to becoming a full ranger, as well.

options.label: Once you are a Junior Ranger, there are many opportunities for you to get a job as a park ranger.
Anyone can become a Junior Ranger by visiting, exploring, and learning about national parks.
Though it is a difficult job, being a Junior Ranger is very rewarding for both kids and adults.
To become a Junior Ranger, children must be physically strong and willing to take risks.

questions.prompt: What is the central idea of the article?

options.label: Falcon's experiences have made him feel proud.
Falcon's experiences have made him more caring.
Falcon's experiences have helped him as a blogger.
Falcon's experiences have helped him as a student.

questions.prompt: What does the word "accomplishment" in paragraph 10 suggest about Falcon's experiences as a Junior Ranger?

options.label: Batty began to see why visiting parks could be fun after completing the program.
Batty earned her final Junior Ranger badge after visiting this national park.
Batty tried to encourage others to learn about Mary McLeod Bethune.
Batty learned about a park she might not have visited otherwise.

questions.prompt: How did attending the Mary McLeod Bethune Council House National Historic Park Junior Ranger program affect Batty?

options.label: by describing the steps you need to take to become a national park ranger
by listing different problems in national parks and how rangers work to fix them
by describing how to become a Junior Ranger and sharing the experiences of Junior Rangers
by explaining what caused Batty and Falcon's parents to sign them up for the Junior Ranger program

How does the author support the idea that lots of people, not just kids, can benefit from being Junior Rangers?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, Batty and Falcon blog about their experiences at different national parks. Have you ever visited a national park? Describe your experience. If you have not visited a national park before, what is a park described in the text or that you have learned about that you would like to visit?

As stated in the text, the Junior Ranger program is available to anyone who wants to learn more about national parks. Would you like to take part in the Junior Ranger program? What do you think would be exciting or rewarding about participating in this program?

What important work do park rangers do? What is one part of the job of a park ranger that a Junior Ranger could help with? After reading about the job, would you like to be a park ranger? Why or why not?

articles.title: Moving Mountains
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke discusses the environmental activism of five young people.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, D.C. In this informational text, the author discusses the hard work of five special young people.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the young people in the text create change.
articles.permissions: "Moving Mountains" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: “How do you move a mountain? One spoonful of dirt at a time.” -Chinese proverb
Protecting endangered species, reversing climate change, and fighting habitat loss. All of these environmental challenges feel mountainous. They are big, controversial, and have no simple solutions.

But as the Chinese proverb reminds, how do you move a mountain? A spoonful of dirt at a time. These five teens — and many more worldwide — took small, spoonful-sized actions that had rippling impacts on their community and the planet.
ALLISON BOYER, CHICO, CALIFORNIA

When Allison was seven, she learned that orangutan habitats on the islands of Sumatra and Borneo were rapidly disappearing. Due to worldwide demand for palm oil — an oil found in about half of supermarket products — tropical forests on the Southeast Asian islands are being chopped down to make way for palm oil plantations. Deforestation has destroyed up to 80% of the orangutans’ rainforest homes in the last 20 years.

Despite living 8,000 miles from Sumatra and Borneo, Allison raised money to help seriously endangered orangutans. In 2011, when she was in the seventh grade, she started Purses for Primates , collecting second hand purses for resale at purse parties. Through selling bags, Allison raised $27,000 for Orangutan Outreach, an organization that protects orangutans in Sumatra and Borneo. Her fundraising helped fund one-third of an orangutan rescue center in Sumatra, providing care for orphaned and displaced orangutans.
LESEIN MUTUNKEI, NAIROBI, KENYA

As a child, Lesein loved the outdoors. He biked, hiked, and camped with his family. When in the countryside outside of Nairobi, Kenya, he planted trees with his grandparents, carving a home in the dirt for a tiny tree and using his hands to pack soil around its juvenile roots.

Lesein first learned about climate change and deforestation when he was twelve years old. His home country of Kenya loses about 29,650 acres of forest each year, according to Kenya’s Ministry of Environment and Forestry. Deforestation destroys animal habitats and leads to devastating mudslides that wipe out towns. In addition, trees — known as the world’s lungs — absorb carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. With fewer trees, more carbon dioxide hangs in the atmosphere and traps heat from the sun, exaggerating the climate crisis.

With a deep love of soccer, Lesein decided to plant a tree for every goal he scored in a match. Desiring to make a more significant impact, he soon planted eleven trees for each goal, one to represent each player on his team. Lesein founded Trees for Goals. The school’s soccer, rugby, and basketball teams all plant trees for points scored in games. In 2019, Leisein and his teammates planted almost 1,000 trees in forests outside of Nairobi.

Moving forward, Lesein hopes to expand Trees for Goals across Kenya and involve FIFA (Fédération Internationale de Football Association) in the project. He already gained support from Kenya's Ministry of Environment and Forestry. The ministry now donates the trees for the youth to plant.
XIUHTEZCATL MARTINEZ, BOULDER, COLORADO

Xiuhtezcatl (Shoe-Tez-Caht) Martinez first stepped on a stage to advocate for the environment when he was just six years old. Raised in the Aztec tradition, his family taught him to value the connection between people and nature. By age nine, he studied the consequences of spraying pesticides in public parks. Xiuhtezcatl spoke at city council meetings and organized press conferences to protest the spraying of chemicals.

He then turned his attention to fracking — the process of using pressure to extract natural gases from rocks underground. He witnessed fracking’s impact firsthand. He saw trees disappear as companies cleared the land for fracking. Wildfires became more common in Colorado, and his community evacuated to escape the flames. Xiuhtezcatl observed poorer air and water quality that made people sick.

Angered and motivated, Xiuhtezcatl used his voice to speak out. He advocates for reducing carbon dioxide emissions and protecting the environment. Xiuhtezcatl has given TedTalks and appeared on national TV. He spoke at the United Nations, delivering his speech in English, Spanish, and his native language, Nahuatl. He is a Youth Director for Earth Guardians and trains teens to lead environmental, climate, and social justice movements. Xiuhtezcatl writes and performs hip-hop to inspire young people to take action. Xiuhtezcatl shares, “From the beginning, there’s been an underlying understanding that my voice has power. If I share it in the right way, I can help share that power with other people.”
KRISTAL AMBROSE, ELEUTHERA ISLAND, BAHAMAS

Kristal Ambrose grew up on Eleuthera Island, one of the 700 islands in the Bahamas. As a teenager, Kristal spent two days pulling plastics out of a sea turtle's intestines. Shocked, she learned that 8 million metric tons of plastics enter the ocean each year.

At 22, Kristal boarded a boat headed for the Great Pacific Garbage Patch, a swirling mass of plastics and debris in the Pacific Ocean. She observed plastic bags, styrofoam cups, and straws churning in the Garbage Patch, all items she regularly used and tossed. She vowed to stop using these products and returned to Eleuthera Island compelled to reduce plastic waste.

In 2013, Kristal founded the Bahamas Plastic Movement. Her nonprofit worked to develop new solutions to plastic pollution and educate Bahamian youth about the issue. In addition, Kristal runs free youth camps to train the country’s next environmental leaders. During camp, students collect plastics on beaches, dissect fish to examine plastics in their stomachs, and connect how their lives on land impact the tropical waters. In January 2018, Kristal petitioned the island’s environmental minister to ban single-use plastics. Later that year, on Earth Day, the government announced a country-wide ban.
CELESTE TINAJERO, RENO, NEVADA

Celeste’s older brother inspired her environmental activism. He started an Eco Warriors club at Reed High School in Reno and encouraged Celeste to join. The club worked to make the school more sustainable, starting with the outdated bathrooms. Eco Warriors installed water-saving faucets and toilets, auto-sensor lights that turned off when bathrooms were empty, and hand dryers instead of paper towels. These updates saved water, electricity, and paper waste. The following year, Eco Warriors raised more money and installed a water bottle refilling station where students could easily refill reusable water bottles. The refilling station has saved an estimated 37,000 single-use plastic bottles.

After high school, Celeste worked at Keep Truckee Meadows Beautiful, a Reno-based organization focused on reducing waste and educating the community. At the nonprofit, Celeste designs curriculum for schools about sustainable living. For Celeste, starting small with Eco Warriors ballooned into a career focused on protecting the environment. In an interview with Teen Vogue , Celeste urges, “Work with a group of hardworking, willing, and determined people. Then create a compelling and efficient plan; the rest will follow. We can fight for all of the issues that we are passionate about forever, but if we don't have an Earth to survive us, none of it matters. Someone has to give our planet a voice.”

options.label: Most young people care more about the Earth than adults.
Most young people feel that it is their responsibility to save the Earth.
Young people who care deeply about the Earth can create great change.
Young people need to work together in order to create great change.

articles.author: J. Patrick Lewis

blocks.text: If you’re looking for good fiction,
Welcome to my jurisdiction
In the Dewey Decimal yard.
I can find the perfect story,
Humor (goofy), vampire (gory),
Novels (grand), adventure (glory)…
Let me see your library card.
No, there’s nothing more exciting
For someone who’s into writing
Than to fricassee a mind,
‘Cause a book is like an oven —
What it’s cookin’ is book lovin’.
Set the temperature, then shove in
Every brain cell you can find.
