articles.title: Louis Braille
articles.subtitle: Opening the Doors of Knowledge
articles.author: James Rumford
articles.description: Louis Braille invented a system of reading and writing for people who are blind.
articles.intro: People who are blind use a system of dots that represent letters to read and write called Braille. In this text, James Rumford tells how Louis Braille invented this system.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Louis Braille’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Louis Braille Opening the Doors of Knowledge" by James Rumford, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Two hundred years ago, if you were blind, you became a beg­gar. Or maybe, if you were lucky, a circus musician or a fortuneteller. Most seeing people pitied you because they thought you had only half their wits. Few understood that it was only your eyes that did not work. Your heart and your mind were like everyone else’s.

In 1784, a Frenchman named Haüy (ah-wee) opened a school in Paris for blind children. He spent all the money he had to prove that the blind could learn. He even invented special books with huge, raised letters so that they could read with their fingertips.

News of Haüy’s school spread across France, arriving one day in 1818 at the doorstep of a poor saddle maker named Simon Braille. Several years before, the saddle maker’s three-year-old son Louis had punctured his eye with a sharp, pointed awl while playing. Infection quickly sealed his eyes shut, and within days he was blind.

Everyone knew what was in store for the boy — a life of begging. But Louis’s father and mother would have none of that. The village priest and the village schoolteacher agreed, and they taught Louis, even though there were no books for blind children to read or ways for them to write. But Louis was bright and in two years had learned all he could at the village school.

That was when the village priest came knocking at the Brailles’ door with an astounding proposal: send Louis to Paris, to Haüy’s school. But how? There was no money. So the priest went to the most powerful man in the county, who wrote a letter to the school.
Within a few months, Louis had a scholarship and left his village of Coupvray (coo-vray) for a new life in Paris. He was only ten.

When Louis was twelve, an extraordinary man named Captain Barbier (bar-bee-ay) came to the school. He wanted to show off his invention, called “night writing.” This was a code of twelve raised bumps that made it possible for soldiers to send messages in the dark.
The sighted teachers called Captain Barbier a clever man, but, when Louis slid his fingers over the bumps, he whispered, “No, he’s a genius!”

In that instant, Louis realized “night writing” was a way for blind people to write down the thousands of ideas that filled their heads. No longer would they have to ask seeing people for help. And “night writing” was a way for blind people to read, really read, instead of slowly tracing their fingers over giant, raised letters.

But “night writing” needed work. Its clumps of dots took too much to write and were almost as hard as Haüy’s letters to read. For three years Louis punched out new combinations of dots, but nothing worked. Then one summer, when he was fifteen, he had a breakthrough. Instead of basing his letters on twelve dots, Louis based them on six.
This made his letters easy to write — and easy to read, for each letter was small enough to fit under a fingertip.

Louis couldn’t wait to teach his friends back at school. Within days of his return, his friends were gliding their fingertips across words they had written. No longer did they need sighted people to write for them. Louis’s dots meant freedom!

The head of the school, Director Pignier [peen-yay], welcomed Louis’s dot system, but some of the teachers scoffed at the boy’s foolishness. How could the blind learn without the help of the seeing? How could the blind lead the blind?
But Louis paid no attention. Before long, dot-filled books appeared in classrooms.

At age seventeen Louis was made a teacher at the school; at twenty-four, a full professor. His students filled his classes, and he in turn filled them with hope and the promise that they each had something valuable to contribute to the world.
Louis even used his dots to write down music. His students no longer had to memorize every piece. Now they could compose music as well. More freedom. More promise.

In 1840, Director Pignier was dismissed and a teacher named Dufau (dew-foe) took his place. Dufau didn’t like Louis’s dots. They made the students too independent. One summer, when Louis was back in Coupvray, Dufau introduced a simplified form of Haüy’s raised letters. Then, to make sure that everyone used the new system, he burned Haüy’s books and Louis’s as well. There was also a new rule: No more dot writing.
But no one gives up newly won freedom without a fight. Louis, even in his grief, knew that. The students defied the director. They wrote in secret. They passed notes at night.

When Dufau saw the support the students had for the dot system and their love for Louis, he gave in. Dot books and dot writing were back. For the next eight years, Louis continued to teach and his system of reading and writing flourished.

Then, in 1852, he became seriously ill and died. No newspaper mentioned his death. Only the school knew that a great man had passed away. They made a statue of their beloved teacher and wrote his life story. Slowly the world adopted his system. Slowly the world came to know of Louis Braille. In 1952, a hundred years after his death, his body was taken from Coupvray to Paris and buried alongside the heroes of France. That day, every newspaper in the world wrote about Louis, and thousands of blind people lined the streets to honor him. Louis Braille had given them the means to prove that their hearts and minds were like everyone else’s.

options.label: Louis Braille created a new way for blind people to read and write.
It is difficult for seeing people to understand Louis Braille's dot system.
Blind people today are able to read and write using a system called "night writing."
No one has been able to learn the dot system since Dufau burned the books explaining it.

articles.title: Rosh Hashanah: The Jewish New Year
articles.author: Debra Friedland Katz
articles.description: Debra Friedland Katz describes how her family celebrates Rosh Hashanah.
articles.intro: Rosh Hashanah is a Jewish holiday that celebrates the New Year. Jewish people speak the traditional, or passed down, language of Hebrew when celebrating their holidays. In this text, Debra Friedland Katz describes how her family celebrates Rosh Hashanah.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people celebrate Rosh Hashanah.
articles.permissions: "Rosh Hashanah: The Jewish New Year" by Debra Friedland Katz, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: It is Rosh Hashanah eve, the beginning of the Jewish New Year. I welcome my grandparents to our house with the traditional Rosh Hashanah greeting, L’Shanah Tovah (luh-SHAH-nah TOE-vah), Hebrew for “to a good year.” My grandparents return the greeting.

Rosh Hashanah means “head of the year” and celebrates the birthday of the world. It is one of the holiest days of the Jewish year and is observed for two days, beginning on the first day of the Hebrew month of Tishri, which falls in September or early October.

We gather around the dining room table for the blessings before our holiday dinner. Mom recites the blessing over two tall candlesticks. Dad holds up a goblet and says the blessing over the wine. Next, we recite the Shehecheyanu (sheh-heh-hee-YAH-noo), a blessing thanking God for enabling us to celebrate this special occasion.

I pick up the challah and begin the Hamotzi (hah-MOE-tzi), the blessing over the bread. Unlike the challah we eat on the Sabbath, which is long and braided, this challah is round, to symbolize the circle of life. Now it is time for apples and honey. We all say the blessing, take a piece of apple, and dip it into the honey. Yum! On Rosh Hashanah we eat sweet foods in the hope of a sweet New Year.
After dinner, I say goodbye to my grandparents, but I know I will see them tomorrow at synagogue. On Rosh Hashanah we don’t work or go to school. We spend the morning attending worship services.

Rosh Hashanah is a joyful holiday, but it is also a serious one. It is the beginning of the Days of Awe, which end 10 days later on Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement. During the worship service we pray that we are inscribed in the Book of Life for another year. We think about our behavior over the past year and about how we can do better in the next. It is also a time to ask for forgiveness from anyone we may have hurt. I think about times I may have been rude, bad-tempered, or argued with my mom.

The Torah, the Jewish holy book, commands us to hear the sound of the shofar (SHOW-far), a hollowed-out ram’s horn, on Rosh Hashanah. The ba’al tekiah (bah-al teh-KEE-uh), shofar blower, practices before the holiday to learn the different notes: tekiah, a short blast; shevarim (sheh-vah-REEM), a group of three blasts; and teruah (teh-ROO-ah), nine short blasts. During the service, our Rabbi calls the notes before each blast. The last blast, the tekiah gedolah (geh-DOE-lah) is the longest. The ba’al tekiah’s cheeks bulge and his face turns red as he holds the note until he is completely out of air. Wow! A murmur of awe and praise rises from the congregation when the blast finally ends.

In the afternoon, my family joins members of our congregation at a lake near our synagogue, for tashlich (tosh-LEEK). We empty our pockets of crumbs and toss them into the water as a symbol of casting off our sins of the past year.

Ten days pass quickly and it is Yom Kippur, a solemn day and the holiest day of the Jewish year. Adults fast from sundown the night before to sundown on Yom Kippur day. During the worship service we recite prayers, confess sins, ask God for forgiveness, and vow to do better in the coming year. We hear one long, final blast from the shofar. As my family leaves the synagogue at sundown to break the fast at my grandparents’ house, I look to the New Year with hope for peace and happiness.

options.label: by explaining how many families celebrate Rosh Hashanah
by telling what different people like about Rosh Hashanah
by describing how Jewish New Year is different from New Years in the United States
by showing how Rosh Hashanah is celebrated throughout the world

articles.title: A Movement's Soul
articles.author: Susan Hurley-Glowa and Cynthia Levinson
articles.description: The authors explain why singing was an important part of the civil rights movement.
articles.intro: Susan Hurley-Glowa is a professor of music and Cynthia Levinson writes books about young people standing up for justice. A movement is a group of people working together to reach a common goal. During the civil rights movement (1954-1968) African American leaders and their supporters fought to end racial injustice. In this text, the authors discuss the music of the civil rights movement.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how singing was an important part of the civil rights movement.
articles.permissions: "A Movement's Soul" by Susan Hurley-Glowa and Cynthia Levinson, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Some people described the civil rights era in the 1950s and 1960s as “the greatest singing movement in our nation’s history.” The Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., called music “the soul of the movement.”

How did songs come to play such an important role in the civil rights movement? How did singing together help the goals of creating a society with equal opportunity for all? Put simply, music united citizens from all walks of life in a new vision for the future.

Integrating singing into the cause was a deliberate choice by the movement’s organizers. It drew upon a rich cultural tradition of music-making in communities with African American roots. Singing spirituals together was a key motivating activity during meetings, church services, sit-ins, mass demonstrations, freedom rides, and jail time. Singing together made people feel strong. Singing inspired activists to stand up to the verbal and physical assaults, to the police dogs, and to the high-pressure fire hoses aimed at them. Singing spirituals united people and focused them on their goals — freedom and equality.

The movement’s songs were mostly updated traditional African American spirituals. “We Shall Overcome” is probably its most famous song. Two gospel songs — “I’ll Overcome Someday” and “If My Jesus Wills” — provided the basis for this unofficial civil rights anthem.

The song, changed slightly to “We Will Overcome,” was taught to labor organizers in the 1940s at the Highlander Folk School. By 1952, a recording of the song was released, but the words had been altered again to “We Shall Overcome” by folk singer Pete Seeger.

By the late 1950s, the song was being taught to civil rights activists at the Highlander. That’s where King first heard it. At the end of meetings, everyone would rise, join hands, and sing the anthem. Its text reaffirmed the movement’s goals: to stand united against all obstacles to freedom and equality, to face opposition with courage and strength, and to live in peace.
We shall overcome, we shall overcome,
we shall overcome someday;
oh, deep in my heart, I do believe,
we shall overcome someday.

Folk singer Joan Baez sang it to entertain and rouse the crowds at the historic March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom in August 1963. As the lyrics predicted, Blacks and whites joined hands. Today, “We Shall Overcome” has spread around the world and can be heard wherever freedom and justice are threatened.

Singing played a big part at the March on Washington. Moved by music’s message, this large political rally that took place at the nation’s capital began with a massive concert. As people gathered on the Mall, Black folk artist Odetta sang, “I’m On My Way to Freedom Land.” The white folk trio Peter, Paul, and Mary sang Bob Dylan’s “Blowin’ in the Wind.” The Albany Freedom Singers, a Black quartet, performed “We Shall Not Be Moved.” Baez and Dylan sang “Keep Your Eyes on the Prize.”

Perhaps most stirringly, the Black opera soprano Marian Anderson sang “He’s Got the Whole World in His Hands.” Twenty-three years earlier, Anderson had been barred from appearing at Constitution Hall in Washington, D.C., because she was Black. Then, Mahalia Jackson rose. “The Queen of Gospel” belted out “How I Got Over.”
How I got over,
how did I make it over,
you know my soul look back and wonder,
how did I make it over.
How I made it over,
going on over all these years,
you know my soul look back and wonder,
how did I make it over.

“Joshua Fit the Battle of Jericho” and “Free at Last” were other popular civil rights songs. Like “We Shall Overcome,” they inspired singers to continue with a difficult struggle. “Joshua Fit the Battle of Jericho” is a traditional African American spiritual that tells the biblical story of a man who brought down the walled city of Jericho despite overwhelming odds. The song represents the struggle for civil rights: resistance, faith, and ultimate victory over wrong. “Free at Last” sings of the dream and promise of freedom, urging everyone to join hands and to not give up hope. King ended his famous “I Have a Dream” speech at the March on Washington by quoting a line from this spiritual: “Free at last, free at last! Thank God Almighty, we are free at last!”

Taking a cue from the strengths of older African American traditions, music expressed the soul of the civil rights movement. Songs and singing proved essential nonviolent weapons in the struggle for freedom.

options.label: Songs based in African American history were important to the civil rights movement.
Singing is a way to deal with many emotions like love, pain, loss, and heartbreak.
Dr. Reverend Martin Luther King sang a song at the March on Washington.
Many important singers felt left out of the civil rights movement.

articles.title: What's the Buzz About Bees?
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains the important role bees play in the environment.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Tuke is a writer based in Washington, DC. In this article, she explains what bees do for the world around us.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people are both helping and harming bees.
articles.permissions: "What's the Buzz About Bees?" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Ouch! It stung me.
Bees may seem pesky — buzzing around sugary snacks, hiding in grasses, and defending with stings that inject itchy venom.

But did you know that bees are vital to growing the world’s food? According to the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), about every three mouthfuls of food that you eat depends on honey bees. They help produce foods like apples, watermelon, peppers, broccoli, peaches, berries, avocados, and even almonds.
BEES’ BIG BUSINESS

Honey bees are pollinators. Bees collect nectar from flowers. As they drink the sweet liquid, pollen clings to bees’ legs and bodies. By buzzing between plants, bees unknowingly move pollen from one plant to another. This movement of pollen fertilizes plants and allows them to produce seeds and fruits. Over 100 foods grown in the United States need bees to pollinate them, reports the USDA.

The USDA also shares that the country has 4,000 native species of bees, but wild bees cannot pollinate all of the country’s food — too many crops and not enough wild bees. So farmers call in professional beekeepers and their bee colonies to help pollinate fruits and vegetables.

Beekeepers and their colonies are quite the travelers. In February, beekeepers load their colonies on trucks and head west to California to pollinate almond trees. Then, beekeepers go south to pollinate citrus trees in Florida. Beekeepers transport their colonies up the east coast to work on apple, peach, and blueberries pollination in the summer. After months of travel, the bees return home for the winter.
BEES IN DANGER

Over the last 15 years, scientists noticed a significant decline in the number of honey bees, according to National Geographic . Between 2018 and 2019, the population of honey bees decreased by almost half. Scientists call this rapid decrease Colony Collapse Disorder. Colony Collapse Disorder happens when a colony’s worker bees vanish. Worker bees serve the colony by collecting food and protecting the hive. Without worker bees, the whole colony dies.

The leading cause of Colony Collapse Disorder is a deadly parasite called varroa mite. This parasite is a tiny, tick-like bug that attacks bees and feeds on their blood. Untreated, this parasite can kill a honey bee colony in just a few days.

Another threat to honey bees is pesticides or chemicals that farmers spray on crops to kill unwanted bugs. Although pesticides are not used to kill bees, some pesticides are harmful to these essential pollinators. Pesticides also kill weeds and flowers that grow at the edges of fields where bees collect nectar. Less food for bees leads to the collapse of bee colonies.
The death of honey bees means that fewer bees pollinate fruits and vegetables. And many of the foods we love and depend on could become more costly.
PROTECTING HONEY BEES

Beekeepers and scientists are working to save honey bees. Beekeepers are learning to identify and treat colonies for parasites. Scientists are figuring out how to replace harmful pesticides with more bee-friendly chemicals and how to spray pesticides in smaller doses, so that pesticides do not kill bees or the food they eat. Additionally, beekeepers and scientists encourage people to cultivate habitats that are healthy for bees. Gardening and planting wildflowers in communities provide bees with homes and food for their colony.

options.label: Bees can live in many different places in America.
Bee stings can hurt, but are usually not too harmful.
Bees are studied by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA).
Bees are important to our world and people want to find ways to save them.

"The USDA also shares that the country has 4,000 native species of bees, but wild bees cannot pollinate all of the country's food — too many crops and not enough wild bees." (Paragraph 5)
"Over the last 15 years, scientists noticed a significant decline in the number of honey bees, according to National Geographic." (Paragraph 7)
"The leading cause of Colony Collapse Disorder is a deadly parasite called varroa mite." (Paragraph 8)

questions.prompt: Which detail from the text explains why bees are disappearing?

options.label: Farmers think that bees are harmful, but beekeepers are trying to teach them that bees are helpful.
Farmers need bees for their crops to grow, and beekeepers bring bees to different farms.
Farmers take care of bees on their farms, but beekeepers want to leave them in nature.
Farmers have lots of bees to keep safe, and beekeepers come to their farms to help them.

questions.prompt: Which statement describes the relationship between farmers and beekeepers in the article?

options.label: are not moving very much pollen.
are not aware they have pollen on them.
are able to pollinate many plants quickly.
are able to make a lot of buzzing noises.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The author starts the story with "ouch!" How do you normally act when you see a bee? Now that you have learned about how important bees are to the planet, do you think you may act differently when you see one in the future? Why or why not?

The author suggests some ways that people can help bees, like planting gardens. What other ways can humans help bees? What ideas do you have of things that you could do at school or at home to help bees?
Imagine a world where there were no more bees. What might happen if this came true? How would your life be different?

articles.title: Taming Threats to the World's Largest Cats
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes the threats big cats face and what is being done to help them.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, DC. Large cats like the Siberian Tiger are in danger of going extinct, or no longer living on Earth. In this informational text, Pratt-Tuke describes the threats big cats face and what is being done to help them.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people’s actions help and harm large cats.
articles.permissions: "Taming Threats to the World's Largest Cats" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Ten feet long and weighing up to 600 pounds, Siberian tigers (also known as Amur Tigers) are the largest and one of the most critically endangered wild cats. Siberian tigers live in the cold climates of Russia and northern China. They endure long winters with eight months of snow and temperatures that fall to negative 4 degrees Fahrenheit. That’s colder than your freezer! Layers of body fat serve as thermal underwear. Shaggy manes and thick coats of fur keep out the chill. Extra hair on their paws acts as tiger snow boots. These cold-weather cats have amazing adaptations to survive a brutal environment.
ALMOST EXTINCT

During the early-1900s, Siberian tigers teetered on the edge of extinction. Habitat destruction from logging, a shrinking food supply, and hunting just about took down the world’s biggest cat. By the 1940s, only 40 Siberian tigers were left in the wild, according to BBC Earth . In response, the Soviet Union, now known as Russia, banned Siberian tiger hunting in 1947 and limited hunting of the tiger’s favorite foods like boar and deer. By the 1960s, the tiger population in Russia rebounded from 40 tigers to over 100.

But Siberian tigers are not the only tigers at risk. Bali and Caspian tigers: extinct. Sumatran and South China tigers: critically endangered. Bengal tigers: endangered. Like the Siberian tigers of the north, tigers across Asia have fallen victim to poaching due to the rising demand for tiger parts — noses, bones, organs, teeth, and pelts.
HEALTH AND WEALTH DOOM TIGERS

For thousands of years, traditional Chinese medicine has depended on tiger parts from across Asia to treat illnesses: people believed noses treated epilepsy, tiger bones healed ulcers, tiger tails cured skin diseases, and tiger whiskers soothed toothaches. In China, tigers symbolize strength and power, and people believed they could take on some of the tiger’s strength by using treatments made from their parts.

Throughout Asia, owning tiger products showcases wealth and status. Business people give tiger pelts as gifts when sealing a deal, and the wealthy decorate their homes with tiger pelts. Think of displaying a skinned tiger like flaunting a fancy sports car. The rich drink tiger bone wine, which sells for hundreds of dollars per bottle. Some believe that drinking this wine gives tiger-like strength and power.

As poaching of Asia’s tigers got out of control, CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora) banned the trade of tiger parts in 1987. But CITES relied on Asian countries to enforce the ban. They urged countries to punish poachers and to create laws that controlled the trade of tiger parts. Countries took little action, and little changed for tigers. Well, except for one change. National and international bans on poaching gave birth to a whole new industry — tiger farming.
FARM-RAISED TIGERS

The first tiger farm was founded in Russia in 1986. After that, hundreds of tiger farms popped up across China, Southeast Asia, and even South Africa. Worldwide, tiger farms keep nearly 8,000 tigers in captivity, more than double the population of tigers in the wild, according to National Geographic.

On these farms, tigers — who are used to roaming hundreds of miles — stay locked in small cages. Female tigers speed breed, having tiger cubs as fast as possible. In the wild, a tiger cub stays with its mother until it is two years old. On tiger farms, cubs are taken from their mothers soon after birth so that the female can give birth to another litter. Many tiger farms operate as zoos where tourists pay to pet tigers or feed tiger cubs bottles. Although snuggling a tiger cub sounds irresistible, tourists’ money fuels tigers’ captivity. According to National Geographic , in 2016, the Thailand government raided the Tiger Temple, a zoo that doubled as a tiger farm. Officials seized 137 adult tigers; found glass jars full of tiger bones, organs, and teeth ready to be sold; and uncovered 40 dead tiger cubs.

Sadly, tiger farms have increased the demand for tiger parts and, in turn, increased poaching of wild tigers. Some people believe that wild tigers are more powerful than farmed tigers and are willing to pay poachers top dollar for wild tiger parts.
PROMISING PROTECTIONS FOR TIGERS

China, the number one consumer of tiger-based products, took action and banned the international trade of tiger parts in 1993. Chinese scientists and doctors removed tiger ingredients from their lists of drugs used to treat illnesses. And in 2010, the World Federation of Chinese Medicine Societies announced that treatments made from tiger parts had no medical benefits. Tiger whiskers do not actually cure toothaches. Throughout the 1990s, the Chinese government led public campaigns to discourage the use of tiger parts and reinforce that they did not cure diseases.

In 2010, thirteen countries and conservation organizations set a goal—double the number of wild tigers by 2020. At that time, an estimated 3,200 tigers lived across thirteen Asian countries. Although countries did not reach this ambitious goal, the future looks hopeful for tigers. Today, the worldwide population has increased to 3,900, reports World Wildlife Fund (WWF).

As for Siberian tigers, their population has grown 15% since 2010 due to Russia protecting and restoring the tigers’ habitat. Once almost extinct, today, conservationists estimate about 540 Siberian tigers roam the frigid north, according to BBC .

options.label: There is very little that can be done to help save large cats like the Siberian Tiger.
Governments play an important part in helping to protect large cats.
Large cats are better off in zoos and tiger farms than in the wild.
Poachers have stopped selling the body parts of large cats.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?

options.label: how rare it is to spot a Siberian Tiger in the wild

when Siberian Tigers started to disappear and why
why people are interested in poaching Siberian Tigers

options.label: Tigers are struggling because people do not have the medicine or money to save them.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Poaching, or the illegal killing of animals, has killed many large cats. In fact, many have gone or are in danger of going extinct, or no longer existing. Why do you think large cats have come to symbolize wealth in many countries and cultures? What are some things that symbolize wealth to you? Why might that be?

In the article, the author describes what happens at tiger farms. Do you think that people should be able to keep tigers on farms like this? How are these farms different from zoos? Would you want to visit one of these tiger farms? Why or why not?

Seeing big cats, like the Siberian Tiger, in the wild is rare. If these cats are hard to find, why do you think it is important for the world to protect them? How might the world be different without the Siberian Tiger?

articles.title: The Plastic Problem
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains the problems that plastics cause for the earth's environment.
articles.intro: Plastics are a part of everyday life, but they can cause problems for the planet. In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke shares the problems with plastics.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view about plastics.
articles.permissions: "The Plastic Problem" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: I grab the last few strawberries from the fridge and cut them for breakfast, tossing them in a bowl and tossing their plastic container in the trash. I pull two slices of bread from a plastic bag, heat them in the toaster, and then load the browned bread with peanut butter scooped from a plastic jar. Next, I pour a cup of orange juice from its plastic Tropicana container to wash down the thick, sticky peanut butter.
Pause and count. How many plastics did you use as you got ready for school today?
Plastic products are so convenient. Use them, toss them, and forget about them.
BUT, SHOULD WE FORGET ABOUT PLASTICS?

Plastics are not biodegradable, meaning they do not decompose. Instead, they break into ever-tinier pieces — each less than five millimeters long — called microplastics. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) reports “every bit of plastic ever made still exists.” So in fifty years, your morning strawberry container will still be around as a handful of sesame seed-sized fragments of plastic.

Because plastics do not break down, they accumulate in landfills, and many end up in the Earth's oceans. About 8.8 million tons of plastics are dumped in the ocean every year, according to National Geographic . This is comparable to a garbage truck dumping a load full of debris into the sea every minute. And scientists predict the number of plastics swimming in the seas will grow — two garbage truckloads per minute by 2030 and four per minute by 2050.

In the Pacific Ocean, the Great Pacific Garbage Patch is a spinning vortex of plastics and debris, like a junkyard in the ocean. The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is twice the size of Texas, and the swirling mass of trash can be seen from outer space.

As plastics accumulate in Earth’s oceans, marine creatures feel the consequences. Fish, seabirds, turtles, and marine mammals mistakenly eat plastics or get tangled in plastic products like nets, bags, and soda rings. Both consuming plastics and entanglement can cause injury or death. According to the United Nations, marine debris — 80% of this debris coming from plastics — affects at least 800 species worldwide.

Researchers estimate that half of all sea turtles have eaten plastic. Loggerhead turtles eat plastic bags thinking they are jellyfish. Green turtles consume smaller plastic bits hidden in algae. A study led by the University of Tokyo found that loggerhead turtles ate 17% of plastics they came across in the ocean, and green turtles ate 62%. Plastics fill turtles’ bellies and cause starvation as they mistakenly believe they ate enough to satisfy their hunger.

Plastics also kill up to a million seabirds each year, including the Laysan Albatross. With a wingspan of over six feet, the large seabird flies over oceans and skims its beak across the top of the water to catch fish. As it catches fish, the bird accidentally collects plastics. When consumed, plastics’ sharp edges can pierce internal organs, causing injury and death. Like turtles, seabirds can consume so many plastics that they have little room left for food. According to National Geographic , in 1960, scientists found plastics in the stomachs of only 5% of seabirds, but as humans’ consumption of plastics rose, so did the amount found in birds’ stomachs. By 1980, 80% of seabirds carried plastics in their bellies.
WHAT CAN BE DONE TO SOLVE THE PLASTIC PROBLEM?

Although there is not a simple answer, scientists and environmentalists are developing new ideas to address the plastic problem. Some scientists are working on developing biodegradable replacements for plastics. A group of researchers from Harvard created a plastic-like substance made from shrimp shells. In Indonesia, a company replaced plastic bags and soap wrappers with packaging made from seaweed.

Instead of engineering plastic replacements, others are taking on the collection of plastics in landfills and oceans. Microbiologists from Pakistan tried using mushroom enzymes to break down plastics in garbage dumps more quickly. Engineers in the Netherlands developed a nearly 2,000-foot long ocean garbage truck, which travels the waters and collects plastics. Known as System 001, this ocean trash collector is heading to the Great Pacific Garbage Patch for some cleanup. A nonprofit organization called The Plastic Bank pays community members for plastics that they collect. By incentivizing plastic pick-ups, The Plastic Bank prevents these materials from ending up in the ocean and recycles the collected plastics into new products.

Environmentalists also recommend that citizens can take action to decrease their consumption of ever-convenient plastics. Environmentalists urge citizens to reduce their use of single-use plastics, plastics used once and then tossed. For example, people can invest in a water bottle rather than drinking water from plastic bottles or use reusable grocery bags rather than plastic ones. Worldwide, 73% of beach litter comes from single-use plastics, like plastic straws, cups, bags, and utensils, reports National Geographic .

Secondly, environmentalists encourage individuals to recycle plastics. In the United States, only 9% of all plastics are recycled, meaning the vast majority of plastics end up in landfills. Recycled plastics can be repurposed into new products — suitcases, furniture, new bottles, laundry detergent, and much more. But it is important to sort recyclables from trash carefully. Tom Szaky, CEO of a recycling company, said, “Typically, 50% of what you put in your recycling bin is never recycled. It's sorted and thrown out. This is partly due to user error, a common problem which occurs when people place unrecyclable materials into recycling bins.”

options.label: No one but scientists knows how to fix the problem with single-use plastics.
Plastics are very easy to use but cause a lot of harm to the environment.
There are better ways to make plastic than the way we make it now.
Everyone should use plastics because they are so convenient.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: by showing how people can make a difference by not using plastic
by giving a real life example of how people use single-use plastics
by telling how scientists use less plastic than other people
by describing why using plastic to store food is bad

questions.prompt: How does the author introduce the topic of the article?

options.label: People who try to clean up the Great Pacific Garbage Patch often hurt the sea animals' homes by accident.
When sea animals eat plastic from the Great Pacific Garbage Patch they can get very sick and die.
The Great Pacific Garbage Patch sucks sea animals inside of it so they cannot get out.
The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is so big that it can be seen from outer space.

questions.prompt: Why is the Great Pacific Garbage Patch a problem for sea animals?

options.label: know how many sea turtles have eaten plastic.
guess how many sea turtles have eaten plastic.
want to find out which sea turtles have eaten plastic.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The author opens the article with a story about her morning breakfast. Think of the products you use every day. How many of them are plastic? Are you contributing to the plastic problem? What could you change to help lessen the amount of plastic you use? Do you think lessening your use of plastic is helpful?

In recent years, there has been a push to use more sustainable, or long lastic, products instead of single use plastics. For example, many people now use metal or rubber straws instead of plastic straws after a successful campaign to "save the sea turtles." Why do you think connecting one single use plastic product to harming one animal in particular is successful? Can you think of an animal that is affected by plastics? How could you encourage people to eliminate plastics?

articles.title: A Bitingly Dangerous Job
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains venom milking.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, DC. Venom milking is the process of taking the venom, or poison, out of a snake. In this text, Pratt-Tuke explains the important job of venom milking.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why venom milking is important.
articles.permissions: "A Bitingly Dangerous Job" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license."

blocks.text: You’ve probably heard of milking cows, but did you know some people milk snakes? Snakes don’t produce the nutrient-rich, white milk that we pour over morning cereal, but they can be milked for their venom. Hold a snake firmly at the base of its head, provoke it to bite down on a venom-collecting vial, and squeeze while a yellowish liquid fills the tube. To be clear, do not try this at home! Snake milking is a job for highly-trained professionals known as venom (or snake) milkers.
WHY MILK SNAKES

Venom milkers extract venom from the deadliest snakes in the world. But why? Hospitals worldwide need snake venom to create antivenom used in saving the lives of humans suffering from poisonous snake bites. According to the World Health Organization, every year, over 2.7 million people are bitten by venomous snakes, and 100,000 die as a result. Antivenom helps the body produce antibodies to fight the snake’s poison.

Medical research also relies on snake venom. Researchers have already found that snake venom can treat blood clots, lower blood pressure, and prevent heart attacks. Scientists are now experimenting with venom treatments for tumors, Alzheimer's, and strokes. Viprinex, a medicine containing pit viper venom, is used to increase the window of treatment for stroke victims. Extra hours between having a stroke and getting treatment could mean the difference between life and death. But all of this would be impossible without venom milkers.
NOT JUST A PAYCHECK, BUT A PASSION

Venom milking is tedious work. Milkers follow strict procedures when extracting venom so that the poison stays uncontaminated and so that the milker stays safe. Venom milkers tend to be curious, methodical, and adventurous. And these professionals must love scaly, slithering reptiles.

You'd have to be adventurous and love snakes to put your fingers inches from the fangs of vipers, cobras, mambas, and rattlesnakes. One mistake, and now the venom milker needs antivenom. Jim Harrison, the owner of the Kentucky Reptile Zoo, has milked venomous snakes for decades. He has been bitten countless times. Most have been dry bites, where the snake did not inject venom, but he has survived nine venomous bites. Four bites sent Harrison to the hospital. Still, Harrison keeps milking. He loves snakes and believes that milking is essential. “Most people think of snake venom and associate it with death,” Harrison said in an interview with Business Insider. “I think of it with life because it truly can help save people.”

In 2015, Harrison collaborated with Animal Venom Research International (AVRI) to develop antivenom that would help snake-bite victims living in the South Asian country of Sri Lanka. This country has the world's highest snakebite death rate, with many victims being children. Harrison's milking is motivated by his belief in the curative power of snake venom and a deep desire to help others.
A VENOM MILKER’S DAY

Venom milkers often work in serpentariums, labs with hundreds of glass tanks full of snakes. Most serpentariums operate inside zoos and universities, although some venom milkers are entrepreneurs and own independent snake milking businesses. Each day, venom milkers extract the toxin from snakes using specialized laboratory equipment. Harrison estimates that he completes between 600 and 1,000 venom extractions each week.

After extracting the potent venom, milkers freeze-dry the substance to store and transport the venom to researchers, labs, and hospitals. Snake venom is valuable. One gram of snake venom can sell for up to $2,000. Between extractions, milkers maintain the lab equipment used during milking and care for the snakes living in the serpentarium. They feed snakes, clean tanks, and ensure that all snakes stay healthy. Venom milkers also breed snakes because more snakes mean more venom.
BECOMING A VENOM MILKER

To become a professional snake milker, students need to attend college and study biology, chemistry, or herpetology, the study of reptiles. Many venom milkers also hold master's degrees and PhDs in biology, chemistry, or zoology. Generally, snake milkers make around $2,500 per month.

However, venom milking is a niche career, and very few positions open up each year. An article in Applied Herpetology estimated that only 34 labs in the world have venom milkers. Such a small group of specialized scientists helps milk all the venom to develop antivenom and advance medical research.

options.label: Venom milking helps people be less fearful of snakes and reptiles.
Venom milking helps hospitals and research labs make money.
Venom milking is necessary to keep snakes healthy.
Venom milking is a rare but important job.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a main idea in the text?

"Researchers have already found that snake venom can treat blood clots, lower blood pressure, and prevent heart attacks." (Paragraph 3)
"Most have been dry bites, where the snake did not inject venom, but he has survived nine venomous bites. Four bites sent Harrison to the hospital." (Paragraph 5)
"Harrison estimates that he completes between 600 and 1,000 venom extractions each week." (Paragraph 7)

articles.title: Grizzly Encounters with Bears
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes problems that occur when bears and humans have contact.
articles.intro: Grizzly bears are large brown bears that live in North America. They can weigh between 800-1,700 pounds and can be up to 8 feet tall when standing on their back legs. In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes problems that can happen when bears and humans meet.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author introduces the grizzly bear.
articles.permissions: "Grizzly Encounters with Bears" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Yellowstone National Park — a bucket list trip! Geysers. Mountains. Waterfalls. Wildlife. And your family gets to experience Yellowstone 24-7 for the next three days as you camp inside of the park.

When you enter the campground, you pull up to the ranger station. The park ranger welcomes you, but before handing over your campsite number and map, the ranger gives you the “the talk.” What’s the talk? Well, it goes something like this...

“Welcome to Yellowstone! This is bear country, a habitat for 700 grizzly bears. For your safety and the safety of the bears, keep your campsite clean and store food and scented products like toothpaste, body wash, and sunscreen in the bear lockers. Every time you eat, please pick up crumbs and garbage. Throw away all trash in bear-proof trash containers. And never, never feed a bear. When bears eat human food, they may return looking for more and even become aggressive."

Grizzly bears do not mean to harm — or even bother — humans. When bears and humans meet, grizzlies often are just looking for their next meal. These bears have an incredible sense of smell — 2,100 times better than humans. But their noses and appetites get them into trouble when they raid campsites, livestock pastures, chicken coops, and backyard trash cans. Sadly, conflict between bears and humans can be life-threatening for these giant, brown bears.
A BRIEF HISTORY OF GRIZZLIES

Grizzlies once roamed from California to Missouri and from Mexico to Alaska. But as Westward Expansion brought more people west during the 1800s and 1900s, people transformed bear country. Humans built towns, farms, ranches, roads, and a transcontinental railroad. They drove bears from their habitat and killed bears that they believed threatened their property. In the early 1900s, bear hunting became popular, and outdoorspeople sought bears for their coats and as stuffed trophies.

By the 1920s and 1930s, grizzly bears occupied less than 2% of their native habitat. Come the 1960s, only 600 to 800 grizzlies remained in the lower 48 states. With diminishing numbers, the U.S. Endangered Species Act (ESA) listed grizzly bears as threatened in 1975.

Under ESA protections, grizzly bear numbers have grown in the last 50 years. According to National Geographic , between 1,400 and 1,700 grizzly bears now inhabit the contiguous United States. Grizzly bears shine as a conservation success story with their numbers increasing fivefold since 1975. They now live in the mountains and plains of Idaho, Montana, Wyoming, and Washington.
HUMAN-BEAR CONFLICT

But these northwest lands changed dramatically since grizzly bears last inhabited them. Today grizzlies find ever-expanding towns, farms, ranches, and roads in what was once vast, open space. As a result, conflict between humans and bears has increased in recent decades. The Yellowstone region reported over 100 grizzly human-bear encounters in 2018.

Sadly, when humans and grizzlies meet, the bears almost always lose. Many westerners that live in grizzly territory consider the bears aggressive and bothersome. Every year, wildlife managers euthanize dozens of bears that disturb property in search of food, giving meaning to the saying “a fed bear is a dead bear.” Montana Public Radio reported that 46 grizzly bears were killed and five bears relocated due to human-bear conflict in 2018 alone. According to National Geographic , in November 2020, two grizzlies were shot and dumped on a roadside. The following week in Montana, a second dead grizzly bear was found dead with its legs cut off, another victim of human-bear conflict.
FROM CONFLICT TO CO-EXISTENCE

Conservationists advocate that humans and grizzlies must coexist. Striving for coexistence means first changing human thoughts and feelings about bears. State and nonprofit organizations educate the public about grizzly bears and how to stay safe during bear encounters. The Montana Bear Education Working Group hands out bear spray, a non-lethal spray that sends bears running, at community events. With the help of a robotic bear that charges like a real one, bear educators demonstrate how to use the spray in close encounters with grizzlies.

Defenders of Wildlife, a conservation organization that protects animals and plants throughout America, helped ranchers build electric fences that zap grizzly bears that hunt cattle, sheep, and chickens. Defenders also purchased food storage lockers for the U.S. Forest Service to add to campgrounds around the northwest and helped fence garbage dumps to keep bears out.

James Jonkel, a biologist for Montana Fish, Wildlife, and Parks explains what must happen so that people and grizzlies can coexist. “The ideal is to have a clean, attractant-free [food and smells that attract bears] landscape where bears can pass through without learning bad habits,” says Jonkel. ​​With purposeful action to protect bears from tasty smells and food, communities and bears can live peacefully together.

options.label: Since humans and bears now live in the same areas, many problems happen when they meet.
When bears come onto humans' land, humans have no choice but to kill them.
Many people have been hurt by bears who came onto their land.
It is not possible for humans and bears to live together.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the section "Human-Bear Conflict"?

options.label: humans moving across the country in large numbers.
humans creating parks for people to visit.
humans leaving trash at campsites.
humans hunting bears for sport.

questions.prompt: The grizzly bear population was first threatened by —

options.label: how much the land has changed.
how quickly the land has changed.
how the land has been celebrated.
how many people have left the land.

questions.prompt: Re-read this sentence from paragraph 8, "But these northwest lands changed dramatically since grizzly bears last inhabited them." In this sentence, "dramatically" refers to —

options.label: "They now live in the mountains and plains of Idaho, Montana, Wyoming, and Washington." (Paragraph 7)
"The Yellowstone region reported over 100 grizzly human-bear encounters in 2018." (Paragraph 8)
"The Montana Bear Education Working Group hands out bear spray, a non-lethal spray that sends bears running, at community events." (Paragraph 10)
"With purposeful action to protect bears from tasty smells and food, communities and bears can live peacefully together." (Paragraph 12)

articles.title: A Dog's Best Friend
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains how trainers work with dogs for different purposes.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, DC. In this text, Pratt-Tuke explains the job of dog training.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what different types of dog trainers do.
articles.permissions: "A Dog's Best Friend" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: “Small but mighty” accurately describes Halo, a Dutch Shepherd puppy. Despite being the runt in her litter, Halo showed drive and intelligence at a young age. So when Halo was just a few months old, Fire Captain Cat Labrada handpicked Halo to train as a Florida Task Force 1 search and rescue dog. The Task Force had high expectations for the pup, and Halo had big shoes — or, more accurately, paws — to fill. Animal trainer Labrada had the job of preparing Halo for the elite team.

Flooding from hurricanes, collapsed buildings after an earthquake, or tangled cars in a massive crash. Labrada and her specially-trained dogs are there, searching disaster zones for victims. To prepare for this life-saving work, Labrada and Halo trained around the country. They even spent time at Disaster City, a fake town designed for response teams to practice in life-like, challenging conditions. After three years of tireless work, Halo must pass a rigorous national exam to serve as a Florida Task Force 1 search and rescue dog.
CONSISTENCY, SENSITIVITY, AND TRUST

When asked what it took to train Halo, Labrada shared, “Positive reinforcement and consistency — over and over.” Labrada taught Halo by disguising training as a game of hide-and-seek. In this training game, one trainer hid while Labrada worked with Halo to search using her incredible nose, which can detect scents 100,000 times better than human noses. Once Halo found the trainer, she barked, and the hidden trainer popped out to reward Halo with ear scratches, praise, and playtime. As Halo’s searching skills grew, Labrada ratcheted up the difficulty. She added noise or food smells so Halo could learn to ignore distractions and focus on the lost person’s scent. Labrada and Halo played hide-and-seek on repeat for three years. On average, training a search and rescue dog takes over 600 hours, and trainers must practice consistency and patience as dogs learn.

As a dog, Halo cannot advocate for herself, so Labrada must be sensitive to Halo’s needs. She notices when Halo is tired, frustrated, or overly excited. Labrada decides when to challenge Halo to build her skill and stamina and when Halo needs a break. Labrada also helps manage Halo’s stress during missions. Searches and rescues are grueling. Once Florida Task Force 1 worked countless hours over six days to find eleven people hidden in rubble. And sometimes searches fail. Dogs hunt but are unable to recover victims. Both dogs and trainers feel defeated. Labrada uses her relationship with Halo and observations of her behavior to help Halo manage stress and pressure.

Halo is more than a search and rescue dog. She is Labrada’s beloved pet and her respected teammate in saving lives. “My dog and I are a team,” explained Labrada in an interview with KidPost. “Together, we can do things that we can’t do separately. The bond, that trust, is actually the real superpower.” Trainers need to develop a strong relationship and mutual trust with their animals. Halo needs to trust that Labrada can lead and protect her. Likewise, Labrada needs to trust that Halo will obey and follow the training.
TRAINERS OF ALL KINDS

Training search and rescue dogs is just one type of animal training. Trainers prepare animals for all kinds of special jobs, from working with the disabled, performing for audiences, acclimating wild animals to zoo life, and teaching pets obedience.

Service dog trainers prepare canines to assist people with disabilities. These trainers look for dogs with balanced personalities — not too timid but not too strong-willed, not overly aggressive or submissive. They chose dogs that are focused and eager to please. Some service dog trainers train guide dogs to assist the blind or the visually impaired. Other trainers prepare dogs to help people with epilepsy. These dogs learn to bring medication, alert a caretaker of a seizure, or push a button to call for help in an emergency. Still other service dog trainers prepare emotional support animals as companions to people suffering from anxiety, depression, or post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).

Show trainers work with animals that perform for audiences, like dolphins at SeaWorld or animals that act in movies. While filming the movie Nim’s Island , show trainers worked with sea lions, pelicans, and lizards on the movie set. The two sea lions, who together played the role of Selkie, arrived trained and ready to woo audiences with flipper waves, flirtatious eyes, and blown kisses. But these show trainers had to prepare the sea lions to perform under the intense lights and cameras of a movie set. And the trainers had to help them build a fast friendship with co-star Abigail Breslin, who played Nim. Their greatest challenge was keeping the sea lions excited about performing the same trick repeatedly as camera crews filmed multiple takes before finally shouting, “that’s a wrap.”

Other animal trainers work with exotic animals at zoos, wildlife parks, and aquariums. One of their responsibilities is teaching animals how to respond during veterinary visits. For example, they teach elephants where to place their feet during a medical exam to keep both elephants and veterinarians safe. Zoo trainers use positive reinforcement to teach animals to step on and off scales, to cooperate with ultrasounds, and to say “ahhh” when getting their teeth cleaned. In addition, zoo trainers feed and exercise animals, clean their habitats, keep records of their behavior, and lead animal demonstrations to educate visitors.

The most common type of trainer is an obedience trainer. Obedience trainers work with dogs and their owners to help dogs learn to behave and listen to commands. Obedience trainers have a deep understanding of dog behavior and use rewards and practice to train dogs incrementally or break bad habits. A major part of the job is educating owners and helping people change their behavior so that their dog's behavior will change too.

Training animals may sound exciting, but the job isn't always games and glamour. Trainers work irregular hours, including nights, weekends, and long shifts. And trainers handle a lot of poop. They often are responsible for animal hygiene and cleaning habitats. For elephant trainers, that’s moving 300 pounds of dung each day. Animal training is physically demanding. Trainers need to lift, bend, run, crawl, and kneel alongside animals as they train. Trainers of marine mammals like dolphins and whales must be strong swimmers, and some jobs require trainers to have a scuba certification.
TRAINING FOR TRAINERS

Just like there are many kinds of trainers, the qualifications for training animals vary as well. At a minimum, trainers need a high school diploma, but many seek additional schooling and credentials. Currently, 44% of trainers have a high school diploma, and 31% hold a certificate or associate’s degree. Before training search and rescue dogs, Labrada worked as an animal trainer at SeaWorld. Then after a stint at a veterinary clinic and falling in love with an adopted dog, Labrada completed an Associate's Degree in Emergency Medical Services and joined the Miami fire department.

Zoo trainer Lora Gillock earned a Bachelor’s Degree in Zoology from Colorado State University. But she admits most of her learning came through on-the-job experience. In an interview with the Denver Zoo, Gillock shared, “I highly recommend that anyone interested in becoming a zookeeper do as much volunteering or internships in the field to gain experience and knowledge about the job.”
As for Halo, Labrada’s dedicated training paid off. Halo passed FEMA’s search and rescue exam and now serves on the Florida Task Force 1, saving victims from natural and man-made disasters.

articles.title: Narwhals: Nature's Climatologists
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains what narwhals are teaching scientists about climate change.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, D.C. Climatologists are scientists who study changing weather patterns and global climate change. In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes what these rare ocean animals are helping scientists learn.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what scientists want to learn from narwhals.
articles.permissions: "Narwhals: Nature's Climatologists" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Narwhals look like mythical creatures. Captivating and curious. Seemingly part whale and part unicorn, complete with a ten-foot horn. Did you know the horn is actually their left front tooth growing through the narwhal’s upper lip?

Although very real animals, narwhals are elusive like mythical beings—such as fairies, leprechauns, and mermaids. They live in the remote Arctic oceans surrounding Greenland, Canada, Norway, and Russia. Narwhals can swim easily under Arctic ice because they lack dorsal fins (the fins that sit on top of fish, dolphins, and whales). And they can dive one mile below the ocean’s surface — deeper than other marine mammals. So even researchers who dedicate their lives to studying narwhals say glimpsing these shy creatures is a rare treat.
Surviving Melting Ice

Narwhals depend on Arctic ice for food and shelter. In October, they migrate to Baffin Bay, off the coast of Greenland, where they spend their winters. They dive and hunt fatty fish that live in the ice-covered waters and shield themselves from predators in the Bay that is 95% covered in ice.

But climatologists report that the far north is warming twice as fast as other parts of Earth. Over the last 25 years, Baffin Bay’s perennial ice (ice that remains all year and never melts) shrunk by 9% each decade, according to Science Buzz . This means the ice shrank 9% between 1993 and 2003, then another 9% from 2003 to 2013, and another 9% in the next ten years.

Scientists warn that shrinking sea ice threatens narwhals. For instance, orcas hunt narwhals, but these large killer whales cannot travel through ice-covered waters. Without the protection of ice, narwhals might more often be orca lunch. In addition, melting ice opens paths for large ships to travel through Arctic waters. Noise and pollution from these ships could disturb the narwhal’s habitat by scaring away their fishy food, interrupting narwhal echolocation, and bothering the shy unicorns of the sea. As reported in The New Yorker , a group of researchers tested this theory by strapping a heart-rate monitor and listening device to a narwhal named Nemo. They tracked Nemo as he swam, hunted, and talked to narwhals in his pod. The researchers observed that when noisy ships passed through the Arctic, Nemo fell silent—no vocalizing to communicate or hunt—for 24 hours.

But, narwhals are fighting back against climate change…even if they don’t realize it. Scientists are using these marine mammals to study how fast the Arctic is warming and the impacts of a shifting climate.
Data-Collecting Divers
Studying the impacts of climate change in the Arctic is tricky, mainly because the Arctic is vast and remote. Measuring water temperatures and glacial melting costs scientists lots of time and money.

So in 2005, Kristin Laidre, a marine ecologist, had an idea — let’s use narwhals deep-sea diving to help collect data. Laidre and her team attached tags with thermometers to fourteen narwhals. The tags read water temperatures as narwhals swam under the ice. Laidre used narwhals to measure temperatures in the Baffin Bay, where narwhals spend their winters.

Later in 2018, she partnered with NASA’s Oceans Melting (hilariously nicknamed Project OMG) to track the impacts of global warming. Narwhal data helps NASA predict glacier melt and rising sea levels more accurately.
Tusks that Talk

Narwhal tusks are also teaching scientists about the impacts of climate change. Narwhals live up to 50 years, and their tusks grow as they age. Think of their tusks like a tree trunk. Scientists can read the rings of a tree over time to learn about the tree’s environment. Similarly, scientists can read the layers of a narwhal’s tusk to learn about their oceanic world.

According to Wired , an international team of researchers studied the tusks of ten narwhals and uncovered data about the Arctic climate going back to 1962. The tusks showed that narwhals’ diets have changed. Before 1990, narwhals ate prey like Arctic cod and halibut in oceans covered in thick sea ice. But in the last 20 years, tusks showed that narwhals shifted to eating fish from open waters (water with only small amounts of sea ice). Scientists hypothesize that this dietary shift is a result of climate change. As the ice melts, cod and halibut populations decline, or these fish move farther north to colder waters.

Either way, this change in the kinds of fish narwhals eat means fewer calories for the narwhal. Arctic cod and halibut are fatty fish. Less cod and halibut, fewer blubbery pounds for the narwhal. As a result, climate change could impact their energy and ability to survive in the frigid Arctic.

Scientists are still exploring theories about why narwhals’ diets are changing. But, one thing is for sure, using narwhal tusks to study climate holds promise. By examining one tusk, scientists can unlock decades of data. And there is potential to look even further back than 1962. Natural history museums around the world have narwhal tusks, some that are more than 100 years old. Think of all the stories these tusks can tell about the Arctic waters.

articles.title: Livin' it up… In Space?
articles.author: Marie Droual
articles.description: Marie Droual explores the possibility of humans one day living in space.
articles.intro: Marie Droual is a former scientist who loves sharing the wonders of the natural world with everyone. She is based in New York City. In this text, Droual explains how we could one day live in space.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view about living in space.
articles.permissions: "Livin' it up… In Space" by Marie Droual. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Have you ever wanted to live somewhere different from where you do now? To see a new neighborhood, eat different food, do new activities? Have you ever considered moving into...space? Though it sounds like something from a science fiction movie, this might someday become a reality. As we learn more about space, scientists are exploring future possibilities of sending humans into space to live permanently — new homes orbiting Earth or even on a completely different planet.

Why leave Earth? We have everything we need here already, right? With our changing climate and increasing population, some people think that we need to look into space for more, well, space and resources. For others, the desire propelling humans into space is simply one to push our limits and explore the unexplored of the cosmos.

Where should we make our new home? How about just orbiting in space around Earth? Technically, there are actually already people living in space. Astronauts from countries all over the world live on the International Space Station (ISS), which orbits Earth. It’s used by astronauts as a research facility for all things space related, like astronomy and physics. The astronauts only live there temporarily, though. The advantage of orbiting in space is that we could stay close to Earth. This would allow people in space to still visit Earth easily. Can you imagine going for a weekend trip to see a friend in space? The disadvantage is everything (food, water, supplies) that would need to be shipped to our orbiting home, since we would just be floating in a vacuum. This would be very costly. For example, for only six people living in the International Space Station, it costs over $2 billion dollars just to send water to them, according to the BBC.
Another option would be to colonize another planet. Living on another planet would allow us to take advantage of that planet’s resources, rather than having to bring everything with us.

But before we can move to an entirely new planet, we have to think about what things we need to survive on Earth so we can make sure our new planet will provide them. What is absolutely necessary to survive? Candy? Video games? Television? While those things are important, let’s think about what is essential for humans to live. There are the obvious things, like water, food, shelter, and oxygen. But there are some less obvious ones too, like enough gravity to keep us on the ground, or our friends and family. Before we can colonize space, we have to make sure all humans' physical and emotional needs can be met.

The most logical choice of planet to live on would be Mars, the closest planet to Earth and one we are in the process of exploring. NASA has sent five rovers, or space vehicles, to Mars to gather rocks, soil, and pictures. What the rovers collect will give scientists a better idea of whether or not life has ever existed on Mars and if we could live there. Water, being essential for any sort of life form, is the primary concern. We could ship it out to wherever we settle, but this would cost a ton of money. We already know this just from shipping supplies to the International Space Station. Mars is a lot further away than the International Space Station. If we want a whole community to be living on Mars, we’re going to need to find water somewhere on the planet.

Scientists across the world have already been considering the possibility of terraforming Mars. To terraform means to transform the planet into something that is livable and replicates the conditions of Earth. As it is right now, Mars is very cold, with frozen chunks of ice at each pole. If terraforming Mars sounds like a big task, that’s because it is. Before this mammoth endeavor can be achieved, scientists believe that three basic requirements must be met, which are water on the surface, carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, and nitrates in the soil, according to a paper written by NASA scientists. From the samples collected from the rovers, it seems like these three conditions are all met on Mars. From these basic requirements, scientists think we could potentially get water we can drink, create an atmosphere that we can fill with oxygen, and grow food from the soil.

Though it’s amazing to think this could all be possible and we could be living on Mars, we are still a long way away from this actually being a reality, according to NASA reports. Like hundreds of years away. Besides the question of whether terraforming would even be achievable, there are still many other problems to be addressed, such as the long term effects of living in space on the human body. Mars also only has 37% of the gravity that Earth has. While this would mean we would be able to leap and jump much higher than we can on Earth, it wouldn’t be all fun and games. Our muscles would deteriorate and our bone density would decrease from this lighter gravity. There are also concerns about emotional well-being. The first colonies on Mars would probably be very small and isolated many, many, many miles from Earth. Would you be able to live without your friends or family? That would be really hard for anyone.

In addition to the physical problems, there are also moral and ethical questions around colonizing space. To make Mars livable for humans, we would have to drastically alter the environment, according to PBS. To terraform Mars into a liveable place for humans, we would heat the environment by pumping out lots of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases, similar to how we are unintentionally warming Earth currently. Greenhouse gases trap heat and make the environment warmer, like how wrapping yourself in a blanket makes you warmer. Many scientists question whether this is something that we should even attempt. If there is already life on Mars, these changes would greatly affect, if not kill, their natural environment.

In opposition to the paper released by scientists proposing how to terraform Mars, another scientist wrote a paper detailing a different option to live in low-orbiting man-made biospheres with artificial gravity, as a small step towards living in space. A biosphere is an area where there are materials for life to exist, so think soil, the ocean, all the places on Earth where life exists. These biospheres are a more likely and achievable next step to living in space within the next century or maybe even less.
We still have a long way to go before we will have “space people” living somewhere other than Earth. But if you had the chance, would you take it?

options.label: Living in space is a smart and inexpensive option for people looking for a new home.
There are many ways to save the planet and the best way is to have people move to space.
It may someday be possible to live in space, but there are a lot of things we would need to learn first.
It would be too difficult for people to live on Mars, but they could live on the International Space Station.
