articles.title: Business and Bows
articles.author: S.Q. Eries
articles.description: A girl in Japan starts a kimono bow business during her summer break.
articles.intro: A kimono is a traditional, or handed down from age to age, piece of Japanese clothing that is made up of a loose, wide-sleeved robe held together by a sash. In this story, a young girl in Japan works as an assistant, or helper, in her grandmother's kimono shop.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Eri and Sis are similar.
articles.permissions: "Business and Bows" by S.Q. Eries, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Nishinomiya Middle School’s last period ends, and summer break begins. As we bustle off, everyone buzzes about vacation plans. Many are taking the bullet train to Kyoto or Tokyo to sightsee. Some talk about the beach and camping. Others have more productive activities.
I gape at the workbooks Shiori shows off as we leave school. “English and Spanish summer classes?” Just looking at the foreign words, so different from our Japanese characters, makes my head hurt.
She pokes me with her elbow. “Eri, some of us enjoy learning languages.”
“If you ask me, that stuff’s too complicated to enjoy.”

Shiori laughs. “I can say the same about kimonos. All those knots and sashes — I don’t know how you can remember them, let alone think they’re fun. Speaking of which, is that what you’re doing? Working extra hours at your grandmother’s kimono shop?”
“For now,” I say hesitantly. “I’m thinking about doing something else to make money.”
“If you want something different, Osaka Waterpark’s hiring —”
“No, I’m not talking about jobs. I’m talking about making my own money.”

Shiori frowns in confusion, so I explain. “My sister Ayano’s been making bags and tablet covers from traditional fabric. At first it was just for her university friends, but she’s gotten so good Granny’s selling them in the shop.”
My friend immediately understands. “What you’re saying is that all the women in your family are successful entrepreneurs, except you.”
I nod. “Granny built her shop from scratch. Mama’s a freelance graphic artist. My aunt has her sake brewery, and now Sis…I feel like I’m falling behind.”
“Then start something,” says Shiori. “What do you want to do?”
“That’s the problem.” I kick a pebble in my path. “The only thing I want is to work with kimonos.”
Shiori blinks, then laughs. “Eri, your grandmother loves having you as her assistant. I’m sure she hopes you’ll take over her business someday.”
“That’s not the same,” I groan. “Yes, I love Granny’s shop, but I want to start something new, try an idea I came up with.”
Shiori pats my shoulder. “You’ll think of something, Eri.”

The shop’s wooden sign reads: Zentoh Kimono Rentals. Even in the twenty-first century, occasions like weddings and coming-of-age ceremonies in Japan demand traditional clothing. However, formal kimonos are expensive — an ensemble can cost a million yen.

That’s where Granny comes in. For a reasonable cost, she provides customers with outfits, accessories, and dressing services. And those dressing services are essential. Wearing kimonos requires practiced hands and special knowledge of folds and knots. After years of training, I can handle a number of ensembles, but I’m still a long way from dressing a bride in wedding attire.
I enter through the rear, calling a greeting as I open the back door. But instead of Granny, my sister bursts in from the showroom. “Eri, you’re here!”

I start, surprised to see her. Most afternoons, she’s busy making or promoting her bags. As Sis pounces on me, our grandmother chuckles by the doorway. “I’ll let you girls negotiate,” she says, leaving for the showroom.
Negotiate? Now I’m really confused. “What’s going on?”
“Opportunity!” Sis is pulling out a flier that reads: Nishinomiya Summer Festival. “I’m selling my bags here next weekend.”
“That’s great,” I say, clapping my hands. “But you said festival booths were expensive.”
She grins. “Not if you can work a deal.”
That’s Sis for you. Whether it’s getting quality fabric for almost nothing or anticipating trends, Sis has amazing business sense. “What’s the deal?”
“A reduced fee for posting flyers in the neighborhood shopping centers. That’s where I need you.” She presses her palms in a pleading pose. “Will you post the flyers for me?”
My excitement crashes to a halt. “I’m sorry. I promised Granny — ”
“I’ll do your store shifts.”
When I gape in confusion, she looks embarrassed. “I want to finish more bags before the festival. I can’t sew while putting up flyers, but if I’m here…”
“...you can sew bags in between customers,” I say, grasping her plan.
“Exactly. Granny’s willing, but only if you agree. Flyers probably aren’t as fun for you, but — ”
“I’ll do it.”
She stops short. “Really?”
I nod. Seeing Sis work so hard to succeed, I can’t help but want to lend a hand.
“Thanks,” she squeals, hugging me tight. “I owe you one.”
As Shiori and I leave the shopping center, I say, “Thanks again for posting these flyers with me.”
“No problem. It’s not like I have anything to do before my language classes start. But did we have to wear yukata ?” she says, fussing with her sash.
“Of course,” I declare. “We’re advertising a festival.”
Yukata are casual cotton kimonos. They’re also the most affordable kimonos and regular attire for summer festivals. “Festivals aren’t festivals without yukata,” I say, flourishing my wide sleeves.
“True,” says Shiori. “But today’s a regular day, and the only ones in kimonos are old people and tourists. Look, see what I mean?”
In the distance are two college-age foreigners: a boy in a striped yukata and a girl whose hair matches her gold yukata. Chatting in English, they take selfies on a bench outside a shrine.
Suddenly, Shiori’s eyes light up. “Let’s invite them to the festival. It’s advertising, plus I can practice my English.”
Before I can protest, she’s dragged me over.

I smile nervously, unable to follow the fast-paced English conversation. However, Shiori and the tourists are having fun talking, so I can’t complain. Eventually, Shiori says, “Eri, Rachel and Malik want to take a picture with us by the shrine gate.”
“Sure,” I say, relieved to hear Japanese. “Let’s go —”
“Oh!”

We jump at Rachel’s cry to see her sash caught on the bench handrail. As I hurry to disentangle the partially untied bow, the embarrassed girl speaks, and Shiori translates. “Rachel doesn’t know how to fix the sash; their innkeeper was the one who dressed them. She’s asking if we —”
“No problem!” I give Rachel a thumb’s up and lead her to the shrine restroom.

Minutes later, we return, Rachel’s yukata better than before. Beaming, she shows Malik the fancy double bow I used to replace her plain one. As he snaps a picture, Shiori says, “Good job, Eri. If you weren’t here, Rachel would’ve been in trouble.”
“You could’ve fixed it, Shiori.”
She shakes her head. “I’m terrible with yukata, especially sashes.”
“It’s not so hard —”
“For you.” Shiori sighs. “Eri, you don’t realize how many of us need our moms and grandmothers to help with our yukata at festival time. If our sashes got undone, we’d be as helpless as Rachel.”
Yukata … festival … sashes …An idea hits me, and I shout, “That’s it!”
Sis chuckles as we finish setting up. “I never imagined I’d repay you by sharing my booth,” she says, “but this looks like it’ll work perfectly.”
I grin back. “And hopefully bring us both business.”

Sis’s wares fill the front in eye-catching displays. Meanwhile, the back half’s curtained off into a makeshift dressing room. And to the side of the booth is a sign in Japanese and English (courtesy of Shiori):
Yukata Adjustments ¥200
Come spruce up your bow!
Excitement flutters my stomach. I’ve never offered dressing services at a festival before, but compared to formal kimonos, I’m sure adjusting yukata will be a breeze.
Just then, the park loudspeaker announces the festival opening. As vendors raise their voices before the oncoming crowd, Sis says, “Ready?”
I pump my fist. “You bet!”
Two hours later my voice is raw, and my profits zero.

Meanwhile, Sis is doing brisk business. Her bags have attracted customers all afternoon. As she hands a purchase to yet another buyer, she says, “And if you’d like your yukata spruced up, my sister here can help you. She’s an experienced kimono shop employee.”
I smile as the customer glances at me and my sign. “That’s interesting,” she says and walks away.

My heart sinks. “That’s interesting” and curious looks are the only responses I’ve gotten. Even with Sis helping, I haven’t had a single customer. Disappointment’s sapping my morale when Sis whispers, “Chin up. The festival lasts till midnight, and yukata bows droop after people walk a long time. Keep telling people you’re here, and eventually they’ll come.”
She’s right, I think to myself. Buoyed by her encouragement, I pull myself together as someone says, “Excuse me, what’s this yukata adjustment about?”
I whirl, eager for a customer — only to see a man in T-shirt and shorts. Somehow, I keep my smile from crumbling while I answer his question.
“That’s interesting,” he replies.
As he leaves, I notice how many festival goers are also in regular clothes. And regular clothes means no business. Maybe this was a stupid idea…
“Eri!”
A girl in a gold yukata waves from a group of foreign students. “Rachel?” I say.
Rachel starts walking over, then stops. Suddenly, she turns to her friends, and before I know it, she’s ushered five yukata-clad girls to my booth.

She points to the sign, but I have trouble understanding Rachel’s rapid English. Fortunately, one of her friends speaks some Japanese. “The bow you made for Rachel before was beautiful,” she says. “We’d like you to give all of us that same bow.”
Excitement zips through me. “Really?’’
They nod. Rachel pulls out the picture of her double bow and gives me a thumbs up.
I’ve never been so happy to tie six sashes. And I’ve never been so grateful to receive 1200 yen. Best of all, my customers are delighted with the results.
They start taking pictures, and as they strike poses to show off their double bows, an elderly woman in a gray kimono watches curiously. “What are those tourists doing?”
“They’re just happy about their bows,” I say and explain my adjustment service.
Interest lights her eyes. “Only 200 yen? Perhaps you can adjust my kimono then. The restroom’s so crowded, and my arthritis is acting up. Can you tie a drum-style knot?”
“Absolutely!” I reply.
As I show her to the dressing room, a student in a rumpled yukata approaches. “Excuse me?” he asks Sis. “I heard someone fixes yukata here.”
“Yes, she’s with another customer, but she’ll be done shortly. In the meantime, feel free to look around the booth.”
“Wow, my girlfriend would love this bag…”
My sister winks at me, and we smile as we get to work.
As we pack up that night, Sis remarks, “I’d say today was a success.”
“Me, too,” I reply, jingling my cash box. Toward the festival’s end, a steady stream of yukata customers kept my hands busy and my spirits flying.
“I should’ve brought some decorative cords to offer as an extra option though,” I add with a little regret. “Oh well, too late now.”
“Maybe not.” Sis grins. “Things went so well, I think I’ll do another festival. Care to join?”
My heart leaps and I flash a big smile. “Sis, I think we’re in business!”

articles.title: The Capital of Irish America
articles.author: Cynthia Levinson
articles.description: Cynthia Hatch describes how Boston became a center for Irish culture in the United States.
articles.intro: Cynthia Levinson lives in Boston, where she writes for young people and enjoys learning about the city's history. In this article, she explains why Boston is called "The Capital of Irish America."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view about Irish-Americans in Boston.
articles.permissions: "The Capital of Irish America" by Cynthia Hatch, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “Look for the Golden Stairs.” That’s what immigrants to Boston were told as their ships docked at the pier. So many Irish climbed the steps to Immigration Hall in the 19th century that Boston became known as the Capital of Irish America. Hardworking Irish enlivened the city with traditional food, music, and a help-your-neighbor spirit. Because they also brought poverty and Catholicism, though, Boston’s Irish suffered almost as much discrimination in their new land as they had in the country they had fled.

Irish began moving to Boston before the American Revolution. Some came for economic opportunities. Thousands of others were deported by the British to populate the colonies. Boston’s Irish held the colonies’ first St. Patrick’s Day celebration in 1737. When General Sullivan drove the British fleet from Boston on March 17, 1776, he used “St. Patrick” as his code word.

The largest numbers of immigrants arrived between 1845 and 1850. These were the Famine Irish. Fleeing the Great Hunger, about 75,000 desperate refugees paid about $15 to board leaky “coffin ships” that could take a month to cross the Atlantic. So many people died on the journey, the ocean was called The Bowl of Tears. Many who survived fell onto the docks, their tongues swollen with ship fever. Others were quarantined and died on Deer Island, never reaching the mainland.

Yet poor, uneducated, and starving immigrants kept coming. In 1850, one-third of Boston’s population was Irish, and local citizens feared their town, called the Dublin of America, would become the “poor house of the world.” Mobs attacked Catholic churches. While the Boston Pilot ran notices to help family and friends find each other, another newspaper demanded that the “miserable gang be sent back at once.” Want ads seeking workers stated, “No Irish need apply.”

The newcomers worked hard in dangerous conditions. For 50 cents a day, they laid Boston’s roads, tunnels, and subway tracks. Many died, leaving poverty-stricken widows and children. Women cleaned wealthy Bostonians’ homes and tended their babies. Irish laborers did jobs others refused and expanded the town into a major city. Gradually, they moved from tenements near the wharves to suburbs.

Because they were also loyal Americans, they began to gain the respect of native Bostonians. The Irishmen of the Ninth Regiment fought so bravely for the Union in the Civil War, their regimental flag hangs in the Massachusetts State House. Irish-American musicians wrote patriotic songs, including “You’re a Grand Old Flag.”

Still, traditional Bostonians, called Brahmins, worried. Irish politicians were becoming powerful by providing jobs and charity to constituents in their neighborhoods in exchange for votes. One candidate even told his supporters on Election Day, “Vote early and vote often!” As Brahmins feared, local neighborhood bosses eventually controlled city government, and the first Irish Catholic was elected mayor in 1885.

When the son of a Famine Irishman became the city’s third Irish mayor in 1906, a journalist wrote, “Boston, not Dublin or Belfast, is the greatest Irish city in the world.” This mayor, John F. Fitzgerald, was the grandfather of President John F. Kennedy and Senator Edward Kennedy.

Boston’s Irish still enliven the city. Thousands of kids march in the annual St. Patrick’s Day parade, which attracts a million observers. The Boston Irish Reporter and the Boston Irish Tourism Association announce events, such as plays, concerts, storytelling, and festivals. The Boston Irish Heritage Trail highlights historic sites, including the Irish Famine Memorial and the JFK Library. Neighborhood pubs serve Dublin coddle (sausage and potatoes). Bakeries sell traditional soda bread. The Irish Culture Center in nearby Canton has a traditional thatched-roof cottage and offers lessons for kids in Irish language, legends, Celtic artwork, and dancing.

Ten-year-old Celia Dolan, whose mother is from Ireland, studies step and céilí (KAY-lee) dancing. She likes soft-shoe reels but her favorites are the hard-shoe hornpipe and travel jigs. Celia also has played Irish football, which is like soccer with hands, and hurling. She plays instruments, too, including the tin whistle and the hand-held, one-sided bodhrán (B-OW-raan) drum.
Like many Boston Irish, Celia says, “I’m proud of my culture!”

options.label: Despite difficulties, many Irish-Americans have made Boston their home.

While they love Boston, many Irish-Americans wish it was a more equal place.
Because of how they were treated, many Irish-Americans enjoy living in Boston.

articles.title: Elizabeth Blackwell: The First Woman Doctor
articles.author: Richard L. Mattis
articles.description: Richard L. Mattis describes how Elizabeth Blackwell became America's first female doctor.
articles.intro: In this text, Richard L. Mattis describes the life of Elizabeth Blackwell, America's first female doctor.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what inspired Elizabeth Blackwell to become a doctor.
articles.permissions: "Elizabeth Blackwell: The First Woman Doctor" by Richard L. Mattis, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Dr. Lee, the dean of Geneva Medical College in Geneva, New York, stood nervously before the anatomy class. “Gentlemen, I have a letter here that contains a very strange request.” The letter was from his friend, Dr. Warrington of Philadelphia. “Dr. Warrington asks that the college admit a woman who wishes to become…,” he hesitated, “a doctor.”

Female doctors were unheard of in 1847. People at that time thought women were not fit to deal with blood, bones, and diseases. But rather than refuse his friend’s request, Dr. Lee asked the students to decide. “If any single student objects,” he said, “then the woman will not be admitted.”
The students gathered that evening and began trading wisecracks. “Women doctors! How about men mothers?”

Many of the young men thought that it would be fun to have a woman classmate. Others thought that admitting a woman would be a good joke to play on the faculty. When the time came to decide, the students shouted down the lone opposing vote and made the unanimous decision to admit a female student.
Elizabeth Blackwell arrived at Geneva two weeks later. The small, fair-haired woman was serious about becoming a doctor. She also hoped that she could promote women’s rights in general.

Elizabeth had been born in England in 1821, and when she was eleven years old, moved with her family to America. She grew up in a home alive with new but unpopular ideas. Elizabeth listened intently as her father and his dinner guests discussed ways to fight slavery and child labor and methods to achieve equal rights for all people.

Her father gave his five daughters the same education in languages, history, and mathematics that he gave his four sons. “Sheer nonsense,” said Elizabeth’s unmarried aunt Barbara, who lived with them. Aunt Barbara saw no reason to educate young ladies. But Elizabeth spent her pocket money on books and found “the present of a new book the greatest delight.”

When her father died, seventeen-year-old Elizabeth and two older sisters opened a school in their home. Their teaching helped support the family, but Elizabeth was not content. She wanted to do something more challenging, something that would stretch her mind and use up some of her restless energy.

She ruled out marriage — she would never find a man who would give her as much freedom as her father had. Yet she did not want to end up like Aunt Barbara, forced to depend on a brother for support. She racked her brains to think of something that an independent, educated woman could do.

One day when she was visiting a dying friend, the woman said to her, “Why don’t you study medicine? My illness would have caused me much less pain and embarrassment if I had been treated by a lady doctor.”

Elizabeth balked at the idea. “I hated everything connected with the body,” she wrote later. “I could not bear the sight of a medical book.” Yet she found herself lying awake at night thinking about it. Becoming a doctor would give her the challenge she needed so badly. It would, she wrote, “fill my emptiness and prevent this sad wearing away of my heart.”

So she decided to become a doctor. She took teaching jobs to earn money. She studied medical books and lost her fear of them. Finally, she went to Philadelphia, the home of the best medical schools in the country.

The doctors Elizabeth visited in Philadelphia all told her that she could not be a doctor. Dr. Joseph Warrington urged her to become a nurse. Or, he said, she could go to Paris, dress as a man, and try to get into one of the medical schools there.
Such outlandish advice only made Elizabeth more determined. “The idea has taken deep root in my soul, and I cannot lay it aside,” she wrote to a friend.
She applied to smaller schools. One application went to Geneva Medical College, along with the letter from Dr. Warrington that led to her acceptance.

Elizabeth’s first days at Geneva were difficult. Townspeople stared at her, rooming houses turned her away, and other students ignored her. But she was determined and finally sensed that her classmates were warming up to her when they began calling her “Blackwell.”

During the summer between her two years at Geneva, Elizabeth returned to Philadelphia to work in the Blockley Almshouse, a hospital for the poor. The food there was bad, and the nurses, undereducated and underpaid, seemed uncaring. Odors hung in the air, but the windows were kept closed.

When Elizabeth’s mother heard of Blockley’s conditions, she was horrified. “Do not fear for me,” Elizabeth wrote to her. “I live simply, do my duty, and trust in God.” The situation at Blockley made Elizabeth dream of having a hospital where kind, well-trained nurses would guide women back to health in pleasant surroundings.
Elizabeth graduated at the top of her class in 1849, the first woman ever to earn a medical degree.

She went to Paris to gain experience, but no hospital there would accept a woman as an intern. Elizabeth had to settle for being a nursing apprentice in La Maternité, a large women’s hospital. She was determined to make the most of the experience. To her sister Anna she wrote, “I shall give baths and make beds and empty slops, and I’ll like it all, for I shall be learning.”

The plight of the poor women particularly touched Elizabeth. She watched in angry silence as a male doctor operated on a woman with no concern for her pain and embarrassment and even less concern for cleanliness. Elizabeth knew she could do better. She decided to become a surgeon.

Soon after, a terrible thing happened. She was treating a baby for ophthalmia, a serious eye disease, when some of the fluid spurted into her left eye. Elizabeth caught the dreaded disease, and after a few weeks her doctors concluded that the sight in that eye was hopelessly lost. Anna, who was in Paris, helped her sister through “two days of unspeakable grief and despair.” The eye was eventually removed. Elizabeth did not want to perform operations with less than perfect vision, and so she abandoned her hopes of becoming a surgeon. But she was still a doctor. Elizabeth soon recovered her strength and drive. She returned to America and opened an office in New York City.

Not many patients came to see her. Elizabeth knew that most of the poor families in the city needed medical care, but could not afford it. So she raised money, rented a house, and started a free clinic.

Slowly people began to come. Elizabeth also went to see them in their unhealthy living conditions. She recalled that in one sick child’s home, “the thermometer varied from 86 to 90 in the house, and it stood at 102 in some rooms downtown. The heat made my brain seem too large for my head.”
Elizabeth realized that people could prevent most diseases if they knew how. She encouraged cleanliness and proper food storage, exercise, fresh air, and plenty of sunshine.

Although absorbed in her work, Elizabeth began to feel that she was missing something by living alone. She paid several visits to an orphanage and eventually adopted seven-year-old Kitty Barry. Kitty, who was thin and under-nourished, blossomed under Elizabeth’s care into a lively, intelligent child. She became fiercely devoted to Elizabeth, whom she called “Doctor” or “My Doctor.” “Who will ever guess the support which that poor little orphan has been to me?” wrote Elizabeth two years later. When Kitty grew up, she stayed with Elizabeth as her traveling companion and secretary.

In 1857 Elizabeth opened the New York Infirmary, a hospital for women and children that is still in existence today. The hospital became a training center for the first nursing school in America. In 1868 she opened a medical college for women, the first school in the United States to teach hygiene, or disease prevention.

When other medical schools for women opened their doors, Elizabeth considered her work in America completed. She and Kitty returned to England where Elizabeth promoted hygiene and worked for women’s causes until a few years before her death at the age of eighty-nine.

articles.title: Salmon Boy, a Haida Story
articles.author: Robert San Souci
articles.description: In this Haida story, a young boy learns a lesson about respecting nature.
articles.intro: Native Americans of the Pacific Northwest Coast tell many tales of the hero Salmon Boy. The Haida are the native, or first, people of the Queen Charlotte Islands, Canada. In this story, a young Haida boy learns a lesson.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the boy changes from the beginning to the end of the story.
articles.permissions: "Salmon Boy, a Haida Story" by Robert San Souci, AppleSeeds , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: A boy once lived among the Haida with his widowed mother. Her husband had been lost at sea while fishing for salmon. The woman loved her son deeply and gave him the copper necklace that had belonged to his father. They lived on the mussels and seaweed she gathered and on the salmon she caught in the warm season.

They were poor, and their sleeping mats were in the worst place in the cedar-plank longhouse where they lived with uncles, aunts, and cousins. The house had birds, animals, humans, and — most important — King Salmon carved on the doorposts.

The boy was always hungry, complaining, “Mother, I am starving.” But he never helped gather food. And he did not honor the spirits of the fish that made it possible for his people to live; he thought of them as flesh to fill his belly. He would not place the salmon bones in the stream behind their village to show honor. His uncles warned, “Do not insult the Salmon People.” But he merely laughed and went on playing.

One year, the salmon catch was small. The boy’s family worried that the Salmon People were offended by the boy’s lack of respect. Soon there was nothing to eat but dried salmon from earlier catches. When his mother gave him some, he cried, “This is moldy! I cannot eat this!” He flung it aside, not caring that the waste of food was an insult to the Salmon People.

He went swimming with his friends. Because he was hungry, he grew faint. He drifted into deep water. When he realized his danger, he tried to swim back, but he was too weak to fight the current. Exhausted, he drowned.

Some Salmon People were swimming nearby. These were the souls of caught salmon whose bones had been cast respectfully into the water and who had been reborn as young fish. They caught the boy’s spirit. His spirit grabbed the copper necklace before his body sank from sight. Then the Salmon People carried his spirit to their village in the ocean depths.

There, the Salmon People changed to human form, and so did the boy. He put on his necklace. Looking around, he found that the village was like his old home, with long plank houses and smoke curling up through the roofs.

Behind the houses flowed a stream, like the one that ran behind the longhouse where he lived. The Salmon Chief took him to its bank and said, “When you are hungry, pull a little salmon from the water and roast it. But be sure to return whatever you do not eat to the stream when you are done.” This time, the boy did as he was told, and each time, the bones became a salmon again.

He was ashamed that he had not shown this respect before and grateful that the Salmon People did not punish him. Instead, they taught him many things, including their secret language, songs, and dances. In return, he taught them the songs and dances of his people, the Haida. Sometimes he left the village, took on a salmon’s form, and discovered the wonders and secrets of the sea. He learned how life and death flow into each other in the circle of life: flesh to bones, and bones to flesh.

In spring, the salmon journeyed back to the rivers to lay their eggs. The boy traveled with them in salmon form. They swam up the stream behind his old village. There he was caught in his mother’s cedar-bark net. “What a fine salmon!” she thought. Then she saw the copper necklace around the fish’s through. “Can my son’s soul be inside?” she wondered. She had heard how the Salmon People sometimes care for drowned souls.

She kept the fish in a shallow pool, watching over it day and night. On the second day, a human head emerged from the salmon. Six days later, the boy stepped out of the water, leaving his fish skin behind.

Joyfully, mother and son embraced. “Oh, mother!” he cried. “I have learned many things while I lived with the Salmon People.” The boy became a wise teacher and powerful healer. Each spring, he called the salmon in such numbers that none of his people went hungry again. But he insisted they respectfully return the bones to the water.

Growing old, he said, “When I die, return my body to the sea.” The people did as he instructed. As soon as his lifeless body entered the waves, he changed into a strong salmon. The fish circled four times, then dove from sight, never to be seen again.

options.label: When we leave our family members alone they miss us.
Kids should learn to swim so they do not drown.
We must value what nature gives to us.
Nature is always listening to us.

If you were in the Salmon People's position do you think you would choose to act the same way? Why or why not?

Do you have a similar belief in your culture about how people should interact with nature? If so, what is it? Do you know other groups of people that share a similar practice to the Haida?

articles.title: The Days Have Names
articles.author: JonArno Lawson
articles.description: A speaker considers why some things are named, while others go nameless.
articles.intro: JonArno Lawson is a writer of books for both children and adults. In this poem, a speaker discusses what receives a name and what goes nameless.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what things have names.
articles.permissions: From Black Stars in a White Night Sky by JonArno Lawson. Copyright © 2008 by JonArno Lawson. Published by WordSong, an imprint of Boyds Mills Press. Used by permission.

blocks.text: The days have names,
the months have names,
and so do clouds and hurricanes.
But not the weeks:
of weekly names
nobody speaks—
it doesn’t seem to bother us
that weeks pass by,
anonymous.

options.label: We have names for some things, but not others.
Counting weeks is difficult because they don't have names.
It's more important for weeks to have names than for storms.
Something must be important enough to earn a name.

articles.title: How Boots Befooled the King
articles.author: Howard Pyle
articles.description: A king issues a challenge: he who is able to fool him shall have his daughter's hand in marriage.
articles.intro: Howard Pyle (1853-1911) was an American author and illustrator, primarily of books for young people. In the following tale, a wise king offers his daughter's hand in marriage to whomever can make a fool out of him.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the point of view, tone, and theme of the text.
articles.permissions: How Boots Befooled the King by Howard Pyle is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Once upon a time there was a king who was the wisest in all the world. So wise was he that no one had ever befooled him, which is a rare thing, I can tell you. Now, this king had a daughter who was as pretty as a ripe apple, so that there was no end to the number of the lads who came asking to marry her. Every day there was two or three of them dawdling around the house, so that at last the old king grew tired of having them always about.

So he sent word far and near that whoever should befool him might have the princess and half of the kingdom to boot, for he thought that it would be a wise man indeed who could trick him. But the king also said, that whoever should try to befool him and should fail, should have a good whipping. This was to keep all foolish fellows away.
The princess was so pretty that there was no lack of lads who came to have a try for her and half of the kingdom, but every one of these went away with a sore back and no luck.

Now, there was a man who was well off in the world, and who had three sons; the first was named Peter, and the second was named Paul. Peter and Paul thought themselves as wise as anybody in all the world, and their father thought as they did.
As for the youngest son, he was named Boots. Nobody thought anything of him except that he was silly, for he did nothing but sit poking in the warm ashes all of the day.

One morning Peter spoke up and said that he was going to the town to have a try at befooling the king, for it would be a fine thing to have a princess in the family. His father did not say no, for if anybody was wise enough to befool the king, Peter was the lad.
So, after Peter had eaten a good breakfast, off he set for the town, right foot foremost. After a while he came to the king’s house and — rap! tap! tap! — he knocked at the door.
Well, what did he want?
Oh! he would only like to have a try at befooling the king.
Very good, he should have his try. He was not the first one who had been there that morning, early as it was.
So Peter was shown in to the king.
“Oh, look!” said he, “yonder are three black geese out in the courtyard!”
But no, the king was not to be fooled so easily as all that. “One goose is enough to look at at a time,” said he; “take him away and give him a whipping!”
And so they did, and Peter went home bleating like a sheep.
One day Paul spoke up. “I should like to go and have a try for the princess, too” said he.
Well, his father did not say no, for, after all, Paul was the more clever of the two.
So off Paul went as merrily as a duck in the rain. By and by he came to the castle, and then he too was brought before the king just as Peter had been.
“Oh, look!” said he, “yonder is a crow sitting in the tree with three white stripes on his back!”
But the king was not so silly as to be fooled in that way. “Here is a Jack,” said he, “who will soon have more stripes on his back than he will like. Take him away and give him his whipping!”
Then it was done as the king had said, and Paul went away home bawling like a calf.
One day up spoke Boots. “I should like to go and have a try for the pretty princess, too,” said he.

At this they all stared and sniggered. What! he go where his clever brothers had failed, and had nothing to show for the trying but a good beating? What had come over the lout! Here was a pretty business, to be sure! That was what they all said.

But all of this rolled away from Boots like water from a duck’s back. No matter, he would like to go and have a try like the others. So he begged and begged until his father was glad to let him go to be rid of his teasing, if nothing else.
Then Boots asked if he might have the old tattered hat that hung back of the chimney.
Oh, yes, he might have that if he wanted it, for nobody with good wits was likely to wear such a thing.
So Boots took the hat, and after he had brushed the ashes from his shoes set off for the town, whistling as he went.
The first body whom he met was an old woman with a great load of earthenware pots and crocks on her shoulders.
“Good-day, mother,” said Boots.
“Good-day, son,” said she.
“What will you take for all of your pots and crocks?” said Boots.
“Three shillings,” said she.
“I will give you five shillings if you will come and stand in front of the king’s house, and do thus and so when I say this and that,” said Boots.
Oh, yes! She would do that willingly enough.

So Boots and the old woman went on together, and presently came to the king’s house. When they had come there, Boots sat down in front of the door and began bawling as loud as he could — “No, I will not! I will not do it, I say! No, I will not do it!”

So he kept on, bawling louder and louder until he made such a noise that, at last, the king himself came out to see what all of the hubbub was about. But when Boots saw him he only bawled out louder than ever.
“No, I will not! I will not do it, I say!”
“Stop! stop!” cried the king, “what is all this about?”
“Why,” said Boots, “everybody wants to buy my cap, but I will not sell it! I will not do it, I say!”
“But, why should anybody want to buy such a cap as that?” said the king.
“Because,” said Boots, “it is a fooling cap and the only one in all of the world.”

“A fooling cap!” said the king. For he did not like to hear of such a cap as that coming into the town. “Hum-m-m-m! I should like to see you fool somebody with it. Could you fool that old body yonder with the pots and the crocks?”
“Oh, yes! That is easily enough done,” said Boots, and without more ado he took off his tattered cap and blew into it. Then he put it on his head again and bawled out, “Break pots! break pots!”

No sooner had he spoken these words than the old woman jumped up and began breaking and smashing her pots and crocks as though she had gone crazy. That was what Boots had paid her five shillings for doing, but of it the king knew nothing. “Hui!” said he to himself, “I must buy that hat from the fellow or he will fool the princess away from me for sure and certain.” Then he began talking to Boots as sweetly as though he had honey in his mouth. Perhaps Boots would sell the hat to him?
Oh no! Boots could not think of such a thing as selling his fooling cap.
Come, come; the king wanted that hat, and sooner than miss buying it he would give a whole bag of gold money for it.

At that Boots looked up and looked down, scratching his head. Well, he supposed he would have to sell the hat some time, and the king might as well have it as anybody else. But for all that he did not like parting with it.
So the king gave Boots the bag of gold, and Boots gave the king the old tattered hat, and then he went his way.

After Boots had gone the king blew into the hat and blew into the hat, but though he blew enough breath into it to sail a big ship, he did not befool so much as a single titmouse. Then, at last, he began to see that the fooling cap was good on nobody else’s head but Boots’; and he was none too pleased at that, you may be sure.

As for Boots, with his bag of gold he bought the finest clothes that were to be had in the town, and when the next morning had come he started away bright and early for the king’s house. “I have come,” said he, “to marry the princess, if you please.”

At this the king hemmed and hawed and scratched his head. Yes; Boots had befooled him sure enough, but, after all, he could not give up the princess for such a thing as that. Still, he would give Boots another chance. Now, there was the high-councillor, who was the wisest man in all of the world. Did Boots think that he could fool him also?
Oh, yes! Boots thought that it might be done.

Very well; if he could befool the high-councillor so as to bring him to the castle the next morning against his will, Boots should have the princess and the half of the kingdom; if he did not do so he should have his beating.
Then Boots went away, and the king thought that he was rid of him now for good and all.

As for the high-councillor, he was not pleased with the matter at all, for he did not like the thought of being fooled by a clever rogue, and taken here and there against his will. So when he had come home, he armed all of his servants with blunderbusses, and then waited to give Boots a welcome when he should come.

But Boots was not going to fall into any such trap as that! No indeed! not he! The next morning he went quietly and bought a fine large meal-sack. Then he put a black wig on over his beautiful red hair, so that no one might know him. After that he went to the place where the high-councillor lived, and when he had come there he crawled inside of the sack, and lay just beside the door of the house.
By and by came one of the maid servants to the door, and there lay the great meal-sack with somebody in it.
“Ach!” cried she,” who is there?”
But Boots only said, “Sh-h-h-h-h!”
Then the serving maid went back into the house, and told the high-councillor that one lay outside in a great meal-sack, and that all that he said was, “Sh-h-h-h-h!”
So the councilor went himself to see what it was all about. “What do you want here?” said he.
“Sh-h-h-h-h!” said Boots, “I am not to be talked to now. This is a wisdom-sack, and I am learning wisdom as fast as a drake can eat peas.”
“And what wisdom have you learned?” said the councilor.

Oh! Boots had learned wisdom about everything in the world. He had learned that the clever scamp who had fooled the king yesterday was coming with seventeen tall men to take the high-councillor, willy-nilly, to the castle that morning.
When the high-councillor heard this he fell to trembling till his teeth rattled in his head. “And have you learned how I can get the better of this clever scamp?” said he.
Oh, yes! Boots had learned that easily enough.
So, good! Then if the wise man in the sack would tell the high-councillor how to escape the clever rogue, the high-councillor would give the wise man twenty dollars.
But no, that was not to be done; wisdom was not bought so cheaply as the high-councillor seemed to think.
Well, the councilor would give him a hundred dollars then.

That was good! A hundred dollars were a hundred dollars. If the councillor would give him that much he might get into the sack himself, and then he could learn all the wisdom that he wanted, and more besides.
So Boots crawled out of the sack, and the councillor paid his hundred dollars and crawled in.

As soon as he was in all snug and safe, Boots drew the mouth of the sack together and tied it tightly. Then he flung sack, councillor, and all over his shoulder, and started away to the king’s house, and anybody who met them could see with half an eye that the councillor was going against his will.
When Boots came to the king’s castle he laid the councillor down in the goose-house, and then he went to the king.
When the king saw Boots again, he bit his lips with vexation. “Well,” said he, “have you fooled the councillor?”
“Oh, yes!” says Boots, “I have done that.”
And where was the councillor now?
Oh, Boots had just left him down in the goose-house. He was tied up safe and sound in a sack, waiting till the king should send for him.
So the councillor was sent for, and when he came the king saw at once that he had been brought against his will.
“And now may I marry the princess?” said Boots.

But the king was not willing for him to marry the princess yet; no! no! Boots must not go so fast. There was more to be done yet. If he would come tomorrow morning he might have the princess and welcome, but he would have to pick her out from among fourscore other maids just like her; did he think that he could do that?
Oh, yes! Boots thought that might be easy enough to do.
So, good! Then come tomorrow; but he must understand that if he failed he should have a good whipping, and be sent packing from the town.
So off went Boots, and the king thought that he was rid of him now, for he had never seen the princess, and how could he pick her out from among eighty others?
But Boots was not going to give up so easily as all that! No, not he! He made a little box, and then he hunted up and down until he had caught a live mouse to put into it.
When the next morning came he started away to the king’s house, taking his mouse along with him in the box.
There was the king, standing in the doorway, looking out into the street. When he saw Boots coming towards him he made a wry face. “What!” said he, “are you back again?”
Oh, yes! Boots was back again. And now if the princess was ready he would like to go and find her, for lost time was not to be gathered again like fallen apples.
So off they marched to a great room, and there stood eighty-and-one maidens, all as much alike as peas in the same dish.

Boots looked here and there, but, even if he had known the princess, he could not have told her from the others. But he was ready for all that. Before anyone knew what he was about, he opened the box, and out ran the little mouse among them all. Then what a screaming and a hubbub there was! Many looked as though they would like to swoon, but only one of them did so. As soon as the others saw what had happened, they forgot all about the mouse, and ran to her and fell to fanning her and slapping her hands and chafing her temples.
“This is the princess,” said Boots.
And so it was.
After that the king could think of nothing more to set Boots to do, so he let him marry the princess as he had promised, and have half of the kingdom to boot.
That is all of the story.
Only this: It is not always the silliest one that sits kicking his feet in the ashes at home.

options.label: Wisdom is no match for cleverness.
Sometimes the most unsuspecting characters are the wisest.
Only fools ask to be made to look foolish.
Everyone has the potential to be a hero.

articles.title: Bringing Baby Home
articles.author: JonArno Lawson
articles.description: A speaker describes bringing their baby home for the first time.
articles.intro: JonArno Lawson is a writer of books for both children and adults. In this poem, a speaker describes bringing their baby home for the first time.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker describes how the baby experiences the world.
articles.permissions: From Black Stars in a White Night Sky by JonArno Lawson. Copyright © 2008 by JonArno Lawson. Published by WordSong, an imprint of Boyds Mills Press. Used by permission.

blocks.text: I was watching the first time sunlight
touched her face on Murray Street,
rolling out of the clouds, dropping
to sleep on her sleeping cheek.
She’d never been away, never been home. The world
was full of firsts,
changing itself forever for her. Renewed by her little
long-fingered hands
moving carefully across the air
like starfish,
busy remaking the world.

options.label: Trying to understand the complex world around them makes babies frustrated and confused.
After they are born, babies need guidance from their parents to make sense of the world.
New life in the world means change for that life and for the world it inhabits.
Babies are not interested in becoming part of their new world when they are brought home.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best expresses a theme in the poem?

options.label: "I was watching the first time sunlight / touched her face on Murray Street" (Lines 1-2)
"rolling out of the clouds, dropping / to sleep on her sleeping cheek." (Lines 3-4)
"changing itself forever for her. Renewed by her little / long-fingered hands" (Lines 7-8)
"moving carefully across the air / like starfish" (Lines 9-10)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: The parent is in awe of all the first experiences that are ahead of the baby.
The parent is jealous of the unique way that the baby gets to experience the world.
The parent is nervous that all of these new experiences will overwhelm the baby.
The parent is impatient for the baby to have all of the amazing experiences ahead of her.

questions.prompt: What emotions are emphasized through the author's use of first person point of view?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the poem, the baby has her first experience outside. In what ways are new experiences an important part of growing up? How do new experiences help you grow up? Cite examples from the text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

In the poem, the parent watches the baby closely as she has her first experience in the world around her. How do you think newborns make sense of the world around them? How do their ways of understanding the world compare to those who are older than them? Cite examples from the text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Weighing in With Words
articles.author: Andrew Matthews
articles.description: Mercy Otis Warren used writing to support the American colonists' fight for independence.
articles.intro: The American Revolutionary War was fought from 1775-1783. American colonists, sometimes called patriots, wanted to be independent from Great Britain. In this informational text, Andrew Matthews describes the life of writer Mercy Otis Warren.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the different reasons Mercy Otis Warren wrote.
articles.permissions: "Weighing in With Words" by Andrew Matthews, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: During the Revolutionary War, the opposing armies often stopped fighting during the coldest months of the year and waited for better weather before resuming the conflict. In the winter of 1775–76, as the British settled into an occupation of Boston, British general John Burgoyne wrote a play, The Blockade of Boston . It made fun of the way that the Americans spoke and dressed. Burgoyne converted Boston’s Faneuil Hall into a theater and arranged for his play to be staged for the entertainment of the British soldiers. But as the play opened in early January 1776, a group of patriots launched a surprise attack on nearby Charlestown. The show was interrupted as the actors and the audience, made up of mostly British soldiers, scrambled to react.

The play provoked an American response — a satire of the British soldiers. The Blockheads ; or, T he Affrighted Officers was published and passed around Boston in pamphlet form. It was published anonymously because a woman, Mercy Otis Warren, wrote the piece.

Today, Mercy Otis Warren is considered one of the great literary minds of the Revolutionary War-era. She was born in 1728, in Barnstable, Massachusetts. Her father, James Otis Sr., was a well-to-do merchant, who hired a tutor to educate her brother, James Otis Jr. Although girls were not offered the same educational opportunities as boys were at the time, Mercy was well educated. She learned how to read and write and was permitted to listen in on her brother’s lessons.

In 1754, Mercy married James Warren, and the couple moved to Plymouth. Both James Warren and James Otis Jr. were early leaders in the Colonial resistance to British tyranny. Boston was a hotbed of rebellion in the decade before fighting began in the war. The Warren home served as a meeting place for colonists who supported a split from Great Britain. As the hostess of those get-togethers, Mercy had firsthand access to information and events as they unfolded.

Through the activities of her husband and brother, she met and kept up written correspondences with other patriots, such as Samuel Adams, Henry Knox, Thomas Jefferson, and John Adams. Mercy also developed and maintained friendships with other women. These women included Abigail Adams, English historian Catherine Macaulay, Martha Washington, and Hannah Winthrop, wife of the acting president of Harvard College. The exchange of letters among the women allowed them to share their hopes and fears and offer one another support.

Warren wrote several satirical plays before and during the war, but they were not initially credited to her. None of the works were performed, either, because Boston had banned theaters and plays. But written material was a powerful propaganda tool in the effort to sway public opinion, and Mercy’s plays were printed and distributed as pamphlets. Her first two plays, The Adulateur (1772) and The Defeat (1773), poked fun at Thomas Hutchinson, the British governor of Massachusetts. They were followed by The Group (1775), which satirized the Tories (pro-British) who were ruling Boston, and then The Blockheads ; or, The Affrighted Officers (1776). The Motley Assembly (1779) was a satire about the new American society. Their attacks on the British and people who supported them made the plays popular among the patriots.

Years after the Revolutionary War ended in 1783, Mercy continued to weigh in on issues. In the 1780s, she did not support ratification of the Constitution. A believer in self-rule, she thought the Constitution gave the federal government too much power over the states. A small volume of her poems and collected writings was published in 1790. It was the first work to carry her name as the author and included two short tragedies, The Ladies of Castile and The Sack of Rome . Those pieces made the case for women to have a voice and a role in public issues.

Mercy’s History of the Rise, Progress and Termination of the American Revolution , three-volume work published in 1805, was the first history of the United States. It caused a split between Mercy and John Adams, who had shared a warm and friendly correspondence for most of the war. Adams did not like the unflattering way that Mercy portrayed his passionate approach to politics. They reconciled before she died in 1814.

Mercy also admitted to being the author of her previously anonymous plays after the war was over. She was one of the first Americans to write plays about her country. She became known as the “Conscience of the American Revolution” for her forceful anti-British views. The fact that she was a woman who expressed her opinion and was listened to by men reveals the strength of her position during the war.

options.label: Boston was the site of many battles during the American Revolutionary War.
Plays were a popular form of entertainment during the American Revolutionary War.
Mercy Otis Warren used writing to tease both sides during the American Revolutionary War.
Mercy Otis Warren became a well-known writer at a time when that was uncommon for women.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Mercy Otis Warren used plays to try to change people's opinion of the British. What type of art (e.g. painting, cartoons, poetry, etc.) do you think is most persuasive? Has a piece of art ever changed your mind about a topic? What was it and how did it change your mind?

Think of someone from your school, community, or in the world today who you think has a strong conscience. How do they live up to the nickname of "Conscience of (your school/community/today's world)"? What do they say or do that shows they have a strong conscience?

At first, Mercy Otis Warren was not able to publish her writing with her name attached because she was a woman. Can you think of any rules or ideas people still have about gender today? How are people trying to push against these rules like Mercy Otis Warren?

articles.title: Playing Pirate: Sailing on the Pride of Baltimore II
articles.author: India James
articles.description: A recreated ship from the 1800s allows visitors a chance to experience life as a pirate.
articles.intro: Pirates are people who rob other sailors who are carrying goods on the water. Privateers were a type of pirate common before the early 1800s that were hired by a country to attack enemy ships. In this article, India James discusses how people can pretend to be a pirate today.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view about the Pride of Baltimore II.
articles.permissions: "Playing Pirate: Sailing on the Pride of Baltimore II " by India James. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: BOOM! A cannon blasts. You look up and see great, white sails stretched above. Two tall masts are tilted slightly toward the back of the boat. There are square sails on the very top. It looks just like a pirate ship from long ago.
But this isn’t the 1800s. You aren’t sitting in a museum. On the Pride of Baltimore II , you don’t just pretend to sail a ship like ones that pirates sailed long ago. You can be part of the crew!
Pride of Baltimore II

Helping people become interested in sailing and teaching people about sailing history are two of the Pride of Baltimore II ’s main missions. As an ambassador for both its home city of Baltimore and its home state of Maryland, the ship helps people learn about both places as it sails. It makes trips up and down the Atlantic coast, into the Great Lakes, and even across the Atlantic Ocean.
It’s easy to see why people enjoy the Pride of Baltimore II — it doesn’t look like any other ship today!

The Pride of Baltimore II is a modern ship. It first set sail in 1988. But the plans for the ship were based off of a type of boat called a tea clipper . Tea clippers were fast ships. Sailors called privateers , a kind of legal pirate, loved to use tea clippers for their missions. They were often used to sneak up on larger ships and steal their cargo.

“ Clipping refers to clipping the time off of a common voyage,” said Captain Jan Miles. He’s been one of the Pride ’s captains since the 1980s. He explained that these types of ships were first designed for speed, like the racecars of their day. “These clippers were really, really fast!”
While the Pride of Baltimore II might look old, it has all of the safety features of a modern ship. GPS, weather reports, and radios all help make it a safe boat to sail.
Today, you can safely go aboard the Pride of Baltimore II and experience sailing like a pirate from long ago.
A Day in the Life of a Sailor

Life on a ship is divided into watches . These watches are like teams who take turns handling the ship. Duties while on watch can include steering the ship, taking care of the engine, keeping the deck clear, organizing the ropes used for sailing, and making sure the sails are helping the ship go as fast as possible. Most watches last about four hours.
Day and night, there are always people standing watch. You might find yourself sleeping at some very odd times and waking up in the middle of the night to help sail the ship!

When it isn’t your turn to be on watch, there are plenty of other things to do. Porpoises often swim alongside the ship. The captain or mates give lessons on navigation and steering. If you’re lucky — and daring — you might also get a chance to climb the rope ladders leading to the very tops of the sails.
“It’s just plain good sailing!” Captain Miles exclaimed.
Racing over the Waves
The tea clippers were the fastest ships of their time, and sailing with that speed is just as exciting today.

One of Captain Miles’ favorite memories is crossing the Atlantic Ocean with the Pride of Baltimore II . “When we have good conditions over a long period of time, we’re able to cover as much as 250 miles in 24 hours. It’s pretty breezy conditions! It’s pretty lively!” he said. “That can be a hugely wonderful experience.”
Sailing on a tall ship like the Pride of Baltimore II is an experience you’ll never forget. On the open ocean with sails stretched over your head, you’ll feel like a real pirate!

options.label: Visitors to the Pride of Baltimore II get to travel to different cities along the western coast of the United States to learn about being a pirate.
Sailors on the Pride of Baltimore II are modern-day pirates who want to teach travelers about their special and dangerous jobs.
Visitors to the Pride of Baltimore II can learn about the experience of pirates in the past while sailing on a modern tea clipper.
Sailors on the Pride of Baltimore II do dangerous jobs like climbing rope ladders, robbing other ships, and sailing very fast.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses a main idea of the text?

options.label: a curious feeling because the author describes many different pirate ships
a scary feeling because the author describes the dangerous jobs on pirate ships

articles.title: A Surrender
articles.author: Audrey DeAngelis and Gina DeAngelis
articles.description: Chief Joseph of the Nez Perce Nation tried to protect his people during the Westward Expansion.
articles.intro: About 500 different Native American nations lived in North America before Europeans arrived at the end of the 1400s. These nations fought to keep their traditional homes and ways of life as European nations and settlers slowly took over the land. In this text, Audrey DeAngelis and Gina DeAngelis write about Chief Joseph (1840-1904), a famous leader of the Nez Perce Native American nation.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the changes that were forced on Chief Joseph’s people.
articles.permissions: "A Surrender" by Audrey DeAngelis and Gina DeAngelis, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Chief Joseph is credited with giving a poignant speech on October 5, 1877. His simple but heartfelt words captured the situation faced by all Native Americans when dealing with the U.S. government.

Born about 1840, Hin-mah-too-yah-lat-kekt (“Thunder Rolling From the Mountains”) was given the Christian name “Joseph” after his father. Young Joseph grew up in the Wallowa Valley in present-day Oregon. He was taught to respect the land and to guide his Nez Perce people.

Like many Native Americans, the Nez Perce believed that the earth was their spiritual mother. They didn’t believe a person could own land. To divide, buy, or sell it was impossible. It was like buying parts of the sky or the ocean.

But westward-moving white settlers did believe in owning land. And they didn’t want to share it with Native Americans. They demanded that the U.S. government prevent Native Americans from roaming freely throughout the West. The federal government sent the U.S. army to protect white settlers and restrict the movement of Native Americans. Throughout the 1800s, the United States broke promises and crafted dishonest treaties with many native groups.

The Nez Perce’s traditional homeland was in the Pacific Northwest. It included parts of present-day Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and Montana. In 1855 and 1863, the U.S. government and a few Nez Perce leaders signed two treaties. They created reservations out of small sections of Nez Perce land. Most of the Nez Perce homeland was taken away. Even though only some leaders had signed, U.S. officials claimed that the treaty applied to all Nez Perce. As a member of a nonsigning group, Chief Joseph tried unsuccessfully to negotiate for his people to keep their homeland.

Many Native American groups found themselves on the losing side of treaties. Some fought back. In June 1876, Sitting Bull led his Lakota Sioux and Northern Cheyenne and Arapaho allies in an astonishing victory over the U.S. army. He defeated Lieutenant Colonel George A. Custer’s 7th Cavalry in the Battle of the Greasy Grass. It is more famously known as the Battle of the Little Bighorn. After that defeat, the U.S. government grew determined to end all Native American resistance in the West.

In May 1877, the non-treaty Nez Perce were given 30 days to get to a reservation at Lapwai in present-day Idaho. The army threatened to use force, if necessary. Chief Joseph and his band of Nez Perce reluctantly set out. As they approached Lapwai, more than 700 Nez Perce from different bands gathered for a final celebration. While they camped, several young men sought to avenge a murder committed by a white man the year before. Their actions set off a series of raids. Young warriors attacked white people. White settlers counterattacked.

Lean Elk, Looking Glass, White Bird, and other Nez Perce leaders decided to resist being forced to live on the reservation. They hoped the Crow Indians would help them. When that failed, they decided to seek aid from Sitting Bull. Sitting Bull had fled to Canada with his people.

The Nez Perce spent the next five months in a race to the border. They traveled more than 1,100 miles from Oregon across Idaho and through Montana. The U.S. army followed, picking fights all the way. Some Nez Perce struck back. At least twice, the army forced the Nez Perce out of their camps. Families fled for their lives, abandoning clothing, blankets, food, and equipment.

Finally, the Nez Perce camped at Bear Paw, only 40 miles from Canada. There, Colonel Nelson A. Miles and his soldiers attacked the camp. About 200 Nez Perce escaped and rode north. Others fled to nearby mountains to hide. A blizzard hit that night. The Nez Perce resisted starting fires because it would reveal to the soldiers where they were.

After a nearly weeklong siege, Chief Joseph ended his people’s flight to freedom. His brother, Ollokot, and several chiefs had been killed. He had no supplies. His band of mostly women, children, and elders could not reach Canada. Through a messenger, Chief Joseph informed Miles, “I am tired of fighting.”

Chief Joseph didn’t speak English. When he surrendered to Miles in October 1877, the men used an interpreter named Arthur Chapman to translate their words. An army officer, C.E.S. Wood, wrote down Joseph’s and Chapman’s words. Three weeks after the meeting, the first printed version of the speech appeared in the Tri-Weekly Tribune of Bismarck, Dakota Territory (present-day North Dakota).

Chief Joseph and about 400 Nez Perce were taken to reservations in Kansas and Indian Territory (present-day Oklahoma). Chief Joseph later traveled to Washington, D.C., to plead for his people’s return to the Wallowa Valley. In 1884, the Nez Perce moved to a reservation in Washington state.

Government officials had mistakenly believed that Chief Joseph commanded all the Nez Perce. But Chief Joseph was not a warrior. He was the leader and spokesman of one band of the Nez Perce. In talks with U.S. officials, however, Chief Joseph earned the respect of many Americans. He spoke for his people with eloquence and dignity.

“Tell General [Oliver O.] Howard I know his heart. What he told me before, I have in my heart. I am tired of fighting. Our chiefs are killed. Looking Glass is dead. Toohoolhoolzote is dead. The old men are all dead. It is the young men who say yes and no. He who led on the young men is dead. It is cold and we have no blankets. The children are freezing to death. My people, some of them, have run away to the hills and have no blankets, no food; no one knows where they are — perhaps freezing to death. I want to have time to look for my children and see how many I can find. Maybe I can find them among the dead. Hear me my chiefs. I am tired; my heart is sick and sad. From where the sun now stands I will fight no more forever.”
— Chief Joseph, surrender to U.S. army, October 5, 1877

articles.title: Anthony Benezet: Teaching Kindness
articles.author: Ruben Brosbe
articles.description: Teacher and abolitionist Anthony Benezet (1713-1784) believed in kindness and equality for all people.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Ruben Brosbe describes the life of Anthony Benezet (1713-1784), a teacher and abolitionist from the 1700s. Abolitionists were people who believed in ending slavery at a time when slavery was still allowed by law.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author describes Anthony Benezet’s achievements.
articles.permissions: "Anthony Benezet: Teaching Kindness" by Ruben Brosbe. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: When the United States declared independence and freedom from Great Britain in 1776, hundreds of thousands of Black people were enslaved. Sometimes when people talk about this time in history, they say, “That’s just the way things were” or “That’s how people thought back then.” But that’s just not true. The hundreds of thousands of Black people who were enslaved knew they were being treated wrongly. And there were also white people alive back then who knew slavery was wrong, too. One of them was a man named Anthony Benezet.

Anthony was born in France in 1713. His family were members of a group of Christians called the Huguenots. It wasn’t safe to be a Huguenot in France at the time, so Anthony and his family ran away. First, they went to Holland, then to London, and finally, they arrived in Philadelphia in 1731.

As he grew older, Anthony became a teacher. After he noticed that most girls were not getting the same education as boys, Anthony opened the first public school for girls in America. To Anthony, kindness was one of the most important parts of being a part of a community. Anthony used kindness as the main building block of his teaching.

While Anthony was in London, he became a Quaker. Quakers are a group of Christians who believe in peace and equality. But in Philadelphia and elsewhere, some Quakers took part in the slave trade. Anthony disagreed with this. Anthony became one of the earliest abolitionists , people who believed in ending slavery. He and his friend John Woolman worked tirelessly to change people’s minds. In 1758, Quaker groups in Pennsylvania and New Jersey announced that slavery was against Christian beliefs.

Anthony wrote pamphlets and books that explained why slavery was unjust. Some of his writing became popular back in Great Britain as well. Anthony’s words built support for ending slavery on both sides of the Atlantic Ocean. In Philadelphia, Anthony helped start the Pennsylvania Abolition Society in 1775.

Anthony continued to teach until the end of his life. He had problems with his health, but he taught enslaved Black children in his home. In 1770, Anthony started a school for Black children. He taught there and continued to teach at the school for girls. Some white abolitionists still held racist ideas about Black people. Anthony believed in treating all people with kindness and respect, regardless of the color of their skin. From his experience teaching Black children, he knew that people who thought white people were smarter or better were wrong. He wrote of the “genius” he saw in his Black students. Anthony told people that Black students were just as talented as white students.
In his life, Anthony also assisted Indigenous Americans and refugees. He was against all war.

Anthony died at age 71 in 1784. When he died, hundreds of Black Philadelphians came out to mourn his passing. Anthony could not end slavery while he was alive, but he used his words and actions to fight against slavery in a time when many white people did not. His example helped others continue fighting for abolition long after he died.

options.label: Anthony Benezet was a popular teacher, and his successful teaching style is still used today.
Anthony Benezet had strong beliefs, and he worked hard to achieve equal rights for all people.
Anthony Benezet worked his whole life to become famous and succeeded just before his death.
Anthony Benezet came to the United States to become a Quaker and became well-known in church communities.
