articles.title: Spies in Petticoats
articles.author: Judith E. Harper and Andrew Matthews
articles.description: Women spies faced danger during the Civil War to support the Union and the Confederacy.
articles.intro: The Civil War was fought in America from 1861-1865 between the North, called the Union, and the South, called the Confederate States of America. In this text, authors Judith E. Harper and Andrew Matthews tell the stories of women spies on both sides of the Civil War.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the results of each spy’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Spies in Petticoats" by Judith E. Harper and Andrew Matthews, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Women of all ages worked as spies on both sides during the Civil War (1861–1865). They had some advantages in those roles. Women at that time were believed to be the weaker sex. For that reason, no one at first suspected that women and girls who traveled back and forth might be carrying secret information. Soldiers not only were unlikely to suspect women, but they also were reluctant to search women. The custom of the time was that only a woman’s husband or her doctor was permitted to touch her. Women spies took advantage of that tradition and their clothing, too. Hoop skirts and petticoats made it easy to hide messages, papers, and small packages. As soldiers learned about female agents, however, women were more likely to be stopped. Here’s a look at some women who served as spies during the Civil War.

Rose O’Neal Greenhow was a prominent widow in Washington society. She was friendly with Northern politicians and military leaders. But she strongly believed that the South had a constitutional right to secede from the Union. She vowed to help the Confederacy achieve that goal. She collected information about the Union army in social situations. She passed along what she learned to Confederate leaders through a network of spies on the Secret Line. Greenhow played a key role in July 1861, before the First Battle of Bull Run (also known as Manassas). She persuaded a Union officer to tell her the strength of the Union troops preparing for that battle. She sent the information to Confederate officers. As a result, even though the South was outnumbered, it won a decisive victory. Union secret detective Allan Pinkerton grew suspicious of Greenhow and eventually arrested her. She was ordered to leave the North. In 1864, Greenhow drowned as she was returning from a mission to England. She was trying to escape a Union gunboat when the rowboat she was in overturned. She was buried with full military honors.

Elizabeth Van Lew was a brilliant spy. She lived in Richmond, Virginia, but she secretly was a pro-Union abolitionist. She volunteered in the city’s Libby Prison, where she learned information from Union prisoners. She led an efficient group of spies. Intelligence gathered by Van Lew and her ring proved crucial in the victories of Union lieutenant general Ulysses S. Grant’s troops later in the war. Van Lew and her fellow spies used an invisible ink to write their messages between the lines of “ordinary” letters. Van Lew also created a cipher to safeguard the facts in her communications.

One of Van Lew’s most important spies was a woman believed to be named Mary Elizabeth Bowser. She was a free, educated black woman. Van Lew arranged for Bowser to work in the Confederate White House in Richmond. Bowser secretly listened to Confederate president Jefferson Davis’s conversations with government and military leaders. Sometimes, she was able to find and read important documents. She passed the information to another spy in the ring. The information was relayed until it reached the appropriate Union army members.

Belle Boyd was 18 years old when Union troops invaded her hometown in western Virginia. She carried information about Union activities to Confederate generals. The highlight of her career came in May 1862. Confederate general Thomas J. “Stonewall” Jackson was preparing to push Union troops out of western Virginia. Boyd raced on horseback to Jackson’s headquarters. She delivered news about the location and the size of Union forces. That intelligence enabled Jackson to force the Union army to retreat. Boyd was arrested a number of times before she finally moved to Canada and then England.

Harriet Tubman escaped from slavery in 1849. She then guided enslaved people to freedom on the Underground Railroad. During the Civil War, she used her knowledge of the South to spy for the Union army. She led an important raid in 1863. The Union army controlled the coast in South Carolina, while Confederate troops held positions inland. Tubman guided three Union gunboats and 300 Union troops up South Carolina’s Combahee River. She led the Union forces to several warehouses full of Confederate rice and cotton. The Union soldiers seized those supplies. They also freed 750 people enslaved on plantations. Many of the freed men became Union soldiers. Tubman also convinced other brave African Americans to join her as spies, even though they risked being hanged if they were caught.

Early in the war, Union soldiers took control of Fairfax, Virginia. Union officers boarded in the Ford family’s home. A Ford son was serving under Confederate general J.E.B. Stuart. For nearly two years, Antonia Ford passed along information she overheard about the Union military to Confederate officers. In early 1863, Ford was arrested and jailed at the Old Capitol Prison in Washington, D.C. Union spies believed she was responsible for the capture of a Union general. The arresting officer was Union major Joseph Willard. Willard had fallen in love with Ford and tried to get her freed. She was released after she took a loyalty oath. She married Willard a year later.

Actress Pauline Cushman was pro-Union, but she pretended to be a loyal Confederate. She wore disguises and roamed the Tennessee countryside in 1863. She used a cover story of being kicked out of the North for supporting the Confederacy. She said she was looking for her brother as she traveled with Confederate forces and learned about their defenses. After several months, Confederate officers grew suspicious. They arrested Cushman. She was found with written plans on her and was sentenced to death for being a spy. Days before she was to be executed, the Union army raided the town where she was imprisoned and freed her. President Abraham Lincoln made her an honorary major.

options.label: women put aside their differences to help each other.
women on both sides faced danger to help their cause.
women spies did not care about the war, but helped anyway.
women spies helped their sides in ways men did not want to.

articles.title: Working on a WWOOF Farm
articles.author: India James
articles.description: WWOOF-ing allows people to experience what it is like to work on an organic farm.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James explains what it is like to visit and work on an organic farm. Organic farming is farming that is done naturally and without any added chemicals.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the benefits of WWOOF-ing.
articles.permissions: "Working on a WWOOf Farm" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: All over the world, farmers work hard to produce food. Some farmers grow plants. Other farmers raise animals. Have you ever wondered what it would be like to work on a farm?

If you’re curious what it’s like to grow food and raise animals, there’s a way to find out! WWOOF stands for World Wide Organization of Organic Farmers. WWOOF is an organization of farmers who like to have visitors, called WWOOFers, work and live on their farms. You can try farming for yourself!
The Life of a Farmer
Every farm is different, but they all require a lot of work. What you do will depend a lot on the time of year.
Rachelle Bostwick is a farmer in Michigan. She grows vegetables and elderberries on her farm. She opens her farm to WWOOFers from May to September each year.

“During this time, they help with everything on the farm,” she said. “May is a lot of greenhouse work: seeding, potting up, filling trays, and labeling plants. June is more field work: planting, weeding, mowing, irrigation. July is getting into harvest season: picking, weeding, lots of tending to crops. August is harvest time for the elderberries: picking, packing, processing berries. September is a big harvest month for vegetables, and we also do a lot of clean up from the greenhouse and elderberries. Through the season, we also do farm maintenance and upkeep projects.”

Farmers need help caring for the plants from the time they’re seedlings to when they harvest food. In the winter, the farmers need help repairing anything that was broken during the warm months. The buildings on farms need care, too, just like plants and animals!

If there are animals on the farm where you’re working, you might have different tasks. You might feed the animals or help gather eggs or bring in milk. Animals need shelter, and you could find yourself building a chicken coop or even helping to repair a barn.

Everything on a WWOOF farm is done to help care for the earth. You’ll learn how to grow plants without harsh chemicals. You’ll also find out how the farmers take care of the ground. That way it can produce food for many years to come.
Not Just Work
Farming is a lot of work, but that’s not all there is to do when you’re WWOOF-ing! Taking time to relax and enjoy the area around each farm is a big part of working with a WWOOF group.
WWOOF farms are often in beautiful locations. There can be places to hike or bike in the summer. Farms that are in cold climates may have skiing and snowshoe trails.

Near Rachelle’s farm, she says there’s a lot to do. “There is a nice park with nature trails and a small beach a couple of miles away. And the town of Sparta is close too. There are summer concerts and events there.”
At the end of the day, it’s important to have a nice place to relax and recharge for the next day of work.
Around the World
WWOOF farms aren’t just in the U.S. You can find WWOOF farms on every continent besides Antarctica!

In Sweden, you might work with horse breeders. In Africa, you could help schools develop gardens for their students. Farms need help from WWOOFers, but it’s also important to connect people with different cultures. Working on a farm and seeing how people live is a good way to make friends.
If you’ve ever wanted to see what farming is like and learn about a new place at the same time, then WWOOF-ing is for you!

options.label: WWOOF-ing programs help farmers from all over the world travel to new areas and teach about organic farming.
WWOOF-ing allows people to try out many different kinds of organic farming before they go to college or get a job.
WWOOF-ing allows people to experience what it is like to live and work on one of the many organic farms around the world.
WWOOF-ing is a way for farmers to teach children about the importance of growing and using food that is original to one place.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?

articles.title: Go on Vacation, Come Back a Cattle Herder!
articles.author: India James
articles.description: At dude ranches, tourists can experience life as a cowboy or cowgirl.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James explains what it is like to visit a dude ranch.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the experiences people have when visiting a dude ranch.
articles.permissions: "Go on Vacation, Come Back a Cattle Herder!" by India James. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Long ago, cattle roamed over wide, open land in western North America. Cowboys and cowgirls tended to herds from the southern part of the U.S. all the way north to British Columbia in Canada. They slept outside to guard the animals. They cooked food over campfires. They were some of the best horse riders around.

The days of wide roaming cattle are long gone, but that doesn’t mean the fun is over. Dude ranches have helped guests experience the cowboy and cowgirl life for more than 100 years! Even today, visiting a dude ranch means helping take care of animals, eating some of the best meals ever, and, of course, riding horses!
Horse Riding

People who lived or worked on ranches used to call people from the city “dudes.” Dude ranches were invented to show people from cities what life tending to cattle was like. On a dude ranch, visitors can have all the fun of being outdoors without any of the danger that cowboys and cowgirls faced long ago.
Horse riding is an important part of being on a dude ranch. Many people who visit dude ranches already know how to ride. But there are always lessons if a guest needs to learn.
What if you’re already an expert rider? Then maybe you can try your hand at cattle sorting.

Real ranchers have to sort cattle. They take growing calves from the adult cows. They also have to separate the cows they take to market. At White Stallion Ranch, a dude ranch in Arizona, team cattle sorting becomes a competition.

“You go in teams of four,” said Susanne Walsh, who lives and works at the White Stallion Ranch. “It’s a timed event. You ride to the other end of the arena, in and amongst all of these little steers. You try and cut one out… Cutting is the idea of taking one animal away from the herd. You can imagine it’s a little tricky… You ride in and cut out one of them. Then you and your team try to ride behind it, and it’s called ‘pushing it’— like pushing cattle — and you push it across the arena, down a little bit, and back up into an enclosure.”
The fastest team wins! It’s an exciting way to put your riding skills to the test.
Living the Outdoors Life
“We don’t have televisions in our rooms!” said Susanne. “You are here to be outdoors in the fresh air. Yourself, your friends, your family… and the beautiful environment you’re in.”

Dude ranches have many activities other than riding horses. Hiking is a fun way to see gorgeous scenery. Many ranches are also near rivers or lakes. Canoeing, rafting, and fishing are all great ways to spend time outside. Learning to cook over a campfire is another way to experience the cowboy and cowgirl life. Even if you don’t make a full meal, enjoying freshly-toasted s’mores is always a treat!
A vacation on a dude ranch is the trip of a lifetime for anyone who has ever dreamed of living the cowboy or cowgirl life!

options.label: People can help cowboys and cowgirls with their difficult jobs at a dude ranch, like sorting horses and farming.
Though cattle herding is no longer a popular job, many people like the thrill and danger of spending time at a dude ranch.
Dude ranches are fun places where visitors can learn about how to take care of cattle and spend time enjoying the outdoors.
Cowboys and cowgirls run dude ranches so that people from the city have a chance to learn about where their food comes from.

articles.title: Sharing a Dream
articles.author: Jim Haskins
articles.description: Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. was a famous civil rights leader who continues to inspire others.
articles.intro: Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. (1929-1968) was a Baptist minister and a leader of the African American Civil Rights Movement. In this text, Jim Haskins explains his important work fighting against segregation, or the separation of people of different races.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what made King “the right man at the right time.”
articles.permissions: "Sharing a Dream" by Jim Haskins, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was a young Baptist minister in 1955. He was thrust into the headlines as a result of the 1955-56 Montgomery, Alabama, bus boycott. The boycott had resulted after civil rights activist Rosa Parks was arrested for refusing to give up her seat on a city bus to a white passenger. Black city leaders saw it as an opportunity to test laws that enforced the racial segregation of public facilities. They called on Black residents to refuse to use the city’s bus system.

King had been reluctant at first to accept election as president of the Montgomery Improvement Association (MIA). The MIA was formed to publicize and raise money to support the bus boycott. But King was excited to practice the principles of nonviolent protest that Mohandas K. “Mahatma” Gandhi had used to help win India’s independence from Great Britain in the 1940s. King had dreamed about the possibility of using Gandhi’s peaceful “soul force” to attack the problem of racial segregation in the United States.
At the time, the church was the center of the African American community. It was no accident that Black ministers led the way in the civil rights movement. They offered moral and spiritual support.

King stood out for his brilliance as an orator. He also believed that Americans had a basic sense of decency. He was convinced that Americans would respond to non-violent protests and the sheer force of moral right. He had the ability to inspire others to follow him.

When the civil rights movement became more confrontational, King grew as a leader. But even a leader as special as King benefited from the timing of certain events. King was the right man at the right time. During the 1950s and 1960s, a combination of events made American society open to greater change.

Change had begun in the late 1940s. In 1948, President Harry S. Truman issued Executive Order 9981. It banned segregation in the armed forces. Change continued in the early 1950s. Attorneys for the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) won a landmark case against segregated education. In its 1954 ruling in Brown v. Board of Education , the U.S. Supreme Court declared segregated education unconstitutional. White and Black Americans realized that the decision extended into every area of society.

In 1957, King and the Rev. Ralph Abernathy gathered more than 60 Black ministers from all over the South to form the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC). The SCLC took charge of the “spiritual strategy” for civil rights protests.

Advances in technology spread the scenes of nonviolent protest. When southern whites responded to civil rights protests with violence, television broadcast the ugly images across the nation and around the world. It was impossible for Americans to ignore what was going on. And it was impossible for local officials to portray the scenes as isolated events. King could speak to a television camera as effectively as he could to a church congregation.

King traveled widely to spread his message. Thousands of people — Black and white, and young and old, educated and uneducated — responded to his call. They risked losing their jobs, their homes, and their lives in the struggle for civil rights. They looked to him as a beacon of hope and inspiration.

On August 28, 1963, an estimated 250,000 people gathered in front of the Lincoln Memorial. They were participating in the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom in the nation’s capital. It was a well-organized, well-run massive civil rights protest. A number of African American leaders addressed the crowd. Then, King came to the podium. He delivered his famous “I Have a Dream” speech. The speech shared King’s hope for the next generation of Americans to be free of racial prejudice. He shared his dream that people would not judge one another based on the color of their skin.

In the name of the civil rights movement, King was arrested, jailed, and stabbed. He survived a bombing at his home. He was killed by an assassin in 1968. But his words touched the nation. And his message of nonviolence inspired people around the world.

options.label: Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. learned about nonviolent protest from Mohandas K. "Mahatma" Gandhi.
Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s message about racial inequality was powerful and inspirational.
The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom was the largest protest in American history.
The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom made changes in America instantly.

articles.title: Meet the New Woman
articles.author: Gwen Hoerr Jordan
articles.description: Life for many American women changed after the 19th Amendment gave them the right to vote.
articles.intro: The 19th Amendment to the United States Constitution, passed in 1920, gave women the right to vote. In this article, Gwen Hoerr Jordan discusses how life for women changed during this time period.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how life for American women changed in the 1920s.
articles.permissions: "Meet the New Woman" by Gwen Hoerr Jordan, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Many American women felt that they finally had become full citizens on August 26, 1920. That was when the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution became law. The amendment granted women the right to vote. Women had struggled for the cause for more than 75 years. With the right to vote came a voice in supporting issues that were important to women — to attend and graduate from college, to own property, and to hold jobs in all types of occupations. Many American women began to enjoy their new liberties in the new decade.

One of the major changes was how young women interacted with society. Historically, women had assumed subservient roles to the men in their lives. They had followed decisions made by husbands, fathers, or brothers. They had behaved modestly and did not go out in public without a chaperone. They had followed fashions that said that they had to wear long dresses, gloves, and hats that together covered most of their bodies. They also had pinned up their long hair to rest on their heads.

Those attitudes and behaviors began to change in the 1920s as flappers hit the social scene. A flapper was an independent woman in her 20s who demanded equality and who showed disdain for “acceptable” women’s behavior. Flappers appeared in movies and magazines as a symbol of women’s newfound freedom.

A flapper went on dates, wore makeup, danced, drank alcohol, and smoked cigarettes. She cut her long hair into a short bob. She wore dresses with a drop waist and a short skirt — that grew shorter through the decade. The short skirt revealed her legs. And if a flapper could afford to, she also wore silk stockings to show off her legs.

Like flappers, college coeds exerted an air of unprecedented independence. Coeds were female students at schools that also admitted male students. In 1837, Oberlin College in Oberlin, Ohio, had become the first coeducational college in the United States. By 1920, there were more than 650 colleges that admitted both men and women. By then, more than seven percent of American women between the ages of 18 and 21 attended college.

High school and college-age women increasingly participated in sports in the 1920s. Women had been active in organized sports since the late 19th century. They also participated in the Olympics in 1900, but they were allowed to compete only in tennis, golf, and croquet. During the 1920, 1924, and 1928 Olympics, however, American women competed with women from around the world in a variety of sports. The list included swimming, diving, skating, skiing, tennis, horseshoe pitching, table tennis, fencing, golf, field hockey, basketball, baseball, archery, and track and field. And new athletic outfits, with their shorter skirts and loose fit, allowed women greater mobility when playing sports.

Life in the 1920s was not all play, however. Most women had to work, too. Working-class women often had jobs as maids in private homes. But throughout the decade, more women were able to branch into new fields. They found jobs in stores and offices as clerks and typists. College graduates pursued professional careers as teachers, nurses, librarians, writers, doctors, and lawyers. As women entered the workforce, they earned salaries that allowed them to grow financially independent.

Some professional women went into politics. Immediately after they won the right to vote, women suffragists founded the National League of Women Voters. The group educated women on their new political liberties and supported female candidates. In 1922, Ohio judge Florence E. Allen became the first woman in the country to be elected to a state supreme court. Women held seats in their state legislatures. More than 10 women served in the U.S. House of Representatives.

When the Great Depression began in 1929, much of women’s independent spirit took a back seat to the difficult economic situation that the nation faced. Male leaders advised women to stay home and take care of their families while men worked. But having had their first real taste of what it was like to be self-reliant and treated equally, women were determined to keep the momentum of their changing role in society.

options.label: After gaining the right to vote, women found many new ways to express their new-found freedom.
When they had the right to vote, women became equal to men in every part of American society.
Because they could not vote, life for women in the 1920s was largely controlled by others.
While fighting for the right to vote, women got more jobs in governments and schools.

articles.title: First Contact with Europeans
articles.author: Stephen Currie
articles.description: Stephen Currie discusses European explorers' first contact with American Indians in 1513.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Stephen Currie discusses Europeans' first contact with the Indigenous Americans who lived where Florida is today. During this time period, Europeans were just beginning to build settlements on the land where Indigenous Americans already lived.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the interactions between Europeans and Indigenous Americans.
articles.permissions: "First Contact with Europeans" by Stephen Currie, Cobblestone , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: In 1513, Spanish explorer Juan Ponce de León led an expedition northwest from Puerto Rico. He and his men were looking for a land known as Bimini. According to rumor, Bimini was full of gold. It also was said to be home to the magical Fountain of Youth — a spring whose waters kept people forever young.

Ponce de León never located Bimini — at least, he found neither gold nor the Fountain of Youth. He did, however, become the first European to see Florida, as well as the first to meet the native people who lived there. As the explorers sailed north along Florida’s southwestern shoreline, they saw some Calusa people beckoning them to come ashore. Curious, the Spanish crew dropped anchor and sent several men in small boats to the beach.

This first encounter between Europeans and Indigenous Americans of the Southeast was far from peaceful. Intrigued by the supplies the visitors carried, the Calusas immediately began carting away tools, oars, and other equipment. When the outnumbered Europeans objected, a Calusa struck and wounded a Spaniard. A brief battle followed, during which two more sailors were injured. The men barely managed to escape back to the safety of the ship.

Ponce de León resolved to return. In 1521, he made a second voyage to Florida. This time, he built a small colony on the shores of the west coast of Florida, the Gulf of Mexico. The Calusas there, however, were even less welcoming than the ones Ponce de León had met in 1513. Within a few months, the Calusa people attacked the colony, killed many of the Spanish — including Ponce de León — and drove away the rest.

Still, the Spanish persisted. In 1539, Hernando de Soto traveled through much of the Southeast, becoming the first European to visit the Indigenous American peoples of Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi. Unlike the Calusas, however, many of these tribes welcomed the explorers. One Creek ruler, for instance, spoke gracious words of friendship and courtesy while giving de Soto a beautiful string of pearls. Unfortunately, de Soto did not respond in the same manner. Backed by well-armed troops numbering six hundred, he forced the tribes to provide his men with food and shelter. De Soto also enslaved many Indigenous Americans.

Like Spain, England and France also sent expeditions to the Southeast. The three countries soon began to build towns, forts, and trading posts on Indigenous American land. In 1564, the French built Fort Caroline near present-day Jacksonville, Florida. The Spanish settled St. Augustine, Florida, one year later. And in 1607, the English established a permanent settlement at Jamestown, Virginia. Little by little, the Europeans were gaining a foothold in the Southeast.

Even when European settlement began, some Indigenous Americans were welcoming to the foreigners. Trade was one important reason for this acceptance. The Europeans brought with them metal tools, firearms, and many other objects unknown in North America. Southeastern Indigenous Americans were eager to trade their own resources for these new valuables.

Other tribes accepted the Europeans for political reasons. Powhatan, the leader of Virginia’s Indigenous Peoples, for instance, probably could have destroyed Jamestown soon after it was built, but decided against it. He hoped instead to make a military alliance with the newcomers and eventually defeat his own local enemies.

There were Indigenous American tribes who were deeply suspicious of the Europeans, however. And as time went on, more and more Southeastern Indigenous Americans came to see the negative side of contact with Europeans. The English, French, and Spanish were not interested in sharing the land in North America with the Indigenous Americans who had been living there. Rather, the Europeans wanted to expand their empires, control the area’s resources, and drive the native peoples away. And, the Europeans’ advanced weaponry gave them an enormous edge in fulfilling these desires. Even the Indigenous Americans’ greater numbers could not make up for the Europeans’ firepower.

European contact brought other problems, too. New and unfamiliar diseases were spread among the Indigenous American populations. Because they had never been exposed to these illnesses and had no built-in immunity, the diseases swept through the tribes. In some parts of the Southeast, nine out of ten Indigenous Americans died of diseases ranging from measles to chicken pox.

By 1754, it was too late to stop the European advance. The first contact between the Calusa people and Ponce de León, two and a half centuries before, had ended with the Spanish being pushed out to sea. But the balance of power had changed. The destruction of the traditional southeastern Indigenous American culture was well under way.

options.label: Indigenous Americans avoided Europeans when they first arrived.
Europeans were willing to share the land with Indigenous Americans.
Indigenous Americans and Europeans both benefited equally from their alliance.
Europeans clashed with Indigenous Americans when they arrived in North America.

articles.title: The Largest and Smallest Dinos Ever (Maybe)
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: Scientists continually use new information to determine the largest and smallest dinosaurs.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes how scientists study fossils, left behind traces of dinosaurs, to figure out which of them were record-breakers.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what scientists have learned about the largest and smallest dinosaurs.
articles.permissions: "The Largest and Smallest Dinos Ever! (Maybe)" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Its name is Supersaurus . No joke. Super — as in huge, powerful, extreme.

And, wow, was it ever extreme! This super dinosaur measured at least 128 feet — possibly even 137 feet — from its snout to the tip of its tail. That's longer than three and a half full-sized school buses lined up end to end. And it’s probably longer than any other creature that ever walked the Earth.

Supersaurus lived about 150 million years ago during the Jurassic period in an area that’s now Wyoming and Colorado. It was a sauropod, a type of plant-eating dinosaur that walked on all four legs and had a small head at the end of a really long neck, a massive body, and a long, whip-like tail. Supersaurus’s tail alone was 60 feet long — the length of a bowling alley — and its legs were 12 feet tall. An adult human could have ridden a bike under its belly without ducking.
You’d think that a dinosaur that long — and with that cool name — might be the biggest dino ever. But, no. Scientists agree that Supersaurus was the longest dinosaur, but not the largest overall.
So who was?

Meet Argentinosaurus , a super heavyweight that tipped the scales at somewhere between 66 to 100 tons — twice the weight of Supersaurus and about the same as an entire herd of 20 or so African elephants. Those amazing figures clearly make Argentinosaurus the champ! Right? Well, probably.

Unless, that is, Patagotitan was even larger, like some scientists think. Or maybe the mysterious Amphicoelias dwarfed them all. Then again, a new sauropod — still unnamed — that paleontologists began digging up in 2012 in Argentina looks like it was even larger.
So why don’t we know for sure?

First, there’s the problem of how you measure something that’s been extinct for millions of years. It is extremely rare to find anything close to a complete dinosaur skeleton. So most dinosaurs, especially the really big ones, are identified by just a few bones — sometimes by only one bone! It’s clear that sauropods, the long-necked, four-legged plant eaters, towered above other dinosaurs, including monstrous meat-eaters like Tyrannosaurus rex and Spinosaurus . But it's hard to tell which one was the largest of all.

Scientists do their best to estimate the dino’s size and appearance by comparing it to similar dinosaurs and other animals. They take all the information they have and use formulas and 3D computer models to come up with an estimate. It works best when paleontologists find the upper arm bone and thigh bone from the same sauropod, because those bones are the most important for an animal when it comes to movement and supporting its weight. As scientists find more fossils of the same dinosaur, they can improve their estimates.

But even when we think we have all the information on dinos sorted out, scientists make new discoveries that often change a lot about what we thought we knew. Scientists learned, for example, that sauropods’ neck bones were filled with air to keep them light — instead of marrow like in mammals’ bones. So you can’t say, Well, that dinosaur was about ten times bigger than an elephant, so we’ll just multiply an elephant’s weight by ten . That won’t work, because the two animals aren’t built alike. As paleontologist Kristi Curry-Rogers told the publication Scientific American , “With nothing quite like sauropods today, our work on these creatures is akin to studying aliens.”

Sometimes our understanding of dinosaurs changes when paleontologists find a new fossil, like the giant sauropod recently discovered in Argentina. Based on its hip, shoulder and tail bones, the sauropod — which lived 98 million years ago during the late Jurassic to early Cretaceous periods — was possibly bigger than Argentinosaurus and Patagotitan . But scientists haven’t found its upper arm and thigh bones, so they don’t know for sure.

Scientists also might learn something new about an old fossil. Paleontologist Brian Curtice uncovered mistakes that a fossil finder had made 50 years ago when Supersaurus was first discovered. He was able to get a better estimate of the dinosaur’s length by re-examining the old fossilized bones, identifying new ones from the same animal, and comparing the skeleton to others found more recently.

It’s not any easier figuring out which dinosaur claims the title of “smallest dinosaur ever” — at least among extinct dinosaurs. (If you want to be really technical, scientists consider birds to be “avian dinosaurs.” So the smallest dinosaur would be the bee hummingbird, which lives only in Cuba. It’s about two inches long and weighs as much as a dime.)

Among extinct dinosaurs, one of the cutest little dinos contending for the honor of “smallest ever” was found back in the 1970s in Argentina. The little dinosaur fossil was around six inches long (not including its tail), about the size of a small lizard. Scientists named it Mussaurus from the Latin word mus , which means “mouse.” They may have been a bit embarrassed later when they realized that their fossil was actually a baby, and the adult “mouse-dinos” grew to be roughly the size of a minivan.

Other dinosaurs grew no bigger than chickens or turkeys. One of the smallest dinosaurs was Parvicursor , which means “slender runner.” True to its name, the slim little dinosaur, which lived during the late Cretaceous period around 72 million years ago in what is now Mongolia, had long, skinny legs. It measured only 15 inches long and weighed about as much as a packet of six wooden pencils. Even smaller was a flightless, feathered dinosaur named Eosinopteryx , which wasn’t even a foot long. Living in what is now northeastern China during the Jurassic period, Eosinopteryx looked a little bit like a bird — except with a short, toothed snout and skinny tail.

Scientists continue to unearth new dinosaur fossils, learn more about existing fossils, and improve their techniques for estimating the size and weight of dinosaurs. As they do, we’ll learn more about these amazing creatures — both whoppers and pipsqueaks alike — and find new record breakers. The folks at Guinness World Records will have a tough job keeping up!

articles.title: The Uninvited Houseguests
articles.author: Pamela Bailes
articles.description: A spirited girl and her mother must figure out how to protect their family from a bobcat family.
articles.intro: As the global population increases and cities grow larger, animals are left with less of their natural habitats, or the areas where they live. In this story, a girl and her mother must figure out how to protect themselves and the family pets before it is too late.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the narrator’s thoughts.
articles.permissions: "The Uninvited Houseguests" by Pamela Bailes, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. blocks.text: I may be a girl, but I’m not a wimp. I’m eleven, and by the time we moved to Arizona from Texas I’d been exploring on my horse with my dogs following along for four years. I collected tadpoles, frogs, horny toads, minnows, orphaned rabbits, wounded birds, and whatever else I ran across. If it was alive and I could catch it, I brought it home to watch and take care of. I was riding with a friend one day when we found a possum asleep on the ground. I picked it up and carried it dangling by its tail all the way back to my house. My dad freaked out, but how was I supposed to know possums have fifty teeth and very unpleasant personalities? Since then I try to find out something about the pets I bring home.

I hadn’t been in Cave Creek, Arizona, long before I realized that even a non-wimp had a lot to learn when it comes to the desert. It wasn’t just the thorny plants that were unfriendly. Coyotes climbed over fences just to grab someone’s dog or cat to eat for dinner. Then there are the javelinas. They look a lot like pigs but are really peccaries. When I looked them up, I found out they aren’t supposed to be aggressive unless they have babies with them; but they must all need glasses. If you startle them by barging into their herd, I figure they might take a chunk out of you. There are also cat-burglar bobcats, scary mountain lions, sleek deer, and slithery rattlesnakes.
It was heaven!

Anticipation of a critter meeting had me hanging out in the backyard a lot last spring. Two things had put my critter radar on high alert. The first wasn’t new or all that exciting. In bed at night, we were hearing the pitter-patter of little feet above our heads. We’d had packrats in our attic before. These crafty critters can sneak into the attic through the tiniest crack. I didn’t know about the social habits of packrats before moving to Cave Creek, but I found out they are party animals. Once they set up housekeeping, wild celebrations go on all night. All that dancing and chewing makes it hard to sleep down below, and Mom has to call Lee from Critter Control.

Lee’s last visit followed the unpleasant discovery of a rat tail dangling down into the bathroom through the vent in the ceiling. I’m the one who spotted it. The tail pointed directly at the only seat in the room. I love all kinds of animals, but watching that tail wave gently back and forth in the breeze from the AC made me shudder. I showed Mom, and she called Lee.
As he was leaving the rat man reported, “That’s it. Everything’s sealed up. You won’t have any more problems with packrats in your attic.”

Lee says that every time he visits, but the notices he posts informing the packrats that our attic is off-limits must blow away before they’re seen. Either that or the local packrats need to take a course for slow readers. They keep finding new ways to enter attic heaven, and I figured they were back again!

The other situation wasn’t so easily solved. It affected one of our dogs, Leezer Beezer, our Jack Russell. JRs aren’t very big, but Beezy doesn’t know that, and she takes her guard duties seriously! She had located a threat in the yard that was grave enough to keep her barking for days. Mom was really getting mad at her, but I knew she was trying to tell us something. It wasn’t her fault her humans were too dumb to understand.

It was her non-stop barking that brought me out to the backyard one more time. This time instead of peering closely at various areas, I half closed my eyes and let them drift, looking for a movement or something I’d missed the past twenty times I’d looked. Suddenly my eye caught a flash to my right. My eyes whirled, my heart gave a thump, and a big grin broke out on my face. A tiny cat face was peering at me over the parapet surrounding the flat part of the roof. There was a baby kitten on our roof! Now that I knew where to look, I spotted another tiny cat face a few feet further down. My brain was grinding along in low gear, but it still took only a minute to realize that those darling little faces didn’t belong to stray kittens. A light bulb went on in my head, and everything made sense. The pitter-patter over our heads at night wasn’t packrats dancing a jig in our attic. Nope! It was bobcats pacing on our roof! The grin slipped off my face as quickly as it had appeared as a new thought popped into my buzzing head. What about the dogs?

Mom spent the next few days talking to various people and offices trying to get a handle on how dangerous this was for Beezy and Bitsy, our little couch potato, and what options we had as landlords for a family of bobcats. The call to Game and Fish was wasted effort.
“Ma’am, we don’t go to people’s houses to check on wildlife.”
“Well, can you give me an idea how dangerous the situation is for our dogs in the yard below?” Mom asked.
“No ma’am, I wouldn’t have any information about that at all,” the bored voice on the other end of the phone stated.
Next, she tried the wildlife groups in the area.
“I’m sorry, we don’t do bobcat removals, ma’am. I strongly suggest you just let momma and the babies stay there for the time being. She’ll move along before too long.”
“How long do you think she’ll stay on our roof?”
Mom was pacing the kitchen by this time, the phone pressed hard against her ear.
“Oh, she’ll move them someplace new in a month or two.”

A month or two! What would happen in the meantime? Apparently, the wildlife groups considered the loss of one little dog or even two a small price to pay for leaving our wild visitors undisturbed. But these were my pups we were talking about! Beezy and Bitsy were my buddies. They snuggled with me at night. I wasn’t going to let a bobcat family eat them for dinner.

That night I had a nightmare about a curious baby bobcat climbing down the tree that hung over the edge of the roof. In my dream Leezer Beezer spotted the intruder and rushed to defend her yard, ignoring my repeated command to “Come here, Beezy!” The sight of Mrs. Bob Catt streaking off the roof on a rescue mission sent me bolting upright in bed, panting with fear and feeling around the bed for my dogs.
I didn’t leave Mom alone the next day until she agreed to call Lee.
“Lee, we have a problem. There’s a family of bobcats living on our roof. Can you help me?”
“Sure! I can trap the whole family and move them for you,” he said with assurance.
“Wow, that’s great!”
I could only hear one side of the conversation, but I heard enough for the knot in my stomach to relax and a smile to tug the corners of my mouth up. Then my mom’s eyes got all big.
“I’ll get back to you on that, Lee,” she said.
“He wants $1,000 a piece!” Mom fumed. “Three thousand dollars in charges to get our roof back. I’m sorry, sweetie. That’s too much money.”
She continued. “OK, we have to face facts. We’re on our own unless we win the lottery, and I’ve been sending our entries in for years without any results.”
“But what about the pups, Mom?”
“I know, I know. We’ll just have to come up with an answer on our own. Get us a pencil and paper and let’s sit down and think. The wildlife people gave us a few ideas, you know.”
I got a pencil and paper, and we started talking about the possibilities.
“Remember they suggested squirting water might send them packing,” I said.

“Yes, but spraying the roof with enough water to bother bobcats would be a challenge. We’d probably have to stand on a ladder with the hose. What if Mrs. Bob Catt decided she didn’t want a bath? I don’t think I’d be very good at making a quick exit from a ladder. Let’s shelve the water plan for now.”
“OK, what about bright lights like the other lady suggested?”

“That has the same drawbacks, honey. The light has to be placed up high so it will shine on them. I hate ladders to begin with, but the idea of my head creeping up over the edge of the parapet to come face to face with an angry momma bobcat freaks me out. I’d probably panic and throw myself backward, pulling the ladder over on top of me.”
I loved my dogs, but I wasn’t ready to give up my mother to protect them. Then I remembered the other suggestion that had been made, and I smiled.
We need to set up a noise attack, Mom!”
Slowly Mom smiled back at me.
“You are right, Smart Girl! That’s something we can handle!”

A few minutes later we were on the way to the drugstore. We purchased a fifteen-dollar radio and were ready to do battle. With Beezy dancing around our feet in excitement, we carefully placed our “noise saber” on the far side of the house, beside the tree from my nightmare, and ran an extension cord out the window. We searched out the most unpleasant hard rock station we could find and cranked that sucker all the way up.
In a couple of days we were no longer hearing feet padding about on our roof after we climbed into bed at night, and Beezy was no longer going bonkers in the backyard.
“Don’t get too excited!” Mom cautioned me. “After all, our guests might be enjoying a quick trip to Disneyland or visiting relatives so Mom can show off her new babies.”
So, we left the radio on, but out of sympathy for the other wildlife in the area, we turned the volume down.

Our guests never returned, but there’s still a bobcat that claims the territory outside the walls around our house. Twice recently I’ve rounded the corner of the barn on my way to feed my horse only to stop short when I spotted him three feet away, resting against the wall atop two old logs that have lain in this same place forever. My breath always catches in my throat when I see him, but there’s never any panic in his reaction. He rises slowly, stretching insolently before stalking off in that regal cat way that makes it clear he’s none too pleased his nap was interrupted. Meanwhile I stand rock still, my heart pounding, until he’s well out of sight. I don’t know if this bobcat is one of our babies all grown up, baby’s mother, or some stranger I’ve never met before, but he’s beautiful. He’s also welcome — as long as he stays out of the yard.

articles.title: The Midnight Rider
articles.author: Virginia Stevens
articles.description: A brave and selfless man races against time to cast a tie-breaking vote for independence.
articles.intro: Great Britain is an island nation in Europe. The Thirteen Colonies that later became the United States were once part of Great Britain. In this story, a brave and selfless man races against time to cast a tie-breaking vote for American independence.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Caesar Rodney’s actions.
articles.permissions: "The Midnight Rider" by Virginia Stevens, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. blocks.text: The King’s Highway was dark and dangerous on the night of July 1, 1776 as a lone rider on horseback, wearing his hat low and a scarf to cover part of his face, galloped through the darkened countryside. Guided only by flashes of lightning, the rider and horse raced across slippery bridges, through rain-filled streams, and sleeping towns.

The rider feared that the sound of the horse’s hoofs on the cobblestone streets would awaken someone, but loud claps of thunder covered the noise, and not even a watchdog noticed them. Though the rain came down in torrents, the rider and his mount barreled through it.

The man leaned forward in his saddle and whispered to the horse. Feeling the urgency in his rider’s voice, the horse stretched its legs, running harder and faster. It seemed to fly toward its destination, as if aware of the dangerous nature of its master’s mission. Caesar Rodney was a well-known political figure in Delaware, and he did not want to be recognized. If the wrong person even suspected how important this ride was to the Colonies, Rodney’s mission and his life might be in danger.

At daylight the rider knew he had only about twenty miles to go, so he changed horses and continued on without rest, wet and covered with mud. All night and most of a day, he urged the horse on to complete this important mission. Neither the July heat nor the rain from a summer thunderstorm would keep Caesar Rodney from reaching Philadelphia.

The journey from Dover, Delaware, to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, was normally a two-day trip — thirty hours on horseback. A person usually stopped to eat and rest at one of the inns along the way. On this trip, however, there was no time to rest, no time to eat. Spurring his horse on, Rodney ignored the tempting breakfast smells that drifted on the morning air. He was determined to reach Philadelphia by late afternoon on July 2.

In 1776, American Colonies were facing a decision that would affect them forever. Benjamin Franklin, John Hancock, Thomas Jefferson, and others of the Continental Congress had gathered at the Pennsylvania State House (known today as Independence Hall) to determine the future of America by voting on Richard Henry Lee’s proposal that the Colonies free themselves from Great Britain.

For three weeks the members of the Continental Congress debated the issue: Should or shouldn’t they do it? What would happen if they did? What would happen if they didn’t? Congress agreed that the vote must be unanimous — either all for or all against independence from England. Finally, on July 1, 1776, the vote was down to one colony — Delaware. Delaware had three representatives, but only two were present. Thomas McKean had voted yes for independence. George Read had voted no. A tiebreaker was needed. McKean knew how Caesar Rodney felt about independence, so he quickly sent a message to Rodney, the absent representative. The final vote was scheduled for July 2, and the future of the United States of America rested with Caesar Rodney.

As the Continental Congress sat in Philadelphia, debating the fate of the Colonies, a British fleet was approaching the New York harbor, and the Americans were positioning themselves in preparation for war.

A tired Rodney had just returned to his home from military duty in southern Delaware when the messenger arrived at his house, carrying the letter from Thomas McKean. Rodney broke the wax seal, read the letter, and immediately saddled his horse for the eighty-mile trip.

What was Caesar Rodney thinking of while he covered the eighty miles to Philadelphia? What would happen if the Colonies declared independence from Great Britain? He was certain it would mean war. It could also mean imprisonment hanging — for treason.

Caesar had a cancerous growth on his nose and the side of his face, which he tried to hide with a scarf. If the Colonies split from Great Britain, he knew that he would not be able to go to England for surgery and that the cancer would eventually take his life. Freedom was so important to Rodney that he risked his life to cast his vote for it.

Caesar was now in Philadelphia, and it was late — too late to stop and take a bath or change clothes. He rode up to the Pennsylvania State House and rushed in. All eyes were on him as he stepped into the room, covered in mud from his ride and still wearing his spurs. It was nearly 4:00 p.m. on July 2, 1776.
“As I believe the voice of my constituents and of all sensible and honest men is in favor of independence, my own judgment concurs with them. I vote for independence,” he said.

The Colonies voted to break with Great Britain and establish a United States of America. Thomas Jefferson was asked to complete the draft of the Declaration of Independence, which John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, Robert R. Livingston, and Roger Sherman helped him write.

Caesar Rodney served the United States for the rest of his life. He was part of the Continental Congress until he was elected President of the State of Delaware in 1778. At the same time, he was a major general in the Delaware Militia and supported General George Washington during the Revolutionary War. Even though the cancer was slowly killing him, Rodney continued with his military and political duties. The cancer finally did take his life in June of 1784, while he was still serving as speaker to the upper house of the Delaware Assembly. The State of Delaware honored Caesar Rodney in January of 1999 by coining his image on the reverse side of the state quarter of Delaware.

articles.title: Breaking Through
articles.author: Gina DeAngelis and Audrey DeAngelis
articles.description: Growing up deaf-blind, Helen Keller overcame adversity with the help of her teacher, Anne Sullivan.
articles.intro: Helen Keller (1880-1968) was a famous author, speaker, and educator who was deaf-blind. People who are deaf-blind are unable to hear or to see. In this biography, the authors discuss Helen and her teacher, Anne Sullivan.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the authors’ points of view about Helen Keller.
articles.permissions: "Breaking Through" by Gina DeAngelis and Audrey DeAngelis, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The Class of 1904 that graduated from Radcliffe College in Cambridge, Massachusetts, was unusual. It was made up of women who had pursued an advanced degree from one of the nation’s top schools when such opportunities were usually reserved for men. It also included one specific student who stood out. She had not only already written a bestselling autobiography but had also earned her degree, “cum laude” (with honor), despite being both deaf and blind. The young woman’s name was Helen Keller.

Helen was born on June 27, 1880, in Tuscumbia, Alabama. She was a healthy baby and a quick learner. She began to walk on her first birthday, and she could say “tea!” and ask for water. When she was 19 months old, however, she caught a bad fever. When she recovered, she could no longer see or hear.

Most young children need to be taught how to understand and express what they think and feel. For Helen, the loss of her hearing and her sight made that almost impossible. Her parents felt sorry for her. They let her run around half wild, and she threw tantrums when she did not get her way. When she was six, however, her mother had another baby. Helen hated sharing her mother. One day, she tipped over the cradle with her baby sister in it. Mrs. Keller caught the baby before she could get hurt, but the Kellers realized they and Helen — needed — help.

The Kellers already had brought Helen to be examined by doctors, who told them that no operation could reverse Helen’s deafness and blindness. But in 1886, they were referred to Dr. Alexander Graham Bell, who suggested that they contact the Perkins Institution, known today as the Perkins School for the Blind, in Boston, Massachusetts. Mr. Keller wrote to Michael Anagnos, the school’s director, and asked him to recommend a teacher. Anagnos suggested Anne Sullivan. Sullivan had just graduated from Perkins at the top of her class. She knew the manual alphabet — a way of using fingers to spell words into a person’s hand.

Sullivan arrived at the Kellers’ home in Alabama on March 3, 1887. At first, Helen didn’t like the strange new person who inserted herself into the family’s household. Sullivan, for her part, initially clashed with the Kellers. She saw that Helen was bright but that she had been spoiled. During family meals, Helen was allowed to wander around the table and to take food from anyone’s plate with her hands. When Sullivan witnessed that behavior, she immediately tried to change it. After the family left the dining room, she spent the rest of the day teaching Helen how to sit at the table, use her napkin and utensils, and eat from her own plate.

Sullivan realized that she needed to earn Helen’s trust without the Kellers’ interference. For two weeks, Sullivan and Helen lived in a small cottage next door to the family house. Sullivan used the manual alphabet to teach Helen, but although Helen quickly learned and repeated the letters pressed into her hand, she didn’t understand that the letters formed words that named things.

About a month after arriving in Tuscumbia, Sullivan brought Helen to the water pump, a device that pulled water from a well in the ground. Helen had shown great confusion in distinguishing among the words “mug,” “milk,” and “drink.” Sullivan gave Helen a mug to hold under the spout. When the water flowed over Helen’s hand, Sullivan spelled “W-A-T-E-R” into Helen’s other palm. Helen had once known the word “water.” At that moment, Sullivan later wrote, “A new light came into [Helen’s] face.” She suddenly understood what Sullivan had been trying to do. Helen excitedly asked the name of everything around her, including the name for Sullivan. Sullivan spelled “T-E-A-C-H-E-R” into Helen’s hand. By bedtime, Helen had learned more than 30 words.

That day changed everything. Helen stopped fighting her teacher and parents. Within a few months, she learned hundreds of words. Sullivan encouraged her to touch and smell everything everywhere they went. When the circus came to town, Helen touched an elephant’s trunk, shook hands with a dancing bear, felt a giraffe’s neck and ears, and even played with lion cubs!

Sullivan also taught Helen to read special books with raised letters. When they ran out of books, Sullivan wrote to Anagnos to send more. During that first summer with Sullivan, Helen also learned to write and to read braille . Helen read everything she could and wrote letters to friends.

The next year, when Helen was eight, Sullivan took her to Massachusetts. Helen visited a beach for the first time, where she was surprised to taste salt in the seawater. That fall, Helen and Sullivan lived at the Perkins Institution, the school where Sullivan had worked before meeting the Kellers. Helen was able to talk and to play with other blind children who used the manual alphabet. She learned arithmetic, zoology, geography, botany, and history. She even learned to play the piano. And Sullivan, whom Helen called “Teacher,” was always by her side.

Since Helen was both deaf and blind, she relied on her sense of touch. In addition to using the manual alphabet, she could read a person’s lips by putting her hand on his face while he spoke. With one finger on his nose, one finger on his lips, and one finger on his throat, Helen could feel vibrations and movements. She learned to understand and to mimic them. She decided she wanted to learn to speak.

In 1890, Helen took some lessons with Sarah Fuller at the Horace Mann School for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing in Massachusetts. In 1894, Helen and Sullivan went to the Wright-Humason School for the Deaf in New York City. Learning to speak was slow, and Helen never felt good at it, but she kept practicing.

Helen wanted to live independently one day. She set a goal of going to college. When people tried to warn Helen about her aspirations, she responded with a prediction that someday women “will all go to college, and some day they will vote. I am going to work for women’s rights, and I am going to earn my own living, too.”

In the fall of 1896, 16-year-old Helen entered the Cambridge School for Young Ladies, where she and Sullivan spent a year preparing her for college. She made friends with young women who could hear and see. Her younger sister, Mildred, came, too. Helen and Sullivan worked hard. Sullivan had to spell everything into Helen’s hands, which included the teachers’ lessons and any textbooks that were not available in braille. At one point, the school principal decided that Sullivan was pushing Helen too hard. He tried to make Sullivan leave. Instead Mrs. Keller took Helen out of school. She arranged for private tutors, and Sullivan remained with Helen.

In 1899, Helen passed Radcliffe College’s entrance exam. The following year, she became the first student who was deaf and blind to enroll at a U.S. college. Once again, Sullivan was by Helen’s side every step of the way. She translated to Helen what the professors said in class. Afterward, Helen would rush back to her room to write down everything she could remember. Most textbooks were not available in braille, so Sullivan read them to her — spelling by hand four or five hours a day. Sullivan’s vision, which was never very strong, started to fail. But Sullivan knew Helen was counting on her, and she would not quit.

While Helen was in college, the editor of Ladies’ Home Journal , a popular women’s magazine, asked her to write about herself. The magazine offered $3,000 for six articles. It was a lot of work, but it was also a lot of money (equal to about $80,000 today!). Helen accepted, and she and Sullivan hired a secretary, John Albert Macy, to help write the articles. The arrangement gave Sullivan time to rest.

In 1903, the articles were republished as a book, The Story of My Life . It was a bestseller that remains in print in more than 50 languages. Everyone wanted to know more about the determined, inspiring young lady. After Helen graduated in 1904, Ladies’ Home Journal asked her to write about her plans. “I shall devote my life to those who suffer from loss of sight,” she wrote. She had already shown that people who were blind could do anything anyone else could do.

options.label: Helen Keller was born with a disability that made her unable to see or hear.
Helen Keller was smart because she taught herself to read and write in braille.
Helen Keller changed what people believed about what people with disabilities could achieve.
Helen Keller attended the Cambridge School for Young Ladies where she learned how to read and speak.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?

options.label: Keller's mom was tired because caring for two children was a lot of work.
Keller often threw tantrums and almost hurt her little sister.
Keller learned how to walk by her first birthday.
Keller's parents felt really bad for their child.

questions.prompt: The authors support the idea that Helen needed help as a child by explaining that…

options.label: "For two weeks, Sullivan and Helen lived in a small cottage next door to the family house." (Paragraph 6)
"Helen and Sullivan lived at the Perkins Institution, the school where Sullivan had worked before meeting the Kellers." (Paragraph 10)
"And Sullivan, whom Helen called 'Teacher,' was always by her side." (Paragraph 10)
"Helen accepted, and she and Sullivan hired a secretary, John Albert Macy, to help write the articles." (Paragraph 16)

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes how Helen felt about Sullivan?

options.label: to realize that something isn't heavy
to feel the sun beating down on one's face

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, the authors discuss how Sullivan fought with the Keller family when she first arrived. Sullivan felt like changes needed to be made in the Keller household to allow for Helen's success, but Helen resisted. Have you ever had a conflict with someone who was trying to help you? Or, have you ever had a conflict with someone you were trying to help? How did you handle the conflict?

In the text, the authors explain that Sullivan removed Helen from school when the principal said Sullivan was being too hard on Helen. Throughout Helen's life, Sullivan served as Helen's teacher and protector. Sullivan set high goals for Helen. Do you have someone in your life who pushes you to be your best self? Who is that person, and how do they help you reach your goals? If you cannot think of a person, talk about an example from the media or literature.

In paragraph 13, the authors mention that people told Helen she may not achieve her goals of living independently and going to college. Helen responded that "someday women 'will all go to college, and some day they will vote. I am going to work for women's rights, and I am going to earn my own living, too.'" What does this quote say about Helen? How would you describe her? What would you say to someone who told you that you could not achieve your goals?

articles.title: Digging Into Doo-Doo
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler explains what paleontologists can learn by studying fossilized animal poop.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes what scientists can learn from fossilized, or left behind traces of, animal poop.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why people are interested in fossilized poop.
articles.permissions: "Digging Into Doo-Doo" by T.J.Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Sometimes the best bundle of information about an animal’s life is found in what it left behind — literally from its “behind” (and, generally, after a good meal).

Yes, we’re talking about poop — or, to be more polite, “stool,” “dung,” “feces,” or “excrement.” Call it what you will, but for many paleontologists, the scientists who study life long ago, finding poop from a dinosaur, saber-toothed cat, or even tiny sea snail is like hitting a jackpot. They eagerly slice and dice a pile of it to learn about the animal and its life.

This probably would be a good time to note that the poop is fossilized, hard as a rock, and, thankfully, no longer stinky or gooey. In fact, fossilized feces can be so beautiful — full of little bits of plants, shells or winding insect burrows — that jewelry makers polish slices or beads of it to create gorgeous necklaces, earrings, or bracelets.

Considering all the poop that animals dump during their lives, fossilized feces is quite rare. Usually animal’s excrement — the remains of food that wasn’t digested — just decomposed, breaking down and fertilizing the ground underneath. Sometimes smaller animals would feast on the poop. (Yeah, gross, but it’s actually nutritious.) And on rare occasion, the animal’s feces would quickly get covered by mineral-rich sediment or water and over a really long time harden into a fossil, creating what scientists call a “coprolite” and other people call a “dung stone.” (Both terms are right. The fossil gets its name from the Greek words kopros , which means “dung,” and lithos , which means “stone.”)

Coprolites are a type of trace fossil, one that records what the animal did, instead of the kind of fossil, like a dinosaur skeleton, that forms directly from the animal’s remains. That kind of fossil is called a body fossil.

Some paleontologists focus their careers on examining coprolites. Unlike a fossil skeleton, which shows what an animal looked like, coprolites give a wealth of other information about an animal’s diet, environment, and even behavior, paleontologist Karen Chin told the public radio program Science Friday .

Coprolites may contain fossilized bits of plants and animals, such as fish scales, shells, bones, or even muscle tissue from the animal’s prey. They reveal whether the animal that produced the poop was a herbivore (plant-eater), carnivore (meat-eater), or omnivore (eater of both plants and animals).

Or even a cannibal. Not long ago, for example, scientists discovered that a prehistoric shark called Orthacanthus ate its own young. They found a distinctive spiral-shaped Orthacanthus coprolite that contained an equally distinctive tiny Orthacanthus tooth. News of their gruesome discovery was a big deal and published in the scientific journal Paleontology and reported by the BBC .

Coprolites even revealed why the famous Tyrannosaurus rex may have been constantly on the prowl for its next meal. It didn’t absorb all the available nutrition from its meals, leaving a lot of undigested flesh and bone in its poop. That meant that the massive dinosaur needed to eat a huge amount of food to get enough energy to keep going, according to Smithsonian Magazine . Poor thing was probably hangry all the time.

It isn’t always easy to identify which animal produced the poop — after all, the food traveled down the animal’s digestive tract and then the coprolite was battered by maybe millions of years of erosion and weathering, paleontologist Karen Chin said. In addition, fossils of the animal that went Number 2 are not usually found in the same location as the pile of poop.

But there are exceptions. Some coprolites — such as those from some prehistoric sharks and other fishes — have really distinctive shapes that give away what kind of animal produced the feces, according to the Natural History Museum in London. Some sharks’ intestines had spiral valves that created similarly shaped grooves in shark feces. Other prehistoric sharks and primitive fish produced scroll-shaped feces that looked like a rolled sheet of paper, according to a study published in Science Reports .

In addition, especially in more recently formed coprolites, some scientists have been able to find enough genetic material to identify the animal that produced the feces as well as the specific kinds of animals and plants it ate, according to a study reported by Science Direct .

Even when scientists can’t identify the animal responsible for the pile of poop, the coprolites are still extremely valuable. Besides revealing the animal’s diet and feeding behavior, the fossils also provide information about the animal’s environment: whether the animal lived in water or on land, what other organisms lived around it, and even — based on how the poop was preserved — what the surroundings were like, paleontologist Karen Chin said. By studying many coprolites, scientists can figure out what ate what and reconstruct prehistoric food webs.

Coprolites come in all shapes and sizes, from teeny insect droppings to a 20-pound T. rex turd. While many coprolites look pretty much like rocks, others look so much like a freshly deposited pile of poop that you’d jump away if you saw it lying on the sidewalk. But maybe that “eww” factor is part of their appeal. Coprolites actually are favorites of fossil collectors. One enthusiast collected more than 1,200 coprolites — so many that he was able to open his own museum, the Poozeum!

options.label: by telling the reader how cool fossilized poop is
by using word play to get the reader's attention
by showing how much fun it is to study fossils
by explaining where coprolites are found

questions.prompt: How does the author introduce the topic in paragraph 1?

options.label: Finding coprolites helps paleontologists understand what an animal looked like and where it lived.
Paleontologists look for clues that help them identify which animal left behind the coprolite.
Beautiful coprolites are put in museums so that more people can admire and study them.
When scientists find a coprolite they have to guess which animal produced it.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of paragraphs 10-12?

options.label: to show the reader how to become a paleontologist
to show what experts have learned from coprolites
to tell how the study of coprolites has changed
to tell the story of a famous paleontologist

options.label: "They found a distinctive spiral-shaped Orthacanthus coprolite that contained an equally distinctive tiny Orthacanthus tooth." (Paragraph 8)

"It isn't always easy to identify which animal produced the poop — after all, the food traveled down the animal's digestive tract and then the coprolite was battered by maybe millions of years of erosion and weathering, paleontologist Karen Chin said." (Paragraph 10)
"Other prehistoric sharks and primitive fish produced scroll-shaped feces that looked like a rolled sheet of paper, according to a study published in Science Reports." (Paragraph 11)

"Even when scientists can't identify the animal responsible for the pile of poop, the coprolites are still extremely valuable." (Paragraph 13) E. "Besides revealing the animal's diet and feeding behavior, the fossils also provide information about the animal's environment" (Paragraph 13) F. "But maybe that 'eww' factor is part of their appeal." (Paragraph 14)
