discussionQuestions.prompt: The author talks about how jewelers and collectors use coprolites. Would you like to wear jewelry made out of fossilized animal poop or visit the Poozeum mentioned in the text? Why or why not?

Paleontologists are scientists that study life from long ago by using clues like fossilized animal poop. What skills and character traits do you think a good paleontologist needs? Would you like to be a paleontologist? Why or why not?

Imagine that a paleontologist 100 million years from now is looking at a layer of earth from the time we live in and finds some fossilized human coprolites. What might they learn from these fossils? What do you think they would think about us based on what they find?

articles.title: The Dinosaurs' Demise
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler explains how an asteroid impact could have caused the dinosaurs' extinction.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, Resler describes how an asteroid — a small, rocky object that circles the sun — might have led to the dinosaurs' demise, or death, 66 million years ago.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what scientists think happened after the asteroid hit Earth.
articles.permissions: "The Dinosaurs' Demise" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Talk about a terrible day.

It starts out like any warm, spring day 66 million years ago. Dinosaurs go about their dino business: a T. rex sniffs the air in search of its next meal, herds of beak-billed Maiasaura munch on tall ferns, a Dakotaraptor sinks its sickle-shaped claw into its unlucky prey. The air buzzes with insects, sharks prowl the oceans, and little mammals scurry through the dense plants.

Suddenly, a bright red fireball streaks through the sky and … BOOM! An asteroid the size of a mountain slams into Earth along the coast of what is now Mexico’s Yucatán Peninsula. The asteroid destroys the rocky surface where it hits, shooting ash, soot, dust and gasses high into the sky. It leaves a scar in the Earth: a crater 18 miles deep and more than 100 miles across. Intense heat from the crash burns everything nearby and even sets grass, trees and shrubs on fire.

But that’s just the beginning of the destruction. The asteroid impact affects the Earth like a big rock thrown into a still pond, creating circular ripples that flow outward. Only in this case, the “ripples” are violent blasts that rocket through the air, land and seas.

Massive waves (tsunamis) as tall as skyscrapers wash over parts of the American continents. A blast of wind as fast as a passenger jet rips through the region. Earthquakes more powerful than anything humans have ever experienced shake the land. As earthquake expert Rick Aster explained in National Geographic magazine, “[An] event of this size would be the equivalent of all the world’s earthquakes for the past 160 years going off simultaneously.”
Any animal close enough to see the asteroid impact dies instantly.
A horrible, new world
The bad news doesn’t end there. Like a row of dominoes tumbling one after another, the asteroid impact starts a chain of events that ends up wiping out more than three-fourths of Earth’s animals.

The ash, soot, dust and gas thrust into the air by the asteroid’s impact fill the sky. Rain provides no relief. Instead, the drops mix with harmful particles and the gasses in the air and come down as muddy “acid rain,” killing many marine animals swimming in the shallow waters of oceans, lakes and rivers.

For the first hours after the asteroid impact, the sky is almost totally dark. But even after it lightens up, it looks like twilight or a very cloudy day — and it stays that way for months, maybe even years, according to planet scientist Gareth Collins.

The darkened sky changes Earth’s climate and environment in a big way. Without the warmth of the sun, temperatures plunge for several years, causing what scientists call an “impact winter.” Even in the tropics — the warmest regions of Earth — the average temperature drops from 81 degrees Fahrenheit to a chilly 41 degrees.

The cold temperatures and reduced sunlight make it difficult for plants to grow. Plant-eating animals can’t find enough food to survive. Many die within weeks, leaving meat-eaters with little to eat. They suffer the same fate. The food chain collapses, taking down those non-avian dinosaurs and many other animals.

For the animal species that survive the asteroid impact, fires, tsunamis, acid rain, years of darkness, and impact winter, a new problem arises. After the impact winter, Earth begins warming rapidly and stays hot for the next 100,000 years.

After ruling Earth for 174 million years — much, much longer than the 6 million years humans have been around — the only dinosaurs to survive the mass extinction 66 million years ago are the avian dinosaurs, which evolved into today’s birds. All other dinosaurs go extinct. They are not the only casualties of the mass extinction. The fearsome serpent-like sea reptiles (plesiosaurs, mosasaurs and ichthyosaurs) also die out, as do the pterosaurs, the mighty winged reptiles that ruled the skies during the dinosaur era.

Some animals do survive the mass extinction. Besides the birds (avian dinosaurs), other survivors include some mammals, amphibians, fish, and reptiles, such as turtles, lizards, snakes, and crocodilians. But the overall loss of animal diversity is extreme, with more than three-quarters of animals wiped out.
Detective work

How did we learn all of this? Well, humans did not live during the time of dinosaurs — no matter what movies and TV shows might have you think — so we didn’t witness their demise (probably a good thing, too). And so, for nearly two centuries, it was a mystery why the dinosaurs went extinct.

Paleontologists — the scientists who study life long ago — brainstormed lots of possible explanations. Did dinosaurs die of a terrible disease? Did they run out of things to eat? Did little mammals eat too many dinosaur eggs? Did dino bodies grow too large to be controlled by their little brains? But the scientists could not find any evidence to support these ideas. In fact, the evidence showed they were wrong.

Then, in the 1970s, geologist Walter Alvarez discovered a strange layer of clay in the Earth. It was much different from the layers above and below it, and it dated back to when the dinosaurs became extinct. He and his father, the noted physicist Luis Alvarez, discovered that the clay contained unusual things: some rare minerals that only form only under extreme heat and pressure, and an element called iridium that is common in space — but rare on Earth. In 1980, the Alvarezes and their scientific partners Frank Asaro and Helen Michel came up with the asteroid-impact explanation (or, in scientific terms, “theory”) for the dinosaurs’ demise.

The theory was so different from earlier ideas that, at first, a lot of other scientists thought it was silly. But over the years, more and more evidence has supported the asteroid-impact theory. In the early 1990s, scientists discovered the Chicxulub crater off Mexico’s coast, the site where the asteroid slammed into Earth. And in the past decade, paleontologists working in North Dakota found fossils from that awful time: a fish that sucked in debris from the asteroid blast, a turtle speared by a stick during the floods that followed the impact, and possibly even a piece of the asteroid itself preserved in a bit of amber (fossilized tree resin).

Today, most scientists accept the asteroid-impact theory as the cause of the mass extinction 66 million years ago. But not all scientists. Some think climate change doomed the dinos. Huge volcanic eruptions right before the dinosaurs’ extinction could have filled the air with gasses and changed the climate and habitats in such a way that the dinosaurs and many other animals could not survive. Some scientists blame both the volcanoes and the asteroid.

Paleontologists are still searching for the answers to many questions: Was the asteroid impact the only cause of the dinosaurs’ demise or just one cause? Were the dinosaurs already dying out before the asteroid impact? If the asteroid hadn’t hit Earth, would the non-avian dinosaurs have survived?
Maybe there’s many more answers to come.

options.label: Most scientists believe the asteroid impact destroyed many plants and animals, but the dinosaurs were not affected by it.
The asteroid impact theory explains that an asteroid killed many plants and animals, and it most likely caused the dinosaurs' extinction.
Most scientists believe the asteroid impact did not affect the Earth at all, and the dinosaurs died off from other causes like volcanoes and tsunamis.
Most scientists believe the asteroid impact caused some damage to the Earth, plants, and animals, and it helped the dinosaurs to live long, healthy lives.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the article?

"In the early 1990s, scientists discovered the Chicxulub crater off Mexico's coast, the site where the asteroid slammed into Earth." (Paragraph 18)
"Huge volcanic eruptions right before the dinosaurs' extinction could have filled the air with gasses and changed the climate and habitats…" (Paragraph 19)

questions.prompt: Which sentence from the article best shows how scientists found evidence of the asteroid impact theory?

options.label: The asteroid impact affected more of the Earth than just the location where the asteroid hit.

options.label: to become less
to cause harm
to stay strong

questions.prompt: What does the word "reduced" mean as it is used in paragraph 11?

options.label: It shows that the asteroid impact was the first theory about the dinosaurs' extinction.
It shows that the author disagrees with all theories about the dinosaurs' extinction.
It shows that some scientists have other ideas about the dinosaurs' extinction.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, the author writes, "Like a row of dominoes tumbling one after another, the asteroid impact starts a chain of events that ends up wiping out more than three- fourths of Earth's animals" (Paragraph 7). Think about an event or experience from your own life or from history, a story, or media where one bad thing caused another bad thing to happen. What happened first? How did that cause other bad things to happen? Think about an event or experience where one good thing caused another good thing to happen. What happened first? How did that cause other good things to happen?

This article describes how scientists found evidence to support the asteroid impact theory. Why is it important for scientists to have evidence to support their ideas? Imagine that you are trying to convince your family or friends about something important. Why is it helpful to use evidence or examples when you are trying to convince people of something?

In the text, the author describes how some scientists have other theories about the dinosaurs' extinction, besides the asteroid impact theory. Do you think it is good or bad that some scientists disagree about the dinosaurs' extinction? Why or why not? What theory do you think is the most likely cause of the dinosaurs' extinction? Why?

articles.title: The Test
articles.author: Shelby Ostergaard
articles.description: A young boy learns an important lesson about fairness after taking a test.
articles.intro: A young boy learns a lesson about fairness after taking a test in school.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, pay attention to the challenges that the characters experience.
articles.permissions: "The Test" by Shelby Ostergaard. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Javon was the shortest kid in the class. It had been that way since the third grade. He was smaller than every boy and all of the girls, too. Sometimes his friends couldn’t figure out how to hold their phones to fit him and everyone else in a selfie. He’d had to ask to get his gym locker changed so that he didn’t have to strain so much to reach it. For most kids, being short would be a problem, but Javon just let being short glide right off of him. Either he would grow or he wouldn’t — there was nothing he could do about it, so why let it bother him?
That was Javon’s attitude about most things. He didn’t like to get all worked up about stuff, especially stuff he had no control over.

The one exception was school work. When he was a little kid, he’d been real chill about his school work. But right before the first day of middle school, his dad had sat him down at the dining room table and made him take his hat off. In their house, that meant it was time for a serious talk.

They talked about college. Specifically, his dad told him that college was expensive. College cost more money than a year’s rent on their apartment did. College cost more money than either his dad or his mom made in a year. College cost more money than what they would have spent if they had bought Javon every pair of sneakers AND every video game he’d ever wanted.
Javon’s eyes had gone big when he heard the last comparison. He remembered thinking that he didn’t understand why anyone would want to go to college if it cost that much.

His dad explained; not everyone paid that much. Some people, his dad told him, who worked really hard and got good grades paid less or nothing at all to go to college. It was called a scholarship. Javon nodded, promising his dad that he would work hard and get a scholarship.
Just then, his mom walked in.
“Stop it, you’re scaring him,” she said, “Javon, relax. Just do your best. You don’t want to put too much pressure on yourself.”
His dad nodded. “That’s true. But remember, people are already going to put pressure on him. He needs to make sure he has options.”

“And he will,” his mom said. “As long as he does his best, he’ll have options. There are scholarships, and there’s also community college, financial aid. But he’ll never have the option of being a kid again.”

Even though Javon knew what his mom was trying to say, he still decided that he wanted to buckle down. If he tried his best, if he was chill about everything but school, then he could be a kid but still get into college without having to pay a ton of money. That day, he promised himself that he would work hard and try to get a scholarship.

And Javon had worked hard. By middle of seventh grade, Javon was one of the top kids in the class. He was still the shortest, but now he also got called one of the smartest. It felt good. He did all his work and still didn’t worry himself too much.

So, he hadn’t been too concerned when Mr. T announced they were starting a new unit in math. The class started normally enough. Mr. T was writing on the projector, same as always. Kids were taking notes or doodling, same as always. But then Mr. T threw a letter into his math problem. He paused and looked out at the class, smiling at them like using letters in math was a perfectly normal thing to do.

For Javon, math used to be easy. He figured that since he had ten fingers, he could do most of it. But he could not wrap his mind around using these letters. Mr. T had given them all sorts of rules for what to do when there were letters in your math problem. But Javon couldn’t remember what they all were and he didn’t think he even understood the rules he did remember. Whenever Mr. T tried to explain, it was like Javon was focused so hard on wanting to understand that he didn’t actually hear what Mr. T said. Before he knew it, he was hopelessly lost in math class.

Every night, Javon would get out his worksheet and his book, eager to study. But he’d end up staring at the problems in confusion, not sure where to even begin. Eventually, he’d close the book and tell himself that tomorrow he’d try even harder. He kept doing that right up until ‘tomorrow’ became the day when the class had to take the chapter test.
The test was brutal.

Javon was confused by the very first question. And then, somehow, the test got worse with each problem. He was nervous and kept losing track of what he was doing. His paper was covered with squiggles and cross-outs. When the class period ended, he hadn’t finished all of the problems. But at least the torture was over.

He tried to put the test out of his mind and wait to get his grade back. It didn’t work. The test kept popping back into his head while he was doing other things. Fun things, too. He’d be playing video games with friends, having a great time, then suddenly he’d be worried about what grade he got. He tried to figure it out for himself and whether he still had an A in math, but it was no use. No matter how much he tried not to worry about it, he was nervous the whole week. He could practically feel his scholarship slipping through his fingers.

The day came when the class finally got their papers back. Mr. T had a student pass them around, face down. Javon took a deep breath before he flipped his test over. His knotted stomach dropped to the floor when he saw it.
D. 64%. Written in red ink on the corner of the paper.
He wanted to tell himself it was no big deal. He wanted to shake the test grade off. He knew that he couldn’t change it, so he tried to tell himself that there was no use being worried about it.
But Javon didn’t really believe any of that. It felt like with this one test grade, he had absolutely ruined everything.

In the seat next to him, Tyler groaned slightly, catching Javon’s attention. Tyler was a nice guy, but right now he looked totally miserable. Javon didn’t mean to peek, but he couldn’t help but see the big, red 72% on Tyler’s paper.
“Hey, man,” Javon said, “that’s not so bad.”

“Yeah, it could be worse,” Tyler agreed. “But I studied really hard. I’m trying to get my grades up enough to play again. And with Language Arts... I don’t think this will do it.” Tyler bit his lip, looking worried. “How’d you do?” Tyler asked Javon.

Javon made a face. He didn’t really want to talk about his terrible grade or about how it had ruined his life or what a failure he felt like. But he’d started the conversation, so he handed his paper over to Tyler. Tyler whistled through his teeth.
“Man, what happened? You’re like one of the smartest kids in class!”
Javon glared at him.

“Sorry,” said Tyler, “that’s probably not what you need to hear right now. But seriously, what happened?” Tyler gave the test back and they both started packing up to go. Javon slid his test into a random section of his math book.

“It’s all these letters!” Javon burst out as they walked out the door. “Letters don’t belong in math class. If you don’t know all the numbers, then why would you try to solve the problem? I just don’t get it!”
Javon was surprised at the forcefulness in his own voice, but Tyler nodded enthusiastically.
“Me either, man! I told Mr. T right after I finished that I was sure half the class didn’t get these letters.” Javon nodded in agreement, and then stopped.
“Wait,” Javon said, “how could you have talked to Mr. T right after you finished your test? We had a sub that day.”
“Oh, it was when I went back at lunch. He was only gone in the morning that day.”
“Why’d you get to go back at lunch?” Javon was, again, surprised by the forcefulness in his voice. Tyler didn’t seem to notice.
“I get extra time on tests. Because of my — ” Tyler said.

“You got extra time?” Javon cried out, interrupting Tyler’s sentence. He could feel his test weighing down his arms, like a painful reminder. “No wonder you did so much better than I did! That makes so much more sense!”
“What’s that supposed to mean?” Tyler said, stopping dead in his tracks and glaring at Javon.

“You said it yourself, I’m one of the smartest kids in class,” Javon replied. He let that hang in the air, but by the way Tyler’s eyebrows furrowed together, it was clear he understood what Javon was implying. Tyler took a step toward Javon, still scowling. Javon dropped his books, his face hot. Other students stopped to stare at the two of them.
Suddenly, Mr. T appeared in the hallway.
“Do we have a problem here, scholars?” Mr. T asked.
“No,” said Javon, anger in his voice.
“No problem here,” said Tyler, sounding furious.
“Riiiiight,” said Mr. T. It was clear that he didn’t believe them. “Let’s all three just step into my classroom anyway, okay?”

There wasn’t really a choice. Begrudgingly, Javon grabbed his books and followed Mr. T into the classroom. Realizing that nothing interesting was going to happen, the rest of the students moved on to their next period.
Mr. T closed the door before turning around to look at the pair of them. “So, what seems to be the issue?” he asked. Both boys stood silent and stone faced.
“Alright then…” Mr. T looked from one to the other. “ Tyler, what happened after my class today?”

“How come Tyler gets extra time on tests?” Javon complained. “It’s not fair! I didn’t even get to finish! I would have done so much better on that test if I had been able to come back and just do all of the problems. I bet a lot of students would have! So why didn’t everyone get to come back and finish?”

Mr. T looked surprised at Javon’s outburst. He glanced at Tyler. “Well,” he replied after a moment of silence, “sometimes students need different things. And as an educator it’s my job to give students what they need.”
“But it’s not fair.” Javon said flatly.
“Well — ”
But before Mr. T could finish what he was saying, Tyler interrupted him. “Javon, do you know what dyslexia is?” he asked, sounding annoyed.
“What?” Javon said, “I don’t know, maybe?”

“Alright look — imagine if when you were trying to read, the letters got all mixed up. The b’s turned into d’s and the l’s turned into t’s. But, like, randomly and all over the place and all the time. And you just have to kind of... look closely and check twice and try to guess what letters would make sense there.”
“Javon, what do you think that would be like?” Mr. T asked. Javon shrugged, thinking about it.
“That... sounds like it would be really annoying,” Javon finally said. “It’d make it hard to read anything.”
“Yeah. It’s not great, but that’s what it’s like when I read. I’m dyslexic,” Tyler explained. Javon looked at him. Tyler shrugged a little bit.
“I mean... that sounds tough, man,” Javon said, “But no offense, what does that have to do with our math test? It’s all numbers.”
“Ah,” said Mr. T, “but, do you think it would take you a longer time or a shorter time to read if you were dyslexic?”
“Longer, probably.”

“Right. So think about that math test again... there are numbers, but there are also instructions that tell you what to do and word problems. If it took you, let’s say, twice as long to read the instructions, do you think you’d still have plenty of time to do the math?”
“No...” said Javon, thinking.
“Man, it takes me forever to read word problems. Stuff jumps around and I have to go slow and read it twice.”
“But... I mean... it’s still not fair. Plenty of people didn’t finish. I didn’t get to finish!”

“It’s not about finishing or not finishing the test, Javon,” Mr. T said. “When students have a disability, and dyslexia is just one type of disability, they’re still entitled to get a public education. So, as educators it’s our duty to make sure that all students get what they need to be able to get their education.”
“But... yeah... even with all that, I still think students should all get the same time on tests. Everyone gets the same thing. It’s only equal.”
“Look,” Tyler said, “if I got the same time as you did on every test, I wouldn’t finish a single one. I’d only get halfway through.”

“It’s not about being equal and everyone getting the same thing,” Mr. T said. “It’s about being fair, so students can get what they need.” He paused for a moment. “Can you think of a time when you got something different than everyone else because you needed it?”
Javon thought about it.
“Yeah,” he said finally, “I mean, this isn’t the exact same thing, but I was the only one that Ms. Haygood let change gym lockers.”
“And why was that?” Mr. T asked.
“Because he’s short,” Tyler interjected.

“Tyler,” Mr. T admonished. “You don’t really need to tell people things that you’ve noticed about their bodies or their abilities. I promise you, that in the whole time they’ve been in that body, they’ve noticed too. You don’t have to tell them.”
“Yeah, but it is because I’m short,” Javon said. “I couldn’t reach the locker she gave me originally.”
“What do you think would have happened if she told you that because everyone didn’t get to switch, you didn’t either?” Mr. T asked.
“I mean — I wouldn’t be able to reach my gym locker. And everyone else would be fine because they didn’t need to switch.”
“Right,” said Tyler, “and if I didn’t get extra time on tests, I wouldn’t be able to pass any of them. And everyone else would be fine because they’re not dyslexic.”
Javon nodded. “That makes sense. But I still didn’t finish. I still didn’t pass the test,” said Javon, his voice catching in his throat. He felt that unfamiliar frustration bubbling up again.
“How did you feel about the material in this unit, Javon?” Mr. T asked with a smile.
“It was really confusing. I don’t understand the letters at all,” Javon admitted.
“Why didn’t you come to me and ask for help?”
“I don’t know,” Javon said. “I’ve never had to do that before. I just kept thinking that if I tried harder then eventually I would figure it out.”

“Javon, it’s my job as a teacher to give every student what they need. That’s my job no matter what. But it’s much easier to help when students tell me what they need. You asked for a different locker when you needed it. You should do the same thing if you’re confused in class.” Mr. T leaned against his desk. “I’ve noticed that you put a lot of pressure on yourself to always try your best. But you have to remember, asking for help is part of trying your best. Trust me, I’ve been teaching for eight years. There’s something that every student needs a little extra help with.”
Javon and Tyler nodded. Then they looked at each other.
“Mr. T?” Javon said, “Neither one of us gets these letters at all .”
“And we both aren’t happy with our grades on this test,” Tyler added.
“What can we do?” Javon finished, grinning at Tyler.
Both Tyler and Mr. T smiled back at him.

articles.title: Diving Deep with Marine Biologists
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains the work of different types of marine biologists.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, DC. In this article, she describes different types of marine biologists and what they do.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the different types of work that marine biologists do.
articles.permissions: "Diving Deep with Marine Biologists" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Oceans cover more than 70% of Earth’s surface, yet humans have explored less than 20% of these waters. The average depth of the ocean is 12,100 feet — more than 2.3 miles down into a dark abyss. The depth and breadth of Earth’s waters make studying them both challenging and expensive.

But where humans have ventured, they encountered amazing creatures. Like the ocean sunfish — the world’s largest bony fish, weighing up to 4,600 pounds and growing 10 feet in length. Or the blobfish, rightfully named the world’s ugliest fish, with its gelatinous face and drooping nose. Or the fangtooth fish, which looks like it swam right out of a horror movie.

Marine biologists are one kind of scientist responsible for exploring Earth’s oceans. From a 400,000 pound blue whale to a microscopic plankton, marine biologists study life in the seas and even the waters themselves. They analyze the behavior and anatomy of marine organisms, threats to these organisms, and how humans impact marine life. Let’s get a closer look at this field by learning about three marine biologists.
ADJANY COSTA, ICHTHYOLOGIST

Adjany Costa is an ichthyologist [ik-thee-ol-uh-jist]. A what? Ichthyologists study bony fish. They analyze fish behavior, their habitats, and fish evolution. Costa grew up in the southern African nation of Angola. She spent much of her childhood outside, exploring beaches and visiting national parks in Southern Africa. “That was my comfort place to be free and enjoy life. I always had an idea that I wanted to study nature, but I didn’t think I could have a future doing that,” Costa shares in an interview with Jopwell .

In her home country of Angola, oil and gas companies dominate the economy, so studying biology felt like a far-fetched dream. Furthermore, she felt the weight of expectations that her schooling should lead to a practical career, one that could provide for her family. Thus, despite her love of nature, ichthyology did not even cross her mind.

After switching majors multiple times in college, her father eventually encouraged her to study biology. Studying marine biology took Costa around the world to Brazil, Germany, Spain, Belize, Portugal, and Belgium.

After graduation, Costa worked on a conservation project for National Geographic called the Okavango Wilderness Project. The project aimed to protect the Okavango River Basin, a wetland in southern Africa that is one of the world's most biodiverse ecosystems and a water source for millions of people in Africa. Sadly, deforestation, growing cities, and uncontrolled fires have threatened the river basin and the many creatures that rely on it.

As the project's ichthyologist, Costa documents and studies the Okavango River Basin's fish. On her first expedition, Costa navigated the rivers using a traditional canoe known as a mokoro. She slept in a river-side tent during her four-month stay and ate most of her meals from tin cans. As she traveled along the river, she collected samples of fish and documented their presence and absence in different areas.

When Costa is not gathering fish data in the field, she spends her days in labs analyzing findings and keeping a record of specimens Costa also works with Angolan communities to educate others about the importance of protecting the river.
HOWARD ROSENBAUM, MARINE MAMMALOGIST

Like Costa, Howard Rosenbaum’s career as a marine mammalogist — a scientist studying sea mammals like whales, dolphins, and porpoises — began with a love for nature. Rosenbaum spent his summers on the waters near Long Island, New York. As a kid, he whale-watched and fished. “You’d be on a boat right up next to some of the largest animals that ever lived. It clearly had some effect. It was awe-inspiring for me,” Rosenbaum said in an interview with Yale’s Graduate School of Arts and Sciences. “Fast forward, traveling through the national parks as a teenager with my mom and cousin, biking through Maine and Nova Scotia, I had some really cool experiences outdoors and in nature.”

Rosenbaum studied marine biology in college and spent two years traveling the world following southern humpback whales. Now fully obsessed with whales, he earned his Ph.D. at Yale University, learning how whales’ DNA teaches about their past — how whales are related and how human activity impacts these marine mammals.

Rosenbaum currently serves as the Director of the Ocean Giants Program, a nonprofit that works to protect sharks, whales, dolphins, and sea turtles. Oceans Giants analyzes whales' genes to understand the differences between whale populations and to examine their health. Whale populations with genetic diversity can more easily adapt to environmental changes. The Ocean Giants Program uses this data to target oceans where whales breed and feed so these waters can be protected. Rosenbaum and his team use this information to help governments and international organizations create whale protection plans.
YASMIN MEEDA, MARINE MOLECULAR BIOLOGIST

Yasmin Meeda asks lots of questions. As a child, she always wanted to know "why" and was fascinated by science. “After learning that over 90% of the ocean remained unexplored, I knew that there were so many unanswered questions, and it was then that I knew that I wanted to study the oceans,” Meeda shared in an interview with Women in Ocean Science .

But unlike other marine biologists, Meeda was not water obsessed. She couldn’t scuba dive and wasn't even a big fan of swimming. Instead, she prefers to work in a laboratory as a marine molecular biologist. “A typical day for me will include spending around 8 hours in the lab collecting data before being able to go back to my desk to write up my findings,” Meeda explains. “What I love about lab-based research is that every day can be different, depending on what the previous results are.”

Marine molecular biologists use marine life to address environmental challenges and help humans. For example, marine molecular biologists are working to develop cancer treatments from deep-sea sponges. Meeda studies diatoms, tiny algae that produce 20% of the oxygen humans breathe. Diatoms are very adaptable. When ocean conditions are just right, they bloom and flourish, but they stop growing and hibernate when the ocean is unfavorable. Meeda analyzes how diatoms' ability to respond rapidly to their environment could be used in health care, food production, or conservation.

articles.title: Dart Mission: Fact or Fiction
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: This article describes how scientists at NASA are working to prevent asteroids from hitting Earth.
articles.intro: Asteroids are small, rocky objects that circle around the sun. They come in all sizes from very tiny to many miles wide. There are thousands of them in our solar system. In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke shares how scientists observe, or watch, these space objects and work to keep us safe from them.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, write down words and phrases that show how the author feels about the DART mission.
articles.permissions: "Dart Mission: Fact or Fiction" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: A meteor shower destroyed Space Shuttle Atlantis in outer space. Now, an asteroid the size of Texas hurtles towards Earth and threatens to destroy life on the planet. Fact or fiction?
Fiction. This scene played out in the 1998 space movie Armageddon , where NASA works with oil-field drillers to blow up an asteroid before it strikes Earth.
An asteroid as large as a six-story building explodes over Chelyabinsk, Russia. The explosion creates shock waves that destroy building windows, injuring more than a thousand people. Fact or fiction?
Fact. About 20 miles above the city of Chelyabinsk, a meteor exploded when it hit Earth's atmosphere and released more energy than 20 Hiroshima atomic bombs.

Asteroid-Earth collisions like the one in Russia are extremely rare. Nevertheless, the US government and NASA have taken steps to detect asteroids and protect Earth against possible harm. Since 2005, NASA has closely watched near-Earth objects (NEOs), or asteroids and comets coming within 28 million miles of Earth's orbit around the Sun. NASA has worked on finding and identifying 90% of these potentially dangerous space rocks.

But, asteroid counting wasn't enough. So, in 2016, NASA established the Planetary Defense Coordination Office to hunt asteroids that could threaten Earth before they become a problem. "You've got to find them [asteroids and other space objects] before you can get them, and you want to find them early,” Kelly Fast, manager of NASA's Near-Earth Object Observations Program, shared. “You want to find these things years or decades in advance."

The Planetary Defense Coordination Office also develops strategies to stop future asteroid-Earth collisions. The DART mission — short for the Double Asteroid Redirection Test — is their first attempt to attack an asteroid in outer space.

On November 23, 2021, NASA launched a small spacecraft aimed at an asteroid over 6 million miles from Earth. This spacecraft will travel for over ten months to meet Dimorphos, an asteroid measuring about 530 feet in diameter. NASA intends to crash the spacecraft into the small asteroid at a speed of 15,000 miles per hour to redirect the asteroid's orbit. Fact or science fiction? 100% fact.

Telescopes will observe the asteroid and measure the change in Dimorphos' course. If the asteroid-bump works, NASA's Planetary Defense Coordination Office could use this strategy in the future to protect Earth.
SCIENTISTS BEHIND THE MISSION

The DART mission took teamwork from over one hundred scientists and engineers, each with unique skills needed to build, test, launch, and monitor DART. For example, Lindley Johnson is NASA's first planetary defense officer. Johnson is responsible for protecting the planet from NEO collisions. He also shares space threats with governmental agencies, such as the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) that respond to natural disasters.

Brent Barbee and Maria Wyssling-horn worked as engineers on the DART mission. Barbee is an aerospace engineer. He designed computer simulations to test how asteroids react when struck by heavy objects, like the DART spacecraft. He determined how fast the spacecraft must collide with the asteroid, how heavy the spacecraft needs to be, and what would happen to the asteroid after the collision. Wyssling-horn works as an integration engineer. Her work also began long before DART launch day. She designed the spacecraft to meet the needs of the DART mission with a team of engineers.

Omar Baez is NASA's Senior Launch Director. He directed the launch of the DART spacecraft, which was sent into space on the top of a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket. His job is all logistics. He works with governments and companies to reserve the launch date and collaborates with engineers and scientists to make sure take-offs go smoothly. Baez also manages launch countdowns. 5-4-3-2-1-blast-off!
DEVELOPING FUTURE DEFENSES

To be clear, DART is merely a test to see if NASA can move objects in space. The asteroid under attack, Dimorphos, is not a threat to Earth. But, scientists estimate that there are thousands of NEOs around the same size as Dimorphos that could cause damage if they hit Earth. That's why NASA's Planetary Defense Coordination Office is developing technological weapons to defend against NEOs that could threaten Earth in the future.

“The results from the DART mission are going to inform our hypothetical work on planetary defense going forward,” said aerospace engineer Barbee. “It's going to be affecting the way we think about planetary defense mission design for many years to come, so the importance of the mission in that regard really can't be understated.”

articles.title: I Found a Four-Leaf Clover
articles.author: Jack Prelutsky
articles.description: A speaker describes negative experiences after finding a lucky four-leaf clover.
articles.intro: Jack Prelutsky is an American writer of children's poetry who has published over 50 poetry collections. He is known for his humorous and playful poems. In this poem, a speaker describes what happens after they find a four-leaf clover.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker feels about the four-leaf clover and the things that happen.
articles.permissions: "I Found a Four-Leaf Clover" by Jack Prelutsky from Kids Pick The Funniest Poems by Bruce Lansky, copyright © 1991. Reprinted by permission of Running Press Kids, an imprint of Hachette Book Group, Inc.

blocks.text: I found a four-leaf clover
and was happy with my find,
but with time to think it over,
I’ve entirely changed my mind.
I concealed it in my pocket,
safe inside a paper pad,
soon, much swifter than a rocket,
my good fortune turned to bad.
I smashed my fingers in a door,
I dropped a dozen eggs,
I slipped and tumbled to the floor,
a dog nipped both my legs,
my ring slid down the bathtub drain,
my pen leaked on my shirt,
I barked my shin, I missed my train,
I sat on my dessert.
I broke my brand-new glasses,
and I couldn’t find my keys,
I stepped in spilled molasses,
and was stung by angry bees.
When the kitten ripped the curtain,
and the toast burst into flame,
I was absolutely certain
that the clover was to blame.
I buried it discreetly
in the middle of a field,
now my luck has changed completely,
and my wounds have almost healed.
If I ever find another,
I will simply let it be,
or I’ll give it to my brother —
he deserves it more than me.

options.label: Bad days are more common than good days.

People who have bad luck are happier than those who have good luck.

articles.title: Digging For Dinos At The Wyoming Dinosaur Center
articles.author: India James
articles.description: The Wyoming Dinosaur Center provides a unique, hands-on experience for its visitors.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James describes what it is like to look for dinosaur fossils at the Wyoming Dinosaur Center. Fossils are the remains or traces of plants or animals that lived long ago.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how visitors spend their day at the Wyoming Dinosaur Center.
articles.permissions: "Digging For Dinos At The Wyoming Dinosaur Center" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Fossils are amazing time capsules.

Paleontologists are people who study animal and plant fossils. They use fossils to help understand animals and plants that lived long before humans were around. The Wyoming Dinosaur Center is jam packed with fossils of dinosaurs and animals from around the world.

Like any museum, displays are carefully set up so that visitors can see the exhibits. But, unlike most museums, the Wyoming Dinosaur Center lets visitors go beyond just looking. At this museum, visitors can help paleontologists dig in a real excavation and maybe even uncover some dinosaur bones of their own!
A Dinosaur’s Favorite Place
The Wyoming Dinosaur Center is located in the modern city of Thermopolis, Wyoming. These days it’s a hilly area covered in reddish-brown dirt and rock. Long ago, however, the area was very different.

Sauropods, some of the largest land creatures ever, lived 65 to 150 million years ago. Back then, there was a river over the area where Thermopolis is today. When the dinosaurs died, the river carried their bones downstream to where they were covered in silt and eventually became fossils.

Visitors to the Wyoming Dinosaur Center can see the largest dinosaur ever discovered in Wyoming. “Jimbo” is a Supersaurus . This dino is so big that it takes up the whole length of the museum just to display the fossils!
These days, there are many dig sites just down the road from the museum. It’s not just trained scientists that get to dig for dino bones. Visitors to the museum can be a part of the excavation, too!
Dig for a Day
Children and adults can both help excavate, or uncover, fossils. It’s hard work, but it’s a lot of fun!
The day begins at 8 am when visitors meet with a museum guide and learn about the dig site. Visitors dig with real tools, so the safety talk is an important part of the day.
Then it’s off to the dig site!

There are tens of thousands of bones buried in the area around Thermopolis. As part of a real excavation team, visitors go wherever the scientists need help. There’s a good chance that no matter where they’re digging, they’ll find something special.

Big tools like shovels and pickaxes are used to remove top layers of dirt and rock. Shouts of joy and surprise burst from around the site as volunteers exclaim as they unearth a fossil. Professional paleontologists act as guides, and they’re never far away. Once a fossil has been discovered, they help visitors use smaller tools like trowels and even dull oyster knives to carefully clear away the rest of the mud and rock.

Scientists teach visitors every step they need to complete in order to preserve the find. The name of whoever made the discovery, even if they’re not a professional scientist, is written down along with the fossil information. It will be recorded in the Wyoming Dinosaur Center archive. The visitor will always be known for discovering that fossil!
After the Dig
A lot of the work that usually goes on behind-the-scenes is open at the Wyoming Dinosaur Center.

One of the first ways that fossils are taken care of behind the scenes is to make sure that each fossil is clean. Fossils are delicate, and they must be carefully handled and stored. Did you know that tools exactly like the ones your dentist works with are used on fossils? These “micro tools” are used to remove as little as a single grain of sand. Special glue can also be used to help hold together a fossil that is especially fragile.

Another way that paleontologists take care of delicate fossils is by making molds and casts of them. This means that scientists can make many copies of a fossil. Sometimes, paleontologists are worried a fossil could be damaged if put on display. They’ll make a copy that looks just like the real fossil. That way visitors can see exactly what the fossil looked like, but the real fossil stays safe.

Digging up dinosaur bones is a way of touching pieces of the past. It’s exciting to discover something that has been buried for many millions of years. At the Wyoming Dinosaur Center, visitors can be a part of the action!

articles.title: Dino Discovery: An Amazing, All-Purpose Body Part
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler describes the Psittacosaurus's multipurpose and unique rear.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes what scientists have learned about a surprising body part on Psittacosaurus .
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view on the Psittacosaurus’s rear.
articles.permissions: "Dino Discovery: An Amazing, All-Purpose Body Part" By T. J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: It’s one of the most amazing dinosaur body parts that scientists have ever found. No, we’re not talking about the giant, dagger-like teeth of T. rex , the mighty clubbed tail of Ankylosaurus , or the super-long neck of Apatasaurus .
We’re talking about a little dinosaur called Psittacosaurus and its remarkable rear.
Amazing discovery

Decades ago, paleontologists — the scientists who study life long ago — unearthed the special Psittacosaurus (SIT-eh-keh-SOR-us). The beaked leaf-eater lived 120 million years ago in an area that is now northeastern China. It was about the size of a golden retriever, had horns on the sides of its head, and sported a bristly tail.

Most of the time, paleontologists find only the fossilized skeletons of dinosaurs. Bones and teeth take longer to decay, so they have a better chance of becoming fossils than an animal’s soft parts. But on rare occasions, scientists find a dinosaur body that still has bits of soft tissues, such as blood vessels, feathers, or skin.
This Psittacosaurus was one of those rare finds. It was in incredibly good shape and even had bits of scaly, reddish-brown skin on it. It also had an intact tush.

OK, if you want to get all scientific about it, the proper term is “cloacal vent.” The vent is the opening under the animal’s tail, and the cloaca (kloh-EY-kuh) is the muscular chamber, or space, behind it. Early scientists didn’t seem to be too impressed with the body part; the word “cloaca” is Latin for “sewer.” But the paleontologists who recently studied the Psittacosaurus were excited to find it. As far as anyone knows, it’s the only dino rear ever discovered.
Multitasker
And, wow, is it amazing! It did lots of things for the little dino. That has helped scientists learn about how the dinosaur lived and how similar or different it was to other animals.

For example, most mammals — including humans — have separate holes for going Number 1 and Number 2. That’s not the case for other animals, like birds, reptiles, and amphibians. Like other reptiles, Psittacosaurus’s cloaca did everything the dino needed to do: peeing, pooping, laying eggs, you name it. (But, no, not eating or breathing. It had a mouth and nostrils for that.)
Paleontologist Jakob Vinther, who studied the rear, used an analogy to explain its multipurpose wonders to Popular Science magazine: “It’s the Swiss army knife of back ends.”
Even among the other animals that have a one-hole-for-everything body part, Psittacosaurus’s cloaca still stands out.
Singular style

The little dino’s backside was not shaped like any other animal’s — and the researchers checked. Biologist Diane A. Kelly, a member of the research team and an expert on animals’ rear ends, told the New York Times that most cloacal vents just look like slits or rounded holes, sometimes with wrinkly skin or scales around them.

Psittacosaurus’s rear was more of a V-shape and darker colored than the rest of its body. Scientists think the color probably helped get the attention of possible mates—much like the bright-red rears of baboons.

Crocodiles, whose rears are a bit similar to Psittacosaurus’s , also have special scent glands used to woo a mate. The researchers think Psittacosaurus may have been able to release its own dino-pleasing perfume, too.

The researchers were so delighted with the discovery of the dinosaur derrière that they were even happy to find a little piece of poop in it. (No worries. It was fossilized and as hard as a rock.) They asked artist Robert Nicholls, who specializes in dinosaurs, to draw Psittacosaurus and reconstruct a 3D model of the dino.
If you’re ever in Frankfurt, Germany, you can check out the dino’s brilliant body part yourself. It’s on display at the Senckenberg Natural History Museum with other amazing discoveries.

articles.title: The Ring of Fire
articles.author: Marcia Amidon Lusted
articles.description: Learn about the area in the Pacific Ocean that is home to many of the world's natural disasters.
articles.intro: Natural disasters like earthquakes, tsunamis, and volcanic eruptions happen all over the world and cause huge amounts of damage. In this informational text, Marcia Amidon Lusted tells about an area in the Pacific Ocean known as the Ring of Fire.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why natural disasters happen in the Ring of Fire.
articles.permissions: The Ring of Fire by Marcia Amidon Lusted. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Indonesia. Beautiful beaches with glittering white sand. Vivid blue seas. Palm trees bending in the breeze as the warm sun shines through the green fronds. It sounds like a tropical paradise! But deep beneath this paradise lies the potential for volcanoes, earthquakes, and monster tsunami waves. This is because Indonesia sits in a region known as the Ring of Fire. The Ring of Fire sounds exotic and fun, like "Untitled" by Julius_Silver is licensed under CC0. an act at the circus. But this is a region of the world that has 75% of the world’s volcanoes and 90% of its earthquakes. The Ring of Fire isn’t actually shaped like a ring. It is a 25,000-mile squiggly upside down U curving around the top of the Pacific Ocean from New Zealand to Japan, around the coast of western North America, down to the tip of South America. Volcanoes form deep beneath the ocean’s floor, and there are more active volcanoes along the Ring of Fire than anywhere else in the world. Most of them are located on the western edge, from Russia’s Kamchatka Peninsula, through the islands of Japan, to New Zealand. It makes sense that a place with so many active volcanoes would be called the Ring of Fire. But why is it also home to so many earthquakes? While it seems like the surface of the earth is solid and doesn’t move, it’s actually made up of about 20 tectonic plates that float on the surface of the 1. Vivid(adjective)bright and strong 2. Paradise(noun)a very beautiful, pleasant, or peaceful place that seems to be perfect 3. the chance that something could happen 4. strange or unusual 5. A volcano is active if it is likely to erupt, or break open, and send out hot melted rock and ash. It is also active if it has erupted recently. earth’s crust. These plates fit together like pieces of a giant jigsaw puzzle. But because they move, sometimes they bump up against each other, like people who are all trying to move through a narrow doorway at the same time. They might even get stuck. The place where two tectonic plates get stuck against each other is called a transform boundary. As the plates keep trying to move past each other, pressure builds, and then suddenly the heavier plate is shoved under the other plate. This is called subduction, a sideways or downward movement. As they slip past another, these plates can release a huge amount of energy. It is the seismic (vibration) waves caused by this energy that create an earthquake. The area of the Ring of Fire has an almost unbroken series of subduction zones because so many tectonic plates overlap there, which is why 76% of the earth’s seismic energy is released there. It also has areas where there are transform boundaries and plates slide past each other. Together, these geologic features are what make this area so unstable.

When two tectonic plates are stuck in a subduction zone under the ocean, the plate that is sliding beneath the other plate may contain water. As subduction forces that plate to go deeper and deeper into the earth’s crust, that water heats up and pressure builds until melting occurs. Extremely hot magma forms, and eventually this magma can rise to the surface and create a chain of volcanoes. Because there are so many earthquakes in the Ring of Fire, there are many volcanoes as well. And when earthquakes happen under the ocean, they can produce huge waves called tsunamis. These waves can sweep away entire towns as much as thousands of miles away. It is these three reasons — volcanoes, earthquakes, tsunamis — that make Indonesia the most disaster-prone place in the world. Earthquakes and volcanoes are dangerous natural disasters by themselves. But the tsunami waves created by earthquakes can be even deadlier because there often is no warning that they are coming. Fortunately, there are tsunami warning centers around the world that run 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. They monitor earthquake activity and any tsunami waves that might be generated and send out warnings. They have made it possible to warn people of potential tsunamis much sooner than ever before. Scientists believe that the Ring of Fire may have existed for 35 million years. In the last 11,700 years, 850-1000 volcanoes have been active there, including the four largest volcanoes ever to erupt on Earth. And most of the volcanoes above sea level that are still active are found there as well. The Ring of Fire may be one of the most dangerous places in the world for natural disasters, but it is also one of the most beautiful. And with help from warning systems and 6. parts of the earth’s surface 7. Occur(verb)to happen 8. Extreme(adjective)far beyond what is normal 9. likely to have a disaster happen 10. Activity(noun)actions of a particular kind 11. created emergency response planning, people can live there, and visit there, safely.

articles.title: Eat Bugs, Save the Planet?
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Tracy Vonder Brink describes how eating insects could help people and the planet.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink describes a surprising idea to help our planet.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view on eating bugs.
articles.permissions: Permissions line: "Eat Bugs, Save the Planet?" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Would you snack on crackers made from crickets, then wash them down with a drink made from fly larvae? Not as a dare, but as part of your everyday eating? It’s possible the future may bring exactly that.

By 2050, it’s estimated there will be 9 billion people on Earth. (Nearly 2 billion more than today.) To feed them all, the amount of food produced will have to almost double. We’ll need more land for crops and livestock. Raising more food will also take more water for the plants and animals.

A lot of natural resources already go toward making what we eat. Producing one person’s food in the United States requires about 1,300 gallons (4,921 liters) of water every single day. How can our resources keep up if we need to double the amount we use now? Some people think raising insects as food could help.

The practice of eating insects as part of a regular diet is called entomophagy (say en-tuh-MAHF-uh-jee), and about 2 billion people around the world do it. In fact, more than 2000 insect species are enjoyed in about 113 different countries. What kinds of tasty insects are eaten? Beetles, caterpillars, bees, ants, grasshoppers, and crickets are most often on the menu. In Thailand, 20,000 cricket farms produce 7500 tons (6800 mt) of crickets for food every year.

Of course, not all cultures are comfortable with eating insects. If your first thought was, “Gross!” you were probably raised by generations of people who believed you shouldn’t touch bugs, much less eat them. So, why should the world consider insects for dinner?

Raising insects uses fewer resources than other animals. Most insects can be raised anywhere because they don’t need wide, open spaces to roam — a cricket “farm” is really a warehouse filled with thousands of bins where the crickets live. Many insects aren’t picky about what they eat and can be fed with waste products created by farming and raising livestock. That means we can recycle waste into insect food. Insects do need water, but they require far less than other animals.

Picture your family’s last trip to a burger restaurant. It took about 25 pounds (11.3 kg) of cow feed and 1800 gallons of water to end up with one pound of ground beef — about four burgers. The same amount of crickets only needs two pounds (0.9 kg) of feed and one gallon (3.8 liters) of water. If a family of four ate insects instead of meat for one day a week, about 172,000 gallons (651,000 liters) of water would be saved every year.

Beyond using fewer resources, raising insects for food could help the planet in another, more surprising way — they burp and fart far less than other animals. Passing gas is a natural result of digestion. As the body breaks down food, gases such as methane and carbon dioxide are released. In a cow, the methane goes out when it burps. (In people, a burp is swallowed air coming back up. Gases are released through the other end.) Methane and carbon dioxide rise into the atmosphere and collect in a layer around Earth. That layer can trap heat and warm the planet, leading to climate change. About 15 percent of the planet’s methane comes from animals such as cows. Insects such as mealworms, crickets, and locusts release 80 to 100 times less methane than cattle.

Insects are also good sources of nutrition. Grasshoppers, crickets, and some kinds of worms are rich in vitamins. A serving of crickets has more iron than a serving of beef. Mealworms have almost twice as much protein as lean chicken breast. Some species of crickets are even high in fiber.

Changing to an insect diet won’t happen overnight. You can’t go into your backyard and grab a bunch of bugs for dinner. Not all species are safe to eat, and wild-caught insects may have been sprayed with poisonous pesticides. Insects need to be raised safely and in healthy conditions, like any other food.

Cultures where insects are thought of as yucky will also need to change their minds — maybe by starting with cricket powder mixed into food instead of eating the bugs themselves. It is possible to make that change, though. People were doubtful about plant-based “meat,” and now you can find Beyond Burgers and Impossible Burgers in most grocery stores. Someday cricket burgers may join them on the shelf.

options.label: If people start eating crickets, climate change will soon come to an end.
Though no one does it today, in the future people may have to eat insects.
Someday bugs may be more popular to eat than common foods we enjoy now.
There would be many benefits to the Earth if people started to eat more insects.

articles.title: Spikes, Armor & Hard Heads: Dino Defenses?
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler describes the purpose of some of the most bizarre features of dinosaurs.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, Resler discusses if dinosaurs' odd body parts were more for defense, like protecting themselves from others, or for display, to show off.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why dinosaurs had body parts like spikes, armor, and hard heads.
articles.permissions: Permissions line: "Spikes, Armor & Hard Heads: Dino Defenses?" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Dinosaurs definitely rocked some bizarre looks: bony plates running down their backs, horns jutting out from their faces, spikes on their tails, armored plates, shield-like frills around their heads. They looked ready for battle. No surprise. Dinosaurs needed some good defenses with the likes of Tyrannosaurus rex and Spinosaurus running around, ready to snatch smaller dinosaurs up for their next meals.

For a long time, dino fans and scientists alike marveled at the amazing variety of defenses and weapons that dinosaurs displayed. But lately, scientists have been studying how the strange body parts evolved and functioned. They examined several theories and came up with a shocking conclusion: Some of those so-called “defenses” were not great protection after all!

Take, for example, dinosaur horns. Paleontologists are people who study the fossils of animals and plants, the remains or impressions preserved as molds or casts in rocks. When the fossils of the first horned dinosaurs were found on Triceratops , paleontologists thought these were probably used for defense. “You have these iconic images of Triceratops doing battle with Tyrannosaurus rex ,” paleontologist Caleb Brown told the Canadian newspaper Calgary Herald . But as scientists discovered more and more horned dinosaurs over the past couple of decades, they realized that the horns and frills (the big shield-like parts) varied a lot among those dinosaurs. That was an important clue — and one that raised doubts about them being used mainly for defense.

Nature tends to figure out the best way of doing things, including how to protect an animal. If horns and frills were the answer, one shape and arrangement of them would have provided better protection than other shapes and arrangements. Over time, all of the different types of horned dinosaurs (a group that scientists call “ceratopsians”) would have evolved to have that ideal protection — or at least something very similar. Rhinos, for example, have one or two horns at the end of their snouts — nowhere else.

But the ceratopsian dinosaurs had an amazing variety of horn and frill arrangements. The famous Triceratops had three spikes — two large ones above its eyes and a smaller one on its snout — and a slightly jagged frill. Regaliceratops , had a much larger nose horn and a large, crown-shaped frill, but only stubby little horns over its eyes. Styracosaurus rocked four to six spikes jutting out from an extra-long frill, and two little horns poking out from its cheeks in addition to a large nose horn. Centrosaurus had just one large horn on its snout but many small ones edging its frill, including a couple that curled forward.

“There are a number of species where their horns would be pretty much useless in defense,” said Brown, who discovered Regaliceratops . “What we’re thinking now is that these were used for display. These were to impress members of the same or opposite sex and communicate with other species. That plate at the back of his skull is pretty much a billboard advertising for that individual.”

Fancy frills — the fancier, the better — helped the dinosaurs get the attention of possible mates. They were all about looking good, putting on a display — like how a peacock spreads its beautiful feathers. Does that mean frills and horns were only for display? Not at all! The ceratopsians’ horns may have added to their dashing looks in the eyes of potential mates, but they also probably helped the dinosaurs fight off rivals — much like two male antelope will lock horns and push each other back and forth until one gives up and leaves. And if a T. rex is coming after you, you use whatever you have to defend yourself!

Over the years, scientists also wondered whether the horns and frills helped the animals recognize members of their own species or maybe even helped decide who was the top dino in a group. But most scientists have ruled out those theories. These days, they generally agree that these amazing and bizarre body parts evolved mainly for display. The horned-and-frilled dinosaurs were lovers, not fighters.

The same goes for the standout features on other dinosaurs. Pachycephalosaurus had a huge, helmet-like skull that was about 20 times thicker than other dinosaurs’ skulls and ringed with short, spiky horns. It was easy to picture Pachycephalosaurs charging head-first into predators or rivals — much like bighorn rams butting heads. But that’s not likely. Scientists haven’t found scars suggesting the dinos rammed into things. Plus, the dinos’ necks were probably curved — not straight and rigid enough to be used as battering rams (or to crash through walls. Sorry, Jurassic World .) So what good were those domes? A dashing dome probably helped the dino attract a mate. And if a rival tried to steal the mate away, Pachycephalosaurus may have whacked it with its dome — but on the side, not straight on.

Even the huge club at the end of Ankylosaurus’ s tail was mainly for show, advertising the animal’s health and strength to potential mates. But yes, if Ankylosaurus needed to protect itself, it probably swung that club — scientists found a couple damaged fossil tail clubs that looked like they had smacked something. But charging into battle with tail swinging? Nope. Same story for the dino’s tank-like armor, which included spikes running along its body and jutting out from its head. The armor probably helped protect the dino, but scientists think it was more important for looking tough in front of rivals. As it turns out, Ankylosaurus’s weird shape probably offered more protection than its armor. On the science news website, Live Science , paleontologist Kenneth Carpenter described Ankylosaurus as being wide and relatively flat: hard for a predator to get a firm grip on it — even without all the armor.

By now, you can probably guess why Stegosaurus had a row of bony plates running down its back: to attract potential mates and scare off rivals. For a while, scientists thought the plates might help the dinos cool down when they were too hot or soak up some warmth when they were too cold. But now we’re pretty sure that the plates were for display — though they may have offered some protection, too. The spikes at the end of Stegosaurus’s tail also may have attracted mates, but paleontologists found plenty of evidence that Stegosaurus used them for defense, too. On many fossils, the sharp spike tips had been broken off but then started to heal, growing new bone. Stegosaurus probably slashed an attacking predator if they were side by side. But if the predator came in at an angle, Stegosaurus’s spikes might drive in so deeply that they’d get stuck and break off.

It’s cool to think of frills as shields and horns as weapons, and kind of disappointing to learn they only sometimes used their horns, frills, and spikes. It turns out that, more often, they relied on defenses similar to what animals use today. Many plant-eating dinosaurs — including Stegosaurus , horned-and-frilled ceratopsians and others — found safety in numbers, eating and traveling in herds, like modern-day wildebeest or springbok antelope. Speedy dinosaurs like Velociraptor and Gallimimus — bird-like theropods that ran on two legs — could run away from trouble (though, as predators, they often used their speed to catch their next meal). And like modern adult elephants, the massive, long-necked sauropods’ size kept them safe from predators, though they often stayed in herds to protect their young.

It’s possible that some dinosaurs even relied on camouflage. Paleontologists recently found rare dino fossils with well-preserved skin — one of a horse-sized ankylosaur called Borealopelta and another of a pony-sized ceratopsian called Psittacosaurus — which showed they were light-colored on the bottom and darker on the top, a camouflage pattern known as countershading.

Though the craziest dino body parts were mainly for attracting mates and fighting off rivals — and only rarely for defense — dinosaurs had a wide arsenal of defenses. Size, speed, coloring, and hanging with their buddies helped them survive in their wild world.

articles.title: The Bone Wars
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: Two rival paleontologists fight for fame and notoriety.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author who loves to tell stories. A former journalist and professor, she researches and writes nonfiction and fiction texts for kids and adults. In this article, she tells the story of the friendship between Edward Drinker Cope (1840-1897) and Othniel Charles Marsh (1831-1899), two important paleontologists, or scientists, who studied life from long ago.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view on Cope and Marsh.
articles.permissions: "The Bone Wars" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: “SCIENTISTS WAGE BITTER WARFARE,” the New York Herald announced on January 12, 1890. With that newspaper article, one of the most bitter rivalries in the history of paleontology — and maybe all of science — burst out into the open.

Edward Drinker Cope and Othniel Charles Marsh were brilliant paleontologists of the late 1800s. The two scientists led teams that dug up fossils at various sites in the United States, and they traveled the world to talk about their discoveries. Thanks to their efforts, the world learned about many extinct animals, including some of the first dinosaurs ever found. Between the two of them, they discovered Triceratops , Stegosaurus , Brontosaurus , Coelophysis and many other extinct animals. Their work helped build the young field of paleontology, increasing the number of known dinosaur species from a mere handful to more than a hundred. Many scientists go about their work humbly — but not Cope and Marsh. They loved being famous. The more famous, the better. And best of all was being more famous than each other. Each man wanted to be the first to discover the fossil of an unknown animal and the first to publish his findings in scientific journals. That way, he’d have the right to name the new species.

But their rivalry — which became known as the “Bone Wars” — soon became as important as their science. They rushed to find the next big fossil discovery, resulting in careless mistakes. They even resorted to ruthless tactics to find fossils — and to hurt their rival’s reputation, according to the Academy of Natural Sciences of Drexel University.
Friendship sours

Marsh and Cope started out as friends. They met in 1863 in Germany, where they were studying natural history, and hit it off. After they returned to the United States, they even named new fossil discoveries for each other. In 1867, Cope named a flat-headed newt-like amphibian fossil Ptyonius marshii after Marsh. The following year, Marsh returned the honor by naming a huge swimming reptile Mosasaurus copeanus .
But their friendship did not last long.

In 1868, Cope invited his friend to a mineral quarry in New Jersey, where Cope had arranged for the miners to send him interesting fossils they unearthed. Marsh was impressed with the fossils — so impressed that he wanted them for himself! Behind Cope’s back, Marsh bribed the miners to send the fossils directly to him at Yale University instead of to Cope. When Cope later found out, he was furious. He never considered Marsh a true friend again.

The bad feelings only grew worse. That same year, Cope announced the discovery of a huge prehistoric sea reptile he called Elasmosaurus , and he prepared a scientific article with a drawing of the beast. But Cope mixed up its long neck and tail — and drew the beast’s head at the tip of its tail!

Cope’s mentor at the Academy of Natural Sciences in Philadelphia, the respected paleontologist Joseph Leidy, pointed out the mistake. Marsh also claimed that he noted the error when he saw the creature’s skeleton in a museum. “When I informed Professor Cope of it, his wounded vanity received a shock from which it has never recovered, and he has since been my bitter enemy,” Marsh wrote in an 1890 letter to the New York Herald newspaper.

Cope was so embarrassed that he tried to collect all the copies of his scientific article and replace them with a corrected version, according to the Academy of Natural Sciences. But Marsh kept a copy of the original article with the mistake and taunted Cope about it for decades.
Battle heats up

The competition to be the first to discover and name cool new fossils grew ever more extreme. Cope rushed to publish his findings first. Early on, he announced his findings via telegram — the only way to instantly communicate information back then. Then, in 1877, he took the bold step of buying a scientific journal, The American Naturalist , so he could publish his discoveries in it. Between 1879 and 1880 alone, he published a whopping 76 scientific papers.

Marsh battled back. He came from a rich family and knew lots of important people — two advantages he used to try to gain the upper-hand in the rivalry. He hired a small army of assistants to help him unearth more fossils quickly. He even hired spies to make sure Cope wasn’t finding better discoveries. Sometimes his assistants even destroyed fossils so that Cope wouldn’t get them, and both scientists’ assistants probably raided their rival’s dig sites.

Marsh also decided to use his wealth and connections to limit Cope’s access to some of the best fossil sites. In 1878, Marsh bought a Wyoming quarry that contained tons of fossils, including a great skeleton of a massive, long-necked dinosaur that Marsh named Brontosaurus . He also got himself named chief paleontologist of the newly formed U.S. Geological Survey, which gave him the power to make sure Cope’s work didn’t receive any government funding — a source of support that both scientists had relied on.
Cope wouldn’t go down without a fight. He took what money he had left and bought silver mines in New Mexico. But the risky move did not pay off, and Cope lost everything.

In a desperate attempt to bring down Marsh, Cope went to the New York Herald with accusations against him. The resulting article, “SCIENTISTS WAGE BITTER WARFARE,” created a public scandal and even prompted a Congressional investigation. As a result, Congress eliminated the U.S. Geological Survey’s paleontology department, costing Marsh his position and most of his income.
Rivals ruined

Despite their immense contributions to the field of paleontology — work that is still respected today — the bitter rivalry between Cope and Marsh cost them their reputations and livelihoods, leaving them angry and poor.

Even as he faced the end of his life, Cope would not give up the fight. He donated his skull to science and challenged Marsh to do the same. That way, he figured, the world would know who had the bigger brain. Marsh did not accept the challenge.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Thinking about the article, how did Cope and Marsh's greed affect them? What is another example of greed you have seen in your own life, media, or another story? What message about greed do you think the author wants the reader to learn?
In the text, Marsh and Cope kept trying to get revenge on each other. Is revenge ever justified? When is it okay to get back at someone? When is it not okay? How did revenge affect Marsh and Cope?

Marsh and Cope's actions were meant to harm each other, but they also affected others. For example, their rivalry led to the government eliminating the U.S. Geological Survey's paleontology department, which took away funding for research. How else did Marsh and Cope's actions affect others? Why is it important to think about the consequences of your actions?

Marsh and Cope wanted to become famous. According to the text, "They loved being famous. The more famous, the better. And best of all was being more famous than each other" (Paragraph 2). If you could be famous for something, what would you want to be famous for? Why? In your opinion, is fame a blessing or a curse?
